actions.thought: I am in UTG position (first to act) with 4s 4h and a 100BB stack. While 100BB is deep enough to set-mine, UTG is the most difficult position to play. Opening small pairs like 44 from UTG leaves me vulnerable to 3-bets (forcing a fold) or playing out of position post-flop with a weak hand that is difficult to navigate on most boards. Standard solid 6-max strategy often removes 22-55 from the UTG opening range to protect against these disadvantages. Since this is the first hand and I have no reads on opponents, I will take the conservative, low-variance line and fold.

3♠4♣ offsuit is a very weak, low connector with little playability from middle position. There are still several players left to act (cutoff, button, and the blinds), so calling 100 commits chips into a likely multiway pot out of position with a hand that performs poorly postflop. A raise to 200 would be thin and iso-raise range should be much stronger here. With a deep stack early in the tournament, preserving chips for better spots is correct. Fold and wait for a better opportunity.

8-2 offsuit is near the bottom of the range and performs poorly even in late position—dominated when it makes a pair and too weak to profitably open or limp. With two players left to act plus blinds, folding preserves stack and avoids low-EV spots early.

I am on the button with 9c 5c and the action has folded to me. In a 6-max game, this is a reasonable hand to open with a raise to steal the blinds or play in position. 95s has some playability post-flop, and applying pressure from the button is a standard strategy. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind.

I have pocket 7s in the SB facing a button raise to 250. This is a standard defend with a medium pocket pair. Button opens wide, and 77 has decent equity but plays better as a call than a 3-bet since I don't want to bloat the pot and face a 4-bet. I'll call and look to hit a set or play carefully postflop.

Q9s is a strong suited connector in the BB facing a button min-raise (wide range) and SB flat-call (also likely wide). Pot odds are excellent at 150 to call into 600 (4:1). Deep stacks (99BB) provide great implied odds for flush/straight potential. Defending wide here builds the pot with equity and playability OOP. Folding is too tight early in tournament; 3-betting risks too much with marginal blocker value against button. Call to see flop.

I have pocket 7s on a 5d-6c-9d flop - an overpair to the lower cards plus a gutshot straight draw (need an 8). Being OOP against the preflop aggressor, checking to them makes sense. I can call a reasonable c-bet with my pair + draw equity, or bet later if checked through.

Top pair with queen kicker on a draw-heavy board (diamond flush draw, open-enders like 78 or 47 possible). SB checked, giving me initiative vs button min-raiser (wide range) and SB defender (also wide). Deep stacks favor building pot with strong hand. Betting for value against Ax, weaker pairs, draws; protects equity by charging them. 2/3 pot size balances value/protection without overbetting OOP.

I have flopped two pair (9s and 5s) on a 9-6-5 board. While this is a strong hand, the board is quite 'wet' with both straight and flush possibilities (diamonds and connectors like 7 and 8). Elon's lead into the pre-flop raiser (me) from the big blind indicates strength or a strong draw. I should raise here to extract value from worse hands like top pair (9x) or draws, and to protect my hand against the many cards that could roll off on the turn to kill my action or outdraw me. A raise to 1500 is a standard size to put pressure on his range.

Facing a raise to 1500 after Elon bet 500 and Flash 3-bet it. Flash showed strength preflop (raised) and now is raising the flop. This screams a strong hand - likely overpairs (99, TT+), sets, or two pair. My 77 is only an overpair to two of the board cards, and I'm behind most of Flash's raising range here. The pot is 2750 and I need 1500 to call with a marginal one-pair hand and just a gutshot. This is an easy fold - I'm not getting proper odds and likely crushed.

Facing a 3-bet on a draw-heavy flop (flush draw, OESD possible) with top pair top kicker (9s Qs). Button villain had wide preflop min-raise range, now polarized post-flop to strong value (sets like 55/66, two-pair 56/59/69, 78 str8) or bluffs/draws (AdXd flush draws, Ax high floats, gutshots). Pot odds excellent: 1000 to call into 3750 (3.75:1, ~21% equity needed), and I have ~35-45% vs typical 3-bet range. Blocks 99 sets. Early tournament (hand 1, deep stacks ~90BB), prefer calling OOP to realize equity, control pot, reassess turn rather than raising (vulnerable to 4-bets/stacks) or folding (too nitty with positionally disadvantaged but equity-rich hand). Backdoor spade draw adds playability.

Heads-up on turn OOP with top pair queen kicker on a highly coordinated board (5d6c9dJd) featuring a completed diamond flush draw, potential straights (78, 7T, 8T), and villain's prior flop 3-bet indicating a polarized strong range (sets, two-pair, straights, big flush draws, or bluffs). The Jd is a scary brick that likely improves villain's range more than mine, while my backdoor spades remain live but thin. Pot is 3750 with deep effective stacks (~82BB), favoring pot control OOP rather than betting into a villain poised to raise value or check back weak hands. Checking induces bluffs from missed draws, allows realization of equity (still ~30-40% vs villain's range), and keeps pot manageable for river decisions. Early in tournament (hand 1), no need to risk stack with marginal made hand facing aggression; folding to a bet would depend on sizing, but check sets up flexible play.

The Jack of Diamonds is a very dangerous card as it completes both the flush and potential straights. Elon's check-call on the flop indicates he likely has a piece of the board or a strong draw. Now that the flush has come in, my two pair (9s and 5s) has significantly decreased in relative strength. Checking behind is the best way to control the pot size and reach a showdown or see if the board improves my hand to a full house on the river without risking a check-raise.

River OOP heads-up vs aggressive button villain who min-raised preflop (wide) and 3-bet flop on draw-heavy 569 rainbow board, indicating polarized range: strong value (sets 55/66/99/JJ unlikely, two-pair 56/59/69, straights 78 incomplete then) or semi-bluffs (AdXd flush draws, gutshots like T8/47). Mutual turn checks after Jd (flush completer, Jx possible) suggest villain missed or trapping slowplay; 7c river bricks most remaining draws (no flush/straight completer for common holdings like AdKd/QdTd) but possible 48 str8 or pairs 7s. My 9sQs tops villain's unpaired Ax/bluffs/missed draws (~40%+ equity vs that) but crushed by Jx+, two-pair+, completed str8s/flushes. Pot 3750, deep ~82BB effective, hand 1 tournament: pot control OOP essential to realize showdown value cheaply. Betting invites value raises (villain pots better) or folds worse; checking induces bluffs from missed range, sets up flexible hero-call vs small bets while folding to large overbets. No value in betting thin top pair on scary board post-check-check.

The board has become extremely connected with both flush and straight possibilities. The 7c on the river completes a straight for any hand containing an 8. My two pair (9s and 5s) has been significantly devalued by the Jack of Diamonds on the turn and the straight potential on the river. Elon's check on the river suggests he might be looking for a cheap showdown or holding a marginal hand. Since there are many hands in his range that could beat mine (flushes, straights, or better two pairs like 9-7), and I am unlikely to be called by many worse hands, checking behind is the most prudent move to realize my showdown value without risking further loss.

Fold. From UTG in a 6-handed game with 100BB effective, Q7o is outside a reasonable opening range. The hand has poor playability and will be frequently dominated or forced into difficult out-of-position, multiway pots. Calling 100 to limp/call or opening to 200 are both thin lines — raising invites 3-bets and calling gives poor equity and maneuverability postflop. Early in the tournament it’s correct to preserve stack and wait for a better spot, so I fold.

UTG has folded and action is on me in middle position with Q3 offsuit. This hand is too weak and dominated to profitably open-raise, and limping invites multiway pots where Qx makes expensive second-best hands. With 100bb stacks, best EV is to fold and preserve stack for stronger opens/spots.

In the Cutoff position with 97 offsuit, the hand is too weak to open under standard 6-max opening ranges. While the action has folded to me, 97o lacks the high-card strength or connectivity to justify a raise, and with the Button and Blinds still to act, it's more profitable to wait for a stronger starting hand.
A6o on the button with 3 folds in front - this is a clear open raise spot. Position advantage and an ace make this an easy steal attempt against only the blinds. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) is standard.

KdJd is a strong suited broadway hand with excellent playability, high card strength, and flush potential. Facing a button raise to 2.5BB in a 6-handed game, this is a clear 3-bet spot to isolate the button, build the pot with equity, and take initiative OOP. 3-bet sizing to ~3.6x the open (900 into 400 pot) is standard for value/protection OOP. BB likely folds wide. Deep stacks favor aggressive play here to maximize stack growth.

I am in the Big Blind with Ah 4c facing a Button open to 250 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 900. A4o is a weak hand to play against a 3-bet, especially cold-calling. My hand is likely dominated by the SB's value range (AQ, AK, bigger Aces) and plays poorly post-flop. Additionally, the original raiser (Button) is still to act behind me and could 4-bet or call, complicating the spot further. The pot odds do not justify a call with this holding. The disciplined play is to fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon in the SB after my button open. A6o is too weak to call a 3-bet profitably - it's easily dominated by better aces in his 3-betting range. Not a good 4-bet bluff candidate either. Standard fold.

UTG in a 6-max game, A9 offsuit is a marginal opener: it’s dominated by the ATo+/AQ+ that will continue versus an UTG raise, and it plays poorly when facing the likely aggressive 3-bets from the button/cutoff (as seen already). With 100bb stacks and no antes, there’s little incentive to force thin EV spots early—folding preserves stack and avoids tough postflop positions with a dominated ace.

I am in middle position with King-Six offsuit. This is a weak hand to open with, especially with four players still to act behind me, including the Button and the blinds. Opening this hand would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop or force me to fold to a 3-bet. Standard professional strategy dictates folding such marginal hands from this position to preserve my stack for more profitable opportunities.
Q4o is a weak offsuit hand. From the cutoff with 3 players still to act behind me (Button, SB, BB), this isn't profitable to open. It's below my opening range from this position. Easy fold.

Folded to me on the button in an unopened pot (standard blinds posted, pot 150, to call 100). Holding T8o, an offsuit one-gapper, which is within a profitable button opening range against blinds (~40-50% hands standard). Deep stacks (86BB effective), excellent position for speculative hands. Raising steals blinds frequently or allows postflop play from great spot. Size to 250 (2.5BB) is standard and matches previous action sizing observed.

I am in the Small Blind with [Kc Qs], a strong broadway hand. The Button (Elon) has opened to 250 (2.5bb). Given Elon's active play in previous hands (raising and 3-betting), his opening range is likely wide. While calling is a valid low-variance option to keep the pot small out of position, 3-betting is generally superior here to isolate the wide Button range, fold out the Big Blind to avoid a multi-way pot, and take the initiative. Since I am out of position, a larger 3-bet sizing (3.4x-4x) is standard. I will raise to 850 to deny equity and build a pot with a hand that likely dominates his opening range.

I’m in the big blind with A♣2♠ facing a re-raise to 850 from the small blind after the button opened to 250. Pot is 1,200 and it costs me 750 to call (850 total). Calling here requires roughly ~38% equity just to break even preflop, and A2o is routinely dominated by a small-blind 3‑betting range (pockets, AQs/AK, broadway combos) and performs worse if the button still comes along. A small 4‑bet to 1,450 is too tiny to gain fold equity and an all‑in is far too spewy with a weak offsuit ace. Early in the tournament I’d rather preserve ~99bb and avoid a marginal, easily dominated spot — fold.

Facing a 3-bet from the SB after my button open-raise with T8o, an offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a strong SB 3-bet range (pairs, strong Ax, broadway). Pot odds are ~2:1 (600 to call into 1200), but postflop SPR would be deep (~10+), and while I have position, this hand doesn't connect well, blocks few value hands, and is a standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament. No fold equity with a 4-bet bluff here given opponent's sizing and history. Prioritize hand selection.

I am UTG with Ten-Six of diamonds. While suited, this hand is too weak to open from early position in a 6-max game. Opening such a wide range from UTG often leads to being out of position against stronger ranges. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.
95 suited is a weak speculative hand in middle position with 4 players left to act behind me. Not worth opening or limping - easy fold.

4c2h is one of the worst possible starting hands: low, offsuit, disconnected, with no high card value or suited potential. In cutoff position with deep stacks and 4 players behind (Button Pro likely to defend, blinds), this hand has zero playability post-flop, high chance of domination, and isn't suitable for a steal attempt given the poor equity realization. Folding is the mathematically correct and disciplined play to preserve stack for premium spots.

I am on the Button with Ace-Queen suited (AdQd), which is a premium starting hand, especially in late position when action has folded to me. With 100bb effective stacks, standard strategy dictates opening the pot to build value and potentially take down the blinds uncontested. A raise size of 2.5bb (250) is standard to balance fold equity with pot control.

I’m in the small blind with K♠9♥ facing a button open to 250 (pot 400, 200 to call). With ~100BB stacks K9o is playable but marginal — good enough to defend but poor as a 3-bet candidate out of position because it’s often dominated and has no flush backup. The pot odds are attractive (risk 200 to win 600 — need ~25% equity) and vs a button’s reasonably wide opening range I have sufficient equity to call. A minimum raise to 400 would be too small to credibly fold out his range and a larger 3-bet would be an overcommit with a marginal hand. I’ll call 200, keep the pot size manageable, and proceed with a straightforward post‑flop plan: continue aggressively when I hit (top pair/strong draws), otherwise fold or float selectively versus c-bets to preserve chips early in the tournament.

We’re getting a very good price to defend: 150 more to win a 600 pot (and we’ll see a flop in position-relative terms with decent playability). A4o is not strong enough to want to bloat the pot out of position versus the Button open and a SB cold-call—if we squeeze and get called, we’re often stuck in a large pot with a dominated ace. Calling keeps the pot manageable while still realizing our equity (ace blocker + wheel/straight possibilities).

I have top pair (9s) with a K kicker on a 9♣‑2♠‑8♦ rainbow flop in a three‑way pot. The board is relatively dry (no real flush possibilities, some straight outs like 10‑7/7‑6 types), and both the button and BB defended wide preflop, so many worse pairs and single‑pair hands are in their ranges. I should bet to extract value from worse pairs and charge the straight draws while keeping the pot controllable OOP. A ~1/3 pot bet hits that balance: it gets value from Kx, Qx, weaker 9x/8x hands and charges draws without bloating the pot. Plan: bet 250; if I face a large raise (show of extreme strength) I’ll re‑evaluate and often fold given possible sets/two‑pair; if called I’ll continue to evaluate turn texture and opponent tendencies.

Minni’s small donk (250 into 1000) multiway is usually some kind of made hand/protection (8x/9x/2x, small pairs) or an occasional stab. With A4o here we have essentially ace-high with only very thin backdoor equity and poor playability out of position, and we still have the Button to act (reducing our ability to realize equity and increasing the chance we face a raise). Calling sets us up for tough turns where we’ll mostly have to fold anyway, and bluff-raising this texture into two players isn’t high-EV with this hand. Best is to just give it up and preserve stack.

Opponent Minni leads (donk bets) for 250 (1/3 pot) on a 9c-2s-8d board. This line often indicates a marginal made hand (like middle pair or a weak top pair) or a draw trying to set a cheap price. I hold AdQd, giving me two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. With the pot at 1000 and 250 to call, I am getting 5:1 odds. My hand has decent equity against a donking range, and I have the advantage of position. Raising might isolate me against sets or strong two-pairs, while folding is too tight given the excellent price. Calling allows me to see a turn card, realize my equity, and potentially take the pot away later if the opponent shows weakness.

I have top pair (9s) with a very strong K kicker. The turn 7 changes the texture — it completes some straights (5-6 and 6-10 type combinations) and gives 7x two‑pair possibilities and some sets are in the button’s range. However, Pro’s line (button open pre, then calling my small flop c‑bet) looks like a calling range of one‑pair hands and some straight draws (8x, 2x, 9x, and the occasional 5‑6/10‑6). I want to extract value from worse pairs and charge draws while keeping the pot controllable out of position. A half‑pot bet pressures hands like clean 8x/2x and worse 9x to pay and makes straights/draws pay to continue — but if I face a large raise I’ll give it up because that line is consistent with sets/two‑pair/strong straights. Plan: bet ~half the pot to get value and protect; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously vs heavy action.

On the turn, the board reads 9c 2s 8d 7d. I hold AdQd, giving me the nut flush draw and two overcards. The opponent, Minni, bets 625 into a pot of 1250 (half pot). To call, I need to contribute 625 to a total pot of 2500, requiring roughly 25% equity. With 9 outs to the nut flush alone (approx. 19% equity) plus potential outs with the Ace and Queen if the opponent is on a single pair, my equity justifies a call. Raising would bloat the pot when I am likely behind a made hand (straight or two pair) indicated by the opponent's donk-lead and double barrel. Position allows me to see the river and decide based on whether my draw completes.

I have top pair (9s) with a K kicker on a 9c‑2s‑8d‑7d‑Jh board. Pro (button) called my small flop c‑bet and my half‑pot turn bet, which is a fairly passive line consistent with single‑pair hands (9x, 8x, 2x) and some weak/medium holdings or missed draws. The river Jack does give Jx some showdowns that beat me, but overall my hand still has good thin value and also blocks some 9‑combos in his range. Checking now would miss value from worse pairs and medium hands that will happily call a reasonable river bet. I want to extract value while keeping the pot controllable and preserve the option to fold to heavy aggression (a raise would be strong and likely show two‑pair/set/rivered straight). Bet half the pot (1250 into 2500) to charge worse pairs and thin calls; if he raises big I will give it up.

On the river, the board is 9c 2s 8d 7d Jh. I hold Ad Qd, leaving me with Ace-high. The opponent (Minni) has taken a 'donk lead' line on the flop and fired three barrels (Flop, Turn, River). The river Jack connects with many hands in a calling range (JT, J9), and any Ten makes a straight (T9, T8, QT, KT, AT). Even 56 makes a straight. While I have the best missed flush draw (Ace-high), the likelihood that the opponent is bluffing with a worse hand (like a lower missed flush draw without a pair or straight interaction) is low compared to their value range of straights, two pairs, sets, or even top pair. I cannot profitably call a half-pot bet with just Ace-high on this coordinated board.

A9o from UTG is a marginal hand. It's playable but not premium. I'll make a standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB). This hand has some playability postflop with the ace high and potential to make top pair. Folding is also reasonable from this position, but I'll play it aggressively.

3h Tc is a complete trash hand: offsuit, low cards, poorly connected, no high card strength. Facing a UTG raise from Claude (strong range), calling or raising is -EV. Pot odds are poor for speculative hands, and my hand has no equity against their range. Deep stack, no need to speculate. Standard fold to preserve chips for better spots.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am facing a raise from Claude who opened from Under the Gun (UTG), indicating a strong range. Even though I am in position (Cutoff), there is absolutely no justification for calling or 3-betting this hand against a UTG open early in the tournament. The correct play is to fold immediately.

6♣2♥ is a junk hand — one of the weakest holdings. Claude opened from UTG in a 6-handed game, so his range is relatively strong. Calling 250 here gives poor post‑flop playability and leaves me vulnerable to domination, and a 3‑bet bluff is thin with two players (SB/BB) still to act. With a healthy ~113 BB stack I can fold and wait for a much better spot on the button rather than invest chips with a virtually dead hand.

Claude opened UTG to 2.5bb, and from the small blind we’ll be out of position the entire hand with the big blind still behind (squeeze risk). 5♦4♦ is playable in position, but versus an UTG range our equity realization OOP is poor, and 3-betting it as a bluff would need an unrealistically high fold frequency while building a bloated pot against a strong range. Best EV here is to avoid the tough spot and preserve stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9c7c, a decent suited connector-type hand. Claude raised to 250 from UTG. Everyone else folded. I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.6 to 1 (or needing about 21% equity), which 97s easily has against a standard UTG opening range. Since I am closing the action and have a hand with good playability and implied odds, a call is the standard defensive play here.

I have flopped a flush draw on a paired board (5c-Qc-Qs). Being out of position against Claude, who raised from UTG, the most standard and effective play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how he continues. Given my flush draw, I have significant equity to continue if he bets, but checking is the best way to control the pot size and realize my equity.

I raised preflop and got called by the BB. Board is 5c Qc Qs - paired with a flush draw. I have A-high with no real connection. This board favors my perceived raising range more than a BB calling range. Standard continuation bet here to represent queens or an overpair. Betting small (around 1/3 pot) is efficient to take this down.

I have a flush draw on a paired board (5c-Qc-Qs). Claude, the UTG raiser, has bet 175 into a pot of 550. This is roughly 1/4 pot, giving me excellent odds to continue. I need to call 175 to win a total pot of 900, meaning I need about 19.4% equity. My flush draw alone has about a 19.1% chance of hitting on the turn. While the paired board increases the risk of Claude having trips or a full house, calling is the most profitable play given the price. Raising is unnecessary as I am out of position and want to see the turn cheaply.

The 7s on the turn gives me a pair of sevens to go with my flush draw. While I have improved, I'm still out of position against the original raiser, Claude. Checking remains the best strategy here to see how Claude reacts. If he checks back, I get a free look at the river. If he bets, my combined equity from the pair and the flush draw likely makes a call profitable, but I don't want to build a huge pot myself given the paired board.

Flash called my flop c-bet and checked again on the 7s turn which is a brick. I only have ace-high with no real equity improvement. Continuing to barrel here is thin - Flash's call on flop indicates some interest (likely a queen, club draw, or medium pair). Checking back for pot control makes sense; I can potentially win at showdown if Flash has a busted draw, or fold to river aggression. No need to bloat the pot with just A-high.

I have a pair of sevens on a board of 5c-Qc-Qs-7s-3s. My flush draw missed, but I picked up a pair on the turn. Claude, the UTG raiser, checked back on the turn, which suggests he likely does not have a Queen or a monster. He might have a middle pocket pair (88-TT) or missed high cards (AK, AJ). By checking the river, I can see if he checks back for a cheap showdown where my pair of sevens might be good, or I can evaluate if he decides to bluff. Betting doesn't accomplish much as better hands aren't likely to fold and worse hands are unlikely to call.

River completes a backdoor spade flush (I have As as a blocker). Flash has check-called flop and checked turn and river. My A-high is unlikely good against hands that called flop, but checking back costs nothing. If Flash has a busted club draw, I win at showdown. No value in betting - only better hands call.

K8o is a marginal offsuit hand from UTG in a 6-handed game. It has poor playability post-flop, especially in multiway pots, and is easily dominated by better kings. With deep stacks (83BB), discipline is crucial early; folding preserves stack for premium spots. No strong reads justify playing it.

I am holding Td 3s in Middle Position. This is a Ten-Three offsuit, which is statistically one of the weaker starting hands in Texas Hold'em due to the lack of connectivity and suitedness, combined with the low rank of the kicker. With UTG folding, I am next to act. There is no strategic merit in playing this hand from this position with active players behind left to act. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately to preserve my stack.

Cutoff open with 7♥6♦ is borderline but fine to play for position and fold equity. I want initiative and to steal the blinds rather than limp into a multiway pot out of position. With ~113bb effective, I can realize equity postflop, but the hand is weak versus 3-bets, so I'll keep the size standard and manageable. Raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to apply pressure to Button/SB/BB, gain fold equity, and play postflop in position if called. Fold to a significant 3-bet; continuation-bet favorable flops, exercise pot control on uncooperative textures.

Cutoff opened to 3bb and we’re on the button with 82o. Even with position, this hand is too weak to defend profitably versus a standard CO range (dominated, poor playability), and 3-bet bluffing with it is unnecessary early with deep stacks and two players still to act behind who can squeeze. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 3h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni has raised from the Cutoff. Even though I have a large stack, there is no strategic reason to defend such a poor hand out of position. It is a clear fold.

T7s in BB facing a 3x raise from cutoff. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). This is a decent suited connector that plays well postflop with flush and straight potential. Standard defend from BB against a steal attempt.

Missed flop completely with T7s on AK8 board. Only have a gutshot (need 9) and backdoor spades. This board heavily favors Minni's preflop raising range. Check and evaluate - will likely fold to a bet.

I opened from the cutoff with 7♥6♦ to take the initiative, but the flop A♥ 8♦ K♦ completely misses my hand. Claude’s calling range from the big blind includes many A/K combos, Kx, pockets and diamond draws that connect well with this texture, so a continuation bluff will be called too often. With deep stacks and essentially no equity (only backdoor possibilities), I prefer pot control — check and see how he reacts. I will fold to meaningful pressure and only continue if he checks back (then take free cards and potentially stab small on a very favorable turn).

I have T7s on Ah-8d-Kd-5h. Complete air - no pair, no flush draw (board is hearts/diamonds), and no real straight draw. Minni checked back the flop which could be weak or pot control with a medium Ax/Kx. This board heavily favors their range. With no equity and facing a player who could easily have Ax or Kx, a bluff isn't profitable. Check and potentially give up or reassess on river.

I opened from the cutoff with 7♥6♦ and both of us checked the flop — the Ace‑King‑8 rainbow texture missed my hand completely. The turn 5♥ doesn't give me any realistic redraw (I only have two cards to a straight: 4 or 9 — ~8 outs ≈ 17% on the river) and Claude’s defending range (Ax, Kx, pairs, and diamond/straight draws) will call or raise my bluffs far too often. Pot is small (650) relative to our stacks, so I prefer pot control and to avoid building a bloated pot with virtually no equity. I’ll check and fold to meaningful aggression; if he checks back I’ll reevaluate the river and consider a small bluff only on very favorable runouts or a value line if I actually improve.

River completes a 3-heart flush and I have complete air with T7s (no pair, no draw). Minni checked back both flop and turn showing weakness - likely has a marginal hand or air themselves. I could bluff representing the flush, but with only 650 in the pot and Minni potentially having any Ax/Kx that will call, it's not worth it. Check and accept the loss or potentially win with T-high against another missed hand.

River is A♥ 8♦ K♦ 5♥ 4♥ and I hold 7♥6♦ — the 4♥ on the river completes a 4-5-6-7-8 straight. Claude cold-called preflop and has been passive (checking/calling), so his range is wide: A-x, K-x, pockets, some suited connectors and two-heart combos. My straight beats single pairs and trips; the only common hands that beat me are two-heart hole cards making a flush. With the pot at 650 and no bet to face, I should extract value from hands like A-x, K-x and medium pairs that will call. I’ll bet for value rather than check back — choose a size (~400, ~60% pot) large enough to get calls from top pairs and medium pairs but not so large that most of those hands fold. If I face a large raise, I’ll give credit to a flush and concede the pot.

I have T-high on Ah-8d-Kd-5h-4h. Minni checked back flop and turn showing weakness, but now bets 400 into 650 on the river. This looks like thin value - likely a weak Ax or Kx that was pot controlling. I'm only beating bluffs, and getting ~2.6:1 odds means I need Minni to be bluffing ~28% of the time. Given their passive line, this river bet is more likely value than bluff. Easy fold.

I am holding 6d 7h in the UTG position. This is a 6-max table. 67o is a weak unsuited connector that performs poorly from early position due to the high likelihood of domination and the disadvantage of playing out of position against up to 5 opponents. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand pre-flop to conserve chips for better opportunities. There is no reason to get involved.

Q♦6♥ is a weak, offsuit hand in middle position with multiple players yet to act behind. To call 100 into a 150 pot you'd need ~29% equity to break even, but Q6o will be dominated by typical calling/3-betting ranges and will perform poorly in multiway pots. A min-raise to 200 is a thin steal that’s likely to get called or 3-bet by the button/BB/SB — not worth bloating the pot with this holding. With a healthy 116bb stack I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

8♣5♦ offsuit is too weak and poorly playable (low, disconnected, unsuited), and limping from the cutoff invites squeezes/iso-raises from the button and blinds while creating reverse implied odds when we make a weak pair. With no dead money beyond the blinds and plenty of better steal candidates, we simply pass.

I am on the Button with King-Jack offsuit and everyone has folded to me. This is a standard opening raise situation to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play a pot in position. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300 total).
96 offsuit is too weak to defend from the small blind against a button raise. This hand plays poorly postflop, lacks connectivity, and will often be dominated. Folding is the correct play here.

AQ suited is a premium hand that plays well against a button steal raise after multiple folds. Flash's range is wide and includes many hands I dominate like weaker Ax, broadway, and suited connectors. 3-betting for value and to deny equity. Sizing to 900 (3x the open) is standard, builds pot OOP with strong hand, and deep stacks allow flexibility post-flop. Calling is possible but OOP vulnerable to floats; 3-bet takes initiative.

Elon has 3-bet my button open to 900, which is the same sizing he used in Hand #2. While KJo is often dominated by a standard 3-betting range, I have the advantage of position and the pot odds are roughly 3:1 (600 to call into a total pot of 1850). Given that Elon has shown pre-flop aggression before, I will call to see a flop and use my positional advantage to navigate the rest of the hand.

Flop board 2d Ad 2h is extremely dry and favorable: paired low cards, Ace high, minimal draws (diamond flush draw possible but few straight draws). AsQs gives top pair top kicker (As pairs Ad), crushing Flash's likely calling range of Ax (dominated), broadway, mid-pairs, and bluffs from wide button open + 3bet call. OOP as 3bet BB vs button, betting initiates for value against worse Ax/pairs and protection vs diamond draw/set-mining hands that called pre. Size ~2/3 pot (1200 into 1850) balances value/targeting floats while keeping pot manageable deepstacked. Checking risks free cards to draws/improves and gives him cheap equity.

Elon 3-bet pre-flop and has now led out with a significant continuation bet on an Ace-high, paired board (Ad-2d-2h). My King-Jack offsuit has completely missed this flop. Given Elon's pre-flop aggression, his range is heavily weighted toward strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) and high pocket pairs that currently beat me. There is no viable reason to float a bet of this size with only King-high and limited drawing potential.

Holding K♠T♥ UTG in a 6-max game is borderline but playable — I want to take the initiative and avoid giving cheap flops to the aggressive players behind me. With ~116bb effective stacks I can open a standard size that prevents too many callers and narrows ranges. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB), which is larger than the minimum raise and balances fold equity with post‑flop playability. Plan: if called by one player I’ll c‑bet many favorable flops; if I face a sizable 3‑bet from known aggressors (Elon/Flash) I’ll fold KTo out of position unless the 3‑bet is very small, in which case I can reassess.

UTG has opened to 3bb and we’re sitting in middle position with 3♠4♦ offsuit—one of the weakest holdings with poor playability, no high-card value, and reverse implied odds when we make a small pair. Calling or 3-betting is -EV versus an UTG range, especially with players left to act and deep stacks. Best decision is to preserve chips and wait for better spots.

Minni raised to 300 from UTG in a 6-max game, which typically indicates a strong range. My hand, Ace-Five offsuit (A5o), is weak against an early position opening range and is likely dominated by stronger Aces like AK, AQ, or AJ. Given the stack depths and my position, calling or 3-betting with such a marginal hand is unnecessary. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
5-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even with button position, calling or raising against an UTG open with this hand is -EV. Easy fold.

79o is a weak, offsuit gapped hand from SB facing an UTG raise from Minni to 300 into a 450 pot. Pot odds require ~35% equity to call 250, but this hand has poor playability OOP, especially vs UTG range which is strong. BB (Pro) still to act and could squeeze or defend. Early in tournament (hand 8/150), deep stacks, better to fold trash and wait for better spots to maximize stack long-term.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh 4h facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from Minni in UTG. The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 450, requiring about 31% equity. While J4s has some playability as a suited hand, it suffers significantly from being dominated (weak kicker, unconnected). Against a standard tight UTG opening range, J4s has approximately 30-32% raw equity. However, being out of position (OOP) post-flop drastically reduces my ability to realize that equity (likely realizing only 70-80%). With the Adjusted Equity dropping below the required pot odds (approx 24% vs 31%), and facing a larger 3x sizing rather than a min-raise, the profitable play is to fold and preserve chips.

UTG 6-handed, A2 offsuit is too dominated to open profitably this deep. It performs poorly versus the likely 3-bet/call ranges behind (lots of better Ax), and limping is a weak line that invites isolation and tough postflop spots. With an active table, I’d rather preserve EV and wait for stronger opens.

I am in the Hijack position with Queen-Nine offsuit. While this hand has some potential, it is generally too weak to open from this position in a 6-max game, especially with several active players left to act behind me. Folding here is the standard disciplined play to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position post-flop.

T7o from cutoff with two folds in front. Marginal hand but decent position with only button and blinds left. I'll make a standard 2.5x open to try to steal the blinds. If I get 3-bet I can fold easily.

Preflop on the button facing a cutoff raise to 250 (2.5BB) from Claude with pocket 44. This is an excellent spot to 3-bet small pocket pairs for value, thin value, and fold equity against a wide cutoff opening range. 44 has good equity multiway or heads-up and blocks some of Claude's value hands. Position is great if called, and deep stacks allow for postflop flexibility. SB and BB are out of position if they continue. Consistent sizing from previous 3-bets (to 900). Fold equity likely high early in tournament; if 4-bet, easy fold. Calling risks multiway pot which is bad for set mining; folding too tight.

I am in the Small Blind with KdQh facing a raise from the Cutoff (Claude) to 250 and a 3-bet from the Button (Elon) to 900. While KQo is a decent hand, cold-calling a 3-bet from the Small Blind is generally a long-term losing play. I would be playing out of position against an aggressive 3-bettor and potentially the original raiser. My hand is often dominated by the 3-bettor's value range (AK, AQ, AA, KK, QQ), and I do not get good enough odds to call (need ~40%, have ~30-35% vs a standard range). The threat of the original raiser 4-betting behind me also makes calling dangerous. 4-betting as a bluff is too high variance given the stack depth. The disciplined and standard play here is to fold.

Facing a button 3-bet to 900 after the cutoff opened to 250. Pot is 1,300 and calling costs 800, giving me only ~38% pot odds (800 / 2,100). I hold 5♥7♥ — suited but a weak, low-card hand with poor playability OOP in a multiway pot (both the opener and 3-bettor remain). A squeeze to the min-raise (1,550) would be a thin bluff and likely costly if called or 4-bet. Given the large 3-bet sizing and the tough postflop spots I’d face, folding preserves my healthy stack and avoids a low-expected-value confrontation. Fold.

T7o facing a 3-bet from the button. This hand is too weak to continue - it's dominated by Elon's 3-betting range, has poor playability out of position, and the pot odds of ~2:1 don't justify calling with such a marginal hand. Elon has shown willingness to 3-bet (did it in hands #2 and #7), but T7o just can't profitably defend here. Easy fold.

I am UTG with a small pocket pair (33). In a 6-handed game, opening 33 from the first position is marginal and often leads to difficult situations post-flop, especially out of position. Given that players like Elon and Minni have shown aggression and a tendency to 3-bet or play back at openers in previous hands, I will fold this hand to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

KTs is a solid hand in middle position - suited broadway cards with good playability. Standard open raise to 2.5x. Elon has been 3-betting me frequently, but KTs is strong enough to open and can defend against some 3-bets if needed.

Preflop in cutoff facing a 2.5x raise from middle position (Claude) to 250 into a 400 pot. Holding Jc8s (offsuit), which is a marginal hand at best. Standard play is to fold J8o here as it lacks the equity, playability, and strength to call or 3-bet profitably against an MP opening range (likely 15-20% of hands). Postflop position on Claude is nice, but the hand is too weak and dominated often. Deep stacked (96BB), no need to speculate early in tournament. Pot odds are 250 to call into 650 (3.85:1 effective), requiring ~21% equity, but J8o realizes poorly OOP for Claude but still underperforms vs range. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

I hold 4h 2s on the Button, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (unconnected, low, offsuit). Facing a standard open raise to 250 (2.5 BB) from a middle position player (Claude), I have no pot odds or equity to justify a call. A 3-bet bluff is unnecessary and risky with zero backup equity. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

I’m in the small blind with Tc5c facing a middle‑position open to 250 (I must put in 200 more). T5s is a weak, easily‑dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position against the opener (and likely the big blind). Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives only ~33% immediate odds but my equity versus a standard MP opening range is lower; a light 3‑bet is a marginal bluff that bloats a pot I’ll play OOP. With deep stacks and many hands left, preserve chips and fold this spot.

Claude is opening to 2.5bb from middle and everyone else folded, so his range should be reasonably wide. Getting a good price in the big blind (150 to win 400, ~27% needed) and closing the action, 9-6 offsuit is a marginal but acceptable defend at this sizing. A 3-bet bluff with this hand is too low quality (poor playability, dominated too often), so I’ll take the pot-odds call and proceed cautiously postflop (mostly check to the raiser, continue on favorable textures).

Monotone K-high flop heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range (strong Kx, higher diamonds, and nut-advantage). With 9h6s and no diamond, we have essentially no equity and very poor ability to continue versus any reasonable bet. Leading as a bluff is low quality here because we don’t block diamonds and can get raised or floated. Best line is to check and realize whatever tiny chance we have, responding later based on Claude’s sizing/tendencies.

I flopped top pair with Kc on a monotone diamond board (2d Kd 4d). I have no diamond, making my hand vulnerable to any diamond completing a flush. FiveTwo checked to me - I should c-bet for value and protection. My top pair is ahead of most of their BB defense range but I need to charge draws. Betting around 1/3 pot is appropriate on this wet board.

Claude’s small c-bet (200 into 750) gives a tempting price, but on K♦-4♦-2♦ with 9h6s and no diamond we have essentially no real equity (no flush draw, no meaningful straight draw), and our “outs” to a pair are weak and often still behind Kx/flushes. With no diamond blocker, bluff-raising is low quality and will get continued against frequently. Best is to cut losses and fold.

A5o from UTG is a weak holding. Looking at recent hands, Elon has been 3-betting me frequently when I open. A5o doesn't play well against 3-bets and is below my UTG opening range. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Preflop in middle position with Tc4d, a complete trash hand with no playability, connectivity, or high card strength. Multiple players behind including button (Minni) who has shown aggression in prior hands. Deep stacks but no value in playing this hand. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold A5o in the Cutoff. While this is sometimes an opening hand from late position, the table dynamics suggest caution. The player on the Button (Minni) and the Big Blind (Flash) have been active and aggressive. Minni, specifically, showed strong post-flop aggression in Hand 4. Opening A5o here risks getting 3-bet or playing a marginal hand out of position against an aggressive opponent. Given the stack depth and the risk of domination or being outplayed post-flop, folding is the disciplined decision to preserve chips for better spots.

I'm on the button with 9♥5♣ — a weak, unsuited hand, but excellent position and only the blinds left to act. Both blinds have shown a tendency to fold or just defend; opening a wide range from the button to steal is standard. A small raise to the minimum (200 total) gives good fold equity while risking very little of my deep stack. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if called, play the flop in position and look to c-bet or take the pot away cheaply. Therefore I open-raise to 200 total.

Button min-raise gives a great price: we’re calling 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 500 if BB folds, so we don’t need much equity. J7s has playable suited connectivity and can realize well on favorable flops, and 3-betting would be a low-quality bluff that often gets called by better hands while we’re out of position. I’ll defend by flatting and play postflop carefully, especially if the BB comes along.

I am holding pocket threes in the big blind. Minni raised from the button and FiveTwo called from the small blind. I am getting excellent immediate pot odds (5:1) to call and close the action. With a small pair, the primary objective is to set-mine, and the price offered makes this an easy call to see a flop. I have a healthy stack of over 110BB, so I can comfortably play this multi-way pot and look to hit a set.

We flop top two (J7) in a 3-way pot on a very dynamic board (Jh7h6c) with tons of pair+draw, heart draws, and straight draws (T8/98/54). Checking risks a frequent check-through multiway and gives free equity; leading also avoids letting BTN realize their range for cheap. I want to build the pot for value while charging draws and worse Jx/7x, so I’ll lead a strong sizing that can still get called by dominated hands and draws.

On a flop of 6c-7h-Jh, my pocket threes are now an underpair to the entire board. FiveTwo has led out for 450 into a 600 pot, which is a strong betting line into two opponents, including the pre-flop raiser. With Minni still to act behind me and having very few outs (only two remaining threes) to improve my hand, the correct play is to fold. The pot odds do not justify chasing such a weak hand against showing strength.

I opened wide from the button with 9♥5♣ but on this flop (6♣ 7♥ J♥) I have no pair — only a one-card (gutshot) straight draw to any 8. That’s 4 outs (~8.5% to hit on the turn, ~16.5% by the river). I also have a backdoor heart possibility (~4% to complete by river), so total equity is roughly ~18–20%. FiveTwo’s bet of 450 into a 1050 pot requires about 30% equity to call (450/(1050+450)=30%), so a call is -EV. Raising as a bluff is possible but risky — it’s likely to get called by Jx, pairs, or bigger draws and would commit chips with weak equity. I’ll fold and wait for a better, cheaper spot in position.

UTG in 6-handed 50/100 NLHE with 96BB effective stacks. Holding 2h4c, a complete trash hand: low, offsuit, disconnected, no potential. Standard play is to fold weak hands from early position to avoid playing out of position post-flop with marginal equity. Opponent tendencies from history show aggression, but no need to speculate with this hand. Preserve stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

I hold Kh 4d in Middle Position. This is an off-suit hand with a low kicker and poor connectivity, making it very weak. With 90 big blinds and four players left to act behind me, opening this hand carries significant reverse implied odds and a high likelihood of being dominated or 3-bet. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand from early/middle position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 5♥9♦ in the cutoff — a weak, unsuited, poorly-connected hand with little playability multiway. It’s folded around to me but the button and both blinds are yet to act and have shown willingness to defend/raise in earlier hands. Calling 100 to see a flop would put me out of position and invite squeezes; a min-raise to 200 is a marginal steal that will often get called or 3-bet, and with deep stacks I don’t want to play a big pot with this holding. Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold preflop.

Folds to us on the button: prime steal spot with position and a hand that plays fine postflop. A8o is comfortably ahead of typical blind defending ranges, and the Ad gives a useful blocker versus strong ace holdings when facing a 3-bet. I’ll apply pressure with a standard open size to win the blinds or take position in a heads-up pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 7h, a very weak unsuited connector. FiveTwo has raised from the Button to 250. Playing this hand out of position against a standard button opening range is generally unprofitable. Given the stack sizes and the poor quality of my hand, the most disciplined and correct play is to fold.

J3o is a weak hand to defend from the BB. Even though FiveTwo is raising from the button (typically a wide range), J3o plays poorly out of position postflop due to the weak kicker. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds isn't enough to justify defending this trash hand. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the UTG position with Kh 8h. This is a 6-max table with 50/100 blinds. My stack is roughly 90 BBs. K8 suited is a marginal hand that is generally not strong enough to open from Under the Gun, as I have 5 opponents left to act who could easily wake up with a better hand. Furthermore, the player in the Big Blind (Elon) has demonstrated aggressive 3-betting behavior in previous hands (Hand #2, #7, #9). Opening a weak suited King into an aggressive 3-better while out of position is a losing play. Therefore, the prudent decision is to fold.

Qc3h is a weak, unsuited, unconnected hand with poor playability and a bad kicker. I’m in MP with four players still to act (Cutoff, Button, SB, BB), so calling 100 will very often make this a multiway pot where Q3o performs poorly and is frequently dominated. A raise would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity against active opponents and is unnecessary with a healthy 114bb stack — better spots will come. Preserve chips and fold preflop.

Folding is a bit too tight in the CO with a suited, connected hand that can realize equity well in position. I don’t want to limp and invite the button/blinds to iso and put me in tough spots with a capped range. I’ll take the initiative and try to pick up the blinds, and if I get 3-bet I can comfortably fold given the hand class. Standard open size to apply pressure without overinvesting.

I am on the button with 7c 6s. FiveTwo has raised to 2.5x from the cutoff. While I have positional advantage, 76 offsuit is too weak to defend against a standard open, even on the button. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.
7-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Out of position against a cutoff raise with no playability - clear fold.

Preflop in BB facing a cutoff raise to 250 after two folds (UTG and MP). Pot 400, to call 150 (27% pot odds superficially). My hand K5o is offsuit king-high with a weak kicker. Cutoff range is wide (~25-35%), but K5o plays poorly OOP postflop: dominated top pairs, few draws, weak floats. Equity vs wide range ~35-40% but poor playability reduces effective equity. SB dead money sweetens pot, but standard solver strategy folds K5o here, preferring suited kings or better. FiveTwo aggressive in spots but deep stacks, no need to defend marginally. Fold preserves stack for better spots early in tournament.

UTG with K♥Q♠ in 6-max is a standard open — strong enough to take the initiative. Pot is just the blinds (150) and call is 100. With a deep ~114bb stack I want fold equity and a clear, common size to discourage limp-calls and isolate weaker hands. I’ll open to 3bb (300). If I face a small 3-bet I’ll consider calling and playing postflop; against a large 3-bet I’ll fold and preserve stack. Postflop I’ll c-bet favorable textures and be cautious out of position.

UTG opened to 3bb in a 6-max game, which is typically a fairly strong, tighter range. Q2 offsuit is heavily dominated by all Qx they open and most of their continuing range, plays poorly postflop (reverse implied odds), and doesn’t have good 3-bet bluff properties (weak blockers, bad playability when called). With plenty of stack and many better spots, the highest-EV decision is to fold.

I am in the Cutoff position with 9d 7c. Minni, in UTG, has opened with a raise to 300 (3x the big blind). 97o is a very weak hand to play against an early position raise, as it is easily dominated and lacks connectivity or high card value. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

7Ts is a marginal hand facing a UTG raise of 300. UTG raises typically represent strong ranges, and my hand doesn't play well enough to call or 3-bet here even with position. Folding is the disciplined play.

67o is a complete trash hand from the small blind facing an UTG raise from Minni. Poor playability out of position, no suited/connectors value here, low equity against a strong UTG range. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Fives [5h 5d], facing a standard 3x open raise to 300 from Minni (UTG). The pot is 450, and it costs me 200 to call, offering pot odds of roughly 3.25:1. Given the deep effective stacks (approx. 88 BBs), I have excellent implied odds (>40:1) to set mine. Pocket pairs have good playability in this spot: I will call to see a flop, hoping to hit a set. If I miss and the board is unfavorable, I can easily fold to continuation bets.

The flop comes [Jc Tc Td], which is a very wet and coordinated board that hits the UTG opener's range (likely consisting of broadway cards and strong pairs) much harder than mine. With Pocket Fives, I have an underpair to the board and very low equity. I am out of position and have no reason to lead out. My best play is to check and fold to a continuation bet, as I am likely drawing nearly dead or way behind.

I opened UTG and villain in the BB just called. Flop Jc Tc Td misses my KhQs but I have two clean overcards and 8 straight outs (any A or 9). As the preflop aggressor I have a range advantage and should apply pressure, but I'm out of position and the board heavily interacts with the BB calling range (T/J/sets), so I don't want to overcommit. I will c-bet a modest ~1/3 pot to take the initiative, fold out some weaker hands and realize my equity cheaply; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to a strong check-raise or large lead.

I hold Pocket Fives on a Jc Tc Td board. The board texture is very wet and paired, heavily favoring the UTG opener's range which consists of strong broadway hands and overpairs. My underpair is likely way behind hands like Tx, JJ+, or even AJ/KJ/QJ. Facing a continuation bet of 250 into 650, I am not getting the right price to peel with just two outs to a set (which might already be drawing dead to a full house). I have very little equity and no potential for improvement. Folding is the only standard play here.

UTG 6-max with 85s is too loose to open, especially with aggressive players behind (Elon has shown frequent 3-bets) and stacks ~85–115bb where we’ll often face pressure and realize equity poorly. Limping invites iso/squeeze and puts us in tough multiway spots. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack.

I am in middle position with T3 suited. This is a very weak starting hand that does not meet the requirements for an open-raise or a call. To maintain a healthy stack and wait for better opportunities, I will fold this hand.
72 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Even in the cutoff with two folds in front of me, this hand has no value to open. Easy fold.

Folds to the button in an unopened pot. 7d4d is a suited gapped connector with good playability postflop, especially in position with deep stacks (95BB effective). Standard to open-raise from the button for value and to steal blinds. Raising to 2.5x the BB (250 total) is a good size based on observed opening sizes in prior hands. SB and BB stacks are deep enough to realize equity if called. Folding or limping would be suboptimal with position and hand potential.

I hold 3d 4h in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em. Elon on the Button has opened to 2.5bb. Although Button opening ranges are wide, my hand has poor raw equity (approx 30-32% vs a wide range) and terrible playability out of position. Furthermore, the Big Blind is still left to act and could 3-bet. Pot odds of 2:1 are not sufficient to overcome the positional disadvantage and hand weakness. I will fold.

I’m in the big blind (100) facing a button open to 250 with K♦9♦ (K9o). This is a marginal but defendable hand vs a button raise to ~2.5bb. Calling 150 (making my total committed 250) is the best play: the pot odds (150 to win ~550) are attractive and K9o has reasonable equity vs a wide button opening range. 3-betting with K9o would be thin — a minimum 3-bet to 400 is unlikely to fold out many hands, and a larger 3-bet commits too much with a hand that plays poorly OOP. Folding here would be too tight; I want to see a flop and play postflop with position disadvantage. Plan: call, evaluate the flop — play for value when I hit top pair or a good draw, and otherwise use pot control or fold to strong aggression on hostile textures (A-high or coordinated boards).

I have K♥9♦ on A♦-8♦-Q♣. I missed: no pair, only K-high and a weak backdoor diamond possibility (need two diamonds on turn+river). Button’s opening range contains lots of A-x and Q-x plus diamond combos that dominate me. I’m out of position and deep-stacked, so I want to keep the pot small with a weak hand that has very little showdown value. Betting here as a bluff OOP is thin against an aggressive opener and will get me raised or called by many better hands. Best play is to check and fold to meaningful aggression. If he checks back I can take a free turn to pick spots to bluff or realize equity; if he gives a small probe I may call very small bets to see a turn, but I will fold to standard/large continuation bets because I’m behind to his value range.

Heads-up pot versus Minni in BB after my button raise. Flop Ad8dQc gives me a strong diamond flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity heads-up) on a draw-heavy board. Minni checked, indicating possible weakness. As preflop aggressor with position, continuation betting is standard to deny equity, extract value from Ax/draws, or semi-bluff against overpairs. Sizing ~half pot (300 into 550) balances value and semi-bluff while keeping pot manageable deepstacked.

I have K♥9♦ on A♦-8♦-Q♣ — I missed the flop (only K-high) and have only a very weak backdoor diamond line (would need both turn+river diamonds to make a flush from here). Elon opened BTN and his 300 into a ~850 pot is a relatively small probe bet; calling 300 requires ~26% equity (300/(850+300) ≈ 26%). Versus a realistic BTN c-bet range I am behind to most A-x and many Q-x holdings but his small bet gives acceptable pot odds to see a turn, and raising OOP with K9 is thin and unprofitable. Plan: call the small bet to keep the pot controllable and see a turn; fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve (pair the K, hit a diamond that meaningfully increases my draw, or the turn gives a favorable board texture).

On the turn (A♦ 8♦ Q♣ 2♣) I have K♥9♦ — I missed entirely. My backdoor diamond draw is dead (only one street left), so I have essentially zero showdown value unless I pair the K on the river. Elon opened BTN and c-bet the flop (300) but checked the turn, which reads as pot‑control or weakness. Betting OOP here as a pure bluff is thin — I’ll often get called by Qx, mid pairs or A‑x, and a raise is an easy fold. With a deep stack and a small pot (1150) the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is to check and fold to meaningful river aggression unless I actually pair up or the river gives a very clear bluffing opportunity. If he checks back the river I can decide whether to bluff or check again based on the river card; otherwise I will release to a bet.

Heads-up on the turn with position against Minni in the BB after my preflop raise and flop c-bet which she check-called. Board is Ad8dQc2c, and I hold 7d4d for a strong diamond flush draw (9 outs, ~19% equity to improve on river) plus backdoor potential. The turn 2c adds a weak club draw but doesn't complete much. Minni's check-call flop suggests a range including Ax (weak/mid), Qx, mid-pairs, or weaker draws; her turn check indicates possible weakness or trap but likely capped without much strength given passive history. Pot is 1150, deep effective stacks (~89BB). With position and initiative, a semi-bluff bet denies her equity (e.g., gutshots, weaker draws), extracts from showdown value like Qx or mid-pairs, and realizes fold equity while keeping stack sizes manageable. Half-pot sizing (600) balances value/semi-bluff, avoids overbetting into potential monsters, and maintains fold equity vs her calling range. Checking risks giving free cards to draws/improvers and forfeiting value; betting maximizes EV long-term in tournament setting aiming to grow stack.

I’m on the BTN’s turn bet with K♥9♦ on A♦-8♦-Q♣-2♣. My backdoor diamond is dead and I have no pair; the only clean outs are 3 kings + 3 nines = 6 outs (~13% to pair either on the river) and pairing will often still lose to Ax/Qx/sets. Calling 600 into a 1750 pot requires ~25.5% equity (600/(1750+600)), so a call is a clear negative EV. Raising as an OOP bluff with K9 is too thin and high-variance versus an opponent who has shown continuity. Per my earlier plan to check and fold to meaningful turn aggression, I will fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the UTG (Under The Gun) position with 4h 2d, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Playing such a hand from early position is statistically unprofitable and highly risky. I will fold to preserve my stack.
4d 6h is a weak offsuit hand in middle position. Not playable - easy fold with players still to act behind me.

Qs7c offsuit is a marginal hand at best from the cutoff in an unopened pot. Standard GTO opening ranges from CO typically include QJo+, but Q7o lacks the equity, playability, and domination potential when facing calls or 3-bets from button, SB, or BB. Opponents have shown willingness to defend (e.g., Minni checks/calls frequently, FiveTwo mixes it up), so risking chips on a bluff-raise has negative EV here with deep stacks (~100BB). Folding preserves stack for superior spots in this early tournament stage (hand 16/150). Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots OOP.

Action is folded to me on the Button with K8o. This is a standard spot to attempt to steal the blinds. The blinds (Minni and FiveTwo) have shown a tendency to fold to raises in previous hands (e.g., Hands 2, 6, 7, 8 for FiveTwo and 1, 5, 9 for Minni). While K8o is a marginal hand, the position and the likelihood of folding out the blinds make a raise +EV. I will open to 2.5x BB (250) to put pressure on them while having the advantage of position post-flop if called.

I have 8♦5♥ — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with very poor playability out of position. Pro opened to 250 from the button; the pot is 400 and I must put in 200 more to call, with the big blind still to act. Calling here would commit chips out of position into a likely multiway pot and be a marginal, low-CEV play. A min 3-bet as a bluff is too light given my holding and the risk of getting isolated by the button or called by the big blind. With a deep stack (~111bb) I should conserve chips for better spots. Fold.

Button open to 2.5x is a wide range, and Q9o is comfortably within a standard BB defend. Calling keeps the pot manageable OOP, realizes equity well enough against a wide button range, and avoids inflating the pot with a hand that can be dominated by better queens if we 3-bet and get called. We’ll take a flop and play with position disadvantage but good enough coverage (top-pair potential, some straight possibilities).

BB vs BTN on 7h6d7s is a board where our defending range contains a lot of 7x/6x and some boats, so we don’t need to donk-bluff with a hand this weak. Q9o has no pair and essentially no meaningful backdoors here, so our best line is to check and let Pro c-bet; we can continue only versus very small sizing with improved equity, but generally we’re prepared to fold to pressure.

The board is paired (7h 6d 7s), which is a texture that often misses the Big Blind's wide defending range. While I only have King-high, I have a range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. The opponent (FiveTwo) has shown a tendency to fold to continuation bets on the flop when they miss (e.g., Hand #10). A small continuation bet of approximately 1/3 pot serves to deny equity to random overcards and frequently takes down the pot immediately against a 'fit-or-fold' player. Checking back is an option for pot control, but betting applies pressure and capitalizes on the likelihood that the opponent has missed.

Even though the sizing is small and we’re getting a good price, Q9o on 7h6d7s has essentially no equity realization: no pair, no flush draw, and only very thin overcard outs that are often dominated by Pro’s c-bet range (pairs like 88–TT, 6x/7x, etc.). This isn’t a good candidate to float OOP on a paired, low board where our turn barreling opportunities are limited. We can defend with hands that have real backdoors or stronger overcards; this one is a clean fold.
92 suited from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak hand with poor card ranks, and UTG requires a tight opening range. No reason to get involved here.

Preflop, UTG (Claude) has folded, leaving me first to act effectively in middle position with ATo offsuit, a strong hand warranting a raise. Standard open-raise sizing of 3x the big blind (300 total) builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against the remaining players (Pro in CO, Minni on button, blinds). ATo has good equity, playability postflop, and dominates many calling ranges. Deep stacks allow for flexibility. Limping is too passive given the dead SB money; folding is too tight for this spot. Opponents from history show some limping/defending tendencies, so raising isolates and takes initiative.

I am in the Cutoff with Kh 2s, a very weak unsuited hand (K2o). The player in Middle Position (Elon), who has shown aggression in previous hands, has opened with a raise to 300. Facing a raise from an early/middle position, K2o is well outside of a profitable calling or raising range. It is dominated by the opener's range (better Kings, pairs, Aces). With players still left to act behind me and poor playability post-flop, the only correct strategic play is to fold.

I’m on the button with 2♥5♦ facing an open to 300. This is a very weak, offsuit, unconnected hand with poor postflop playability and very little equity versus a raiser’s continuing range. The pot is 450 and calling 300 would require ~40% equity to break even (300 / 750), which this hand does not have. 3-betting would be reckless—blinds still to act and I have no blockers or fold equity to make that profitable. With a healthy 11,175 stack and plenty of hands left, the correct play is to preserve chips and wait for a much better spot. Fold.

Elon opened to 3x from middle position and we’re in the small blind with Q2o, a hand that is heavily dominated and plays poorly out of position. Calling invites the BB in and realizes equity badly; 3-bet bluffing here is also low quality since Q2o has weak blockers and poor playability when called. Best EV is to preserve the stack and wait for stronger spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Three of Spades (T3o). Elon has raised to 300 from Middle Position. T3o is an extremely weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Even with the pot odds offered, defending such a wide and disconnected hand against a standard open is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

52o is one of the worst hands in poker, completely uncoordinated, low cards, offsuit. UTG in a 6-handed game is a tough spot to open with trash. Opponents have shown aggression in prior hands, but no value in entering the pot here. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots and avoid difficult post-flop situations with a deep stack (~100BB).

I hold [3c Tc] in Middle Position. This is a very weak hand with a large gap between the ranks. Although it is suited, the playability is low, and I have several players left to act behind me. With a healthy stack of roughly 89 BB, there is no need to loosen my opening range for this specific holding. The standard and most profitable play is to fold.

Pocket queens in cutoff — premium hand and I have position on the button and the blinds. Pot is 150 and to-call is 100 (just the BB); min-raising to 200 is too small and will invite multiway callers. I want to build the pot and take away limps / speculative hands, so I raise to a standard 3x BB (300). If I get isolated or one caller I’ll proceed aggressively post-flop; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on opponent and sizing (call or 4-bet as appropriate).

Minni opens to 300 from the cutoff and we’re on the button with J2o. Despite position and decent immediate pot odds, this hand is far too weak and dominated (Jx, 2x) with poor playability and reverse implied odds. There’s no compelling reason to turn it into a 3-bet bluff versus an active opener when better bluff candidates exist. Best EV is to release and wait for a stronger spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine suited, a strong hand in 6-max play, especially against a late-position raise from the Cutoff. Calling here would leave me out of position against Minni and potentially invite Claude in the Big Blind to squeeze or see a cheap flop. By 3-betting to 1050, I take initiative, define the opponent's range, and give myself a chance to win the pot immediately. If called, I have a hand with good post-flop playability and nut-flush potential.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash with 5d9d. This is a weak suited connector that doesn't play well against a 3-bet range. I'd need to call 950 into a 1450 pot, getting about 1.5:1. Even with suited diamonds, this hand is too weak to call a 3-bet out of position. Clear fold.

I opened cutoff with QQ and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1,050 — a large sizing but from a position/player that will 3‑bet fairly wide. This is a premium hand and I will have position post‑flop, so I want to take the initiative: 4‑betting isolates, gets value from AK/TT/JJ and folding equity against lighter 3‑bets. I choose a sizing of 3,000 (well above the min 1,800) — big enough to get fold equity and price weaker hands out, but small enough to leave plenty of chips behind for post‑flop play (~75–80 BB effective). Plan: if he folds I take down the pot; if he calls I’ll play aggressively in position (c‑bet most favorable boards, pot‑control/detach on highly coordinated/ace runouts); if he 5‑bet‑jam I’ll re‑evaluate (most shoves will be AA/KK and I’ll often fold to an overbet jam unless I have a strong read of lighter action). Calling passively here surrenders initiative and folding QQ is too weak, so 4‑bet to extract value and define the hand.

Minni's 4-bet to 3000 is a very strong response to my 3-bet, typically representing a premium range of JJ+, AK, or occasionally AQs. While Ace-Nine suited is a viable 3-betting candidate from the Small Blind to put pressure on a Cutoff opener, it performs poorly against a 4-betting range. I need approximately 32% equity to call the 1950 difference, but against a range of [JJ+, AK], I only have about 28% equity. Additionally, playing out of position with a very low stack-to-pot ratio on the flop would make it difficult to realize my equity effectively. Folding allows me to preserve my stack and look for a more favorable situation.

I am in UTG position with JTo (Jack of Spades, Ten of Diamonds). In a 6-handed game, this hand is generally too weak to open raise from the earliest position. It is easily dominated by hands that call or raise me (like AJ, KJ, QJ, AT), and being out of position for the rest of the hand makes it difficult to play profitably. Furthermore, players behind like Elon in the Big Blind have shown a tendency to 3-bet, making stealing or playing marginal hands even riskier. Therefore, the standard and disciplined play is to fold.

Ten-three offsuit is a very weak, unplayable hand in middle position in a 6-max game. There are four players left to act (cutoff, button, SB, BB) so opening or calling 100 into a 150 pot will put me in multiway pots dominated by stronger ranges and against opponents who have shown frequent preflop aggression. Pot odds and implied odds don’t compensate for the hand’s poor playability. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Folds to me in the CO with 7c4c. This is a very low suited gapper that plays poorly when called and is especially vulnerable to a squeeze/3-bet from the Button (Flash has shown aggression). If I open, I need a high combined fold rate from Button + blinds to show immediate profit, and I’ll frequently be forced to fold to pressure. Given stack depths and opponent tendencies, this is a clean pass and I preserve chips for higher-EV steal spots and playable hands.

I am on the button with Ace-King offsuit, a premium starting hand. Since everyone has folded to me, I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and take control of the hand, aiming to either win the blinds immediately or play a favorable pot in position against the small or big blind.
8s3s is a very weak hand, even suited. I'm out of position against a button raise. This hand has poor playability and lacks the connectivity or high card strength to profitably defend. Easy fold.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after folds from UTG, MP, CO, and SB fold. Pot 450, to call 200. Hand is T2s, a weak suited connector with poor high-card strength and low playability OOP. Button range is wide (~40-50% hands), but T2s has limited equity (~35-40% vs. that range) and tough postflop spots OOP on deep stacks (~100BB). No strong blockers for 3-betting bluff. Standard play is to fold marginal hands like this to avoid difficult OOP situations. Prioritizing discipline early in tournament to build stack long-term.

UTG with 9♥4♥ — a weak, low-suited hand with poor connectivity and a bad kicker. In early position against 5 opponents this hand plays very poorly postflop and is often dominated; calling 100 invites multiway action out of position with little equity, and raising is not justified. With a healthy stack there's no need to gamble in a marginal spot — fold and wait for a much stronger opener in position.

We’re in middle position facing only the option to limp for 100 or open-raise. Ks2o is a very weak offsuit king with poor playability and dominated when called, and it doesn’t benefit from limping (invites multiway pots and tough postflop spots). With no need to take marginal edges this early and decent stacks behind, best EV is to release.

I am in the Cutoff position with 7d 4c, which is a very weak offsuit hand. Given that there are still three players to act behind me, including the Button and the blinds, and there is no reason to enter the pot with such a low-equity hand, the standard and most profitable play is to fold.

Pocket 6s on the button with folds to me. This is a clear raise spot - I have a solid pocket pair in position with only the blinds left to act. Standard button open to 250 (2.5x) to steal or build a pot in position.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 250 after three folds. Button range is very wide: any suited ace, broadway cards, suited connectors, Ax suited, pairs, etc. Ac8c is a strong defensive hand with suited ace blocker (blocks AA, AKs, AQs), good playability postflop, flush draw potential, and straight potential. Getting about 2:1 on the call (pot 400 to call 200 more, total pot ~600 with my call). OOP but deepstacked tournament, early hand 20/150, prefer realizing equity OOP with strong speculative hand rather than folding equity from 3bet or folding outright. BB still to act but will likely fold or call weak; if they squeeze, we have fold equity issues but hand has blockers. Folding too tight vs button; raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand. Call to see flop and control pot OOP or play postflop dynamics.

I am in the Big Blind with [5s, 3s] facing a Button raise to 250 and a Small Blind call. The pot is currently 600, and it costs me 150 to call, offering me 4:1 pot odds (requiring 20% equity). My suited one-gapper has approximately 25-30% equity against a wide Button opening range and a capped Small Blind calling range. Although I will be out of position relative to the initial raiser, closing the action at this price with a hand that plays well in deep-stacked multi-way pots makes calling the standard and profitable play.

Post-flop in SB OOP three ways on Jc2s9s rainbow-ish board (two spades). Ac8c is Ace-high with backdoor club flush draw but completely missed: no pair, no straight/flush draw. Board favors sets (JJ,22,99 unlikely in called ranges), two-pair combos, straight draws (T8 suited spades strong), Jx, 9x. Claude's BTN range wide after folds, likely cbets frequently; Pro's BB calling range capped weak (low pairs, suited connectors). No showdown value, no strong draw to realize equity OOP multiway. Betting air risks getting check-raised or called by better; check to pot control, induce cbets from Claude I can fold to profitably, or see free turn if checks around. Early tournament, preserve stack; check-fold to aggression most likely outcome, rarely check-call with air.

I hold a flush draw (5s, 3s) on a Jc 2s 9s board. I am in the Big Blind in a multi-way pot against the Small Blind and the Button (pre-flop aggressor). The Small Blind has checked. It is standard strategy to check to the pre-flop raiser in this position. Leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal as the board favors the pre-flop raiser's range (broadways, sets).
