Checking gives me the option to see a free turn if the Button checks back, or to check-call/check-raise if the Button bets, leveraging my strong draw and implied odds.

I have pocket 6s on a J92 board with two spades. Both opponents checked to me showing weakness. My pair is below the board but I should c-bet here to take down the pot. If called, I likely need to shut down. Betting ~50% pot (375) to apply pressure while keeping my investment reasonable with this vulnerable hand.

Facing Claude's continuation bet of 375 into a 1125 pot after both blinds checked on Jc2s9s flop. Ac8c is pure Ace-high with minimal backdoor equity (club draw blocked somewhat by Jc, no spade draw). Board hits Claude's wide button raising range with Jx, 9x, sets (JJ/99/22 unlikely but possible), combo draws like T8ss, KJss, etc. Pro's BB calling range is weak (Ax weak, low pairs, suited junk) but unlikely to have hit hard. Multiway OOP with no pair, no draw, and poor playability postflop. Pot odds ~3:1 but need ~25% equity to call, which Ace-high lacks against villain's betting range (they fold worse but call/raise better). No fold equity on raise. Early tournament, deepstacked; preserve stack by folding air to cbet, realize equity preflop only with speculative hand but realize it's a miss. Standard check-fold.

I hold a flush draw (5s, 3s) on a Jc 2s 9s board, facing a half-pot continuation bet (375 into 750) from the Button after the Small Blind folded. The pot odds are 25% (375 to call into a total pot of 1500). My flush draw has approximately 19% equity to hit on the turn and 35% by the river, making a call immediately profitable when accounting for implied odds. Raising is an option but risky with a low flush draw out of position, as it risks bloating the pot against better draws or strong value hands. Calling closes the action for this street and allows me to realize my equity for a reasonable price.

On the turn, the board is [Jc, 2s, 9s, 3c]. I have improved to a pair of threes with my [5s, 3s] and still hold a flush draw. The pot is 1500. Being out of position against the Button (Claude) who showed aggression on the flop, the standard line is to check. Leading out (donk betting) does not accomplish much as worse hands will fold and better hands will call or raise. Checking allows me to see if the opponent wants to check back for a free river or bet, at which point I can evaluate calling based on the bet size given my pair and flush outs.

Pro called my flop c-bet and checked the turn. The 3c is a complete brick. My pocket 6s are an underpair to the board (J-9-2-3). Pro's calling range likely includes Jx, 9x, spade draws, or slow-played hands - I'm behind most of this range. Betting again would be turning my hand into a bluff with minimal fold equity against a player who already called once. Best to check back, control pot size, and take a free river card where I might hit my 2-outer or get to showdown cheaply.

I hold [5s, 3s] on a board of [Jc, 2s, 9s, 3c, Jd]. I have made two pair (Jacks and Threes) by the river. The action went: pre-flop call, check-call flop bet, check-check turn. The opponent (Button) checking back the turn suggests a capped range, likely a marginal made hand (like 9x or a low pocket pair) or a draw that missed. My hand beats all missed draws (Ace-high, etc.) and specifically hands like A2 (Jacks and Deuces), but loses to any pocket pair 44+, 9x, or Jx. Betting here has no value as worse hands fold and better hands call. Checking allows me to try to get to showdown for free to beat his bluffs/missed draws, or evaluate if he bets (though I would likely fold to a significant bet given my weak two pair).

River paired the Jacks. My pocket 6s have minimal showdown value but I beat missed spade draws. Pro checked, showing weakness - could have 9x, missed draws, or a weak Jack. Betting here would only get called by hands that beat me. Check back for showdown value against busted draws.

UTG 6-handed, J2s is too weak and dominated to open profitably, especially given the table’s tendency to 3-bet/raise aggressively (e.g., Elon/Minni). Better to preserve stack and avoid playing a bloated pot out of position with a dominated jack-high hand.

I am in Middle position with 9c5c. This is a speculative suited connector type hand, but it is generally too weak to open from middle position after a fold. Standard strategy suggests folding 95s in this spot to maintain a tight-aggressive image and avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop.
T3o is a very weak hand. From the cutoff with 3 players behind, this isn't worth opening. Better to save chips for stronger spots.

Folds to the button with A4s, a speculative suited ace hand with good playability, straight potential (wheel), flush draw potential, and ace blocker. Excellent position to realize equity postflop against blinds who may defend wide. Standard strategy is to open-raise for value/thin value and fold equity against likely folds or calls from blinds. Raising to 3x the big blind (300 total) is a balanced size based on tournament history where opens are around 250-300. Avoid limping as it invites multiway pots and reduces fold equity. Stack depth supports this aggression to build the stack over 150 hands.

I hold J2s in the Small Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button (Elon). Although the Button's range is wide, J2s is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. The pot odds do not justify calling with a hand dominated by most of the opponent's range (better Jacks, better flush draws). 3-betting is too risky with this holding. Preserving chips is the prudent play early in the tournament.

Elon opened to 300 from the button (3x). I'm in the big blind with Qc5h — a marginal, non‑suited hand but one that can still realize equity and occasionally hit a queen or a backdoor play. The pot is 450; calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives ~30% pot odds, which is reasonable to defend against a button open, especially with deep stacks (I have 12225). 3-betting as a bluff with Q5o is unattractive (no good blockers, poor kicker); folding is too tight versus likely wide BTN opens. Plan: call 300 to defend the blind, play cautiously postflop — fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, continue vs small c-bets, and look to extract value or bluff-catch when I improve.

Board K♦ 2♦ 4♠ is very dry and my hand Q♣5♥ has almost no showdown value and no real draw. Elon opened wide from the button, so he will c-bet a lot — I want to keep the pot small and let him bluff. Checking controls the pot, induces bluffs, and preserves my stack depth to call small c-bets or float and try to take the pot on a later card. Plan: check; call a small continuation bet (~≤1/3 pot) to realize equity and possibly bluff-turn, but fold to large pressure (big c-bets or raises) because Q5o has poor equity versus Kx and value hands.

Preflop raiser in position vs BB defender who checked flop. Flop Kd-4s-2d hits my A4hh for bottom pair with Ace kicker, decent showdown value, and backdoor heart flush draw. Board has diamond flush draw and straight potential (53), so c-bet to protect against draws and charge worse hands like weaker pairs, Ax, Kx. Minni's calling range includes those, and history shows she defends wide but folds to pressure. 1/2 pot bet sizes well for thin value and fold equity, maintaining balance. Deep stacks support aggression to build over 150 hands.

Call 325. Elon opened wide from the button and his small ~1/3‑pot c‑bet on the very dry K♦ 2♦ 4♠ is exactly the spot to defend cheaply. Q♣5♥ has little showdown value and no diamond backdoor, but calling is inexpensive to realize equity, keep bluffs in his range, and control pot size. Raising is unjustified (no blockers, very weak hand); plan is to fold to significant turn aggression if I don't improve, but continue only with a queen, a pair of fives, or a clear read/favorable runout.

I have Q♣5♥ on K♦2♦4♠A♦ — I missed completely and have no diamonds. The turn Ace heavily favors the button's opening/continuation range (A, K, 2, 4 combos and possible two‑diamond hands that now make a flush). Betting as a bluff has poor fold equity (he likely continues with many A/K hands) and I have no good blockers or showdown value. Checking controls the pot and lets me fold to any meaningful turn bet; I can call a very small probe but will fold to standard/value sizing. Plan: check and fold to turn aggression; only continue if I pick up a pair or a clear read.

Turn card Ad pairs my Ace and gives me top two pair (Aces and Fours) with a strong holding on a coordinated board (diamond flush possible with 2d Kd Ad, straight draws like 53). Preflop I raised A4hh, which has good playability; flop 2d Kd 4s gave bottom pair, I c-bet thin value/protection, Minni called (likely Ax, Kx, pairs, draws from BB defense). Now checked to me in position. This is a value bet spot: charge draws (flush incomplete? Wait, three diamonds complete flush), worse Ax (A2-A3 straight possible but I block A), sets unlikely. Minni history shows calling flops but vulnerable post-turn. Bet 2/3 pot (900 into 1300) balances value from worse made hands/draws, fold equity vs air, builds pot with strong hand in tournament to maximize stack. Avoid check-back as it gives free river cards and pot control suboptimal with value range.

Elon opened wide from the button, c‑bet the dry K♦2♦4♠ flop and now barrels the turn A♦. Pot = 2,200 and my call is 900, so I need roughly 900/(2,200+900) ≈ 29% equity to make a call correct. I hold Q♣5♥ with no diamonds and only six realistic outs (3 queens + 3 fives) — about 13% to pair on the river — and many of those pairings still lose to A/K/2 combinations that are very much in his continuing range. Raising is unjustified (I have no blockers or fold equity). Given his line and the math, calling is a long‑term loser and my prior plan was to fold to significant turn aggression when unpaired. I fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

In a 6-max game, King-Eight suited (K8s) is a marginal hand from Under the Gun (UTG). While it has potential as a suited king, opening from the earliest position with several aggressive players like Elon and Minni behind increases the risk of being outplayed or dominated. Standard GTO ranges typically suggest folding K8s from UTG to maintain a tighter, more robust range from early position. Given the stack depths and the tendency of opponents to raise or re-raise, I will fold and wait for a more advantageous starting hand.
J7o is a weak offsuit hand in middle position. Not in a standard opening range - too easily dominated and plays poorly postflop. Should fold and wait for a better spot.

J5o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor connectivity and no suited potential. In cutoff position with deep stacks (~100BB effective), folding avoids committing chips to a low-equity hand preflop. Blinds and button are deep and likely to defend/3bet, making a steal attempt low EV. Preserve stack for premium spots in this early tournament stage (hand 22/150).

I am on the Button with Ad 8h. The action has folded to me. A8o is a strong enough hand to open-raise from the Button to steal the blinds. With position and an Ace, I have good equity against the blinds' defending ranges. A standard raise size of 2.5bb (250) puts pressure on the Small Blind and Big Blind while keeping the pot manageable if called.

I'm in the small blind with K♦9♦ facing a button open to 250. This is a marginal but playable hand — I don't want to 3-bet light out of position with K9o, and the price to see a flop is reasonable (I need ~33% equity calling 200 into a 400 pot). Plan: call the 200 more (total of 250 committed) to see a flop, proceed cautiously OOP, and fold to a substantial 3‑bet. If I hit the flop I'll look to extract value or pot-control depending on texture.

Button open to 250 is likely wide, and SB’s flat keeps ranges wide as well. With Q9o in the BB we’re getting a very good price (150 to win 600) and have decent playability vs two wide ranges. A squeeze would put us OOP in a bloated pot with a dominated, offsuit hand and a caller in the SB, so it’s lower EV than taking the price and realizing equity.

Check. I have K9o on a 3d-5c-Qd flop — just an overcard (K) and a weak backdoor diamond draw. I’m out of position to the preflop opener (Pro) with the big blind still behind, so I don’t want to barrel or lead with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, lets me call a small continuation bet to realize equity (and possibly take a free turn), and fold to large, committed barrels. If checked around I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue with improvement or a clear price/read.

Multiway on Qd-5c-3d, the preflop raiser (BTN) retains a range advantage and will do a lot of betting for value/protection with strong Qx, overpairs, and diamond draws. With Qc9s we have a good but not nutted hand and no diamond blocker, so leading risks getting raised by sets/strong Qx and puts us in a tough spot OOP. Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, lets BTN c-bet at high frequency, and we can comfortably check-call most reasonable sizes while keeping the pot manageable versus stronger queens.

I hold Ad8h on a 3d 5c Qd board. This gives me the Nut Flush Draw (Ad + two diamonds on board) and an overcard. The pot is 750 chips. It is a multi-way pot with 3 players. Both opponents checked to me. While a continuation bet is standard with a draw to build the pot and leverage fold equity, doing so multi-way on a board that hits calling ranges (Queen high) risks getting check-raised, which would force a tough decision. Checking back guarantees I see the turn card for free, realizing my equity fully. It also disguises the strength of my hand. If I hit the flush on the turn, I have the nuts and can extract value then. If I hit an Ace, I have top pair. If I miss, I can comfortably evaluate the turn action. Given the multi-way dynamic and having a hand with good showdown value (A-high) plus a strong draw, checking is a prudent, low-variance play.

Turn brought the T♦ giving a 3♦-5♣-Q♦-T♦ board. With K♥9♦ I currently have only K-high (no pair) but a one-card diamond flush draw and a J-gutshot — roughly 12 outs (~26%) to improve on the river. I’m out of position and there are two callers behind me; betting here as a bluff is thin because many hands that call (paired Q/T, small pairs, or draws) will continue or beat me, and I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with only a draw. Pot-control and equity realization are best: check to see the river for free or to call a small bet and fold to a large raise if I don’t improve.

Flop checked through multiway, which caps everyone’s range somewhat and makes it more likely value gets missed if we check again. The Td adds a lot of equity to many hands (diamond draws, J9/KJ/QT, etc.). With top pair (Q9) but no diamond, I want to charge those draws and get thin value from worse Qx/Tx/5x-type hands while keeping the pot controllable OOP. A small-to-medium lead also makes it harder for BTN to realize a free river with overcards/diamonds when he might otherwise check back again.

I hold the Ad, giving me the Nut Flush on this 3d 5c Qd Td board. I have the absolute best possible hand. The Big Blind (FiveTwo) has led out for 300 (roughly 40% pot) into two opponents after the flop checked through. This bet could represent a lower flush, a straight, a set, or a bluff. Since I have the nuts in position, my primary goal is to maximize value. Raising here would likely force out bluffs and weaker made hands that might fire again on the river or check-call a river bet. Additionally, the Small Blind (Minni) is still left to act behind me; just calling gives Minni a better price to come along with a pair or a smaller diamond, whereas raising would likely isolate me against the Big Blind's strongest range or end the hand immediately. Checking/Calling disguises my hand strength, making it look like a float or a marginal made hand, potentially inducing further aggression on the river. There is no risk of giving a free card since I cannot be beaten, and while a board pair on the river is a threat for full houses, it is a statistically small risk compared to the value gained by keeping opponents' ranges wide.

I’ll call 300. On the turn I have K♥9♦ on 3♦-5♣-Q♦-T♦: I currently have K-high but a very strong one-card diamond draw (I hold 9♦ giving me four diamonds) plus straight outs with a Jack and two non-diamond Kings. Counting unique outs: 9 remaining diamonds + 2 non-diamond Kings + 3 non-diamond Jacks ≈ 14 outs, so ~14/46 ≈ 30.4% to improve on the river. The bet is 300 into a 1350 pot, so I need only ~300/(1350+300)=18.2% equity to break even — calling is +EV. I’m out of position and there’s a caller (Pro), so raising as a semi‑bluff would bloat the pot and commit extra chips with a draw that can still be dominated (e.g. someone holding K♦ or A♦). Calling realizes my equity cheaply, keeps the pot controlled, and lets me reevaluate river — value-bet or check/call when I hit, fold to heavy action if I miss.

River: 3♦ 5♣ Q♦ T♦ 5♦. With Kh 9♦ I’ve made a 9-high diamond flush (the board now has four diamonds and my 9♦ completes a five-card flush), but the board is paired so full houses/quads (QQ/TT/33/5x) and higher diamond flushes (A♦/K♦/J♦) can beat me. Turn action (FiveTwo led 300, Pro called, I called) was fairly small/passive, suggesting medium-strength/value hands and some single-diamond holdings in the ranges. I want to extract thin value from worse hands (lower diamond cards, some one-pair hands that will still call a modest bet) while keeping the pot controlled against sets/full houses or a higher flush. Bet ~50% pot is the right balance: not so small that it’s ignored, not so large that a raise commits me when I’m likely dominated. I’ll bet 800 to get value from weaker holdings and fold to significant aggression (a large raise likely represents full house/higher flush).

River is a very bad card for our specific Q9: the 5d both pairs the board (bringing some full houses into play) and, more importantly, puts 4 diamonds out there—so anyone with a single diamond now has a flush. Minni’s lead into two players after check/calling the turn is heavily weighted toward value (flushes, some 5x, occasional boats), and we have no diamond to block those hands. Even though we’re getting ~25% pot odds (800 to win 3250), our equity multiway is worse because (1) Minni’s betting range is strong here and (2) Pro is still behind and can call/raise with a diamond, making our realized win-rate even lower. With no good blockers and no profitable raise option, this is a disciplined fold.

I hold the Nut Flush (Ad) on a board of 3d 5c Qd Td 5d. While I have a very strong hand, the river card (5d) paired the board, making a Full House a distinct possibility for the Small Blind (Minni), who could hold hands like 33, TT, Q5, T5, or 35. Minni checked the flop and turn before leading out for 800 (half pot) on the river. This line is consistent with both a flush valuing itself or a boat. Raising would likely only get called by a Full House (which beats me) or perhaps the King-high flush, but it opens me up to a 3-bet shove which would be a difficult spot. Calling maximizes value against inferior flushes and bluffs while controlling the pot size against superior Full Houses.
2-3 offsuit from UTG is a clear fold. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands and I'm in the worst position at the table. No reason to invest chips here.

Qh3s is a trash hand offsuit with no straight potential, poor high card strength, and dominated by any Qx or better. UTG folded, unopened pot in middle position, but this hand has zero equity to justify limping or raising. Standard fold to wait for premium hands and maximize EV over 150 hands.

I am in the Cutoff with Kc 8h (King-Eight offsuit). The action has folded to me. While the Cutoff allows for a wider opening range, K8o is at the very bottom of a stealing range and often considered a fold, especially with 100BB stacks. Looking at the players behind, Flash in the Big Blind has shown capability to 3-bet (Hand #18), and Minni on the Button is active. Opening here risks getting 3-bet or called by better hands (dominating Kings), leading to difficult post-flop situations. Therefore, folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for better spots.

I’m on the button with T4o — a marginal hand but in the best position at the table. Versus only the blinds to act, I want to seize initiative and apply standard steal pressure rather than limp-call. A minimum open to 200 buys fold equity and keeps the pot manageable with ~100bb effective stacks. If I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold and preserve my stack; if I get called I’ll play the flop aggressively when it’s favorable (Tx, low coordinated boards I’ll c-bet; high/overcard boards I’ll proceed cautiously or check-fold). Raising is the most +EV line here.

Button min-open is likely wide, but Th2o has very poor playability and is heavily dominated when we make top pair. Defending from the small blind forces us to realize equity out of position versus the raiser with the big blind still to act, and we have little implied odds or strong draw potential. Best is to conserve chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 52offsuit. Minni has raised from the Button to 200. While I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, 52o is one of the weakest hands in the game and performs very poorly out of position. It is better to fold and preserve my stack rather than playing a marginal hand from a disadvantageous position.

3h7s is an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, high card value, and playability. From UTG in a 6-handed game at 100BB depth, the standard opening range is tight (premium pairs, broadway hands, suited connectors starting from higher cards). Entering the pot voluntarily with this hand risks getting dominated post-flop or facing 3-bets, leading to poor EV. Folding preserves our stack for better opportunities in a long tournament where position and hand strength matter. No reads suggest loosening up against passive opponents.

I am in Middle Position with Q5o, which is a very weak hand well outside the standard opening range for this position. With four players left to act behind me, the probability of someone waking up with a better hand is high, and this hand plays poorly post-flop. Folding is the only standard play here.

Open-raise to 300 (3xBB). Q♦T♣ is a standard late-position opener: it has decent playability and equity, and I want to leverage my cutoff position to put pressure on the blinds. The table’s typical open sizes have been in the 250–300 range, so 300 is a natural size that reduces limp/callers and preserves fold equity while leaving plenty of postflop maneuvering with ~105 BB effective stacks. If I face a sizable 3-bet (especially from Flash in the SB), I'll fold to heavy aggression; I may consider calling a small, noncommittal 3-bet depending on the villain and sizing, but plan to C-bet favorable flops and play cautiously on A-high or heavily connected boards. Overall raising maximizes expected value vs folding or limp-calling.

Minni opened to 3bb from cutoff and we’re on the button with 5-2 offsuit. Even with position, this hand realizes equity very poorly, is dominated when we make a pair, and doesn’t have good straight/flush playability. Versus a likely reasonably strong cutoff opening range, calling burns chips and turning it into a 3-bet bluff is unnecessarily high variance with a bottom-tier candidate. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 6h. Minni, in the Cutoff, has opened for 3 Bet. 8-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a standard opening raise. While the price to call is 250 more into a 450 pot, this hand has poor playability and realization of equity. Folding is the standard disciplined move here to preserve my stack.

9-4 offsuit is a very weak hand. Minni raised from cutoff and I'd need to call 200 into a 450 pot. Even with decent pot odds (~2.25:1), this hand plays poorly postflop and is dominated by most raising ranges. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten of Hearts and Seven of Hearts (T7s). In a 6-handed game, UTG is the earliest position and requires a tight range because 5 players are yet to act. T7s is a speculative hand (suited gapper) that is generally too weak to open profitably from early position, as it risks domination and playing out of position against stronger ranges. With 100BB stacks deep early in the tournament, there is no pressure to make loose opens. Folding is the standard disciplined play.

J♣4♥ is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and is easily dominated by most opening and defending ranges from players to act. I’m in middle position with several players yet to act (cutoff, button, blinds) and the table has shown frequent preflop aggression — likely facing a raise or a tough multiway pot. Calling 100 out of position with such a hand yields very little equity and poor implied odds; open‑raising to 200 is also unreasonable given the hand quality and likely 3‑bet/flat ranges behind. With a healthy 10650 stack, preserving chips for better spots is the correct long‑term choice. Fold.

Folded to us in the cutoff with QTo (good enough to open in 6-max). We have strong steal equity versus the blinds, and the hand has decent playability when called (can make top pair with good kickers, broadway straights). Limping invites squeezes and multiway pots; raising takes initiative and wins the 150 pot often. Use a standard, non-committing open size.

I am on the button with King-Trey offsuit. While I have position, FiveTwo has opened from the cutoff. K3o is a very weak hand and does not perform well against a standard opening range from that position. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with poor equity and high reverse implied odds. 3-betting as a bluff is an option, but with two active players behind me in the blinds and a fairly standard open size from FiveTwo, it is more profitable to wait for a better spot. Folding is the standard professional play here.

I have JJ in the small blind - a premium hand. FiveTwo raised to 250 from the cutoff. With pocket jacks, I should 3-bet for value to isolate and build the pot. A standard 3-bet size here would be around 3x the open, so approximately 750-800. This puts pressure on FiveTwo and Elon in the BB while extracting value from my strong hand.

3c5h is a trash hand: low offsuit connector with minimal playability, poor high card strength, and no flush potential here. Facing a SB 3-bet over a CO open-raise (Claude to 800 after FiveTwo's 250), Claude's range is strong (TT+, AQ+, maybe some bluffs but tight given history of aggression with value). FiveTwo's open range medium-wide but now facing squeeze. Calling 700 into 1150 pot (~27% needed) OOP vs two players is disaster: dominated equity (~15-20% vs typical 3bet), multiway bad. No fold equity to raise/shove profitably with this hand. Preserve stack for better spots; auto-fold.

We opened CO with QTo, but versus a small blind 3-bet to 800 this offsuit Broadway is a dominated, low-realization defend (often crushed by AQ/KQ/QJ/AT and all strong pairs). The immediate pot odds (550 to win ~1700) look tempting, but our equity and realization versus a likely value-heavy SB 3-bet range aren’t good enough, and a 4-bet bluff would need an unrealistically high fold rate. Best stack-maximizing decision is to preserve chips and wait for better 3-bet defenses.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 9♦8♠ offsuit. This is early position facing five players and I'm out of position postflop — 98o is playable in late position or as a 3-bet/facing a small raise when suited, but UTG it’s too weak. Limping (call 100) invites multiway pots and domination; an open-raise would be thin and likely to face 3-bets from tighter players. With ~106bb I can wait for a better spot and preserve my stack to exploit later, so fold preflop.

Unopened pot (only blinds), but K8o from middle position in a 6-max, deeper-stacked (90bb) lineup with several aggressive/3-bet-prone players behind is too dominated to profitably open. Limping is weak and invites squeezes; raising creates lots of tough, dominated postflop spots. Preserve stack and wait for a better opening hand/spot.

I am in the Cutoff position with Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo). Since the action has folded to me, this is a premium hand that I should open for a raise to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds. A standard raise size of 3 big blinds (300 chips) is appropriate here.

52 suited is too weak to defend against a raise, even with position on the button. While suited gives some playability, this is bottom of range and not worth calling 300 with two players still to act behind. Easy fold.

Q2o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by Flash's likely Cutoff raising range, which includes any broadway cards, pairs, and suited connectors. Facing a 3x raise from CO, in SB position (out of position postflop), folding is standard to avoid difficult spots OOP with marginal equity (~25-30% vs typical CO range) and poor implied odds. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 (pre-BB action) are not compelling enough for this trash hand. No reason to raise a bluff with this. Preserve stack for better spots early in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 9c, a very weak off-suit unconnected hand. The player in the Cutoff (Flash) has raised to 300 (3 BB). I need to call 200 to win a pot of 450, offering me pot odds of roughly 30.7%. Against a Cutoff opening range, 96o has approximately 32% raw equity. However, playing this hand out of position is difficult, and I will likely under-realize my equity significantly. Defending trash hands like 96o to a 3x raise is a losing long-term strategy. I will fold.

UTG in a 6-max game, J3 offsuit is far below an opening range: it’s dominated by every Jx and most broadways, plays poorly multiway when we limp, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position. With no ante pressure and 90BB+ stacks, there’s no need to defend the 100 here.

I am in middle position with 9d 4d. This is a suited hand but very weak in terms of high-card value. FiveTwo has already folded. While the hand has some flush potential, it is generally too weak to open from middle position in a 6-handed game. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
K8 suited in the cutoff with two folds in front. This is a solid opening hand from this position - suited broadway with decent playability. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB.

Preflop on the button with A4o facing a cutoff raise to 250 (2.5BB) from Claude, who has been aggressive in prior hands. Excellent position postflop. A4o blocks strong Ax hands like AA/AK, making it a solid semi-bluff 3-bet candidate. Deep stacks (~100BB), opponents (SB Pro and BB Minni) have folded frequently to aggression. 3-bet sizing around 3.4x the open (850 total) to apply pressure, build pot with equity when called, or take it down preflop. Folding too tight on BTN, calling risks multiway pots or domination OOP postflop despite position.

I am holding 7h 5c in the Small Blind, facing a raise from the Cutoff and a 3-bet from the Button. 75 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor equity against a 3-betting range. Being out of position against two active players, one of whom has shown significant strength, makes this an easy fold. There is no strategic justification for calling or bluffing in this spot.

I’m in the big blind with K♠4♥ facing a button 3-bet to 850 after a cutoff open to 250. The pot is 1,250 and I must call 750 (≈37.5% pot odds). K4o is a weak, easily‑dominated hand vs a button 3‑bet range and there’s still the original raiser (Claude) who can act again, making a multiway pot likely. Out of position with a poor kicker and limited equity, calling or 4‑betting is too speculative — preserve the 10,550 stack for better spots. Fold.

Elon 3-bet me to 850 from the button. K8s is a reasonable open from the cutoff but not strong enough to continue against a 3-bet. I'm out of position postflop against the button, and K8s doesn't play well in 3-bet pots - often dominated by hands like AK, KQ, KJ. Elon has shown aggression with 3-bets throughout this session. Calling 600 into a 1250 pot with this marginal holding out of position is -EV. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the UTG position with 8h7c, which is a weak offsuit connector. In a 6-max tournament, opening this hand from the earliest position is generally unprofitable and risky, especially given that there are five players left to act. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

AJs in middle position is a strong hand. With Flash folding UTG, I should open raise here. Standard sizing of 2.5x BB (250) is appropriate. This hand plays well both for value and has good playability postflop.

AcJd is a strong offsuit Ace from the cutoff facing an open from Claude in middle position. Claude has shown a pattern of folding to my previous 3-bets (Hands 2, 9, 27). Deep stacks (~100BB), excellent position, and good blockers make this a profitable 3-bet spot with significant fold equity. Sizing to 850 (roughly 3.4x the open) is consistent with prior successful 3-bets against Claude and builds a nice pot if called while maintaining fold equity. Post-flop playability is strong if it goes further. Avoid calling to prevent multi-way pots with blinds behind; raising isolates and applies pressure.

I am holding King-Ten offsuit (KsTd) on the Button. The action started with a raise to 250 from Middle Position (Claude), followed by a 3-bet to 850 from the Cutoff (Elon). Facing a raise and a re-raise, my hand is significantly behind the likely ranges of my opponents. KTo is easily dominated by hands like AK, KQ, KJ, and pocket pairs TT+. Calling would be negative EV as I am risking 8.5 BBs cold with a marginal hand, and the original raiser is still left to act behind me. The prudent play is to fold.

Facing a cutoff 3-bet to 850 after a middle-open, K♣Q♥ from the small blind is a marginal hand. Pot is 1,250 and it costs me 800 to call (850 total). Calling puts me out of position vs the 3-bettor and likely into a multiway pot with the opener still in — a very uncomfortable spot when my hand is often dominated by the 3-bettor’s range (AK, QQ+, AQs, etc.). The pot odds (risk 800 to win ~2,050) require ~39% equity, which KQo rarely has vs a typical 3-bet range. A min‑reraise to 1,450 is too small to credibly fold out hands and just invites a 5-bet or call, and a larger 4‑bet shove is too committed with a non‑premium. With healthy stacks and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better, cleaner spot is the best line. I fold.

We’re in the BB facing an open to 250 and a 3-bet to 850 with 9♣2♦. Calling 750 to go 3-ways in a 3-bet pot gives us terrible equity and extremely poor playability (dominated, no strong straight/flush potential, hard to realize any equity multiway). A 4-bet bluff is also inappropriate here given hand quality and two opponents’ demonstrated strength. Best EV is to surrender the blind and wait for a better spot.

AJs is too strong to fold to a 3-bet. Elon has been 3-betting my opens multiple times (hands 2, 9, 27, and now 28), suggesting he may be exploiting me. While I'm out of position, AJs has good playability postflop with nut flush potential and top pair possibilities. Calling is standard here - folding is too tight and 4-betting puts us in an awkward spot if he 5-bets.

Excellent flop for me - I hit top pair top kicker (Jacks with Ace kicker) on J64 board, plus I have a backdoor nut heart flush draw with Ah. Standard play is to check to the preflop aggressor. This allows Elon to c-bet with his range, and I can either call to let him continue barreling or check-raise for value. Checking keeps his bluffs in and extracts value from worse hands.

Post-flop with AcJd on 4s6hJh board, I have top pair top kicker (TPTK). The board is relatively dry: two hearts (no flush draw yet), possible gutshot straight draws (e.g., 5x), but few strong draws. Claude check-called my pre-flop 3-bet, so his range includes pairs (77+, AT+), suited broadways, and some suited connectors, but skewed toward value after calling the 3-bet. His check suggests weakness or trapping, but with position and deep stacks (~100BB), betting for value and thin value/protection is standard. Betting ~65% pot (1200) builds the pot, denies equity to draws/overcards, and exploits his check-call tendencies seen in prior hands (e.g., Hand 1,5,20). Checking risks giving free cards to improve hands or bluffs. Excellent playability and fold equity make this +EV.

I flopped TPTK (Jacks with Ace kicker) plus backdoor nut flush draw. Elon's 1200 c-bet into ~1850 is a standard sizing. With top pair top kicker, I'm ahead of most of his 3-bet range except AA/KK/QQ. Calling is optimal - it keeps his bluffs and worse value (AK, AQ, TT, 99) in his range. Raising risks folding out worse hands while only getting action from better. I'll call and re-evaluate the turn.

Turn 5s is a blank. I have TPTK on J64-5 board. I'm first to act and Elon was the preflop aggressor who c-bet the flop. Standard play is to check again - if he bets, I call with my strong hand. If he checks back, I can value bet the river. No need to lead out and potentially fold out his bluffs. My hand is strong enough to call another barrel but not strong enough to build a huge pot against his tight 3-bet range.

The turn 5s completes the wheel straight (3-4-5-6) and brings a second spade, increasing draws and possible made hands like 3x or J6/65 two-pair. However, AcJd remains TPTK, the strongest pair, blocking top pair. Claude's check-call flop and check turn line indicates medium strength (e.g., 77-TT, weaker Jx, flush draw, gutshots) rather than nuts, as monsters often lead or check-raise. His history shows passive check-calls (Hands 1,5,20) and folds to pressure. With position, deep stacks (Claude ~7300 effective), and pot 4250, betting ~60% pot (2500) extracts value from worse hands/draws, denies equity, protects against river villains (7x,3x,spades,hearts), and maintains aggression from pre/ flop. Checking concedes pot control and free cards to improvers. This sizing balances value/protection with fold equity vs. draws.

I have TPTK (Jacks with Ace kicker) on J-6-4-5 board. Elon has been 3-betting me frequently and barreling. Turn 5s is a blank. Pot is 6750, need 2500 to call (37% pot). With top pair top kicker, I'm ahead of his bluffs (AK, AQ, suited connectors) and value bets from worse pairs. Only AA/KK/QQ/JJ beat me. Folding TPTK to two barrels would be too exploitable against an aggressive opponent. I have 7300 left so calling leaves me with 4800 for river decisions.

Elon checked the river after betting flop and turn. I have TPTK on J-6-4-5-9 board. The 9d is mostly a blank - only 99 improved to a set, and 78 made a straight but that's an unlikely 3-bet holding. With Elon checking, he likely has either a give-up bluff (AK/AQ) or is trapping. Given his aggression throughout, I should bet for thin value - worse Jx, TT, and possibly some curiosity calls will pay me off. Betting around 40% pot (2000) extracts value while not overcommitting.

On the river facing Claude's 2000 bet into a 11250 pot (~6:1 odds, needing ~14% equity), holding TPTK (Jh-paired with Ac kicker) on a 4s-6h-Jh-5s-9d board. Key dangers: straights like 3x7x (wheel from turn) or 7x8x (5-6-7-8-9). Claude's line—calling preflop 3-bet, check-calling flop 1200 (~1/3 pot) and massive turn 2500 (~60% pot)—caps his range to medium pairs (77-TT, weaker Jx), two-pairs (J5/J6/J4/65/46/45), or hero-called draws that bricked (spade flush draw missed, heart draw missed). Pure bluffs unlikely after tank-calling turn bomb; monsters (sets JJ/66/55/44, combo draws) would raise earlier. Sudden river lead-after-passivity suggests thin value or semi-bluff, but my blocking Ac/Jd caps villain's top value. History shows Claude passive check-calls (Hands 1,5,15,20), folding to pressure, but here committed. Pot odds excellent, equity >25% vs. his range (beats worse Jx, 1-2 pairs, dead draws; loses to ~30% straights/two-pair+). Calling 2000/6275 stack commits ~32%, villain's 2800 behind sets up ICM/dynamic if he jams but favors my range. Raising risks cooler calls/folds out worse; pure call maximizes EV exploiting his capped range.
A9o in UTG is a marginal but playable hand in 6-max. I've been getting 3-bet by Elon frequently, so I should be prepared to fold to a big 3-bet. Standard open to 2.5x.

79s is a speculative suited connector with good playability in deep-stack tournament. UTG raiser (Claude) has a strong range, but calling from middle position offers position on him postflop and potential multiway pot value with implied odds. Pot odds are favorable (250 to call into 400+). Folding is too tight; 3-betting is not ideal as it's not a premium. Will call and see flop, ready to play aggressively if it hits.

I hold 9d2h in the Cutoff. This is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em (disconnected, off-suit, low ranks). An Under-the-Gun player (Claude) has raised to 250, indicating strength, and a Middle Position player (Elon) has called. My pot odds are roughly 28%, but my equity against two reasonable ranges is significantly lower than that. Furthermore, the hand has zero playability post-flop, and there are still players left to act behind me. Squeezing is ill-advised given the UTG open strength. The only correct play is to fold immediately.

QsTs suited on the button is a strong, playable hand with position. Claude opened UTG and Elon just called, so this is multiway; I'm not strong enough to 3-bet for value and a small raise to 400 won't credibly isolate the callers and could get me 3-bet. Folding is too tight. Call 250 to see a flop, preserve positional advantage, realize equity (flush/straight/broadway), and play post‑flop for value or as a controlled bluff depending on texture.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open plus two callers, with the big blind still to act. 7♥5♦ is offsuit and has poor playability multiway OOP (weak top pairs, dominated straights, and hard to realize equity). The price is tempting, but the positional and reverse-implied-odds tax is too high, and squeezing would be spewy versus an UTG range and multiple callers. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind holding Kc 6d. Claude raised from UTG and received two callers. The pot is currently 900 and it costs me only 150 more to call, giving me pot odds of 7:1. Although K6o is a weak hand that plays poorly multi-way, the price is too good to pass up. I will call to see the flop and proceed with caution, as I am likely dominated if a King hits.

I am first to act in a four-way pot on a 2c-8c-5s board. I hold King-high (Kc 6d) which has very little equity against three opponents. I have a backdoor club flush draw and a backdoor straight draw, but nothing substantial enough to lead out with a bet. My strategy is to check and likely fold to any significant aggression, as my hand is weak and out of position.

Multiway pot with 4 players on a 2c8c5s board. I have A9o - complete air with just ace high. This is a very wet board with a club flush draw. In a 4-way pot, c-betting with no pair and no draw is burning money. I should check and potentially realize equity if an ace comes, or fold to significant action.

Preflop call with 79s was correct for speculative playability multiway with position. Flop 2c8c5s gives me a weak gutshot straight draw (4 outs to the 6 for 5-6-7-8-9) and a backdoor spade flush draw (3 spades total). No pair or strong draw in a 4-way pot of 1050. Both Flash (BB) and Claude (UTG preflop raiser) checked, signaling possible weakness, but betting here risks getting raised by better draws or made hands while my equity is only ~10-15% against ranges. Checking realizes equity for free multiway, preserves stack early in tournament (hand 29/150, stacks ~10bb effective? Wait no, stacks 10k+ into 1k pot, deep), and keeps pot small. Will check and evaluate turn.

Multiway pot (Claude UTG opened, Elon called, Flash in BB) and I’m on the button with QsTs. Flop 2c 8c 5s is very dry with two clubs — I have no club draw, only two overcards and a backdoor spade (need both turn+river). I have ~6 clean outs to pair by the river (~24% to hit by river), but my fold equity is minimal with three players in and the preflop raiser still present. Betting here will often be called by better hands or club draws and put me in difficult spots on turn/river. With a ~10k stack vs a 1,050 pot, pot control and realization of equity is the +EV line: check and see a turn, then decide based on whether I pick up a pair, a spade/queen/ten, or face significant aggression. If called by a single player I can use position to barrel selectively; if bet into, I’ll fold to large turn bets and continue with small calls against passive lines.

The turn 5h pairs the board. Given that all four players checked the flop, it is unlikely anyone has a dominant hand like a set or a strong 8, although a 5 is now a possibility. My King-high (Kc 6d) still has very little showdown value, but I do have a flush draw if a club hits the river. I will check to see if the action remains passive, allowing me to potentially see a free river card or evaluate based on others' bets.

Turn brings the 5h, pairing the board. Everyone checked the flop showing weakness, but I'm still in a 4-way pot with just ace high. The paired board doesn't improve my hand. While I could try to take a stab after everyone checked, betting into 3 opponents with complete air is too risky. If I bet and get called or raised, I'm in a terrible spot. Better to check and potentially catch an ace on the river or win at showdown if everyone keeps checking.

On the turn, the board is 2c-8c-5s-5h, pairing the fives. My 7s9s still holds a live gutshot straight draw to the 6 (4 outs for the nuts straight 5-6-7-8-9), plus backdoor spade flush potential, but no pair or strong made hand. The pot is multiway (Flash BB, Claude UTG, Minni button) at 1050 with all checks so far, indicating likely weakness or traps, but betting risks isolation against better made hands (sets, two-pair like 85, 25, 55) or stronger draws while my equity is thin (~8-12% against calling ranges). Deep stacks (10k+), early tournament (hand 29/150), checking realizes equity for free multiway, controls pot size, and keeps opponents' weak ranges in. Positionally, Minni acts last, but no strong reason to bluff here with so many opponents and board texture favoring made hands. Will check and re-evaluate river.

Check. I have QsTs on the button in a multiway pot (Claude opened, Elon & BB called). The turn (2c 8c 5s 5h) removes my backdoor flush hope — even with two spades in hand a five‑card spade flush is impossible now. I only have 6 clean outs (3 Qs + 3 Ts) to make a pair on the river (~13% single‑card equity). Against an opener and multiple callers my fold equity is very low, so a bet will be called too often by better hands (pocket pairs, 8x, 5x) or by club draws that still have a chance on the river. Pot control is the +EV line: check and see the river. If I pair the river I can bet for value; if I miss and face a large bet I can fold without risking a big portion of my ~10k stack. If everyone checks down I show down cheaply or consider a small river bluff only if the river and player actions make it believable.

The board is 2c-8c-5s-5h-3s and I have King-high. After everyone checked both the flop and the turn in a four-way pot, it's possible that the range of my opponents is weak. However, with three other players remaining, the likelihood that someone has at least a small pair or Ace-high that beats my King-high is high. I don't see much value in bluffing here as I'd have to get three people to fold, and I'm happy to take a free showdown if it checks around again. I will check and fold to any bet.

River completes with 3s on a 2c8c5s5h3s board. Everyone has checked through all streets showing weakness. I have ace-high which could actually be good here. Betting would likely only get called by better hands (pairs, two pair) and fold out worse hands I'm already beating. Better to check and see if A-high takes it at showdown.

River 3s completes the board to 2c-8c-5s-5h-3s. My 7s9s missed the gutshot straight draw (no 6), spade flush draw (only four spades total: 3s5s7s9s), and made nothing--9-high air. Multiway pot (Flash BB, Claude UTG raiser, Minni BTN) with all checks through three streets signals weakness across the board: unlikely sets/two-pair/top pair check back multiway. Deep stacks (~10k into 1050 pot), early tournament (29/150), no fold equity on paired board favoring made hands/draw completers. Betting risks calls from any pair (55, 85, 25, 35, 38, 23s) or rivered flush/straight draws, while thin value bets get raised. Checking realizes showdown equity with 9-high (beats pure air/bluffs) and induces Minni's bluffs or chop. Consistent with prior streets' pot control and equity realization strategy.

I missed — QsTs never paired and the river (2c 8c 5s 5h 3s) leaves me with only Q-high. This is a multiway pot with the original raiser still involved; there’s a high likelihood opponents hold a pair (5x, 8x, pocket pair) or will call a small river bet with marginal hands. My fold equity is essentially nil and a small bluff will be called too often, while a larger bluff risks a meaningful chunk of my ~10.25k stack for little reward. Pot-control and stack preservation are the +EV lines: check and fold to any real aggression, otherwise take showdown for free.

UTG in a 6-handed game with deep stacks (~100BB), K9o is not in my opening range. It's offsuit, dominated by better kings, and vulnerable post-flop. Folding preserves stack for better spots. No reads from history justify a limp or raise here.

I am in Middle Position with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o). The UTG player has folded. In a 6-max game, A7o is a marginal hand from this position. It is often dominated by better Aces (A8+, AT+) that opponents in late position or the blinds might defend with. Opening this hand creates reverse implied odds, as I could win a small pot or lose a large one if dominated. Given the tournament stage (early-mid) and stack depth (100BB), there is no pressure to make loose opens. The disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Cutoff with A♥T♥ is a strong, positionally playable hand — I want to take the initiative and avoid giving the Button and blinds a free or cheap flop. With ~102bb effective, a standard 3x open to reduce callers and gain fold equity is appropriate. I’ll raise to 3x the BB (300). Postflop I’ll continuation-bet on ace/heart/two-tone flops, control the pot on dry boards, and re-evaluate if I face a sizable 3-bet (fold vs very large 3-bets from tight players, consider calling or 4-betting bluff selectively vs aggressive opponents).

CO open to 3bb from Minni looks like a reasonably wide raising range. QTo is playable on the button with position, but it’s not strong enough to happily 3-bet and then face a 4-bet (and it’s also somewhat dominated when we make one-pair). Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity well in position, and lets us apply pressure postflop versus a wide range while avoiding unnecessary variance preflop.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 6d. There has been a raise to 300 from Minni (CO) and a call from FiveTwo (Button). I have to put in 250 more to call into a pot of 750. While I am getting decent pot odds (3:1), 63 suited is a very weak hand to play out of position against two opponents. I also have the Big Blind (Claude) yet to act behind me, who could potentially squeeze. Given my position and the relative weakness of my hand, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid difficult post-flop situations.

Pocket 4s in BB facing a raise to 300 from CO and a call from Button. I need 200 to call into a pot of 750, getting 3.75:1 direct odds. While not ideal set-mining odds, with two opponents and potential implied odds if I hit a set, this is a standard call. Folding 44 here would be too tight, and raising with a small pocket pair against two opponents doesn't make much sense.

Flopped bottom set on 9h 2d 4c - this is a monster hand on a dry board. I should bet for value to build the pot. A small to medium bet (around 1/3-1/2 pot) looks like a standard probe/donk bet and can get called by 9x, pocket pairs, or overcards. Checking risks missing value on this dry texture where draws won't pay off.

I have A♥T♥ on a dry 9♥-2♦-4♣ flop: two overcards plus a backdoor heart (runner-runner) but no pair. Claude's bet is small (350 into ~1300, ~25–30% pot), which is consistent with a wide c-bet/probe or a small value bet. Calling 350 is cheap to see a turn where I can realize equity (ace/ten pair, a heart, or take it down if he checks) and I keep weaker hands and bluffs in the pot. A minimum raise to 700 would bloat the pot and commit chips with only Ace-high and limited equity; folding is too weak given my blockers and outs. Plan: call now, re-evaluate on the turn — fold to sizable turn/river aggression without improvement, or build/exploit if I pair the Ace or pick up a heart.

We have two overcards and decent backdoor equity, plus position, in a multiway pot. Claude’s small donk looks like a wide range (lots of 9x/pairs/small hands) and Minni’s call keeps many one-pair hands in. Raising here as a bluff is less attractive multiway (we’ll get called often and be forced to barrel on many turns). Calling realizes our equity, keeps the pot manageable with a somewhat dominated one-pair type hand, and preserves the option to take it away on favorable turns (Q/T, broadway, spade, or when checked to).

I flopped bottom set and now the turn brought a Jack. This is a great card for me as it could have given someone top pair or two pair (J9). With a set on this board, I need to continue betting for value. The pot is 2000 and I have two callers who showed interest on the flop. A bet around 60-70% pot should extract value from Jx, 9x, and any overpairs while building the pot with my monster hand.

I have A♥T♥ on 9♥-2♦-4♣-J♠ and did not improve on the turn. The turn card removes my backdoor heart possibility (I needed both turn+river to make that flush), so my only clean outs are 3 Aces + 3 Tens (~6 outs ≈ 13% to hit on the river). Claude’s line (call preflop, small flop bet, now a sizable 1,300 turn bet) strongly indicates a value-heavy range (Jx, 9x, sets/two-pair) and this is a multiway pot which further reduces bluff equity and my fold equity. Pot math: calling 1,300 to win ~4,600 (post-call) gives ~22% required equity — well above my ~13% raw equity and I’d still often be behind even if I pair up. I previously planned to fold to significant turn/river aggression without improvement. For stack preservation and because the call/raise are +EV negative here, I fold.

Turn goes heads-up after Minni folds. With QsTc on 9h-2d-4c-Js we pick up a clean open-ended straight draw (8 or K) plus some additional equity from pairing Q/T at times. Calling 1300 into a pot of 3300 gives us about 28% required equity; the OESD alone is ~17% and the extra pair outs/implied odds (when we hit a straight) plus positional advantage make the call profitable. Raising as a semi-bluff is higher variance and risks getting shoved on by Jx/sets while not folding out enough better hands; in position we can realize equity and potentially apply pressure on many rivers if Claude slows down.

I have a set of 4s on 9h 2d 4c Js 8s. This is still a very strong hand. FiveTwo called my 1300 turn bet, indicating they have something - likely Jx, 9x, or maybe a missed draw. The 8s completes T7 for a straight but that's an unlikely holding. I should value bet to extract from top pair and second pair hands. Around 2000-2300 (roughly half pot) should get called by Jx holdings while not overcommitting if villain happens to have a weird straight.

We river a made straight (8-9-T-J-Q) with QsTc on 9h-2d-4c-Js-8s, so we’re almost always ahead of Claude’s one-pair/two-pair/set value and any bluffs that kept firing. The key issue is that the one very plausible better hand is KT (king-high straight), which is not blocked by our hand and is definitely in a BB defend range; his flop donk → big turn barrel → river value bet is consistent with arriving at the river with KT as well as with sets/two pair. Raising here is high-variance and tends to get action disproportionately from the top of his range (including KT), while worse hands (like 9x / some two pair) may fold to a raise. Calling takes the excellent price (2200 to win ~9000) and locks in strong EV without risking our tournament stack against the narrow portion that beats us.

I am in UTG position with 9s 7c, which is a very weak unsuited one-gapper (97o). Opening this hand from early position is a losing play in the long run due to the high likelihood of being dominated and playing out of position against 5 other players. With 100BB effective stacks, there is no incentive to get involved with trash hands. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Pocket deuces in middle with a ~96bb stack are worth opening for fold equity and set-mining. The pot is just the blinds (150) so I’ll open to a standard 3bb (300 total) — bigger than the min-raise to discourage multiway callers and give myself initiative postflop. Plan: continue aggressively only if I hit a set; otherwise check/control the pot and fold to heavy action on dangerous boards. If faced with a small-to-medium 3-bet I may call to set-mine given deep stacks; fold to very large 3-bets (too much risk vs a low pair).

7♠3♥ offsuit is near-bottom of range with no connectivity, no suitedness, and poor realization in position versus a 3bb MP open. It has no relevant blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff and calling would be -EV even deep due to domination and lack of playable flops. Preserve stack and avoid marginal spots.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from middle position. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well against an opening range, even with the advantage of position. I have no reason to defend or bluff here, so I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Qd9d is a decent suited hand with flush potential. Facing a min-raise from Minni, calling 250 to see a flop is reasonable. While I'll be out of position, suited connectors-ish hands play well multiway. Not strong enough to 3-bet but too good to fold at this price.

J8o is a marginal offsuit hand with limited playability. Facing a middle position raise to 3BB and a small blind call, the villain's range is strong enough that my hand lacks sufficient equity and fold equity to justify a call or raise. Pot odds are decent (200 into 700), but multiway pots favor premium hands, and positionally I'm out of position postflop. Standard play is to fold and wait for better opportunities with deep stacks this early in the tournament (hand 31/150).

I flopped top pair with Qd on a Jc Qs 8s board. Decent spot but the board is draw-heavy with the spade flush draw and straight possibilities. I'm out of position against the preflop raiser. With a medium kicker (9), checking to Minni is standard - I can call a bet or check back if they check. No need to lead out and bloat the pot when I could be dominated by AQ/KQ.

I have pocket deuces and missed the J♣ Q♠ 8♠ flop — no set and only a remote backdoor spade possibility. Claude (SB) called preflop so his range is wide and contains many hands that currently beat a lone pair (Qx, Jx, broadways, pocket pairs). Pot is 700 and stacks are deep enough that I don't want to inflate the pot with such a weak holding. Checking controls the size of the pot, lets me realize equity cheaply, and keeps options open — I can call a small c-bet to see a turn but will fold to significant pressure unless I improve. Therefore I check.

Third spade hit the turn, completing possible flushes. I have top pair with Q9 but no spade. This is a bad card for my hand. Betting into a completed flush draw is risky - if Minni has any two spades they now have me crushed. Better to check and control pot size. If Minni bets, I can call a reasonable size with my top pair, but I don't want to bloat the pot when the board just got significantly worse for my hand.

I missed my set and only hold pocket deuces on a very wet board (J♣ Q♠ 8♠ 5♠). There are three spades on board so flushes are possible and many hands (Qx, Jx, two pair, sets, flushes) currently beat me. Pot is small (700) and stacks are deep, so I want to control the pot and realize equity cheaply rather than bloating it with a weak hand. I’ll check and fold to significant turn/river aggression unless I improve or get a very favorable read/spot to bluff on the river.

River brought Js - now there are 4 spades on board and paired jacks. I have Queens and Jacks (two pair) with no spade. Any single spade beats me. Minni checked turn and river, suggesting weakness or a trap. Betting for value is bad - I only get called by flushes or boats. Check and showdown my two pair which may be good if Minni also has no spade.

Check. I have pocket deuces and never improved on an extremely wet board (J♣ Q♠ 8♠ 5♠ J♠). The river put four spades on board, so any single spade in Claude’s hand is a made flush; his calling line (preflop call, passive check-calls on flop/turn) means his continuing range contains many hands that beat me (any spade, Qx, Jx, 8x/5x, mid pocket pairs). Fold equity for a river bluff is very low against this player and line, and there’s virtually no value to extract. Pot is small (700) vs deep stacks, so I control the pot by checking and will fold to meaningful river aggression rather than bloating the pot with a hopeless hand.

I’m UTG in a 6-handed game with 9♦8♦. Deep stacks (~100BB) and a relatively passive table make this a playable hand to open. Suited connectors realize a lot of equity postflop but I don’t want a multiway pot, so I’ll open to a standard 3x pot-size-ish raise to isolate the blinds and gain fold equity. Size (300) is consistent with my previous opens and keeps postflop play manageable. Plan: C-bet favorable flops, control pot on overcards, and fold to a large 3‑bet unless pot odds + position justify a call.

Facing a 3x UTG open, 85o is too weak to continue: it plays poorly multiway, is frequently dominated when we make a pair, and we have no positional advantage (still players behind who can squeeze). With deep enough stacks, we’d rather reserve calls/3-bets for hands with better equity realization (suited connectors, broadways, strong aces/pairs).

I am in the Cutoff with Five-Deuce suited. Minni has opened from Under the Gun (UTG) with a 3x raise to 300. Five-Deuce suited is a very weak speculative hand that does not play well against an early position opening range. The pot odds are not favorable enough to justify a call, and a 3-bet would be an overplay with such a low-equity hand. Standard play is to fold.

Suited connector 34h on the button facing UTG open to 300. Position is great, and suited connectors can flop well. Getting ~1.5:1 pot odds. Hand is marginal but playable on the button with position. Calling to see a flop.

Hand #32, pre-flop in SB with J5s suited connectors. Facing an UTG raise to 300 from Minni (tight early position range) and a button call from Claude (potentially wider but still adds strength). Pot is 750, to call 250 into a likely multiway pot with BB to act. J5s has speculative value but poor playability multiway, low equity against strong ranges, and I'm out of position post-flop. Deep stacks (105BB), early in tournament (32/150 hands), better to fold marginal hands and wait for stronger spots to build stack without unnecessary risk.

I am in the Big Blind with Q3o facing a raise from UTG (Minni) and a call from the Button (Claude). The pot odds are roughly 3.75:1 (calling 200 to win 750), requiring about 21% equity. However, Q3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position in a multi-way pot. It suffers significantly from reverse implied odds (domination on Queen-high boards). Against an UTG range, I am likely far behind. Folding is the standard disciplined play.

I have top pair (9♦) with an 8 kicker on a low, fairly dry 9♠-3♦-2♠ flop. We're heads-up versus a button caller who likely has a wide/capped calling range (small pairs, broadways, suited connectors, some A-x). This is a clear c-bet spot: take the initiative to get value from worse pairs and charge any spade/backdoor draws while denying free cards. I’ll size ~half-pot to extract and not overcommit with a medium kicker. If called I’ll reevaluate on the turn and be cautious facing a large check‑raise or pot-sized raise (fold to very large aggression; call/smooth‑continue vs small resistance).

I flopped bottom pair (3s) with a gutshot straight draw (need a 5). Board is 9s3d2s with a spade flush draw. Minni c-bet 400 into 750. Getting ~2.9:1 pot odds needing ~26% equity. My bottom pair is very vulnerable - any overcard or spade completer beats me. With only ~8 outs at best (2 for trips, 2 for two pair, 4 for gutshot), I'm around 16% equity. Not getting the right price and likely crushed by Minni's range. Fold.

UTG in 6-max with J7o is too loose: it’s frequently dominated by the calling/3-betting ranges behind (AJ/KJ/QJ, bigger Jx, suited broadways), plays poorly out of position, and creates reverse implied odds when we make a weak top pair. With plenty of playable hands and no dead money beyond blinds, best is to pass.

I have been dealt 7c 2s, which is mathematically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am in middle position with several players yet to act behind me. There is no strategic justification for playing this hand; therefore, folding is the standard and correct play.
AKo in the cutoff is a premium hand. With two folds in front, I should raise for value. Standard 2.5x raise to 250 to build the pot and isolate.

96o is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and playability post-flop. Facing a cutoff raise to 2.5BB into a pot with blinds still to act, the correct play is to fold. Even on the button, this hand does not warrant a call or 3-bet. Pot odds are not compelling enough for a speculative call, and villains may squeeze. Preserve chips for premium spots in a deep-stack tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with Qh 7d, facing a raise to 250 from Claude in the Cutoff. Q7 offsuit is a statistically weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. It is dominated by many of the raiser's potential Queen-X hands (AQ, KQ, QJ, QT) and pairs. The pot odds of calling 200 to win 400 (2:1) are not sufficient to overcome the positional disadvantage and reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

I'm in the big blind with pocket 2s facing a CO open to 250. Calling the extra 150 (250 total) is the standard set-mining play: good implied odds against Claude's ~52BB stack and I get to see a cheap flop heads-up. A min 3-bet to 400 with 22 is unattractive — the pair plays poorly postflop and I don't want to inflate the pot without a strong hand. I'll call to see the flop; if I hit a set I'll extract value, if I miss I'll play cautiously and fold to heavy turn/river pressure. If Claude jams over my call I'll re-evaluate (likely call given pot odds/effective stacks).

I called preflop as a set-mining play, but the Js Tc Ac flop completely missed my 22. I have no club and only two remaining deuces (≈8.4% to hit a set by the river), so my hand has very poor showdown equity and almost no fold equity. Claude (CO opener) will c-bet a large portion of his range here (Ax, broadways, clubs), so the correct line is pot control: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If Claude checks back I’ll reassess on a safe turn card, but I won’t inflate the pot with a weak underpair.

Flopped top pair top kicker (aces with king kicker) plus the nut straight draw (need a Q for Broadway). Board has two clubs so there's a flush draw out there. I should c-bet for value and protection. Around 60% pot is standard here.

I called preflop as a set-mining play, but the Js Tc Ac flop completely misses my 2♣2♦. Pot is 900 and Claude’s flop bet is 350 — calling would make the pot 1,250, so I need ~350/1250 ≈ 28% equity to break even. My chance to hit a set by the river is only ≈8.4%, and my hand has almost no showdown value vs a CO opener’s c-bet range (lots of A-x, Jx/T x, broadways and club combos). Raising or floating here with a small underpair is negative EV: I have poor fold equity and don’t want to inflate the pot out of position. Save the chips and fold.

In a 6-max game, King-Queen offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from under-the-gun. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here to narrow the field and take control of the pot early. My stack is healthy at nearly 100bb, allowing for standard positional play.

QTs is a solid suited broadway hand with good playability. Facing a standard 3x open from UTG, I'm getting decent odds to call (300 into 450). The hand plays well post-flop with straight and flush potential. Calling is better than 3-betting against UTG range from this position, and folding is too tight.

Preflop in cutoff with Jd8c offsuit, facing a UTG raise to 300 from Flash (tight range) and a call from Claude in middle position (likely decent hand). J8o is too weak, gapped, offsuit, and dominated by typical calling/raising ranges. Pot odds are about 2.5:1 (300 to call into 750), but implied odds don't justify due to poor playability postflop. Deep stacks but no position advantage outweighs folding weak hand to early action. Preserve stack for better spots.

I hold a pair of Fives on the Button. Flash (UTG) raised to 300 and Claude (MP) called. This provides excellent pot odds and implied odds to 'set mine' (call hoping to hit three-of-a-kind on the flop). My stack (9850) and opponents' stacks are deep enough (>15x the call amount) to justify calling purely for the potential payout if I hit a set. Additionally, having position on the Button allows me to navigate post-flop play effectively. Raising would likely isolate me against a stronger range or force me to fold to a 4-bet, while folding forfeits a profitable spot.

3♣5♦ is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with almost no playability out of the small blind. Flash opened UTG to 300 (likely a strong range) and two players have already called, so I’d be playing a multiway pot out of position against decent ranges. Calling 250 to see a flop would put me in tough spots with little equity or fold equity; a squeeze/raise with this holding would be a pure bluff that’s likely to get called or re-raised by better hands and needlessly risk my 101 BB stack. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

We’re getting a good price to call, but AQs plays much better as a squeeze here: UTG opened 3x and got two callers, so their ranges are capped/medium-heavy and we can win a lot immediately or isolate to a lower SPR with a hand that dominates many continues (AJ/AT/KQ/QJ, suited broadways) while denying equity. Calling invites a multiway pot out of position where our top-pair hands are harder to realize. I’ll 3-bet to a size that pressures the callers and still gets value from worse, while accounting for being OOP and the extra dead money.

I opened from UTG with KQo, and after two callers, FiveTwo in the Big Blind has executed a large squeeze to 1500. A squeeze from the BB against an UTG opener and two callers typically represents a very strong range, likely TT+, AQ+, or AK. KQo is significantly dominated by this range. While the pot odds for a call are somewhat tempting, playing a bloated pot out of position with a dominated hand against a player who has shown strength is a recipe for a large loss. Additionally, I still have two players behind me who could call or re-raise. Folding here is the most disciplined and long-term profitable play to preserve my stack.

FiveTwo 3-bet to 1500 from BB after Flash opened and I called. QTs is decent but I'm OOP against the 3-bettor, Pro is still behind, and calling 1200 more would commit ~22% of my stack with a speculative hand. The SPR would be poor post-flop and QTs doesn't play well against a 3-bet range when out of position. Clear fold.

The Big Blind (FiveTwo) has squeezed to 1500. I need to call 1200 more. My stack is 9550. To profitably 'set mine' with pocket fives, I generally look for implied odds of at least 15-20x the call amount. Here, the call is 1200, requiring effective stacks of 18,000-24,000. With only ~9,500 behind, the implied odds are approximately 8:1, which is far too low to justify calling solely to hit a set. The squeeze indicates a strong range (likely TT+, AK) against which 55 has poor equity if it doesn't improve immediately. Despite having position, the stack-to-pot ratio will be low (~2.3), leaving little room to outplay the opponent post-flop without hitting. Therefore, the mathematically correct play is to fold.

KQo is a strong hand in UTG position. With 55BB stack, I should open raise to 2.5x BB (250) as I've been doing with premium hands throughout the tournament. This hand plays well and can make strong top pair or broadway draws.

Facing a UTG raise from Claude to 250 into a 400 pot. My hand is 6d9d, suited connectors with some potential but marginal at best against a strong UTG opening range (likely 66+, AJ+, KQ+, suited broadways). I'm in middle position, which gives me decent but not great post-flop position, with multiple players behind including Pro in CO and Minni on BTN who could call or raise. Stacks are deep (100BB effective), but calling commits 250 chips OOP with a hand that has poor equity (~30-35% vs tight range) and relies on implied odds that may not materialize if Claude has a strong hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots; speculative hands like this are better in late position or multiway without a raise. History shows Claude raises frequently but this is a standard fold.

I am in the Cutoff with Qs5s facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG. A UTG opening range is typically strong, often consisting of premium pairs, strong aces, and strong broadways. My hand, Queen-Five suited, is too weak to call profitably. It is easily dominated by better Queens (AQ, KQ, QJ) and vulnerable to being squeezed by the Button or Blinds if I flat call. 3-betting is not an attractive option against a UTG open range with this holding. Therefore, folding is the standard and correct play.

Button with J♥5♥ — a suited J-x that has decent playability in position but is too weak to 3-bet profitably versus an UTG opener. Calling 250 into a 400 pot is inexpensive (pot will be ~650 after my call) and lets me see a flop in position against a fairly wide but still strong UTG range. I avoid a min 3-bet (to 400) because it’s unlikely to fold enough of Claude’s range and may invite a 4-bet or a multiway pot out of position. Plan: continue on flops that give me a pair or strong heart/straight draws; fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I don’t improve.

7c3d offsuit is too weak to defend from the small blind versus an UTG open plus a button call. Even with a decent immediate price, we’ll be out of position multiway with poor playability and bad reverse implied odds, and the big blind can still squeeze or come along. This is a low-EV spot to continue.

I am in the Big Blind with 2c3c. Claude raised from UTG and Minni called on the Button. Although I am getting excellent pot odds (150 to call into a 650 pot, approximately 4.3:1), 2c3c is the weakest of suited connectors. Playing this hand out of position against an UTG opener and an active Button player is generally a losing proposition as I will rarely hit the board hard enough to continue, and my equity realization will be poor. I'll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

I completely missed this 6-T-4 flop with KQ. However, as the preflop aggressor heads-up against a caller, I should c-bet this dry board. A small continuation bet of about 1/3 pot can take this down often enough to be profitable, and I still have two overcards if called.

I’m on the button with Jh5h facing a small UTG c-bet (225 into a 875 pot). I have no pair and only a backdoor heart possibility (needs both turn+river), with 6 clean outs (three J’s and three 5’s) — roughly ~24% to hit by the river and ~12.8% on the turn. Calling 225 into a 875 pot gives me ~20.5% break-even equity, so a call is justified on pot-odds and because I’m in position. Claude’s small bet can be a standard c-bet to take the pot down; raising as a bluff here is risky versus an UTG opener and could commit chips unnecessarily against his range and his 5k stack. Plan: call to realize equity and see the turn; fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, or extract value if I pair or improve.

The Ace on the turn is an excellent card to barrel. As the preflop aggressor, Aces hit my perceived range hard. Minni likely called flop with a ten, a club draw, or was floating. The Ace is a great scare card and I picked up a gutshot to broadway (need J for straight). A second barrel around 65% pot should get folds from medium pairs and floats.

Fold. I called the flop to realize equity but the turn bet (700 into a 1800 pot) demands ~28% equity to break even (700 / (1800+700) ≈ 28%). I only have 6 clean outs (three Jacks, three 5s) → ~6/46 ≈ 13% to hit the river, and my backdoor heart draw is dead after the Ad turn. Claude’s line (UTG open, c-bet, then a larger turn bet) strongly indicates an Ace, Ten, or another made hand that won’t fold to a raise, so a bluff-raise is unlikely to be profitable and a call is −EV. Per my plan to fold to heavy turn aggression without improvement, I fold and preserve my stack for better, more profitable spots in position.

UTG in 6-handed with 7cTc (suited connector). Standard opening range UTG is tighter: pairs 66+, broadway hands like AJo+, suited aces, KQs. 7Tc is speculative and plays better multiway from late position where I can realize equity and have position. Folding preserves stack for premium spots early in tournament with deep stacks.

I am in Middle Position with 5c 6h, which is a weak off-suit connector. With four players left to act behind me and 95 BB deep, this hand does not have enough playability or equity to justify an open raise or a limp. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

2♣7♠ offsuit is the worst starting hand — virtually no showdown equity and poor playability out of position. I’m in the cutoff with the button and both blinds still to act; Button (FiveTwo) and the blinds have shown willingness to defend/3-bet in prior hands, and Claude (BB) tends to call and fight postflop. Calling 100 to see a flop with 2‑7o is a negative‑EV, and opening/raising to try to steal risks a 3‑bet or getting raised/called by hands that crush me postflop. With a healthy ~96 BB stack, there will be plenty of better spots to pick off blinds or apply pressure. I’ll preserve my stack and fold.

Folds to us on the Button and we have a playable hand with good equity and decent blocker/value potential (Q-high, connected-ish). With position and deep stack, the highest EV line is to open-raise and pressure the blinds, who must defend out of position. I’ll use a standard steal size that still keeps my range wide and gives good fold equity.
