I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. FiveTwo, the button, has raised to 250. J4o is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against a button raise which could be quite wide but still dominates my range. I have over 95 big blinds and there is no reason to defend with such a poor hand at this stage of the tournament.

Q3o is a weak hand to defend even against a button open. While I'm getting decent pot odds (150 to win 400), Q3o plays poorly post-flop - it's often dominated and has little playability. Folding is the standard play here.

I am in UTG position with 6h 3d, which is an extremely weak hand (off-suit, unconnected, low cards). Given the poor position and the lack of playability or potential for this hand, the only standard play is to fold immediately. My stack is healthy at over 95 BBs, so there is no pressure to make loose moves.

J♥7♠ is a weak, easily dominated hand in middle position. There are four players yet to act (cutoff, button, SB, BB) who can and often do raise — opening here would be a thin bluff and calling 100 to see a flop is a passive play with poor equity, especially multiway and out of position. With deep stacks I want to preserve chips for better spots and playable hands. Fold.

We’re in the cutoff with J6o, which is near the bottom of a reasonable CO opening range. The players behind aren’t ideal targets: Flash can apply pressure from the button, Claude in the SB has shown willingness to defend/call, and Elon in the BB has been fairly aggressive with 3-bets and postflop betting. With poor playability when called and a higher chance of facing a 3-bet, the steal EV isn’t attractive enough here. I’ll pass and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8d 4c, which is a very weak hand. Since it has folded to me, I have the option to try and steal the blinds, but 8-4 offsuit is significantly below the threshold of hands I'd like to use for a button open, especially against Claude and Elon who have been relatively active. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.
Folded to me in the SB with KTo. This is a strong hand heads-up against the BB. Standard play is to raise here for value and to take initiative. Raising to 250 (2.5x).

Facing a small raise from Claude in the SB after all other players folded. Claude has been aggressive with frequent preflop raises in recent hands (e.g., Hands 33, 35, 28, etc.), suggesting a wide stealing range from the SB. My 9c7c is a suited connector with good playability, potential for straights, flushes, and two-pair hands. Pot odds are excellent: calling 150 more into a 350 pot (~2.3:1). Equity against a wide SB opening range (including offsuit broadways, suited aces, suited connectors, Ax suited, pairs) is around 40-45%, sufficient for a call. Postflop, I'll have initiative if I hit and implied odds are strong given deep stacks. Folding is too tight; 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand as it's speculative rather than polarized. Calling keeps the pot small OOP while realizing equity.

I completely missed this flop with KTo. Board is Qc 3c Ad - no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw. This is a board that connects well with Elon's calling range (Ax, Qx hands). C-betting into this texture with king-high as a bluff is marginal at best. Better to check and either give up to a bet or see a free card that might improve me.

On this flop of Ad Qc 3c, I have the nut flush draw with 9c7c (9 outs, ~35% equity), checked to me in the BB after calling Claude's SB steal raise preflop. The board is somewhat dry (few strong made hands in his wide opening range), and his check suggests weakness or a trap, but betting realizes my equity, applies fold equity against his missed draws/overs, denies him equity, and builds the pot for when I hit. Checking risks giving a free card to his A-high or draws. Half-pot bet of 300 into 500 is standard size for protection/value thin vs. floats. Deep stacks support implied odds if called.

I have KTo on Qc3cAd - complete air with just a gutshot straight draw (J gives me broadway, 4 outs ~8.5%). Elon bet 300 into 500, I'm getting ~3.7:1 pot odds which isn't enough for a gutshot. This board texture hits Elon's BB calling range well (Ax, Qx). No pair, no flush draw, minimal equity. Folding is correct - don't want to invest more with such weak holdings against a board that favors villain's range.

UTG with J♦5♥ — a weak, easily-dominated offsuit jack with poor postflop playability. In 6-max my UTG opening range should be much stronger; I’ll be out of position against five players, several of whom have a habit of opening and 3-betting. Limping just invites raises and multiway action where this hand performs poorly, and raising to steal would be reckless with such a marginal holding. Pot is small — preserve chips and wait for a better spot in position.

Unopened pot with blinds only. J7o from middle position is too weak and dominated by callers/3-bets (Jx, better broadways) and plays poorly postflop out of position versus the button/ blinds. No need to limp; we preserve stack and wait for better open/steal spots.

I am in the Cutoff with 4c 3s, which is a very weak hand. Even though two players have folded, the players behind me (Claude, Elon, and Pro) have shown a mix of aggression and willingness to defend their blinds. Raising with 4-3 offsuit as a steal attempt is generally unprofitable in this configuration, and calling/limping is not an option for a professional strategy. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play.
On the button with Q7o after 3 folds. This is a good spot to steal the blinds with position. Q7o is a reasonable button open - not premium but playable in late position. Standard raise to 2.5x.

A9o is a strong enough hand to defend from the small blind against a button open-raise from Claude, who is likely raising wide from that position. The Ace provides a strong blocker to AA, AK, AQ, reducing the likelihood of running into premium hands. We get a good price (200 into 400, roughly 2:1), and A9o has decent playability postflop despite being OOP. Folding is too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand as it plays well multiway or heads-up postflop. Stacks are deep enough to realize equity. Call to continue.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Button (Claude) to 250 and a call from the Small Blind (Elon). The pot is 600 and it costs me 150 to call, offering 5:1 pot odds (16.7% equity needed to break even, or 20% if looking at contribution/total). While the price is attractive, my hand [2h Qc] is extremely weak (Queen-high, weak kicker, off-suit) and has poor playability. I will be out of position relative to the aggressor and sandwiched in a multi-way pot. My ability to realize equity with this hand is low due to reverse implied odds and position. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

Flop board Kd-6d-Ts gives me the nut flush draw with Ad (9 immediate outs to the flush) plus Ace overcard for additional equity against non-Kx hands. This is approximately 35% equity heads-up, a very strong draw OOP vs Claude's wide button opening range which hits this board somewhat but includes many Ax suited, worse flush draws, straight draws (J9, 97s, etc.), and floats. Checking is optimal to disguise strength, induce a wide c-bet from Claude (who frequently cbets postflop based on history), and set up a check-raise for value/protection. Betting out risks him folding too much weak stuff we dominate, and we prefer getting stacks in with our equity. Donk betting is suboptimal OOP here as it bloats the pot without inducing action from worse. Check to build the pot multi-street.

I have Q7o with no pair on a K-6-T board with two diamonds. Elon called my preflop raise and checked to me. While a c-bet is standard as the preflop aggressor, I have zero equity here - just queen high with no draws. If I bet and get called or raised, I'm in a terrible spot. Checking back gives me pot control and a free card to potentially improve. The board is relatively connected and could hit Elon's calling range well.

Turn board 2c is a blank that doesn't complete any obvious draws except possibly some wheel str8s, but it misses my nut diamond flush draw (still 9 outs). Equity remains strong (~20-25% vs Claude's button calling range, which includes Ax suited like Adx we block, Kx, Tx, suited connectors, pairs). Check-check flop likely means Claude has a weak Kx, Ax high, pocket pair below K, or missed draw/float that he gave up on. Betting turn takes initiative OOP, denies equity to his floats/OESD (e.g., 98, 75), gets value from worse made hands (77-TT, weak Kx without diamond blocker), and builds pot for river shove if we hit. Checking again risks giving free card to his better hands or letting him check back weak stuff. Pot bet or 2/3 pot sizes well here given deep stacks (10500 effective vs his 5500). Bet 400 (2/3 pot) to get called by his medium strength while protecting our draw.

I have Q7o - just queen high with no pair, no draws on K-6-T-2. Elon donk bet 400 into 500 after I checked back the flop. I have zero made hand and no diamond draw. Calling here with queen high to potentially bluff later or hit a queen is too speculative. Easy fold.

UTG 6-handed with K6o is too weak to enter: it’s heavily dominated by opponents’ calling/3-betting ranges (K7+, KTo+, suited Broadways, etc.) and plays poorly out of position. With no antes, there’s little incentive to loosen up; preserving stack and avoiding tough postflop spots is best.
I am in middle position with 4h 3c, which is a very weak hand. Since the action folded to me and this hand has almost no value or playability, the standard and most profitable move is to fold.

Ad8s in the cutoff is a solid opening hand, especially with UTG and MP already folded. This is a standard late position raise situation. I'll open to 2.5x BB (250) to try to take down the blinds or play in position against callers.

T3o is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity, suited potential, or high card strength. Position on the button provides some advantage, but facing a standard raise from the cutoff (Claude with 5250 stack, raising 2.5x BB after two folds), the hand has poor post-flop playability, low equity against a calling range, and is easily dominated. Pot odds are 400:250 (~1.6:1), insufficient for this marginal hand. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this deep-stacked tournament spot (hand 39/150). Opponent tendencies from history show Claude raises variably but often with strength; no need to speculate with trash.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s4s, a suited connector with good playability and implied odds. The opponent 'Claude' opened from the Cutoff to 250 (2.5bb). Claude has been active and opening a wide range of hands from late position. Given the stack depths (I cover Claude, and effective stacks are over 50bb), I have sufficient implied odds to call and try to hit a straight, flush, or strong draw. A 3-bet is an option to isolate and take initiative, but playing 54s out of position in a bloated pot against a potentially 4-betting or sticky opponent is higher variance. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to realize equity fairly well. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be at least 600, offering reasonable direct odds.

I'm in the big blind with Qd Jc facing a cutoff open to 250 and the small blind (Pro) has called. Pot is 600 and I must call 150 more — total committed = 250. QJo is a defendable hand, but with a caller in front of me this is a multiway pot and I'll be out of position versus both opponents; a 3-bet is therefore less attractive (min-raise to 400 is available but I don't want to bloat a multiway pot or get isolated with a marginal holding). Calling gives ~20% breakeven pot odds and lets me realize equity on many flops while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously OOP — continue with top pair/strong draws and fold to large, committed aggression when I miss.

The flop comes [Kd Qs Qh], which is an incredibly bad board for my hand (5s4s). This texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Claude) who has all the strong broadway hands in his range, and also connects with the Big Blind's calling range. I have 5-high with only a backdoor flush draw. Being out of position against two opponents on a board that misses my range entirely, I have zero fold equity if I bet and negligible pot equity. The standard play is to check and fold to any bet.

I hold Qd Jc on Kd Qs Qh — that's trips (three queens). Very strong but this is a three‑way pot and I'm out of position vs the preflop raiser (Claude) and the caller (Pro). Hands that beat me (KQ full houses, pocket Kings) exist but are relatively few; many worse hands (single K, weaker Q combos, broadways) will pay me off. I want to extract value while keeping the pot controllable against the narrow range of full houses. I will bet a modest amount — 300 (~40% pot) — to charge worse kings and queen‑kickers, deny a free turn card, and fold to heavy, telltale overbets. If I'm called I'll re-evaluate on the turn and continue extracting on safe cards; if I face a large raise or shove I will put the raiser on a very narrow full‑house/set range and fold unless the price and reads justify a call.

Board is KdQsQh - completely missed my Ad8s. I have ace high on a paired board with two broadway cards. Minni's 300 bet into a 750 pot could be a queen, a king, or a probe. With just ace high and no real equity, I'm folding. Can't profitably continue here against a bet with Pro still to act behind me.

I hold 5s4s on a board of Kd Qs Qh. This board texture completely misses my range and hits the opponents' ranges very hard (broadway cards). Minni, in the Big Blind, has led out for 300 into two opponents. This 'donk bet' or lead often indicates strength (like a Q or K) or a strong draw (JT). Even against a bluff, my 5-high is likely behind, and I have effectively zero equity to improve to a winning hand. My backdoor flush draw is too weak to chase. Folding is the only correct option.

I am UTG with pocket sixes. This is a standard opening hand in a 6-max game. I will raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or set-mine if I get callers. This size is consistent with my previous opening raises.

A2o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. While having an ace provides some blocking value, the 2 kicker is terrible and I'm out of position. Calling or 3-betting with this hand against an UTG range is -EV. Clean fold.

KsJh is a strong offsuit broadway hand with good playability, especially in the cutoff position facing a single UTG raise to 3x from Flash. Flash has shown aggression but also folded to 3-bets in the past (e.g., Hand #34). Calling keeps the pot small, realizes equity in position against Flash's strong UTG range, and allows me to play post-flop effectively with deep stacks (111BB). Folding is too tight multiway potentially; 3-betting risks getting 4-bet by players behind or isolating vs a strong range OOP. Pot odds are favorable (calling 300 into 450, ~1.5:1), and position on Flash justifies seeing a flop.

I hold AQo on the Button facing a UTG raise from Flash and a flat call from Elon (Cutoff). A UTG opening range is typically strong, often containing hands that flip with or dominate AQo (like 88+, AK, AQs). While a 3-bet squeeze is a viable option to collect dead money and isolate, it risks facing a 4-bet from the UTG player which would force me to fold significant equity. By flat calling, I keep the pot manageable, utilize my position, and keep Elon's capped calling range in the hand, which likely contains dominated hands like AJ, KQ, or lower pairs. This line minimizes variance while allowing me to realize my equity in position.

3♠9♣ in the small blind facing an UTG raise to 300 with two callers (Elon and Pro). Pot is 1,050 and it’s 250 to call. This is a multiway pot where I’ll be out of position and the hand has very poor equity and playability postflop. Calling 250 or making a light squeeze/raise here is unnecessary risk against multiple stronger ranges. Preserve the 10k stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

We’re getting a good price (200 to win 1250), but this is a 3x UTG open that’s been called twice, so ranges are stronger and we’ll be out of position multiway. J7o is heavily dominated when we make a pair (Jx/7x), has poor playability, and a squeeze isn’t attractive with this hand profile. Preserving EV and avoiding dominated top-pair spots is best here.

I have flopped a set of sixes on an Ace-high board. This is an extremely strong hand, and given the pre-flop action where two players called my UTG raise, it is highly likely that at least one opponent holds an Ace or a Ten. My goal now is to build the pot and extract maximum value. I will lead out with a bet of 550, which is approximately half the pot. This sizing is consistent with a continuation bet I would make with a wide range of hands, encouraging calls from weaker Aces, Tens, or gutshot draws like KQ.

On an ace-high flop (Ah Td 6s), KJo connects poorly: gutshot straight draw to Q (4 outs, ~8% equity heads-up but less multi-way), no pair, no flush draw, dominated by Flash's UTG range which includes Ax, Tx, sets, and strong combos. His 1/3 pot c-bet into a 1600 pot is standard for value or semi-bluff protection. Calling floats with weak showdown value, poor implied odds if I hit (often behind A), and Pro on button can raise/squeeze. Raising as a bluff is -EV vs Flash's strong range and multiway. Deep-stacked (108BB), folding preserves stack for better spots in 110 hands remaining. Pre-flop call was fine for playability, but this texture misses hard.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (AQ) on an A-T-6 rainbow board. The opponent (Flash) opened UTG and is c-betting into two opponents, which indicates strength (likely AK, AT, Sets, or AJ). However, I am ahead of AJ and underpairs (KK-JJ) that might be turning into bluffs or protection bets. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (AK, Sets, Two Pair) and fold out worse hands. Folding is too conservative given my hand strength and position. The best line is to call, keep the opponent's range wide, and use my position to evaluate the turn.

I have a set of sixes, which is a powerhouse hand on this board. While the King on the turn is a bit of a 'scare card' because it completes the QJ straight and improves hands like AK and KT to two pair, I am still ahead of the vast majority of my opponent's calling range (including Ax, KT, AK, and AT). I will bet approximately half the pot (1100) to continue extracting value from these worse made hands. If the opponent raises, I will need to evaluate the possibility of a straight, but a set remains too strong to check here.

On the turn, the board is [6s Td Ah Kc]. The King is a terrible card for my hand (AQo). It completes the Broadway straight (QJ), improves AK to top two pair, and fits perfectly with a UTG opening range which likely includes AA, KK, TT, AK, AT, and QJs. Flash's line of opening UTG, c-betting into two opponents on an Ace-high flop, and then firing a second barrel on the King turn signifies immense strength. The only value hand I realistically beat is AJ, but AJ would often check this turn for pot control or fear of the King. My blockers to QJ and AK are noted, but not sufficient to justify calling down against a range that creates this much pressure. Preserving my stack of ~83bb is prioritizing over bluff-catching in a spot where the population tendency is value-heavy.
J8 suited from UTG is too weak to open. While suited, it lacks connectivity and J-high has poor playability. UTG requires a tighter range, and with 52.5BB I should preserve my stack for better spots.

Preflop in middle position with 2s3s, a very weak suited connector. Deep stacks (108bb), but hand has low equity, prone to domination (e.g., by higher suited connectors), poor playability postflop, and reverse implied odds. UTG folded, but limping risks multiway pot without strong implied odds. Several strong players behind (e.g., Pro in CO, Minni BTN). Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in tournament aiming to maximize over 150 hands.

I am in the Cutoff with 9h 2s, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em (unsuited, disconnected, low ranks). Although I have position on the blinds, I still have the Button left to act. The risk of being 3-bet or called by a dominating hand is extremely high, and this hand has virtually no post-flop playability. Preserving chips for a better spot is the only logical move here. I will fold.

On the button with Ts3c I have a very weak hand, but I'm facing only the blinds so I can use position and fold equity. Limping or flat-calling 100 invites multiway pots with a dominated hand; folding surrenders an easy chance to pick up the blinds. I will open-raise to apply pressure while keeping the size modest relative to deep stacks. Raise to 300 (≈3×BB): this is large enough to get credit and fold out some blind defenses but small enough to fold to a substantial 3-bet without losing much. Plan: fold to a 3-bet, play straightforward postflop if called (c-bet favorable, check/pass in multiway or unpromising spots), look to take advantage of position rather than bloat the pot with T3o.

Pocket fives plays well as a set-mining/low-variance defend versus a Button open, especially with ~100bb effective. 3-betting 55 from the small blind tends to bloat the pot out of position and often just gets called by better hands; we also don’t want to face a 4-bet. Calling keeps Minni’s range wide, invites dominated overcards to continue, and we have excellent implied odds when we flop a set.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Eight offsuit. Minni opened from the button and FiveTwo called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me only 200 more to call, giving me pot odds of 4.5 to 1. Since I am closing the action and my hand has decent high-card value against a wide button opening range, calling to see a flop is the most mathematically sound and prudent play.

Multiway pot OOP with 55 on 3-6-2 rainbow gives us a marginal one-pair (ahead of overcards, behind any 6x/overpairs/sets). Leading as a bluff/value-denial into two players is less effective and risks getting raised off our equity. Best is to check and play a controlled check/call versus small bets (denying overcards cheaply) while comfortably folding to larger pressure/raises.

The flop is 3h-6s-2c, which is a very dry and low board. Holding Ace-Eight offsuit, I have Ace-high with a decent kicker. FiveTwo (SB) has checked, and I am next to act before the pre-flop aggressor, Minni. There is no reason to lead out here with just Ace-high into two opponents. Checking is the most standard play to see how the button reacts to this board and to maintain pot control while preserving my showdown value.

I was the preflop raiser and have position on a very dry flop (3h 6s 2c) that pairs my 3c. Both blinds checked, so a continuation bet will often take down the pot and extracts thin value from missed overcards/Ax hands. My pair of 3s is real but vulnerable to 6x/2x/sets, so I want a modest size to get fold equity without bloating the pot. Bet ~½ pot (450). Plan: fold to a large raise; if called proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate.

Minni’s 1/3-pot c-bet in a 3-way pot is very range-wide, and 55 on 3-6-2 is still ahead of a lot of her overcard air (A-high/KQ/QJ, etc.). We also have strong implied odds when we spike a 5 (set) and some extra equity via the gutshot to a 4 (2-3-4-5-6). Raising doesn’t accomplish much here OOP multiway (we’d get called mostly by 6x/overpairs/sets and fold out the hands we already beat), so the best line is to take the price and keep the pot manageable. Plan: check again on most turns; continue more comfortably on a 5/4, and be cautious versus larger turn barrels on high cards.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. The flop of 3-6-2 is very dry. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has placed a continuation bet of 450 into a 900 pot, and FiveTwo in the Small Blind has called. While my pot odds are 4-to-1 to see a turn card, the call from FiveTwo is concerning. FiveTwo's call into two players on such a low, uncoordinated board typically indicates a made hand like a pair of sixes, a small pocket pair, or a straight draw like 4-5. Being sandwiched between the Small Blind and the Button for the remainder of the hand makes it extremely difficult to realize the equity of Ace-high. Given the multi-way action and my poor relative position, folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving play.

Turn pairs the deuce, which is generally good for our flop check/call range: it reduces the likelihood Minni can credibly represent many strong value hands, while our 55 still beats a large chunk of her flop c-bet range (A/K/Q-highs, broadway floats). Leading doesn’t accomplish much—better hands (6x/overpairs/2x) continue and can raise, while worse hands often fold—so we keep the pot controlled and let her continue bluffing/value-betting thin. Plan is to check-call versus a reasonable sizing and re-evaluate rivers; fold to heavy pressure that targets our capped one-pair range.

Turn pairs the board with 2♦, which meaningfully reduces the relative strength of my one-pair (3♣). FiveTwo called my flop c‑bet, so their range contains a lot of hands that improved (any 2, any 6, pocket pairs, sometimes slow‑played 3s) as well as some weaker hands and floats. There are no meaningful draws to charge and my betting range here would be thin value with little fold equity, while exposing me to a check‑raise (trips/two‑pair) that I don’t want to commit to. Pot control is the priority: check back the turn, keep the pot manageable (pot = 1800) and re-evaluate on the river — call small bets from marginal hands, but fold to large raises representing trips/two‑pair.

River Kd is a bad card to turn 55 into a value bet: hands that call (Kx, any 6x/3x, most pocket pairs 77+, occasional 2x) largely beat us, while the hands we beat (A/Q/J-high floats) mostly fold. Since Minni c-bet flop then checked back turn, her range contains plenty of missed overcards and some medium-strength hands, so checking keeps her bluffs in and avoids value-owning ourselves with a thin/block bet. Plan: check and bluff-catch versus small/medium sizing; fold to large, polar pressure.

Turn paired the board which converted my marginal pair into two pair (3s & 2s) but also hugely increased the number of hands that beat me (pocket 22, pocket 33, or any hand that holds both a 3 and a 2). FiveTwo check‑called the flop and then checked turn and river, showing passivity rather than strength, but that check can also hide full houses from slowplays. The pot is 1,800 and my stack is 9,375 — I don’t want to inflate the pot with a thin value bet that only gets called by Kx/worse pairs and that risks a costly check‑raise. Priority is pot control and taking a cheap showdown with a vulnerable two‑pair. I will check and go to showdown; if they bet a small amount I’ll call for value, but I’ll respect large raises as likely full houses and fold.

UTG in a 6-handed game with QTo offsuit is a clear fold. This hand has poor playability, dominated equity against typical calling ranges, and doesn't block strong hands well. Preflop discipline is key to maximizing stack long-term; limping or raising invites trouble from later positions. Opponents have shown aggression in steals and 3-bets historically, so preserving stack for premium spots.

I am in Middle Position (UTG+1) with K9o. This is a 6-handed table with blinds at 50/100. K9o is a weak off-suit broadway hand that is generally not strong enough to open from early/middle position. It is often dominated by calling ranges (KQ, KJ, KT, AK, A9) and plays poorly post-flop out of position if called by the button or cutoff. With a healthy stack of 83.5 BBs, there is no need to force play with marginal holdings. The standard and disciplined play is to fold.

Cutoff with 9♦8♣ — a playable late-position connector but offsuit. There’s been no raise yet (only blinds in), so I want the initiative and to avoid a limp-heavy multiway pot where this hand loses equity. Standard 3x-ish open gives fold equity against the button and blinds while keeping postflop playability. Plan: raise to 300; fold to a large 3-bet, and if called proceed to C‑bet favorable flops or pot-control on bad textures.

KhTh suited plays very well in position versus a cutoff open and benefits a lot from having the betting lead. Minni has shown a fairly active opening range and will have to fold a meaningful portion of it to 3-bets; meanwhile calling invites the blinds in and lets Claude (shorter stack) realize equity too cheaply. I’ll 3-bet to a standard in-position size to isolate Minni, pick up the dead money often, and set up profitable c-bets on many boards. If Minni 4-bets large, this hand is an easy fold given stack-to-pot dynamics.

I am holding Ace-Nine offsuit in the Small Blind. There has been an open from the Cutoff (Minni) to 300 and a 3-bet from the Button (FiveTwo) to 900. While Ace-Nine is a decent hand, it is significantly behind the range of a typical 3-bet from the button. Furthermore, playing this hand would put me out of position for the remainder of the hand against two active players. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here to preserve my stack.

4d7s is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from Minni and a 3-bet to 900 from FiveTwo, I have no business continuing here. This is a clear fold - I'd be calling 800 with a hand that has poor equity against any reasonable 3-betting range.

I opened to 300 from cutoff with 9♦8♣ — a standard steal attempt. The button (FiveTwo) 3‑bet to 900, putting me out of position postflop. 9♦8♣ offsuit has poor equity and playability OOP against a typical button 3‑bet range (value hands, broadways, some suited connectors). Calling 600 into a 1350 pot would put me in a marginal spot with awkward postflop decisions; a minimum 4‑bet to 1500 as a bluff is too thin and needlessly commits chips. With deep stacks and plenty of spots left, I preserve my stack and fold this marginal hand.

I am in UTG position with King-Four suited (Ks 4s). This is a weak hand to open from early position in a 6-handed game. With 5 players left to act, the likelihood of being dominated or facing a 3-bet is significant. The hand has poor playability out of position. My stack is healthy (83 BBs), so there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. The standard and disciplined play here is to fold.

Ad9o is a standard open-raise from middle in a 6-max game with 90bb effective stacks and nobody has entered the pot yet (UTG folded). I want to take the initiative, pick up the blinds, and avoid playing a dominated ace multiway, so I’ll open to 3bb (300). That size is standard here, gives good fold equity, and keeps postflop play manageable. Plan: if I get single-called I’ll c-bet favorable A-high or dry boards and proceed cautiously; if I face a large 3-bet from the CO/BB/BTN I will usually fold this hand versus a clear 3-bet range, but I may call smaller 3-bets depending on opponent and sizing.

Minni is opening to 3bb from middle quite frequently, and we’re in position with a suited hand that has decent playability plus a queen blocker. Flat-calling invites a Button squeeze and leaves us in a multiway pot with a dominated queen; 3-betting takes initiative, can win the pot immediately at a good frequency, and sets up profitable c-bets when called. If Minni 4-bets, we comfortably fold this combo.

I am on the button with 6d 5s, which is a very weak hand. In the current action, Minni (Middle) raised to 300 and FiveTwo (Cutoff) 3-bet to 900. Both players have significant stacks and their range for a raise and a re-raise is significantly stronger than my 6-high. Calling or 4-betting here would be a waste of chips. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

AQo is a strong hand facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo after Minni's open. I'm in the SB with 5100 chips. Calling puts me in an awkward OOP spot with ~2 SPR. A 4-bet commits me anyway given stack depths. Best play is to 4-bet shove for 5100 - this applies maximum pressure and AQ plays well as a squeeze. FiveTwo has shown some aggression and may fold worse Ax hands while I'm ahead of most 3-bet bluffs.

K2s is a speculative suited hand with poor playability and dominated equity against the likely premium range of Claude's massive 4-bet sizing (51BB) after a 3-bet and open. Pot is 6400, calling 5000 commits nearly half my stack (10700) into a low-SPR pot postflop where K2s struggles against strong hands. No fold equity on a raise given the action behind. History shows Claude aggressive but this sizing screams value (AA, KK, AK). Instant fold to preserve stack for better spots in tournament.

I opened to 300 and got 3‑bet by CO to 900, then SB (Claude) shoved/4‑bet to 5100 leaving only 50 behind. That commits a huge amount and makes Claude effectively all‑in for 5100. I would need roughly 4800/(6400+4800) ≈ 42.9% equity to make a call break‑even if everyone else folds — and that’s optimistic because FiveTwo (the 3‑better) may still call and create a side pot, which only worsens my equity. A9o is frequently dominated by a 4‑bet/all‑in range (QQ+/AK and strong Ax/QQ+ lines) and performs poorly multi‑way. Jam/calling for most of my stack is too risky and low EV. Preserve my ~88k stack and wait for a better, more profitable spot. Fold.

Claude’s cold 4-bet from the SB to 5100 is an extremely large sizing versus our 3-bet to 900, and he’s effectively committed (only ~50 behind). This strongly weights his range toward premiums (QQ+/AK, maybe some AQ/JJ at widest). With Q7s we’re too dominated and don’t realize equity well even if we call and see a flop. We’re being asked to call 4200 to win ~10600 total (need ~40% equity), and Q7s won’t get there versus a tight/linear 4-bet range. No profitable 5-bet bluff either given the commitment. Preserve stack and move on.

J♣8♥ offsuit is a marginal hand and I’m first to act (UTG) in a 6‑hand game. Pot is 150, call is 100 and the min raise would be to 200 (typical open to 300). From UTG I need a stronger, less‑dominated holding — J8o is easily crushed by callers/3‑bettors behind and we’ve seen several aggressive players (Claude, FiveTwo, Elon) who will 3‑bet or squeeze. With a healthy ~88BB stack I’m preserving fold equity and waiting for a better spot in position rather than playing a dominated, margin hand out of position. Fold and conserve chips for a stronger open or a playable spot later.

Qc3c is too weak to enter from middle position with three aggressive players still to act (Flash/Claude/Elon) who can 3-bet and put us in tough spots. Limping is also unattractive because we realize equity poorly multiway and invite squeezes. With a healthy stack, we can wait for better steal/pressure spots rather than bleed chips with dominated queens.

I am in the Cutoff position with Ten-Seven offsuit. Minni and FiveTwo have already folded. While the field has thinned, T7o is too weak of a hand to open from this position, especially with three active players behind me (Button, Small Blind, and Big Blind). Maintaining a solid opening range is key to long-term success in this tournament, and T7o falls well outside the standard opening range for the Cutoff. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Button with Q3s after 3 folds - decent spot to steal blinds. Suited queen has some playability if called. Standard button open to 2.5x.

2s9c is a trash hand: offsuit, unconnected, low cards with poor playability. Facing a button raise from Claude, who raises frequently but my hand has no value, weak blockers, and is terrible OOP postflop. Calling risks the pot with no equity realization; shoving is overkill without fold equity against his range. Standard fold from SB to preserve stack for better hands in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 8s facing a raise to 250 from Claude on the Button. The pot odds are 150 to call into a pot of 400 (roughly 27% equity required). Against a standard Button opening range, J8o has about 40-43% raw equity, but being out of position makes it difficult to realize that equity. Standard defensive ranges against a 2.5x open typically include J9o+ and maybe T8o+, but J8o is often considered a fold due to reverse implied odds and lack of playability (not suited, gapped). Given the stack depth (82 BB) and positional disadvantage, defending here is likely -EV. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG in a 6-max lineup, 98o is too weak to open: it has poor high-card value, plays badly out of position, and is dominated when we make marginal pairs. With several opponents showing willingness to 3-bet and apply pressure, opening this hand risks bleeding chips and getting forced into tough postflop spots. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for stronger UTG opens (suited connectors, broadways, pairs).

I am in Middle Position with a low pocket pair (4h, 4d). In a 6-max game, this is a standard opening hand to raise and attempt to take down the blinds or set-mine if called. Given the stack sizes around the table, a standard 3x open (300 chips) is appropriate to exert pressure while remaining pot-committed only if I improve on the flop. Claude and Elon have shown aggression lately, so if I face a significant 3-bet, I will have to evaluate whether the implied odds for set-mining justify a call.

T9o in the cutoff facing a 3x open from Flash. Decent hand with straight potential but marginal. I have position if I call and Elon folds behind. With reasonable implied odds and playability, calling is fine here to see a flop. Not strong enough to 3-bet.

On the button with KJo offsuit (KsJd), facing a standard 3x raise from middle position (Flash) and a flat call from cutoff (Claude). Position is excellent, providing significant postflop advantage against both opponents. Pot odds are favorable (300 to call into 750, roughly 3.5:1), and KJo plays well multiway or heads-up with good equity against typical calling ranges. Raising could isolate but risks a 4bet from Flash or tough spots OOP for Claude; folding is too tight given position and hand strength. Calling balances ranges, controls pot size preflop, and leverages implied odds if I connect with top pair or better. Opponents' histories show Flash aggressive but folds to 3bets sometimes, Claude calls wide; prefer realizing equity here deep-stacked.

I hold 2h 5d in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in poker. Facing a raise to 300 from Flash and calls from Claude and Elon, the pot is offering me decent odds (calling 250 to win a pot of roughly 1300), but playing 5-2 offsuit out of position in a 4-way or 5-way pot is extremely unprofitable. Even if I connect with the board, I will likely be dominated or face difficult decisions against multiple opponents. Preserving my 8200 stack is more important than gambling on trash cards.

I'm on the big blind with Th6c facing Flash's open to 300 and callers from Claude and Elon. The pot is 1050 and it costs me 200 more (300 total) to see the flop — roughly 5.25:1 pot odds (~16% equity). T6o is a marginal, often-dominated holding, but the price is cheap and my stack (~86 BB) is deep enough to realize equity. Raising out of position into a multiway pot with this hand is high-variance and not +EV here, so I will defend by calling. Post-flop plan: proceed only with clear improvement (pair or strong draw); on dry boards I will check and fold to significant bets and avoid bloating the pot.

Check. I have T6o with one club on a J-5-4 double-club flop — no pair, only a very remote backdoor flush/straight potential. This is a multiway pot (Flash opened, Claude and Elon called), so bluffing or barreling out of position is low EV and I shouldn't bloat the pot with air. Pot control and seeing a turn cheaply is the right play: if someone fires a small c-bet (~≤1/3 pot) I can call as a cheap bluff-catcher/realizer of potential backdoor equity, but I'll fold to a larger bet or strong action. If I pair the Ten or pick up a real draw on the turn I'll re-evaluate and become more aggressive; for now I check to keep the pot manageable.

I have flopped bottom set (4-4-4) on a board of Jh-5c-4c. This is a very strong hand, but with three opponents in the pot and a flush draw present, I must bet for value and protection. A bet of 700 into a 1250 pot (approximately 56% pot) is a standard sizing to extract value from Jacks, straight draws, and flush draws while not being so large as to immediately fold out middle pairs.

I have T9o on Jh5c4c - just a gutshot straight draw (need an 8). Flash bet 700 into ~1250. I'm getting about 3.8:1 on a call, needing ~21% equity, but my gutshot only gives me ~8% equity. Two players still behind me. This is an easy fold with such weak equity.

Flopped top pair top kicker (Jd on Jh) with Ks kicker on a coordinated board (Jh5c4c) featuring a strong club flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., 63, 36). Flash's aggressive preflop raise and flop bet into a 1950 pot (700) suggests a wide range including draws, overpairs, sets, or bluffs. Minni's BB limp/call pre and check-flop indicates weakness. Calling is too passive, allowing cheap equity realization for draws and potential multiway pot. Folding is overly tight with TPTK. Raising protects against draws, extracts value from worse hands (e.g., weaker J, Ax), and leverages button position. Sizing to 2100 (3x the bet) builds the pot while leaving room for further action. Effective stacks deep (~10k each), implied odds support value-raising.

Fold. Elon’s raise to 2,100 after Flash’s c‑bet is a very strong line in a multiway pot. The pot is 4,050; calling 2,100 would require ~34% equity (2,100 / (4,050+2,100) ≈ 34%). T♥6♣ has no pair and only tiny backdoor potential (needs multiple perfect runouts), so realistic equity vs Elon’s raising range is well below that threshold. Calling would commit ~25% of my 8,475 stack with essentially air and leave me in difficult out‑of‑position decisions (and raising as a bluff into two players is too high variance). Preserve the stack for better spots and fold this flop.

I have flopped bottom set (4-4-4) on a Jh-5c-4c board, which is an extremely strong hand. However, the board is quite wet with both flush and straight draws possible. Elon's raise to 2100 after my 700 C-bet indicates significant strength—likely a strong Jack (like AJ), a higher set (55 or JJ), or a powerful draw (like the nut flush draw). Given Elon's history of aggression (e.g., Hand #1 and Hand #28), he is capable of making this move with a wide range of hands I currently beat. I am only losing to 55 or JJ, but since I hold two of the fours, the probability of him having a higher set is low. I want to build the pot now while my hand is likely the best and protect against the many cards that could ruin my action or beat me on the turn (any club, 6, 3, or 2). A raise to 5000 commits Elon to the pot, making it mathematically difficult for him to fold any Jx or strong draw, and sets up a potential all-in on the turn.

Facing Flash's large flop 3-bet to 5000 (needing 2900 more to call into 8350 pot, ~2.88:1 or 26% equity required) on a highly coordinated J♥5♣4♣ board with TPTK (JdKs). Flash's MP raise pre, c-bet, and now massive 3-bet after my value raise suggests strength: possible sets (55/44), combo draws (clubs + 63/36 suited), strong clubs (KJc/AcXc), or overpairs turning into thin value/protection. However, my hand blocks JJ and top pair Ax/Jx, and position on button heads-up allows controlling the pot postflop. Equity is solid vs draws (~60% vs FD/SD) and decent even vs two-pair/sets (~25-30%). Folding TPTK after raising is too tight given pot odds and implied odds (Flash has 5175 behind, pot will be ~11k); raising all-in commits 8250 total (~minraise 7900) risks getting called by better while denying equity to worse. Calling realizes equity in position, builds massive pot (~11k), and allows turn decisions (e.g., check-fold to jam on bad cards, value bet good ones). Flash's history shows aggression but occasional folds/calls down; exploit by realizing equity here deep enough (~80BB effective).

On this Jh-5c-4c-2s board, I have bottom set (4-4-4). The pot is currently 11,250 and I have 5,175 chips remaining, resulting in a stack-to-pot ratio of approximately 0.46. Given the massive action on the flop—where Elon raised my C-bet and then called my 3-bet—it is highly likely he holds a strong hand like a top-pair top-kicker (AJ), a higher set (55 or JJ), or a significant draw (like the nut flush draw). The 2s on the turn is largely a brick. At this SPR, I am committed to the hand; I cannot fold a set. My goal is to maximize value from a Jack or a draw that may call a shove due to the pot odds. Checking risks Elon checking back with a draw or a weaker Jack to see a free river. Therefore, I will move all-in to put the pressure on Elon and realize my hand's equity immediately.

Facing Flash's all-in shove of 5175 on the turn into a 16425 pot with TPTK (JdKs on Jh5c4c2s board), requiring a call of 5175 (96% pot commitment with 5350 stack) for pot odds of approximately 24% equity needed. Flash's aggressive line (MP open, flop c-bet then massive 3-bet vs my value raise, turn all-in shove) polarizes their range to nuts (sets like 55/44/JJ blocked by my Jd, straights like 63s especially suited clubs completing combo draw), strong draws (flush draws like AcXc/QcXc, gutshots turning into protection), or rare bluffs (missed draws turning into max pressure). However, history shows Flash aggressive (frequent raises, cbets, occasional folds), and this spot exploits over-aggression by realizing equity in position (though all-in eliminates further play). Equity analysis: vs nuts ~20%, vs draws ~50-65%, vs bluffs ~100%; weighted range likely >30% equity. Deep effective stacks pre-turn justify flatting flop 3-bet; consistent to call here with massive odds, live Ks kicker (no K on board), and blocked JJ. Folding top pair here leaks value long-term in tournament; call locks in +EV spot to maximize stack.

I am holding J3 offsuit in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is an extremely weak hand and is not worth playing from the earliest position, especially as the current chip leader. Standard strategy dictates a fold here to preserve chips and wait for a more favorable spot.
T2 suited is too weak to open from middle position. Poor playability and low equity against typical calling ranges. Easy fold.

Short stack of 175 chips (1.75BB) in cutoff with folds to me. 95o is a playable shoving hand from late position with such a tiny stack. Standard push/fold strategy applies here to maximize fold equity against deep-stacked blinds and button while denying them the chance to isolate. Limping is terrible with this stack size, and folding lets the blinds eat into my stack further. Equity when called is around 35% against typical calling ranges, and fold equity makes this +EV. Shove all-in for 175.

I am on the Button with 6h 5h (suited connectors). The Cutoff (Elon) has moved all-in for 175 chips, which is less than 2 big blinds. Given the blinds are 50/100, this is an under-raise all-in. The pot contains 325 chips (SB 50 + BB 100 + Elon 175). My direct pot odds to call are 175 to win a total pot of 500, requiring about 35% equity. Against a desperate short-stack range of 'any two cards', 65s has approximately 44% equity. Calling is superior to raising because 6-high is a speculative hand that plays well multi-way if the blinds call, and I avoid isolating myself against better hands from the blinds for a larger amount. The price is too good to fold.

Call 175 total. A6s is a playable suited Ace and calling the extra 125 into a 500 pot is cheap given Elon’s small all‑in and a caller — strong pot odds and decent equity. I don’t want to iso‑raise out of position with a marginal hand; call to see a flop and re-evaluate based on the big blind’s action and the board.

We’re getting an excellent price: 75 to win a 625 pot (~12% immediate odds) and we close the action. J4o is weak and dominated, so we don’t want to bloat the pot with a squeeze out of position, but the pot odds and multiway dynamics justify defending the big blind and realizing equity when we flop pair/2-pair/straight texture (and we can comfortably fold to heavy pressure on bad boards).

Check. I’m out of position in a three‑way pot with A6s on Jd‑7d‑Ts — I only have ace‑high and no real draw. Elon is already all‑in for a small amount (dead money), but that isn’t enough to justify voluntarily building a big pot OOP against both the Button and the Big Blind, who have shown a tendency to call and continue. A flop c‑bet here has low fold equity multiway and will often get called by Jx/Tx, diamond draws, or better overcards; I’ll control the pot and see a turn. Plan: if the turn pairs my Ace or 6 I can start betting for value; if it’s a diamond or a heavy action bet comes in, I’ll fold most of the time — otherwise re‑evaluate on the turn for a delayed bluff or pot‑control line.

We flopped top pair but with a very weak kicker (J4) on a dynamic board (Jd-7d-Ts) in a multiway pot. Leading builds a pot where we’re often dominated by better Jx and also exposes us to getting raised by strong made hands/strong draws. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets the preflop raiser (and the field) bet their stronger hands/draws, and we can continue with a check-call versus reasonable sizing while comfortably folding to heavy multiway pressure.

I hold 6h5h on a Jd7dTs board, giving me only 6-high with no pair and no significant draw (only a runner-runner straight draw). The pot contains an all-in player (Elon), and two active opponents (Minni and FiveTwo) have checked to me. Betting would create a side pot, but with no equity, I have no incentive to bluff into two players on this wet texture, nor do I have value. Checking back allows me to realize any faint equity for free and avoids losing chips unnecessarily.

I’m OOP with A♥6♠ and the turn (9♦) completes a lot of diamond draws on a J♦-7♦-T♠ board. I have only Ace‑high with no diamond and no realistic backdoor outs. This is a multi‑way pot — very low fold equity for a turn bluff and very little value left to extract (almost every hand that continues either already has me beat or will call). I’ll control the pot and check. Plan: concede the street and fold to any significant bet (call only trivial probes if the sizing is tiny and pot odds are attractive); if checked through to the river I’ll re‑evaluate — only bet for thin value if I pair or the river is harmless.

Multiway pot where flop checked through, so ranges are capped/weak and we can often take it down now. We have top pair but a very weak kicker on a highly dynamic turn (Jd-7d-Ts-9d): lots of equity to deny (diamond draws, Qx/8x/6x straight draws, Tx/9x pairs that can improve). Checking invites a bigger bet from behind and gives a free river too often. I’ll lead small as a blocker/value/protection bet—trying to get folds from unpaired overcards, charge draws, and get thin value from worse pairs—while keeping the pot manageable and being able to comfortably fold if someone shows big strength (raise) multiway.

On the turn, the board reads Jd-7d-Ts-9d, which is very coordinated with three diamonds and four cards connected (7-9-10-J), making an 8 a straight. FiveTwo leads out for 250 into the dry side pot. I hold 6h5h, giving me only 6-high with no draw to a straight (I need an 8, which I don't have) and no flush draw. I am drawing dead or nearly dead against any made hand or draw. With a player all-in and active betting in the side pot, I have zero equity to continue.

I have A♥6♠ in the SB on J♦-7♦-T♠-9♦. I checked the turn planning to concede the street unless faced with a very small probe; FiveTwo now bets 250 into a 950 pot (~26% pot). That sizing is small, so the pot odds to call are attractive: calling 250 to win ~1,200 requires ≈20.8% equity. My realistic equity with Ace-high (plus a ~13% chance to pair A or 6 on the river) versus a wide continuing range is in the neighborhood where a call is justified. I’m behind to obvious value hands (made diamond flushes, any 8 for the straight, J/T/9 two‑pair/top-pair combos), and I will fold to large river aggression if I don’t improve. But given the cheap price and possibility of opponent bluffing or checking the river, I’ll call the small turn bet to see the river. Plan: call now; if the river is harmless or I pair an Ace/6 I can pursue value; otherwise fold to significant river pressure.

I called the small turn probe with A♥6♠ to see the river. The K♦ on the river is a very bad card for me: there are now four diamonds on board (J♦-7♦-9♦-K♦) so any player holding a diamond has a flush, and Kx/Jx/9x combos also beat my Ace-high. I have no diamond and didn’t pair, so I have essentially no showdown value. Betting as a bluff here has very little fold equity — the opponent who bet the turn showed willingness to put chips in and a small river bet will often be called by worse or already-beating hands; value-betting makes no sense with just Ace-high. With a healthy stack and a marginal holding, the prudent line is to concede the pot and avoid building the pot OOP. I will check and fold to meaningful river aggression (only consider calling an extremely tiny bet from a known blatant bluffer).

River brings the 4th diamond (Kd), completing a huge portion of Minni’s turn-calling range (any diamond draw, Qx straight, some slowplayed 8x straight). With Jh4s we have only one pair and we don’t block diamonds, so a value bet is very unlikely to get called by worse (Tx/9x/Jx without a diamond will often fold facing a bet on this texture), while better hands (flush/straight) can call or even check-raise and punish us. Since we have decent showdown value and the board is extremely polarized, the highest-EV line is to take the free showdown and avoid turning our hand into a thin value/bluff-catcher spot for extra chips.

Pocket Kings in UTG - a premium hand. I should raise to build the pot and get value. Standard open to 250 (2.5x BB) is consistent with my previous raises and will look standard. With KK I'm happy to play a big pot and can 4-bet if someone 3-bets me.

The current game is 5-handed. The player Claude opens from UTG to 250 (2.5 BB). I am in the next position (UTG+1) with A3s. While A3s has flush and straight potential, calling leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the three players acting behind me, and I would be playing a pot potentially dominated by UTG's stronger Ax range. 3-betting is an option as a bluff, but given the early position of the raiser and my position relative to the field, the most disciplined and standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Button with Ah6d facing an UTG open to 250. With ~80 BB effective and aggressive players behind (big stacked Flash and FiveTwo), I don't want to isolate or build a big pot with a marginal A6o. Calling keeps the pot manageable and uses position to realize equity and apply postflop pressure if favorable; a small min-raise here invites 3-bets and difficult spots. Plan: call 250, fold to a large 3-bet (or re-evaluate vs a small squeeze), and continue postflop—value/raise when I hit top pair or strong draws, control pot otherwise.

Qs3s is too weak and frequently dominated (all better Qx in both raiser and caller ranges), and we’d be out of position against at least two players with an aggressive big blind still to act. The pot odds to complete are tempting, but the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a low-EV defend. A squeeze with this hand also risks getting flatted/4-bet by deeper stacks and playing a bloated pot OOP. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 5d. Claude (UTG) raised to 250 and Minni called on the Button. I only need to call 150 more into a pot that will be 800, giving me 4.3 to 1 odds. While my hand is relatively weak, it is suited and the price is too good to pass up given the pot odds and the potential to hit a strong diamond draw or two pair. I will call to see the flop.

I have completely missed this flop with Queen high and no draw. Since I am first to act on this street and facing two opponents (the pre-flop raiser Claude and the button caller Minni), the most prudent play is to check. I have no equity to justify a bet and will likely fold to any significant bet from my opponents.

I have pocket Kings on a 3h-8c-Ts board - a great flop for my overpair. No overcards, no flush draws, relatively dry texture. Flash checked, and I should bet for value. With two callers preflop, I need to protect my hand and extract value. Betting around 50-60% pot (400-500) is standard here.

Call 450. Preflop I wanted to keep the pot manageable with A♠6♦ and use position — same plan applies on this flop. I missed the board (3♥ 8♣ T♠) and have only A‑high with no meaningful draws (no real flush/straight outs), so raising as a bluff is thin: it bloats the pot with a marginal hand, can get 3‑bet or called by better hands, and I lack strong blockers. Claude (UTG) c‑betting 450 into this pot is consistent with a wide continuing range (Tx, middle pairs, some A‑x); calling is inexpensive and lets me use position to re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan: call now to see a turn, fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve (an A or safe runner), or take a controlled line for value/pressure if I hit. Maintain pot control and preserve stack depth for better spots.

I have completely missed the flop with Qd 5d on a 3h-8c-Ts board. I am facing a bet from the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) and a call from Minni. With no draws and only Queen high, I have negligible equity in this pot. Even with the pot odds offered, there is no justification for continuing in this hand against two opponents who have both shown strength on this street. I will fold.

I have KK on 3h-8c-Ts-4s. The turn 4s adds a backdoor flush draw but doesn't change much. My overpair is still very strong. Minni called my flop bet - likely has a ten, a draw, or a smaller pocket pair. I should continue betting for value around 65% pot to charge draws and extract value from worse hands.

I called the flop with a plan to fold to significant turn aggression unless I improved. I haven’t improved — I only have A‑high (A♥6♦) with no flush/straight draws and only three outs to pair an Ace (~6–7% equity). Claude’s line (UTG open, flop c‑bet, sizable turn bet of 1,100) polarizes toward value (T×, 8×, pocket pairs, sets) or a very infrequent bluff. Pot is 2,800 and I must call 1,100 (≈28% pot‑odds) which is far above my actual equity. Raising would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and could get called or jammed by better hands. Per my preflop/flop plan and to protect my stack for better spots, I fold.

I am in UTG position with [7h 9d], which is a very weak off-suit hand (9-7 offsuit). In a 5-handed game, UTG is the earliest position and requires a tight opening range. This hand is far below the threshold for opening. I have 80 BBs and no reason to get involved with a trash hand out of position. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Short-handed (5 players) and UTG+1, A♣5♣ offsuit is marginal but playable — I want to take the initiative rather than limp and give the big stacks cheap flops. With ~73BB effective I will open-raise to a standard 3x sizing to 300 to discourage multiway calls and to steal the blinds or isolate a caller. Plan if I face a sizable 3-bet: fold A5o (unless the 3-bet is tiny or I have a clear read it’s light). If I get a single call, I’ll continuation-bet selectively (bigger on A-high flops for value, check and control pot on dry/connected boards) because my kicker is weak.

5-handed, Minni’s UTG+1 open is likely fairly wide. 9♠8♦ is marginal offsuit, but it has good connectivity and plays much better in position with deep enough stacks (we can realize equity and win pots on many runouts). I don’t like 3-betting this hand because it has poor blocker properties and gets called/4-bet in bad shape; flatting keeps the pot controllable and lets me outplay postflop. If either blind squeezes large, I can comfortably fold and lose only the call amount.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit (Th 2s), which is a very weak hand. Minni raised from UTG+1 and FiveTwo called on the button. Even with decent pot odds, calling with such a garbage hand out of position against two active opponents is a losing play. I will fold and maintain my chip lead.

K7 suited in the BB facing a minraise with one caller. Getting 3.75:1 pot odds (750:200). K7s has decent playability with flush potential and a king. Standard defend from the big blind in a multiway pot.

I flopped top pair (7s) with the board's pair of 6s, giving me two pair 7s and 6s with a king kicker. This is a decent hand in a multiway pot but vulnerable to overcards and draws. I should lead out for value and protection. A bet around half pot (~475) is appropriate to get value from worse hands and deny equity to overcards.

I opened to 300 preflop and got called; flop is 7♠-6♣-6♥ and I hold A♥5♣ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Claude has just put me to 475 into a 1,425 pot, so a call would require roughly 25% equity (475/(1425+475) ≈ 25%). Claude’s line on this paired, relatively dry flop polarizes to strong value (sets, full houses, 7x/6x combos, and overpairs) with only a small bluff frequency. There’s also a caller (Button) behind who can still act. My hand has very little equity vs a value-heavy raising range and I’m out of position; raising to 950 as a bluff is too large and risky. With ~70BB effective and lots of better spots ahead, folding and preserving the stack is the correct play here.

Claude’s small donk/lead on a paired 7-6-6 board can be a lot of one-pair/protection hands (7x, medium pairs) plus some 6x for value. With 9♠8♦ we have an open-ended straight draw (5 or T) plus some additional equity from pairing 8/9, and we’re getting a very good price (475 to win 1425). Raising as a semi-bluff risks getting shoved on by 6x and forcing us to fold after putting in more chips, while calling keeps his weaker hands/bluffs in and lets us use position to realize equity and apply pressure on many turn cards if he slows down.

I have two pair (7s and 6s) with King kicker. The turn Js is concerning - it could have connected with FiveTwo's range. After they called my flop bet, they could have a 6, pocket pair, a 7, or now a Jack. My hand is decent but vulnerable. Checking here makes sense to control pot size and get to showdown cheaply. If they bet, I can call a reasonable amount; if they check, I get free river card.

Claude’s flop lead then turn check on 7♠-6♣-6♥-J♠ is a classic spot where his range contains a lot of one-and-done/protection hands (random 7x, small pairs, occasional air) that don’t love continuing on the J. With 9♠8♦ we have an open-ended straight draw (5/T) and good equity when called, so betting is higher EV than checking behind because we can win the pot immediately a meaningful portion of the time and still have outs when called. I’ll use a mid-size bet to generate folds from his weaker holdings without committing myself vs a check-raise from trips/boats.

I have two pair (7s and 6s) with King kicker. FiveTwo's turn bet of 850 into ~1900 (45% pot) could represent a 6 for trips, a Jack for a better two pair, or a bluff/weaker pair. Pot odds are 3.2:1, needing ~24% equity. My hand is too strong to fold - I beat 7x with worse kicker, pocket pairs below J, and bluffs. Raising seems unnecessary as I'd only get called by better. Calling is the balanced play to control pot size and see a river.

River completes with Td. I have two pair (7s and 6s) with King kicker. This is a showdown-value hand that's not strong enough to bet for value - I'd only get called by better (trips, J-T two pair, straights like 89). The Td makes 89 straights possible and improves hands with a Ten. Checking to control the pot and showdown my medium-strength hand is optimal. If FiveTwo bets, I'll need to evaluate sizing but likely call reasonable bets.

River brings Td completing our 6-7-8-9-T straight (using 6/7/T on board plus our 8/9). After Claude’s flop lead, turn check-call, and now river check, his range is heavily weighted to bluff-catchers (7x, Jx, Tx, pocket pairs) and some trips-6x; the hands that beat us (full houses like 76/J6/T6/77/JJ/TT) exist but are a smaller portion and often go for a check-raise.

We should value bet, but avoid overbetting/shoving because that mostly gets called by better. A small-to-mid sizing targets the wide call range and keeps our loss smaller if he springs a check-raise with a boat. About 1/3 pot is ideal here.

River 1200 into 3600 pot (~33% pot). Pot odds are 4:1, needing ~20% equity. I have two pair 7766 with K kicker. FiveTwo's line (call pre, call flop, bet turn, bet river) suggests some strength. They could have trips (6x), better two pair (Jx, Tx), 89 straight, or 88-99. But I also beat 7x with worse kicker, small pairs 22-55, and bluffs. At these pot odds, folding two pair is too exploitable. Their sizing is relatively small which suggests they may not be super strong. Calling.

I’m UTG in a 5‑handed game with Q♣5♣. Preflop pot is 150 (SB/BB) and it would cost 100 to see the flop or 200 to min‑raise. Q5s is a weak, easily‑dominated hand from early position: while suited, it relies on hitting a queen or a club, and when a queen pairs it’s often behind better Qx hands. Calling 100 requires roughly ~40% equity against the field to be profitable, which Q5s doesn’t have multiway or versus likely opening/3‑bet ranges. Raising is too loose here — it invites 3‑bets/squeezes from deeper stacks behind. Limping is passive and will get exploited. Preserve the ~70BB stack and fold.

5-handed after UTG folds, 99 is well ahead of the blinds’ defending ranges and plays best with initiative. Limping invites the Button to attack and lets the blinds realize equity cheaply. I’ll open for a normal value raise that still allows me to comfortably call versus the shorter stacks’ shove ranges and play in position vs the blinds when called.

I am holding K5 offsuit on the button. FiveTwo, who has the second-largest stack, raised from UTG+1. K5o is a weak hand that is frequently dominated by an early-position raising range. Given my chip lead and the strength of the opponent's position, there is no reason to risk chips with a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K8o in the small blind facing an UTG+1 raise to 300. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. K8o doesn't have great playability - it's easily dominated by KQ/KJ/KT and doesn't have strong suited/connected potential. With Pro still to act behind and being out of position, this is a fold.

I hold 4d 2s in the Big Blind, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. An opponent in UTG+1 (FiveTwo) has raised to 300. The pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 650, requiring ~31% equity) do not justify defending with such a weak hand, especially since I would be playing out of position against a likely stronger range. Folding is the only standard play here to preserve my stack.

5-handed UTG is effectively a middle position open, so we can play a bit wider than full-ring. Td6d has decent playability (suited, some straight potential) and benefits from taking initiative to win blinds or isolate. I’ll use a small open to keep risk controlled and allow an easy fold versus 3-bets/jams (especially from the short Button).

I am holding Queen-Ten suited (Qc Tc), which is a strong suited broadway hand that performs well in multi-way pots and in position. FiveTwo, the other big stack at the table, has raised from UTG. While a 3-bet is an option to isolate, calling is a solid play here to see a flop with a hand that has great post-flop potential, especially since I will have position on the initial raiser throughout the hand. My stack size allows me to play speculatively against the other leader without overcommitting pre-flop.

K2o is a weak hand. Even with position on the button, calling a raise and a cold-caller with K2o is not profitable. I'm dominated by better kings and the hand plays poorly post-flop. With 44bb effective, I should fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with A9o facing a UTG raise to 2.5bb and a flat call from UTG+1. While the pot odds (calling 200 to win 850, ~23.5%) are seemingly attractive, playing A9o out of position against a UTG opening range and a caller is a classic reverse implied odds situation. I am often dominated by better Aces (AT+, AJ, AQ, AK) which are well within a UTG range, and I will be playing the entire post-flop session out of position. A squeeze play is risky against deep stacks and an early position opener. Folding is the most prudent long-term play to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots.

Fold. 9♣5♦ (9h5d) offsuit is a very weak holding here — dominated by most UTG raising ranges and has poor playability multiway. Pot is 650 and it costs 150 to call (pot odds ~150/800 ≈ 18.8%), but against an UTG raise plus a caller my realized equity is well below that and I’d be out of position post‑flop (I’ll act before them on later streets). A reraise is thin and unnecessary. Preserve the 6,950 stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on Qh-Jc-5h, we retain a meaningful range advantage (strong Qx/Jx, overpairs, sets) and can apply pressure with a small, high-frequency c-bet. Td6d has no made hand and only backdoor straight potential, but it does block some of villain’s continuing Tx combos and benefits from immediate fold equity versus A-high/small pairs and some suited broadway whiffs. I’ll use a small sizing to risk little, keep my range protected, and cleanly fold if Flash responds with a raise on this texture.

I have flopped top pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker on a board of Qh-5h-Jc. My opponent, FiveTwo, who opened from UTG, has made a small continuation bet of 225 into a pot of 650. This small sizing could indicate a range of hands, including overcards, draws, or a weaker pair. Given my hand's strength and the fact that I am in position, calling is the most balanced play. It allows me to see how the turn develops without bloating the pot against potentially stronger hands like AQ or QJ, while keeping their bluffs and weaker holdings in the hand.

Turn pairs the low card (5c), which is a dynamic card for both ranges: we can still credibly have overpairs/strong Qx and some 5x, but Flash also has plenty of continues (Qx/Jx/5x, plus sticky pairs and some heart draws from the flop). With Td6d we have essentially no equity and no meaningful draw to improve, so a second barrel is mostly just lighting chips on fire against a player who appears capable of floating and applying pressure in position. Checking keeps the pot controlled, protects my checking range (I can check strong hands here too), and lets me comfortably fold versus a bet rather than inflate a pot I can’t win at showdown.

The 5c on the turn pairs the board, making my hand Queens and Fives with a Ten kicker. FiveTwo's check after a small flop c-bet often signifies a desire for pot control with a marginal hand like a Jack or a weaker Queen, or perhaps they are giving up with missed overcards. By checking back, I maintain pot control against the only other player who can significantly impact my chip lead and ensure I get to a cheap showdown while not over-committing against a potential trap or a better Queen like AQ.

River improves us to a small pair (6s6d), which has real showdown value after the turn checked through. If we bet, we rarely get called by worse (A-high and missed draws mostly fold), while better hands (any Qx/Jx/5x and some pocket pairs) continue and can even raise—so betting tends to value-own ourselves. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity, and also leaves room for Flash to bluff missed hearts/air; we can then bluff-catch versus reasonable sizing.

The river 6s is a brick. FiveTwo has checked twice (turn and river) after a pre-flop raise and a flop c-bet. This suggests they either have a marginal hand like a Jack or a weak Queen, or they are giving up on a draw. While my hand (Queens and Fives with a Ten kicker) is strong, betting here might only get called by hands that beat me (AQ, QJ) or marginal hands that might fold (KJ, AJ). Since FiveTwo is the only player who can significantly damage my stack, checking back is the safest way to realize my equity and take the pot without risking a check-raise.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with 6h 2d, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Given the table dynamics and the fact that I have a significant stack lead, there is no reason to play such a poor hand from the most disadvantaged position. This is a clear and easy fold.
Q9o is a weak hand from UTG+1. With several players behind including big stacks, this is a clear fold from early position. Not worth risking chips with this marginal holding.

I am on the Button with Jc8s (Jack-Eight offsuit). The action has folded to me. This is a 5-handed table. While the Button is the best position to steal blinds, J8o is a very marginal hand, often considered below the threshold for a profitable open-raise, even in late position. The Big Blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader with a deep stack and has shown capability for aggressive play. Opening light here risks getting 3-bet or called by a range that dominates me. It is safer and strategically sound to fold this hand and wait for a better spot.

Folded to me in the SB with K♥9♣. Pot is only 150 and it’s heads-up preflop against the big blind (button has already folded). K9o is marginal but playable heads-up and has some blocker value. I want to apply pressure and pick up the blinds rather than just completing and giving the big blind good pot odds to see a flop. A standard 2x BB open to 200 is the right size here: it preserves most of my deep stack, gives good fold equity, and keeps postflop play manageable if called. Plan: fold to a sizeable 3-bet; if called, play postflop aggressively on favorable textures (c-bet good K/high flops) and proceed cautiously if I face heavy resistance.

SB is min-raising and we’re heads-up; in BB we should defend quite wide getting a great price (100 to win 400 total after we call). T5 suited has decent playability (can flop flushes, pairs+draws) and we cover, so calling keeps in worse hands and avoids creating a spot where a short-ish stack can jam over a 3-bet and force us to fold too much equity.

I opened to 200 preflop and was called by the big blind. Flop K♠ T♥ 2♠ gives me top pair (K9) with a decent kicker against a wide defending range. This is a textbook c-bet spot: board is relatively dry aside from the two spades and my hand has strong showdown value. I want value from worse Ks, some Tx and weaker pairs while charging draws and denying free cards. Bet ~50–65% pot is appropriate — 250 into 400. If I face a large raise I'll re-evaluate and likely fold to heavy aggression; if called I'll control the pot on the turn and extract more value when favorable.

Heads-up blind vs blind, Minni’s range is very wide after the small preflop open. On K♠ T♥ 2♠ we have 2nd pair with some backdoor potential (clubs/5-runner) and we’re getting a very good price (250 to win 900). Raising mainly builds a pot where we’re dominated by Kx and better Tx, while folds out her bluffs and worse hands we want to keep in. Best is to call and re-evaluate on turns (especially versus big barrels on scary cards).

Turn pairs the board with K♣ giving me trips (K♠ K♣ + K♥ in my hand). Villain called my 2x open and a 250 flop c‑bet, so his range is wide: worse Kx, Tx, medium pairs, and some two‑card spade combos — only a few hands (pocket TT, 22, or rare K+T combos / Kd with a better kicker) already beat me. I want value and to charge/deny equity to draws, so I should bet rather than check‑back and give free or cheap rivers. A ~2/3‑pot bet is a good compromise: big enough to charge tens, pairs and two‑card spade draws, but small enough to keep worse Ks and many marginal hands in. Bet 600 into the 900 pot; re‑evaluate on the river if called. If I face a large, polarizing raise (pot‑sized or shove) I’ll assume a full house or very narrow value range and fold or proceed cautiously; I’ll call small/medium resistance.

Turn pairs the K and upgrades us from 2nd pair to two-pair (KKT T) with a weak kicker. Minni’s SB opening range is very wide and she can double-barrel a lot of worse hands (spade draws, random floats, underpairs), so folding is out at this price. Raising is awkward versus her value range: any Kx continues (often crushing us with trips), and some hands like KT/TT/22 already have us in very bad shape; meanwhile a raise folds out the bluffs we want to keep in and risks committing vs a short stack. Best is to call and re-evaluate river based on card and sizing (comfortably continue versus small/medium bets, but be cautious versus a big polar shove).

I have trips (K♥9♣ with K♠ K♣ on board) — a very strong hand but vulnerable to full houses (notably KT combos) and Kx with better kickers. Villain called my open, flop c‑bet and turn bet, so their range includes Kx, pocket pairs (TT/22), and some two‑card combos. The river 4♦ doesn't change many equities. I want value from worse hands (TT, 22, weaker Kx, Tx, etc.) while still charging and denying showdown cards. A moderately large value bet (about 2/3 pot) extracts value from those hands but doesn't overcommit if I face heavy aggression. Plan: bet 1,400 into the 2,100 pot. If I face a pot‑sized raise or shove I will assume a full house or very narrow value range and fold; I will call a small/medium raise to go to showdown.

We have KKT T with a weak kicker (T♣5♣), so this is mostly a bluff-catcher: we lose to any Kx and boats, but we beat all missed spade draws and a bunch of triple-barrel bluffs/gutshots from a very wide SB opening range. The sizing (1400 into ~3500, needing ~29% equity) gives us a good price, and importantly our T♣ blocks some of her best value (KT) and removes TT entirely. Raising is bad because it folds out the bluffs we want to keep in and gets called by Kx/boats that crush us. So we take the price and call.

JQo from UTG is a marginal hand but playable. With 44BB stack, I should maintain an active image. Standard 2.5x open to 250 is appropriate - the hand has decent playability postflop with straight potential.

I hold AQs in UTG+1 facing an open raise to 250 from Claude in UTG. Claude has demonstrated a very loose opening range in previous hands (e.g., opening 54s, 75o, TKo from early positions). AQs is significantly ahead of his range. I want to isolate him and play a pot in position, preventing the button and blinds from seeing a cheap flop. A 3-bet is the standard play here. I will raise to 750 (3x the open), which is a solid size to extract value and deny equity.

Fold. Qc4s is too weak to continue facing an UTG open to 250 and a UTG+1 3‑bet to 750. The pot is 1150 and a 750 call requires roughly ~39% equity to break even — Q4o has very poor equity and is frequently dominated by the 3‑bettor’s range of big pairs and broadways. A small 4‑bet bluff to 1250 is too thin here (players in the blinds can still act and I lack meaningful blockers), and with better spots available on the button I’ll preserve chips.
