Fold and wait for a stronger hand or clearer fold equity.

Facing an UTG open from a short stack (Claude) and a 3-bet from Pro, A9o is too often dominated (AJ+/AQ+/AK, A9s+/AT+, etc.). Calling puts me out of position in a potentially multiway 3-bet pot (BB still to act), with poor playability and reverse implied odds when I make top pair. A 4-bet bluff is also high-variance here because Pro’s 3-bet vs UTG tends to be stronger and he can shove for ~7k, forcing me to burn chips. Best stack-preservation and EV choice is to fold.

I am holding Jack-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind. Claude (UTG) opened the action with a raise to 250, and Pro (UTG+1) 3-bet to 750. JTo is a weak hand to play out of position against two early position raisers, one of whom is showing significant strength with a 3-bet. The risk of being dominated by hands like AJ, KJ, or pocket pairs is high. Given my large stack and the strength shown by the opponents, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

JQo facing a 3-bet from Pro who is UTG+1. My UTG open was already marginal, and now I'm facing significant pressure. With ~42BB effective and being out of position, JQo doesn't play well against a typical 3-bet range. The pot odds (500 to win 1150) aren't compelling enough for a hand that will often be dominated or need to hit well to win. Folding is the disciplined play here.

In a 5-handed game, AJo is a strong enough hand to open-raise from UTG (effectively the Hijack position). With a stack of roughly 83BB, I want to take the initiative. A standard open size of 2.5BB (250 chips) allows me to build the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop or play in position against the blinds, while keeping the price to play reasonable if I face a 3-bet.

Call 250. Q♦T♣ is a playable broadway but it's marginal for a light 3-bet out of position against an UTG open. Pro's raise to 250 (2.5x) is standard; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity — 250 is a small chunk of my 9,400 stack (~2.7% of stack) and gives good odds to see a flop. If I face a sizable 3-bet or a shove (particularly from short-stacked Claude), I'll fold. Postflop plan: continue when I hit top pair, two pair, or strong draws; exercise pot control or fold to heavy aggression on A/K-high or unhelpful textures.

We’re on the button getting a good price (250 to win 650) with position versus an UTG open that is likely wider 5-handed plus a caller. 5d3d is too weak to 3-bet/squeeze profitably into two players (we’ll get called by better and play a bloated pot with poor equity), but it’s playable as a call due to implied odds when we flop strong draws (flush/straight) and we can realize equity well on the button. Plan postflop: keep pots small with one-pair hands, continue mainly with strong draws/two-pair+ and apply pressure when ranges are capped.

I am holding pocket fours in the small blind. UTG has opened for 250, and there are two callers (Minni and FiveTwo). The pot currently stands at 900 chips. I need to call 200 more to see a flop. This gives me pot odds of 5.5:1, which is excellent for a small pocket pair with high implied odds in a multi-way pot. My goal is to 'set-mine'—if I hit a four on the flop, I will likely have the best hand and can play for a large pot. If I miss the set, I can fold cheaply to any further aggression. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here.

I have 73o in the BB - a very weak hand. While I'm getting ~7:1 pot odds to call 150 into 1100, this hand plays terribly postflop in a multiway pot. Even if I hit a pair, I'll have poor kicker issues and reverse implied odds. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I missed my set on the flop, and with three other players in the pot, my pocket fours are very vulnerable. This board of 6-2-8 with two clubs doesn't favor my hand, and since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser and two other callers, the best course of action is to check and see how the street develops. I will likely fold to any significant aggression.

I am in a 4-way pot as the pre-flop aggressor from UTG. The flop comes low (8c 6c 2s) and connects better with the calling ranges of my opponents than my high-card heavy opening range. With 3 opponents, the probability that someone has hit a set, two pair, or a strong draw is significant. Although I hold the Ac (blocking the nut flush draw) and have two overcards, c-betting into three players on this texture is generally negative EV as I will rarely get everyone to fold and have little equity when called. Checking allows me to control the pot size, potentially realize my equity if I hit an Ace or Jack on the turn, or fold comfortably if faced with significant aggression.

Multiway pot (Pro UTG open + BTN + SB) and checked to me on a very dry 6c‑2s‑8c. I have Q♦T♣ — two overcards but no pair and only a runner‑runner club flush possibility (very low equity). Out of position versus the opener and with multiple callers, bluffing has poor fold equity and betting would bloat a pot I’m likely behind in. I’ll control the pot and realize equity: check back and see a turn for a pair or a realistic draw; fold to heavy aggression or call small, non‑committal bets to try and see showdown cheaply.

Flop checks through from SB, preflop raiser, and the caller, which caps a lot of their ranges (many A-high/broadway whiffs and some medium pairs that don’t love building a pot multiway). Even though we have only a gutshot to the 4 plus backdoor diamonds, we have strong positional advantage and can profitably stab small to pick up the pot immediately at a good frequency. Sizing small keeps our risk low multiway and sets up turn barrels on favorable cards (4, diamonds, some high cards) while we can comfortably give up versus a raise or heavy action.

I missed my set on the flop, and the board of 6-2-8 with two clubs contains two overcards to my pocket fours. FiveTwo has bet 400 from the button after everyone else checked. While the pot odds are roughly 3.75:1, I am out of position and still have two other players (Pro and Minni) to act behind me. In a four-way pot, it is highly likely that someone has a pair of sixes, eights, or a strong draw that has significant equity against my hand. Without a set, pocket fours are extremely vulnerable here. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity, rather than calling and potentially facing further aggression on later streets.

I opened pre-flop from UTG with AJo and faced three callers, leading to a 4-way pot. The flop comes 8c-6c-2s, which is low and coordinated, favoring the callers' ranges (sets, two pairs, flush draws) over my high-card range. After I checked, the Button bet 400 into a pot of roughly 1100. While I hold the Ac (backdoor nut flush draw) and two overcards, I am out of position against the bettor and still have a player (Minni) left to act behind me who could call or check-raise. Ace-high is rarely the best hand here, and 'floating' out of position in a multi-way pot is generally negative EV. Conserving chips by folding is the disciplined play.

Flop is very dry (6♣‑2♠‑8♣). I have Q♦T♣ — two overcards and a backdoor club possibility (T♣). FiveTwo's small c‑bet (400 into a 1500 pot) looks like a wide probe and gives me attractive pot odds: calling 400 to potentially win ~2300 requires only ~17% equity. I have ~6 immediate outs to pair (3 Qs + 3 Ts) and ~24% to hit by the river, plus minor backdoor equity. I'm out of position, so raising would bloat the pot with marginal holdings and little fold equity. Plan: call the 400, see the turn, continue if I pair or pick up a meaningful draw; fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss.

The turn Q♥ pairs my Q♦ and gives me top pair with a T kicker. FiveTwo’s flop raise looked like a wide c‑bet/float or a small value hand (6x/8x/medium pair/float/draw). Heads‑up I’m now likely ahead of much of that range and I want to both extract value from worse pairs and charge club/runner‑runner draws. Pot is 1,900 — a value/protection bet of about half the pot denies equity to draws while getting called by worse (6x/8x/Tx/second pair/float). I don’t want to check and give a free river. If FiveTwo raises big I’ll treat it as strong and re‑evaluate (likely fold or pot‑control depending on sizing); if he calls I’ll re‑assess on the river for further value or pot control. Bet ~50% pot to achieve that.

We have 5d3d on 6c-2s-8c-Qh after c-betting/stabbing the flop and getting called. The turn Q is a strong improving card for Minni’s flop call range (Qx floats, Q8/Q6, and it doesn’t push 8x/6x off by itself), and her lead for 1000 into ~1900–2900 looks much more like value/protection than a weak blocker bet. Our equity is extremely thin (basically a 4 for a wheel straight; no flush draw), and we don’t have good blockers to make a raise bluff credible/profitable. Calling needs ~25% immediate equity and we don’t have it, plus we’ll face many river barrels from Qx. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack for better pressure spots.

UTG in a 5-handed game with Q♥4♦ offsuit — a weak, easily dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability out of position. The current pot is just the blinds (150) and the min open is 200, but opening or limping here risks playing multiway pots OOP against deeper, often aggressive opponents (Button and UTG+1) and a capable SB/BB. With ~106 BB I can wait for a much better, positionally advantaged spot. Fold to preserve stack and avoid difficult spots.

5-handed we can open fairly wide from UTG+1 (HJ), but 5h2h is near the bottom of playable suited hands: it makes weak, dominated pairs and low/weak flushes, and performs poorly versus the aggressive 3-bet tendencies shown (frequent opens/3-bets). With deep stacks behind (especially Button) and a 40bb SB who can pressure, this is a low-EV steal attempt. Preserving chips and waiting for better spots is higher EV here.

I am on the button with 83o, which is a very weak starting hand. While I am the chip leader and could attempt a steal against the blinds, Claude (SB) is relatively short-stacked and might look for a spot to shove, and Pro (BB) is a capable defender. There is no reason to put chips into the pot with one of the worst hands in poker at this stage of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Ah9h is a strong suited ace in the small blind with everyone folding to me. Heads up against the BB, this is a clear raise. I'll make a standard raise to 250 to put pressure on Pro.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 5c (65o). The Small Blind (Claude) raises to 250 (2.5bb). I am in position post-flop, but 65o is a weak hand. The pot odds are 150 to call into a total pot of 500 (30%). Against a standard SB opening range of ~50%, 65o has about 36% raw equity. However, offsuit low connectors suffer from poor equity realization (often realizing only ~60-70% of their equity). 36% * 0.7 is approx 25%, which is less than the required 30% pot odds. Therefore, calling is likely -EV. Folding is the disciplined play.

5-handed, UTG plays much wider than full-ring and JTo is a reasonable open-raise candidate. Limping invites multiway play where JTo performs poorly and gives up initiative. A small open also pressures the shorter stacks (Claude/Pro) while keeping the pot manageable; if we face a sizable 3-bet from Flash/Minni, we can comfortably fold this offsuit Broadway.

I am holding Queen-Eight of diamonds in UTG+1. FiveTwo has opened from UTG with a raise to 250. While Q8s has some playability due to its suitedness, it is generally too weak to call a raise from an UTG player, especially since I have several players left to act behind me, including the short-stacked Claude on the button and Pro in the Small Blind who has shown aggression recently. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to maintain my chip lead.

I have AKo on the button - a premium hand. Facing a UTG raise to 250, this is a clear 3-bet spot. AKo is too strong to just flat call here, and I have position. I'll raise to around 3x to isolate and build the pot with a strong holding.

I am in the Small Blind with Ks 8s. FiveTwo raised from UTG and Claude 3-bet from the Button. Facing a cold 3-bet, my hand is significantly dominated by both opponents' ranges. K8s plays poorly out of position in a 3-bet pot, and the UTG player is yet to act behind me, increasing the risk of a squeeze or 4-bet. The pot odds do not justify a call given the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I'm in the big blind with 4♦6♦ facing a button 3-bet to 750 after an UTG open to 250. To continue I must call 650 into a 1,150 pot (~36% immediate pot odds) and will be out of position postflop against the button (and potentially multiway if the original raiser continues). 4♦6♦ is a low suited connector with poor high-card value and limited playability in a 3-bet pot, and squeezing or 4-betting with this hand would be unjustified. Preserving a healthy ~105BB stack for better spots is the priority — fold here.

Claude’s button 3-bet versus our UTG open creates a tough spot: we’ll be out of position and the effective stack is only ~34bb (he has ~2.7k behind after 3-betting), so if we call the SPR will be low and we’ll frequently be forced into stack-off decisions with a hand that’s often dominated (AJ/KJ/QJ/AT/KTs, etc.). JTo has poor realization OOP and isn’t a great 4-bet bluff candidate (weak blockers, and his stack size makes him close to committed if we 4-bet). Best play is to preserve chips and fold.

I am in the UTG position with 8s 3h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. In a 5-handed game, opening with such a hand from early position is extremely unprofitable and serves no strategic purpose given my current stack and table image. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

9h2h suited is a very weak hand, especially in early position (UTG+1). Even though it's suited, it has poor connectivity and playability. With 4 players still to act behind me and a below-average stack, I should fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with Ten of Diamonds and Five of Hearts (T5o). The action has folded to me. This is a very weak hand (trash) that falls well outside of a standard button opening range, even considering the positional advantage. With 78 big blinds, there is no pressure to make loose steals. Opening here would be over-bluffing and likely -EV against the blinds' defending ranges. I will fold.

With everyone else folded it's effectively heads-up vs FiveTwo (BB). Q♥6♠ is marginal but suited and playable in heads-up; I should take the initiative from the button/SMB and try to steal the blind rather than passively calling. A min-raise to 200 applies pressure, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and gives me position postflop to c-bet or control the pot. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call small 3-bets rarely only with good odds, and continuation-bet selectively on Q/heart/favorable dry flops. Calling the 50 would be too passive and limp-fold often; raising is the +EV play here.

Minni is opening very small from the SB and it’s folded to me. Getting a great price (100 to win 400 immediate if we see a flop) and BB vs SB ranges are wide, so 9♣7♠ is a standard defend. I prefer a call rather than a 3-bet with this offsuit, middling connectivity hand to avoid bloating the pot out of position while still realizing decent equity.

Flop: Q♦ 8♦ A♦. My hand Q♥6♠ is only second pair (there's an Ace on board) on a very wet, diamond-heavy flop. Lots of hands in FiveTwo's calling range beat me outright (any A) or already have a flush (two diamonds) or strong draws; my kicker is weak and a c-bet here would build a pot I don't want to face resistance in. I originally planned to continuation-bet Q flops, but this one is not dry — pot control is the priority. I'll check to avoid bloating the pot; plan to call a small probe bet (roughly ≤ ~200) to realize equity, but fold to large bets/raises which typically represent an Ace or made flush. If FiveTwo checks back I get a free card to re-evaluate on the turn.

SB opened very small and checked this flop on a monotone A♦Q♦8♦ board. With 9♣7♠ and no diamond, I have virtually no equity (no pair, no meaningful draw) and I don’t block the flushes or strong one-diamond continues that will call/raise. While I can stab some of these spots in position, this specific combo is a poor candidate; checking keeps the pot small and lets me potentially take a delayed stab on a favorable turn (pairing/low card that weakens their range).

I hold Q♥6♠ on Q♦ 8♦ A♦ / A♠ (turn). My Q pairs the board queen, so I now have two pair (Aces & Queens) — a strong hand heads‑up but still vulnerable to trips (an Ace in villain’s hand) or a made diamond flush. FiveTwo called preflop and checked the flop, which reduces (but doesn’t eliminate) the likelihood they already have the nut flush or a slow‑played Ace. Checking again surrenders value and allows free river cards that can beat me. I want to extract value from worse Qx and medium pairs while charging diamond draws, so I’ll lead for value/protection with a sizing big enough to get called by worse hands but not so large that a light raise forces an immediate ±commitment. Bet 300 chips into the 400‑chip pot (~3/4 pot). If I face a large raise I’ll presume trips/flush and fold; if called I’ll re‑evaluate the river and plan to extract more from thin calls or pot‑control against obvious danger.

Minni’s turn lead (300 into 700) after checking the monotone A♦Q♦8♦ flop is consistent with a lot of her range: Ax that now improved to trips, Qx with a diamond, and plenty of one-diamond hands that decided to start betting once the board paired. With 9♣7♠ I have essentially no equity (no pair, no diamond, no real draws), I don’t block flushes or strong continues, and calling needs ~30% which I can’t realize. Turning this into a raise-bluff is also poor because I lack key blockers (a diamond) and would be risking a lot versus a range that can comfortably continue. Best play is to fold and preserve stack.

Q9o from UTG is a marginal hand that's too weak to open from early position. It doesn't play well against the ranges that will call or 3-bet me. With 45BB effective, I should stick to stronger opening hands from UTG like broadways, pairs, and suited aces. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in UTG+1 (effectively Middle Position in a 5-handed game) with Q3o. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, unconnected cards, and a low kicker. It is well outside the standard opening range for this position. With 78 big blinds, there is no pressure to play marginal hands. Folding is the only standard play here.

I'm on the button with K♠6♥ against only the blinds (SB and BB). With ~107.5bb effective this is a standard spot to open a wide stealing range — K6o is marginal but playable in position. I want to seize initiative and use fold equity rather than passively giving the BB a free look. Raise to the minimum open (200) keeps the range wide, applies pressure to the blinds, and lets me play postflop in position if called. If I face a sizable 3-bet I will fold this hand; if I'm called I'll proceed cautiously and use position to c-bet or check-back depending on the flop texture.

Button open to 200 is relatively wide 5-handed. A3s is a strong SB 3-bet candidate: it blocks a lot of their continue/4-bet range (Ax), has good playability when called (suited, wheel/straight potential), and benefits from taking initiative OOP while also pressuring a shorter BTN stack. I’ll choose a standard SB vs BTN 3-bet sizing that discourages the BB from coming along and sets up profitable c-bets.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (3h 4c). The action has already seen a raise from the Button (Minni) and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). There is no reason to put chips into a 3-bet pot with such a weak hand out of position. Folding is the only viable play.

I opened to 200 on the button with K♠6♥ and FiveTwo (SB) 3‑bet to 800. That 3‑bet is relatively small (8bb) so a call is preferable — folding surrenders position and a 4‑bet is too strong with this hand. Calling 600 more puts 800 into the pot for me and creates a ~1700 pot (about 35% equity needed), and I have clear post‑flop position to play draws and top‑pair/king flops. Plan: call, see the flop, c‑bet selectively on dry favorable boards, check/float or pot control on bad textures, and fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I don’t improve. If FiveTwo jams or shows extreme aggression I’ll fold.

As the preflop 3-bettor I retain a range advantage (overpairs, strong broadways) and this flop is still very c-bettable. With Ad3d I have the nut flush draw (plus some A-high showdown value when checked through), so betting lets me realize equity with fold equity, deny hands like KQ/QJ/J9, and start building a pot for when I hit a diamond or can barrel favorable turns (A/K/Q/diamond). I’ll use a small-to-medium 3-bet pot c-bet sizing to keep my range protected and not overinflate when villain can have Tx/sets.

I have K♠6♥ on 2♥‑6♦‑T♦: middle pair (6s) with a decent King kicker. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop (small) then made a small flop bet (600 into ~1700) — a sizing consistent with a wide c‑bet range that includes bluffs/draws and thinner value hands (pocket pairs, Tx, some diamonds). Calling 600 gives me good pot odds (~600/(2300+600) ≈ 20.7%) and lets me use my positional advantage to see the turn and control the pot. Raising here would be thin and commits lots of chips with a vulnerable one‑pair hand; folding is too weak because I beat many of his bluffs/draws. Plan: call, evaluate the turn — proceed for value or pot‑control in position, fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I don’t improve.

Turn Jc is a very good barrel card for my SB 3-bet range (I have lots of AJ/KJ/QJ, overpairs, and even JJ), while it puts real pressure on Minni’s flop-calling range that contains many 6x/TT–99/T9s/diamond draws that don’t love facing a second bet. With Ad3d I have the nut flush draw, so I can profitably bet both for fold equity now and to build a pot for when I hit a diamond (and I can also keep barreling on strong river cards like A/K/Q/diamond). I’ll use a strong, but not committing, sizing that sets up a river shove on many runouts while still allowing me to fold if Minni jams.

I have second pair (6s) with a strong K kicker and I’m in position. FiveTwo’s line (small preflop 3‑bet, modest flop bet, turn bet 2,000 into a 4,900 pot) looks wide — it includes diamonds/straight draws, Tx/Jx, and medium pairs, so I beat a lot of bluffs/draws but am behind to many clear value hands. Calling 2,000 requires only ~29% equity (2,000 / (4,900+2,000) ≈ 29%), so calling to use my positional advantage and keep the pot manageable is the best play. A raise would be thin and likely commit too many chips with a vulnerable one‑pair hand; folding is too weak. Plan: call the turn, evaluate the river — fold to heavy river aggression if I don’t improve, call smaller river bets for value or river-shopped bluffs.

River bricks the main draws (diamonds/straight), and my Ad3d improves to a small pair that has some showdown value versus busted diamond draws and KQ/Q9 type floats that called turn. After Minni calls a large turn barrel, her range is more weighted toward Tx/Jx/sets that won’t fold often enough to justify a pot-sized shove, and Ad is actually a poor bluff blocker here (it removes some missed AdXd combos and blocks some of my strongest Ax value). Best is to check and realize equity; if Minni bets big, I can comfortably fold this weak pair.

Check. The river is checked to me and I only have second pair (6s) with a good kicker — a marginal showdown hand that’s vulnerable to many of FiveTwo’s value hands. FiveTwo’s line (small preflop 3‑bet, small flop bet, large turn bet, then checking the river) maps to a wide range: Tx/Jx, medium pocket pairs, and missed draws/bluffs. A river bet would get value from very few worse hands and has poor fold equity vs hands that actually beat me; it also opens me up to a check‑raise that would commit a large portion of my 7,350 stack into this ~6,900 pot. Pot‑control is the +EV, low‑variance line here — check to showdown and avoid getting raised off a marginal one‑pair holding. If FiveTwo bets after I check (unlikely since it’s checked to me), I’ll evaluate the sizing and pot odds before committing further.

I am in UTG position with 5h 7s, which is a weak off-suit one-gapper. Given the early position and the number of players left to act behind me, this hand has very low playability and negative expected value if played. My stack is healthy (approx 79 BB), so there is no need to take unnecessary risks with poor holdings. The standard and correct play is to fold.

UTG+1 with Kc Jd in a 5-handed game — a very playable, value-oriented opening hand. Pot is 150 and I face 100 to call; with deep stacks (~142bb) and three players left to act behind me (Button, SB, BB), a standard 2.5–3x open is best to build value and deny free odds to speculative hands. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x) to get fold equity, isolate weaker kings/broadways, and keep postflop playability against callers. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and who 3-bets, but this size keeps options open and maximizes expected value from this position.

We’re on the button 5-handed facing a 3x open from UTG+1. 6c5c is a suited connector that plays well in position with good implied odds (straights/flushes), and calling keeps the pot manageable versus what is likely a relatively strong opening range. A 3-bet here would mostly be a bluff against an early-position open and also risks getting played back at by the deep SB; I’d rather realize equity in position and be comfortable folding if the short-ish BB jams over the top.

I am in the Small Blind holding 9s6s. UTG+1 (Minni) opened with a raise to 300, and the Button (FiveTwo) called. While I am getting decent pot odds (250 to call into a 750 pot), 96s is a weak speculative hand to play out of position against two opponents, one of whom raised from early position. Additionally, the Big Blind (Claude) has a relatively short stack and could potentially squeeze or jam over a call. Given the early stage of the hand and my position, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid difficult post-flop situations with a marginal hand.

I have 96o in the BB facing a raise to 300 from Minni and a call from FiveTwo. I need 200 more to call into a pot of 750, giving me about 3.75:1 odds. While 96o is a weak hand, I'm closing the action and getting decent pot odds. I can flop straights, two pairs, or trips occasionally. This is a marginal spot but the price is good enough to see a flop.

I flopped an open-ended straight draw with 96 on a 3-5-7 board - any 4 or 8 gives me the nuts. I'm first to act with 8 outs (about 32% to hit by river). Checking makes sense here - I can see a free card if it checks through, or call a reasonable bet with my draw equity. No need to bet as a semi-bluff out of position against two opponents.

I opened to 300 preflop with Kc Jd and am the preflop aggressor facing two callers on a dry flop (3s 5d 7h). This board is low and disconnected — I have two overcards but no real draws — so a continuation bet is standard to leverage my initiative and deny free cards to small pairs and floaters. In a 3‑way pot I size down to ~1/3 pot to apply pressure without bloating the pot with marginal equity, so I’ll bet 300 into the 950 pot. Plan: if I take it down now great; if I get one caller I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and play cautiously (check back many turns that don’t improve me, fold to significant raises). If I face a substantial flop raise I’ll let KJ go as it’s likely behind.

We flopped middle pair with 6c5c on 3s-5d-7h plus a gutshot to the 4 and backdoor clubs. Minni’s 300 c-bet into 1250 is very small and likely contains a wide range (overcards, some 7x/5x, and occasional strong hands using a sizing that keeps our range in). Raising would mostly turn our hand into a bluff/protection raise in a multiway pot and risks getting played back at by better 5x/7x/overpairs. In position, the best EV is to call, realize our equity cheaply, and reevaluate on the turn when we often get more information from both players’ actions.

I have an open-ended straight draw with 96 on 3-5-7 board. Any 4 or 8 gives me a straight. Getting 5:1 pot odds (300 to win 1550) and I need roughly 5:1 with 8 outs. Easy call with implied odds if I hit.

I still have my open-ended straight draw with 96 on 3-5-7-J. Any 4 or 8 gives me the straight. The turn J doesn't change much for my draw. With the action checked to me, I should take the free card. Betting as a semi-bluff out of position into two players is risky - if I get raised, I'm in a bad spot. Check and see the river for free.

Turn gives me Kc Jd = top pair with a very strong kicker in a 3‑way pot (pot = 1,850). I was the preflop and flop aggressor and both opponents called my small c‑bet on the 3♠5♦7♥ flop, so their ranges are full of worse pairs (7x/5x/3x), worse Jx, and some speculative/connector combos. Betting for value and protection is the right plan — I want to extract from worse jacks and small pairs and charge any straight-type combos while keeping the pot controllable. A ~1/2 pot size (1,000) accomplishes that: it gets value, denies free cards, and doesn't overcommit me. If I face a large raise or shove I will fold/re-evaluate (KJ is vulnerable to sets/two‑pair/AJ); if I get one caller I'll re-evaluate on river based on the card and opponent's tendencies.

We have 6c5c on 3s-5d-7h-Js: third pair (5s) with a gutshot to the 4, but no flush equity. Minni’s small flop c-bet going multiway into a much larger turn barrel on a high card is a strong line that heavily favors her range (lots of Jx, overpairs, and strong 7x/55/77), and our hand is near the bottom of what we arrive here with. Calling 1000 to win 3850 (if Claude folds) needs meaningful equity, and in practice our ~6-out improvement plus thin showdown value won’t realize well because Claude is still to act with a short stack and can frequently check-raise jam or overcall, putting us in high reverse-implied-odds spots. Best play is to let this go and preserve stack for better turn continues (stronger 5x/7x, Jx, sets, and better draws).

I have an open-ended straight draw (96 on 3-5-7-J) needing a 4 or 8. I need to call 1000 to win 2850, getting ~2.85:1 odds. With 8 outs and one card to come, I'm about 17% to hit, needing ~5:1 odds. The pot odds are insufficient - even with implied odds, my remaining stack is only ~3k and Minni may not pay off if a scare card like 4 or 8 hits. Fold is correct.

Td8 offsuit from UTG in a 5-handed game is a marginal, poorly‑playing hand out of position. Calling 100 (the big blind) just to limp in will likely create multiway pots where my hand is often dominated and I’ll be out of position postflop. Raising out of UTG with this holding is too loose — I can face 3-bets from the big stack on the button or a shove from the shorter SB (Claude ~39 BB) and be in a very uncomfortable spot. With a healthy 155 BB stack I should preserve chips and wait for better position/stronger holdings. Fold.

5-handed with Minni folded, A6o is a reasonable UTG+1 open. Raising pressures the blinds, leverages our ace blocker versus stronger Ax holdings, and avoids the EV leak of limping and playing a capped range multiway. I’ll use a standard-but-not-oversized open to keep worse hands in while still denying odds to the blinds.

I am holding 3s 4d on the button. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. FiveTwo has opened with a raise from UTG+1, and even though I have position, there is no strategic justification for calling or raising with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I have 8h9c in the small blind facing a UTG+1 raise to 250. This is an offsuit connector that plays poorly out of position. I'd be sandwiched between the raiser and the BB, and 89o doesn't have great implied odds or playability OOP. With my stack at 3925 (about 39 BBs), I should be selective about defending from the SB. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Opponent FiveTwo raises to 2.5x from the Cutoff (UTG+1 in 5-handed). I hold A6o in the Big Blind. The pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win a pot of 400), requiring only about 27% equity. Against a Cutoff opening range, A6o performs well enough to justify a defend, despite being out of position. I will call and see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad6h on a flop of 7s 2s 2h. The pot is 550. As the pre-flop defender on a paired board, the standard play is to check to the aggressor (FiveTwo). My hand has some showdown value with Ace-high, but betting out (donking) serves little purpose as it isolates me against better hands and folds out worse ones. I will check and evaluate based on FiveTwo's subsequent action.

We have A-high with the As blocker on a paired, low flop (7s-2s-2h). As the preflop raiser we retain a range/nut advantage (overpairs, strong Ax) and Pro’s BB defend contains lots of unpaired overcards that will fold to a small c-bet. Pro has shown relatively low aggression, so a small stab should win the pot often and deny equity without risking much. If check-raised, we can comfortably fold this hand.

I hold Ad6h on a 7s-2s-2h board. After checking, the opponent bets 200 into a pot of 550. This is a small c-bet (approx 36% pot). Given the paired and relatively unconnected board texture, the opponent is likely c-betting their entire range. My Ace-high has significant showdown value against their air (missed broadways like KQ, QJ, etc.), and I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 950, needing roughly 21% equity). Folding Ace-high here would be over-folding against a wide cutoff opening range. Raising is unnecessary as it isolates me against better hands. I will call to keep their bluffs in and re-evaluate on the turn.

I am out of position on the Turn with Ace-high (Ad6h) on a board of 7s-2s-2h-5s. The 5s is a scare card that completes the flush draw present on the flop. After check-calling the flop, the standard line is to check again to the aggressor. Leading out (donking) would serve no purpose as it likely folds out the bluffs I am beating while getting action only from better hands (flushes, trips, overpairs). Checking gives me the chance to see a free river if the opponent checks back, or evaluate their bet size if they continue aggression. My hand has some showdown value against missed broadway cards, so I want to keep the opponent's range wide.

Turn brings the third spade, giving us the nut-flush draw with the As plus decent fold equity versus Pro’s flop-calling range that contains many one-spade hands, underpairs (33–66), and some overcard floats. Pro checked again and has shown a generally passive line, so a second barrel should take it down often enough, while also building a pot we can win big when we hit a spade on the river. I’ll size around 60–65% pot to pressure 7x-less and non-spade pairs without overcommitting; if check-raised, I’m happy to fold because that line heavily polarizes to trips/boats/flushes against this opponent profile.

The turn card is the 5s, putting three spades on the board and completing possible flushes. The opponent fires a second barrel of 600 into a pot of 950. My hand is merely Ace-high with no flush draw (I hold the Ad, not the As). While I beat pure air bluffs, the opponent's range on this board texture includes made flushes, trips (2x), sets, overpairs, and semi-bluffs with a high spade. Against value hands, I am drawing nearly dead. Against semi-bluffs (e.g., KsQx), I am in a precarious spot often facing a river decision. With no spade in hand and weak showdown value, A6o is at the bottom of my range here. I should be continuing with pairs, sets, or hands holding the As. Folding is the prudent play against this aggression on a scary board.

5-handed but still UTG with T2o is far too weak: poor playability, dominated when we make a pair, and no real suited/connected equity to realize out of position. With competent/aggressive opponents behind, limping just invites isolation and raising is burning chips. Preserve stack and wait for a better open/steal spot from later position.

I am holding King-Jack of clubs, a strong suited connector/broadway hand that plays very well in a 5-handed game. With the largest stack at the table and being in middle position (effectively the Hijack), I should open for a standard raise to exert pressure on the shorter stacks (Claude and Pro) and build the pot with a premium hand. A 3x big blind raise to 300 is standard and helps narrow the field.

KJs on the button is a strong hand. Flash opened from UTG+1 to 300. With position and a premium suited broadway hand, I should 3-bet for value. This builds the pot when ahead and gives me initiative. A 3-bet to ~900 is standard sizing here.

I am holding 7h 5c (75 offsuit) in the Small Blind. The action has been opened by Flash to 300 and 3-bet by Claude on the button to 900. This indicates significant strength from the button or a positional play, but regardless, my hand is extremely weak (trash tier). I am out of position against two active players, one of whom has shown aggression with a re-raise. The pot odds do not justify a call, and a cold 4-bet bluff with this hand is ill-advised given the stack depths and the strength represented. The only correct play is to fold immediately.

I’m in the big blind with Qc7d facing an UTG open to 300 and a button 3-bet to 900. To call is 800 more into a 1,350 pot (final pot 2,150) so I’d need ~37% equity to break even. Q7o is a weak, easily dominated hand against a button 3‑bet range and plays poorly out of position — and there’s a real chance the original raiser peels, turning this into a multiway pot against two strong ranges. A light 4‑bet bluff is attractive only with blockers and fold equity; with this hand and Claude’s ~3,025 stack (able to shove/call), it’s too risky. Preserve chips for better spots — fold here.

I opened with King-Jack of clubs, a strong broadway hand in a 5-handed game. However, Claude's 3-bet from the button is significant given his 30-big-blind stack. If I call the 600, the pot will be nearly 2,000 with only 2,125 remaining in Claude's stack, creating a very low SPR situation where I will likely be forced to play for stacks on the flop. King-Jack is often dominated by a standard 3-betting range (AK, AJ, KQ, or JJ+). Given my significant chip lead, I choose to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than taking a high-variance, potentially dominated spot against a short stack who is effectively committing himself.

I am holding 9-3 offsuit in the first position (UTG) at a 5-handed table. This is a very weak hand with almost no post-flop playability. Given my stack size and position, there is no strategic reason to open this hand, especially with a short stack like Claude directly to my left who might be looking for a spot to shove. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

7h3h is a weak suited hand in UTG+1 position. This is outside my opening range from early position - suited connectors are more playable, but 73s has poor connectivity and low card values. With 44BB effective, I should stick to premium hands and solid broadways from this position. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with a weak hand (4d 6s), an unsuited one-gapper. While the Button is a strong position to steal blinds, 64o is too weak to open profitably, especially with deep-stacked opponents in the blinds (Minni and FiveTwo) who may defend frequently. There is no reason to risk chips with such low equity. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Only the big blind remains (Pro folded), so this is effectively heads-up preflop against FiveTwo. Kc3c is marginal but suited and has a K-blocker — playable as a small steal attempt from the small blind. I don’t want to simply limp and concede initiative (and let the BB see a cheap flop with position), nor fold a hand that can win the blinds. I’ll make the standard minimum open to 2x the big blind (200 total) to generate fold equity and keep the pot manageable if called. If FiveTwo 3-bets big I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously OOP and use backdoor club equity and the K-blocker to continue selectively.

Minni is opening from the small blind to a small sizing (2x), and I’m getting a very good price in the big blind: 100 more to win a pot that will be 400 after I call (need ~25% equity). Even though 2h4s is near-bottom and plays poorly, SB’s opening range should be very wide 5-handed, so my raw equity is likely enough and the cost is minimal. I’ll defend to avoid over-folding vs frequent SB opens, then play a mostly check-and-evaluate strategy postflop (avoid bloating pots, continue on boards where I pick up equity/stronger interaction).

I opened preflop to 200 and was called; pot is 400. Flop Jd Kh 2s gives me top pair with a very weak 3-kicker and backdoor club equity, and I’m out of position. As the preflop aggressor against a wide BB calling range, a standard c-bet is correct to get value, deny equity to overcards/draws and fold out some hands. I’ll size about 60–65% of the pot to 250 — big enough to apply pressure but small enough to keep the pot manageable with a weak kicker. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn (press with improvement or a club, otherwise control the pot); if he check-raises large (polarized) I’ll give up.

We flopped bottom pair (2s) with a weak kicker on K-J-2, so we’re rarely ahead of Minni’s value region (Kx/Jx/overpairs), but SB’s opening range is very wide 5-handed and this c-bet will include a lot of air/A-high and some medium pairs we can still be ahead of. Calling 250 to win 900 (after call) gives us a good price to realize equity in position and avoid over-folding vs frequent c-bets. Raising would mainly isolate us against better hands and bloat a pot with a marginal pair, so best is to call and play turn cautiously (mostly fold vs strong double barrels, continue when we improve or she slows down).

Check. The turn (6♥) is a blank for me — my backdoor club potential is gone and I’m left with top pair K with a very weak 3‑kicker. Villain cold‑called my 250 flop c‑bet, which narrows his continuing range toward many Kx combos with better kickers, Jx/22/sets and some hands that comfortably beat or are flipping with me. Betting again OOP here would bloat a 900 chip pot while getting value mainly from hands that already beat me or inviting sizable raises from better Ks/sets; pot‑control is the higher EV line. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs, and call small‑to‑medium turn/river bets to realize equity, but fold to large raises since my kicker is frequently dominated.

Turn goes check to me in a heads-up blind-vs-blind pot on K-J-2-6. With 2h4s I have a very marginal made hand (bottom pair, very weak kicker) that doesn’t want to build a pot. When Minni checks, her range contains some give-ups (A-high/Q-high) that I already beat, but also plenty of hands that will continue strongly vs a bet (Kx/Jx, better 2x like A2/K2/J2, and occasional traps/check-raises). Betting doesn’t get meaningful value from worse (there’s essentially no worse pair to pay me) and risks getting blown off my equity by a check-raise or bloating the pot when I’m often behind. Best is to take the free card, realize showdown value, and reassess river vs her sizing.

Check. I opened and c‑bet the flop (called), then controlled the pot by checking the turn. On this river (J♦ K♥ 2♠ 6♥ 8♥) I still have top pair with a very weak 3‑kicker. Villain’s line — calling the flop then checking turn and river — is consistent with Kx, Jx or medium strength hands; many of those hands beat me and few worse hands will reliably call a river bet. Betting would risk folding out the thin calls I want and may get raised or called by hands that dominate my kicker. The lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to check and take showdown rather than inflate the pot with a marginal value bet.

River is K-J-2-6-8 with hearts completing a backdoor flush possibility. With 2h4s we have a very weak made hand (bottom pair, worst kicker) and essentially no credible value target: worse hands (A-high/Q-high) will often just fold to any bet, while better hands (any Kx/Jx, most 2x, 6x/8x, and made hearts) can call or even check-raise. We also don’t hold useful blockers to strong hands, and turning this into a bluff is poor because Minni’s line (c-bet small, then check turn and river) contains a lot of medium-strength hands that will still bluff-catch vs small bets. Best is to take the free showdown and win against her give-ups without risking extra chips.

Pocket deuces in UTG with 44 BBs. Small pairs play best for set mining with implied odds. I'll open raise to 250 - standard open. If I get 3-bet I can fold, but if I get callers I can look to hit a set on the flop cheaply.

I hold 8s 4d, which is an extremely weak offsuit hand. Facing an opening raise from the UTG player (Claude) indicates strength, and I have several players left to act behind me. This is a trivial fold.

I have A♦K♠ on the button facing Claude’s UTG open to 250 in a 5‑handed game — a premium hand in position. I want to take the initiative, get value, and isolate Claude, so I’ll 3‑bet rather than just call. A standard 3‑bet to ~3.5x the open (900 total) applies pressure, folds out weaker holdings, and still allows me to comfortably call a shove from Claude (he’s relatively short at 4125). Postflop I’ll c‑bet favorable boards and pot‑control or check‑call on scary textures, re‑evaluating if the big blind or Claude 4‑bets aggressively.

Facing an UTG open and a Button 3-bet, Q4o is far too weak to continue—it's dominated by both players’ value and bluff ranges and plays terribly out of position from the small blind. Even with seemingly decent immediate pot odds, our equity realization is awful (often making weak pair/no kicker and getting pressured), and there’s also the risk of the BB coming along or squeezing, plus Claude can still back-raise/jam with his shorter stack. No profitable call or 4-bet bluff here.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. The action started with a raise to 250 from Claude (UTG) and a 3-bet to 900 from Minni on the Button. Q7o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a range that likely includes strong pairs and high Broadway cards. The pot odds are not favorable enough to justify a call, especially since I would be the first to act on subsequent streets against two opponents. Folding is the standard professional play here to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3-bet to 900 with pocket 2s. I need 650 to call into 1300 pot (2:1 odds). Set mining needs ~7.5:1 implied odds. With only ~3475 behind after calling into a ~1950 pot, SPR would be ~1.8 - poor for set mining. I'd need to extract significant value postflop to profit, but the stack depths don't support it. Fold is correct here.

I am in the UTG position with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Qd 2h), which is a statistically very weak hand. Given my position, I would need a much stronger hand to open the betting, as I have four opponents left to act behind me who could easily wake up with a better hand. There is no strategic merit in playing this hand from this position. The standard and optimal play is to fold immediately.

Ah-Qc in UTG+1 (5-handed) is a clear open — strong, high-card hand that plays well multiway but is best when I take the initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB): a standard sizing at this table that gives fold equity, discourages loose multiway calls, and keeps maneuvering room with my deep stack. Plan: c-bet favorable A/Q/high-card flops, pot-control on dangerous/connected boards, and re-evaluate vs 3-bets (call small 3-bets to see a flop; call a shove from the short-stacked BB (~40BB) but fold to very large, polarizing 3-bets from deep stacks).

Button vs UTG+1 3bb open, A8s is too strong to fold and plays very well as a position 3-bet: we block strong Ax, can win the pot pre often, and when called we have suited equity + good board coverage. Calling invites the blinds in (and BB is 40bb, which can create awkward multiway pots), while a 3-bet pressures Minni’s wide/active opening range and isolates in position. If Minni 4-bets, we can comfortably fold A8s given stacks and their shown willingness to apply pressure.

I am holding 9-3 offsuit in the Small Blind. There has been an initial raise to 300 from Minni (UTG+1) and a 3-bet to 900 from FiveTwo (Button). 9-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker and has no playability against a raise and a re-raise, especially out of position. This is an easy fold to preserve my chip lead.

Q2o is a very weak hand. Facing a raise and a 3-bet, I have no business calling 800 chips with such poor holdings. This is dominated by virtually all 3-betting ranges and has terrible playability postflop. Easy fold.

I opened UTG+1 to 300 with AhQc and the Button 3-bet to 900. AQo is too strong to fold to a standard 3-bet but not strong enough to 4-bet for value out of position against a button’s likely polarized/value-heavy 3-bet range. I’ll call the 600 more (900 total) to keep the pot controllable, realize equity and see a flop OOP. Postflop plan: c-bet favorable A/Q/high-card flops, pot-control or check on wet/connected runouts, and fold to heavy continued aggression if I miss. If FiveTwo turns this into a very large/polarized 4-bet or jams, I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold.

I opened and then flat-called the button’s 3-bet with AhQc to see a flop OOP. Flop (6s 7d Jd) is J-high with two diamonds — somewhat favorable for a c-bet as it contains a high card and many 3-bettor bluffs/missed broadway combos will miss. I have two overcards and block some Ace combos, but no diamond draw and no pair, so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP. Make a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) to take the initiative, fold out hands that missed, and keep the pot manageable if I get called. If I face a large raise I’ll fold (I’m not going to commit with just Ace-high); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn for improvement or pot-control.

Minni is donk-leading small (650 into ~1950), which gives me excellent immediate price (~20%) and keeps their range wide (TT–99, some diamond floats, occasional Jx/protection bets). With As8s I’m unpaired but I have: (1) A-high showdown value vs parts of that range, (2) strong turn playability in position (any spade gives me a real nut-flush draw, A/8 improve), and (3) the ability to take it away on later streets when they slow down. Raising here with this specific hand is unnecessary (I’d isolate myself vs stronger continues), while folding is too tight given the sizing and positional advantage. Plan: call and respond aggressively on favorable turns (spade/A/8) and be cautious versus big barrels on bricks.

I have AhQc; I opened and then flat-called the button’s 3-bet, c‑bet a small ~650 on the J‑high flop and got called. The King on the turn substantially improves the 3‑bettor’s continuing range (Kx, KQ, KJ, many pocket pairs) and turns my hand into only Ace‑high — I block some A combos but I no longer have real showdown value OOP. With a healthy stack and a 3‑bettor who called the flop, I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Pot control is best: check the turn, allow bluffs to manifest, and plan to call a small/medium bet as a bluff‑catcher but fold to large raises or shoves. If FiveTwo checks back we go to the river and re‑evaluate. Action: check.

Minni’s flop donk-lead was small and now they’ve checked this turn in a 3-bet pot, which heavily caps their range toward one-pair/marginal hands (7x, 88–TT, some weak Jx) and diamond floats. The Kh is an overcard that’s good for my perceived 3-bet range (AK/KQ/AJ) and pressures their Jx/medium pairs. With As8s I don’t have a real draw, so checking back just invites a tough river spot; instead I’ll take the initiative and try to fold out the bulk of their range while charging diamond draws. I’ll use a controlled sizing to keep risk reasonable and still generate folds.

Turn Kx completed the board and I checked as planned. Villain (Button) now bets 1,250 — a relatively small/medium-size bet into the current pot. I hold A♥Q♣: only Ace-high, no diamond draw, but I block some A combos and have decent showdown blocking value. Raising OOP here with no pair would be a pure bluff and I don’t want to bloat the pot against a player who called my flop c-bet. Folding is too nitty given the price — the bet is roughly one‑third (or less) of the pot, giving good pot odds to call as a bluff‑catcher and realize equity if I improve on the river. Plan: call 1,250 to see the river, re-evaluate if I pair or face a large river bet (fold to big river jams; call small/medium river bets selectively).

Recap: I opened UTG+1 with A♥Q♣, villain (Button) 3‑bet and I flat called. I c‑bet a small 650 on the J‑high, got called, checked the K turn and called his 1250 — line consistent with Kx, Jx, some pocket pairs or missed diamond draws. The river 9♠ is a blank for my hand; I have only Ace‑high. Evaluation: I block some A and Q combos but have no pair or draw and I’m out of position. Betting as a bluff into a 3‑bettor who has shown willingness to continue is thin EV — many hands in his range (Kx, Jx, pairs) will call a river bluff, and getting raised would force a clear fold. With good pot odds to check to showdown and avoid bloating the pot with air, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to check back and see his showdown action. Action: check (0).

River comes 9s and Minni checks after donk/calling flop and check-calling the K turn in a 3-bet pot. My actual hand (As8s) has essentially no showdown value here (we lose to any pair and even some missed AdXd floats beat A8), so checking back is too passive.

Range-wise, this runout (Kh turn) is very good for my perceived 3-bet range (AK/KQ/AA/KK/KJ/JJ), while Minni’s line looks capped toward one-pair hands (Jx, QQ–TT/88) and missed diamonds that are now giving up. The 9s river doesn’t meaningfully strengthen most of those bluff-catchers, and I also block T8 (a key straight that could comfortably call), making a bluff a bit cleaner.

I’ll apply pressure with a strong, polar sizing that targets folds from Jx/medium pairs and all missed draws, while expecting to get called mostly by Kx+ and sets.

Recap: I have A♥Q♣ on 6♠7♦J♦ / K♥ / 9♠. Pot is 10,050 and FiveTwo fires a large river bet of 4,300 — calling 4,300 would be to win 14,350, so I need ≈30% equity. Range/read: Button 3‑bet preflop, called my flop c‑bet and bet the turn — a line heavily weighted toward Kx, Jx and pocket pairs that now beat my Ace‑high. The turn/river sizing and the prior action make a high bluff frequency unlikely; I have no diamond and only block a handful of A/Q combos, not nearly enough to justify a hero call. Per my earlier plan (fold to big river jams with only Ace‑high), this is a fold — avoid donating more chips with essentially air.

UTG in a 5-handed game with 5♥8♣ is too weak to open or limp. This hand is unsuited, low and disconnected — it plays poorly out of position and performs badly in multi-way pots. There’s no raise to punish, but limping here simply invites aggressive actions from the button and blinds (Flash is very active, Pro defends the big blind), and Claude (SB) is short-stacked which increases shove/squeeze risk. With a healthy stack (13450) I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better, position-friendly spot. Fold.

Qs3s is too weak/dominated to open from UTG+1 even 5-handed, especially with an aggressive button who can 3-bet and shorter blinds who can defend/shove. There’s little postflop playability when called and we don’t need to force thin steals with a healthy stack. Preserve chips and wait for better spots.

I am on the button and it has been folded to me. Ten-Eight offsuit is a standard opening hand in a 5-handed game from this position to attempt to steal the blinds. While Claude in the Small Blind is relatively short-stacked (approx. 40 BBs), a 2.5x raise is high enough to discourage a defend with marginal hands but low enough to fold comfortably if faced with a shove. I will raise to 250.

Q2o is a very weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button raise. Even though the button likely has a wide stealing range, Q2o has poor playability and will struggle postflop out of position. This is a clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with T3o facing a button raise to 2.5bb from a large stack. The pot odds are decent (calling 150 to win 550, requiring ~27% equity), but T3o is one of the worst hands in the deck, with poor playability and domination issues. Being out of position with a trash hand against an aggressive stack is a losing proposition. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

5-handed from UTG we need to open fairly tight. 2♠3♦ offsuit has very poor playability, is dominated when it makes a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position versus multiple opponents. Limping invites isolation and creates reverse-implied-odds spots; raising is burning chips. Preserve stack for better steal/3-bet opportunities.

I am holding King-high suited (Kc 4c) in a 5-handed game. While the kicker is weak, King-high suited hands have good playability post-flop and are generally strong enough to open from middle position in short-handed play. I have the largest stack at the table, allowing me to apply pressure on the shorter stacks, specifically Claude and Pro. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 chips to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately.
Holding 2d8s on the button facing a raise to 300. This is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high card value. With ~40 BBs, I need to be selective. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 3h, a very weak offsuit hand. The player in UTG+1 (Flash) has opened to 300 (3BB). Even though Flash has been active, calling from the Small Blind with 53o would be a significant mistake due to being out of position with a hand that has very poor playability and equity. The pot odds do not justify a call. Folding is the only reasonable option.

Fold. Facing an early-position open to 300 in a 5‑handed game, Flash’s opening range is relatively strong. I’m in the big blind out of position with 4♠5♥ offsuit — a low, offsuit connector that plays poorly post‑flop and is often dominated. The pot is 450 and to call 200 I’d need ~30.8% equity to break even; vs an UTG+1 opening range my true equity is likely below that once position and frequent c‑bets are considered. A min‑raise to 500 is a weak bluff here (I’ll get called or 4‑bet by better hands) and would commit unnecessary chips. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 7c 8c UTG in a 5-handed game. This is a very playable suited connector that benefits from being played aggressively to realize its potential equity. As the current chip leader, I want to continue applying pressure to the shorter stacks behind me, particularly Claude and Pro. While I have opened the last two hands, 78s is a standard opening hand in this position at a short-handed table. I will raise to 300 to stay consistent with my previous betting patterns and take control of the pot early.

Ah Td is a decent hand facing a UTG open. With my stack at ~40BB, I can call and see a flop in position relative to the raiser. 3-betting would be too aggressive given stack depth and hand strength. Folding is too tight - ATo has good playability. Calling to see a flop is the best option.

I hold 6h 2c on the button, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Facing a raise from UTG (Flash) and a call from UTG+1 (Claude), I am up against ranges that completely dominate my hand. My equity is very poor, and there are no pot odds or strategic reasons to enter this pot multi-way. Folding is the only correct decision.

Facing an UTG raise to 300 and a call from UTG+1, I'm in the small blind with J♠5♣ — weak, offsuit, and poorly connected. Calling 250 to play multiway out of position against deep stacks is negative EV, and a light squeeze with this hand is too risky given the raiser and caller. Preserve my 13,300 stack for better spots and fold.

Pocket sixes are a solid defend in the big blind getting a great price: I’m calling 200 more to win a pot of 750 (need ~21% equity), with strong implied odds vs Flash’s deep stack if I flop a set. A squeeze here risks bloating the pot out of position versus an UTG raiser, and it can induce a short-stack Claude jam that I don’t love calling with 66. Keep the pot manageable and realize equity multiway.

Multiway pot on 3c-8s-2s with 66: we have a medium underpair with some showdown value but not strong enough to lead into the UTG raiser and a caller. Donk-betting just isolates us versus better (8x, overpairs) and denies ourselves the chance to realize equity cheaply. Best is to check, let Flash c-bet his range, and then continue versus a small sizing while comfortably folding to heavy pressure (especially with Claude’s short stack behind).

On a flop of 3c 8s 2s, I have top pair (eights) with a 7 kicker. This is a strong hand on such a low, relatively dry board. FiveTwo checked from the big blind, indicating weakness or a desire to trap. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have top pair, I should bet for value and to protect against overcards or potential spade draws. A bet of around half the pot is appropriate here to extract value from middle pairs or draws while keeping the pot manageable against potential sets. I will bet 500 into the 950 pot.

I completely whiffed this flop with Ah Td. Board is 3c 8s 2s - I have just ace high with no pair and no draw. Flash is betting 500 into a 450 pot, showing strength. With about 37BB left and no equity on this board, calling or raising would be burning chips. Easy fold.

Flash’s 1/3-pot c-bet (500 into 1450) is a classic range bet from UTG in 5-handed; it contains a lot of unpaired big cards (AK/AQ/KQ, etc.) that we’re currently ahead of with 66. We’re getting a good price (500 to win ~1950 after we call), and folding a hand with this much immediate showdown value would be too tight. Raising doesn’t make sense: we don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a medium underpair and we’d mostly isolate ourselves versus 8x/overpairs or spade-heavy continues. Plan: call once, then play turns cautiously—continue mainly on low bricks versus small barreling, and fold to significant turn pressure or on very bad runouts (A/K/Q with bigger sizing, or heavy spade aggression).

Turn brings the As, which is a major range-advantage card for Flash’s UTG opening range (a lot of Ax improves past our 66). Our direct showdown value drops, but holding the 6♠ gives us some backup equity to make a flush on a spade river. Leading doesn’t accomplish much (we rarely get better to fold, and we risk getting raised off our equity), so the best line is to check and play a bluff-catch/realize-equity strategy versus his sizing—continue only versus small bets and fold to heavy pressure.

The Ace of Spades is a highly significant card that changes the texture of the board considerably. It completes any spade flush draws that FiveTwo might have called with on the flop and hits many Ace-high hands that a player in the Big Blind would defend with. While I have second pair, my hand's relative strength has decreased. Checking back here is the most prudent play to control the pot size, realize my equity, and avoid being forced to fold to a potential check-raise on such a coordinated board.

River pairs the Ace, giving us Aces and sixes (two pair). That’s a clear value hand versus the big part of Flash’s range that checked back the turn: broadways/spade draws that now just have one pair (AA on board) and some medium pairs (44–KK) that are worse than our two pair. At the same time, when Flash has real strength (any Ax for trips, 8x/3x for a higher two pair, or big pairs like KK/QQ that beat 66), he can raise. So the best play OOP is a small block/value bet that gets called by worse one-pair hands and controls the river sizing; if raised, we can comfortably fold because that line is heavily weighted to better hands.

On the river board of 3c 8s 2s As Ac, I hold Aces and Eights with a 7 kicker. FiveTwo, who called my flop bet and checked the turn, has led for 650 into a 1950 pot (about 1/3 pot). The board pairing the Ace is interesting; if FiveTwo had a spade flush, the board pairing potentially reduces his relative strength if I have an Ace. However, his lead is small enough that it could be a blocker bet with a weaker 8, a small pocket pair, or a missed draw (like 54 or 43) trying to steal the pot. While I am beaten by any Ace, any flush, or a better 8, the pot odds are excellent (5:1), and FiveTwo has shown a willingness to be aggressive and bluff in previous hands (e.g., Hand #63). Folding 2nd pair on a double-paired board to such a small bet given the odds would be too tight. Raising would only get called by hands that beat me. Therefore, a call is the most profitable decision to catch bluffs or thin value bets.

6s3s is a very weak hand to open from UTG position. Even though it's suited, it has poor playability and I'm out of position against 4 players. With a stack of ~37bb, I need to be selective. This is a clear fold.

I am holding 2s 5h in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with 5 players remaining. This is an extremely weak hand (unsuited, low rank, disconnected) with virtually no playability. With a stack of 72 BBs, there is no pressure to make desperate moves, and opening with this hand would be a significant mistake. The only standard and profitable play is to fold.

I’m on the button with Js7s (deep stack ~133bb). This is a good spot to open-raise: the hand has suitedness and decent postflop playability in position, and there’s strong fold equity vs the small and big blind. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — big enough to discourage easy cold-calls/squeezes that a min-raise invites, but small enough to keep pots manageable if called. Plan: use position to C-bet favorable flops, realize equity on coordinated/flush-draw boards, and be prepared to fold to a large 3-bet from Flash while considering calls vs small 3-bets from FiveTwo.

We’re in the small blind, out of position, facing a Button 3x open. AKo is a clear value 3-bet here: we want to build the pot versus Minni’s wide opening range, deny equity, and—importantly—make it harder for the big blind (deep-stacked Flash) to come along and realize position in a multiway pot. Calling invites a squeeze and plays a tougher pot OOP. I’ll use a standard SB vs BTN sizing around 4x the open.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c-3d, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni opened from the button and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1200. Given the strength indicated by a 3-bet from the small blind and my poor hand quality and position, this is a very easy fold. I have a healthy stack and do not need to take unnecessary risks with garbage hands.

I opened to 300 on the button with Js7s and FiveTwo (SB) 3-bet to 1200. Js7s is playable in position — suited, has backdoor flush/straight potential and can realize equity postflop. Calling 900 more (1200 total invested) gets me ~36% equity to break even (900 to win ~2500), which is reasonable against a potentially widened SB 3-bet range. A 4-bet to 2100 would be a thin bluff and would bloat the pot OOP vs a competent opponent; folding surrenders good positional leverage with a playable hand. Plan: call, see flop in position, C-bet favorable textures (spade/connected/J-high boards), control pot on dry boards, and fold to heavy action if I miss or face very strong resistance. If FiveTwo 4-bets, I’ll re-evaluate but lean to folding Js7s.

3-bet pot BTN vs SB: we retain a range advantage on Q-7-2 (we have AA/KK/QQ/AQ/KQ more often), and AK has two overcards plus some backdoor equity. A small c-bet pressures Minni’s floats/underpairs (like JTs, T9s, 88–TT) and defines the hand while keeping our range protected. If raised, we can comfortably fold; if called, we can continue on A/K turns and slow down on bad runouts.

I have Js7s on 2c Qs 7d — middle pair (7s) with a Jack kicker and backdoor spade possibilities. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop so his range is weighted to strong hands but he can also be continuation-betting a lot of missed overcards; his 700 into a 3200 pot gives me ~18% pot odds to call. Calling is cheap and correct in position: I have showdown value, decent equity to improve (~24–30% to two‑pair/trips/flush by river), and can control the pot and use position on the turn. A raise here would be a thin bluff that will often be called by better hands, so I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn (bet/raise if I improve, check‑fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss).

Turn is a relative brick for ranges (4d doesn’t materially improve most of Minni’s flop-calling hands), and in a BTN vs SB 3-bet pot we still retain a meaningful range advantage (AA/KK/AQ/KQ more often). With AKo we have poor showdown value after flop call, but we do have 6 clean outs to top pair and we can generate folds from a chunk of her continuing range (88–JJ without strong diamonds, some AJs/KJs/JTs floats). A medium second barrel also denies equity to hands with overcards/backdoors and sets up a credible river shove line on A/K or good runouts. If we get raised, we can comfortably fold this combo.

I have Js7s on the button: second pair with a decent Jack kicker and a small backdoor spade possibility. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop so his range is weighted to strong hands, but he’s also shown willingness to c‑bet and pick on missed flops; his turn wager of 1800 into a 5700 pot gives me ~24% equity required to call (1800/(5700+1800) ≈ 24%). That’s inexpensive to realize position and showdown value against a mixed 3‑betting/c‑betting range. Raising here would be a thin, bloating play that often sees calls from better hands or a shove; folding throws away a lot of equity. I’ll call 1800, keep the pot controllable, and re‑evaluate on the river: if I improve (J/7/spade) I’ll start extracting value; if I miss and face big river aggression I’ll fold to strong lines from a capped 3‑bettor.

River pairs the 4 and we still have Ace-high. After Minni calls both a flop c-bet and a turn barrel in a 3-bet pot, her range is heavily weighted to Qx (AQ/KQ/QJ suited), some slowplayed sets (QQ/77/22), and a smaller portion of missed diamond draws. The 4h doesn’t meaningfully weaken her made hands, and our Kd blocks some of the natural diamond-draw floats that would fold, reducing our bluff’s fold equity. A third barrel—especially for a size that matters—gets called too often by Qx and better, while only folding out the thinner portion of her range. Checking keeps us from lighting off a low-equity bluff, and gives missed diamonds a chance to check back (or bluff, which we can evaluate based on sizing).

River: board 2c Qs 7d 4d 4h, I hold Js7s — second pair with a Jack kicker. FiveTwo (the preflop 3‑better) bet flop and turn but checked the river. That line smells like pot‑control or a missed draw rather than a hand I can thin‑value‑bet profitably. Many hands that beat me (Qx, better 7x, sets/trips) are in his 3‑betting/CB range and will either check back or call a value bet; worse hands that would fold to a river bet are rare. Betting here risks being called or raised by dominators and gains little value from worse. With no bet to face, the least risky, highest EV line is to check to showdown and preserve my stack.

I am holding Pocket Tens (Tc Ts) in UTG position, which is a premium pair strong enough to open for value. With a stack of over 72 BBs, I should raise to build the pot and define opponent ranges rather than limping. A standard open size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is appropriate to deny equity to random hands in the blinds while balancing my opening range.

Pro opened UTG to 250 (early position) and I’m UTG+1 with 9♥7♥. This is a marginal, speculative hand that plays poorly out of position against an UTG opening range. Calling 250 into a 400 pot commits significant chips to a multiway/OO P situation where I’ll often be dominated postflop; the BB (Claude) is short and could shove, creating an ugly decision. A 3-bet bluff here is too loose vs an UTG opener. I’ll preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Button vs UTG open in a 5-handed game, Q2s is still too weak and heavily dominated by Pro’s continuing range (all better queens, most broadways, many suited aces). Calling creates reverse implied odds and doesn’t realize equity well even in position, and 3-bet bluffing risks getting flatted by Flash in the blinds or jammed on by Claude’s shorter stack, forcing us into a bad spot with minimal blockers. With a healthy stack, we don’t need to take a marginal -EV peel here.

Pro raised from UTG, which typically represents a strong range in a 5-handed game. Ace-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an UTG opener. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting against UTG with this hand is too loose. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I have As9s in the BB facing a UTG raise to 250. Suited ace is playable here - I have good implied odds with nut flush potential. UTG raise suggests strength, but calling 150 more to see a flop in position relative to the raiser is reasonable. 3-betting is an option but calling is the safer play with a hand that plays well postflop.

Missed the flop completely - I have As9s on a 6c Th 3h board. No pair, no flush draw (hearts on board, I have spades). Only ace high with backdoor possibilities. Against a UTG raiser, checking to see what they do is the standard play. No reason to bluff into someone who showed preflop strength.

I have flopped top set (Tens) on a board with a flush draw (6c Th 3h). This is a very strong hand. My opponent Claude is in the Big Blind and likely has a wide range that includes flush draws, pairs (6x, 3x), or perhaps a Ten (though I block those). I unblock hearts, increasing the likelihood he has a flush draw. Checking back risks giving a free card to a draw or missing value from a worse hand. I want to build the pot to potentially get stacks in by the river. A bet of roughly half the pot (275) is a standard continuation bet size that extracts value from draws and pairs while not folding out his entire range.

Missed the flop completely - As9s on 6c Th 3h gives me just ace high with no flush draw (wrong suit). Pro c-bet 275 into 550 from UTG. I have ~6 outs to pair my ace but I'm out of position against likely a strong hand (UTG opener). No real draws, just overcards. The 3:1 pot odds aren't enough for ace high against a range that likely has me dominated. Fold is the disciplined play.

5♥7♣ is a very weak, off‑suit hand with poor postflop playability and lots of domination by the ranges behind me (Ax, broadways, pocket pairs). Even though calling 100 is cheap, I’ll be out of position against 4 opponents and likely face raises from aggressive players (Flash, FiveTwo) — that yields negative EV and reverse implied odds. With a deep stack I can pick much better spots later; this is not one of them. I’ll fold and conserve chips for profitable opportunities.

5-handed and first in after UTG folds, ATo is strong enough to open for value and as a steal. Raising pressures the blinds, leverages our ace blocker versus 3-bets, and avoids limping a hand that plays poorly multiway. I’ll use a slightly larger-than-min open to reduce loose calls from the button/blinds and take initiative.

I am on the button with 3d 4d. While the hand is suited and connected, it is very weak (lowest possible straight/flush potential). FiveTwo raised from early position (UTG+1), suggesting a stronger range than mine. Although I have the largest stack at the table and position, calling a raise with 3-4 suited against an early position open is generally unprofitable. Furthermore, the small blind (Claude) has a short stack and might look to shove, squeezing me out of the pot. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K5o is a weak hand, especially out of position against a UTG+1 raise. This hand doesn't play well postflop and lacks the equity to profitably call or 3-bet. With a stack of ~34BB, I should be more selective. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s5s, a suited connector that plays well post-flop. Facing a standard 2.5x open (250) from FiveTwo in UTG+1. The pot odds are favorable: I need to call 150 to play for a total pot of 550, getting roughly 2.7:1 odds (requires ~27% equity). 6s5s has solid equity and playability against an opening range, allowing me to flop straights, flushes, or draws. Although I will be out of position, the stack depth (74 BB) allows for post-flop maneuverability. Folding here would be too tight given the price, and 3-betting is unnecessary and risky OOP. Therefore, calling is the optimal play.

I have flopped a flush draw on a board of Qs-8c-2s with 6s-5s. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, FiveTwo. The board texture favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Queen-high hands (AQ, KQ, QJ) and overpairs. Leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal here as it allows the opponent to play perfectly against my range. My plan is to check to the aggressor. If they bet, I can decide between check-calling or check-raising based on their sizing, as my flush draw gives me significant equity to continue. For now, the standard action is to check.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser, this Q-high flop favors my UTG+1 range (AQ/KQ/QQ more often than BB). With A♠T♦ I have two overcards plus the A♠ giving strong backdoor equity on spade turns. A small c-bet pressures BB’s whiffs and underpairs without bloating the pot too much; if I face a check-raise I can comfortably fold.

I hold 6s5s on a Qs-8c-2s board, giving me a flush draw. The opponent (FiveTwo) has bet 200 into a pot of 550, offering me excellent pot odds of roughly 4.75:1 (calling 200 to win 950). My equity with the flush draw is approximately 36%, which is well above the ~21% equity required to break even on a call. Raising is a viable semi-bluff option, but given the small bet size, calling is a low-variance play that keeps the opponent's bluffs in the range and realizes my equity cheaply. I will call to see the turn.

I have turned a flush with 6s5s on a board of Qs-8c-2s-4s. While I have a strong hand, it is a low flush (6-high), leaving me vulnerable to higher flushes if the opponent holds a higher spade. The opponent has shown aggression pre-flop and on the flop. Checking here is the standard line for several reasons: 1) It disguises the strength of my hand, potentially inducing bluffs from the opponent who might perceive the 4s as a blank. 2) It allows the opponent to bet with hands like Top Pair (Qx) or sets that might otherwise fold to a lead. 3) Leading out (donk betting) often forces folds from hands I beat while building a pot against better flushes. I intend to check and evaluate the opponent's action; if they bet, I will likely call to control the pot size and avoid getting blown off my hand by a raise.

Turn brings the 3rd spade, giving me the nut flush draw with A♠ plus a very strong blocker to villain’s strongest flushes.
