After Pro check-calls a small flop c-bet and then checks again, this is a great card to barrel: it credibly improves my UTG+1 range (A♠X♠, K♠X♠, etc.) and puts immediate pressure on Qx/8x without a spade and pocket pairs that hate this texture. I’ll use a solid sizing to generate folds while still keeping my risk controlled; if I get check-raised, population tendency here is heavily value (made flushes/sets), and my equity vs that range isn’t enough to continue, so I’m bet/folding. If called, I can value-bet spade rivers hard and choose selective blocker bluffs on bricks.

I have turned a flush with 6s5s on a board of Qs-8c-2s-4s. While a flush is a strong hand, mine is only 6-high, which is vulnerable to any higher spade in the opponent's range (As, Ks, Js, Ts, 9s, 7s). The opponent (FiveTwo) has bet 600 into a pot of 950 (~63% pot), showing significant strength. Raising here would likely only get action from higher flushes, while folding out hands I beat like sets or Top Pair. Calling is the prudent play to control the pot size, keep the opponent's bluffs in their range, and realize my equity without overcommitting against a potentially superior flush.

I hold a 6-high flush (6s5s) on a board of [Qs 8c 2s 4s 3h]. There are three spades on the board, so my flush is valid but vulnerable to any higher spade (7s+). The opponent has shown significant aggression, betting the flop and turn. Leading out (donk betting) on the river is unlikely to get called by worse hands (like sets or top pair) given the scary board texture, and it opens me up to being raised by higher flushes, putting me in a difficult spot. Checking is the optimal line: it allows me to potentially induce a bluff from missed draws or catch a value bet from a hand like a set or two pair that might think I have a missed draw. If the opponent checks back, I likely win against their non-flush range. If they bet, I will evaluate the sizing, but for now, checking is the correct first move.

River bricks and we miss the nut flush draw, so we have no showdown value vs Pro’s double-call range (lots of Qx/8x and pairs with a spade). However, this runout is heavily polar: after I c-bet flop and barreled the 3rd spade on the turn, I credibly represent many two-spade holdings that make a flush. Holding A♠ is an excellent blocker to Pro’s strongest flushes (and reduces the chance he can comfortably bluff-catch), making this a high-quality third-barrel bluff. I’ll choose a strong sizing that puts one-pair hands (especially Qx without a spade) in a tough spot while still being efficient for fold equity.

I hold a 6-high flush on a board of Qs-8c-2s-4s-3h. Facing a large river bet of 1600 into 2150 (approx. 75% pot) after the opponent fired on the flop and turn. The opponent (FiveTwo) raised from UTG+1, so their range includes many higher flushes (AsXs, KsXs, JTs, etc.) and sets. A 6-high flush is the absolute bottom of my flush range here; I lose to any spade 7s or higher. The opponent's large sizing on the river suggests a polarized range of strong flushes or bluffs. While I beat bluffs and potentially overplayed sets, sets would typically check or bet smaller on this 3-flush board. Since I unblock the opponent's value flushes and beat only bluffs, and considering this is a triple-barrel line from an early position opener, calling is too risky. Folding preserves my stack of roughly 65BB.

5-handed UTG plays closer to a middle position open, and K9o is just inside a reasonable chip-EV opening range. Raising is better than limping because it lets us realize fold equity, keeps initiative, and avoids getting put in tough spots vs an isolation raise. I’ll use a standard small open that’s consistent with prior sizing; if we face a 3-bet (especially from the big stacks or a short-stack shove from Claude), we can comfortably fold and preserve stack.

I am holding 7h 3h in UTG+1 position. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from UTG. 73 suited is a very weak hand and does not play well against an UTG opening range, especially when I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against the raiser and still have three players left to act behind me. There is no reason to commit chips to this pot.

I have 2s3c - one of the worst hands in poker. Even with button position, calling an UTG raise with 23 offsuit is burning chips. My stack is 3375 (34 BBs), so I need to protect it for better spots. Easy fold.

I hold J3o in the Small Blind, facing a raise from FiveTwo in UTG. This is a very weak hand that is dominated by the opener's range. Being out of position and lacking both connectivity and suitedness, I cannot profitably call. The pot odds do not justify defending with this trash hand. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Facing an UTG open to 250 from FiveTwo while I'm in the big blind with T♥7♣. Pot is 400 and I must call 150 more (total 250) — about 27% pot odds. In a 5‑handed game an UTG opener is a relatively strong range and my T7o is frequently dominated by his Tx/Ax/Kx/broadway hands and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. 3‑betting as a bluff here is thin and unnecessary given deep stacks and live tournament structure — better to wait for hands with more equity or positional advantage. Fold and conserve chips for better spots.

I am in the UTG position with 98o, which is a weak hand to open with, especially from early position. Even in a 5-handed game, 98o lacks the equity and playability needed to justify a raise. Given my current chip lead and the tournament progress (hand 71 of 150), there is no reason to get involved in a pot with a sub-par hand from the worst position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
9d2d is a weak hand even suited. In UTG+1 position with a short stack of ~34BB, this is not playable. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with Ace-Queen offsuit (Ah Qc), which is a premium starting hand. The action has folded to me. This is a clear opening raise situation to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a strong hand. A standard raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is appropriate given the stack depths (64 BB effective for me).

4♦8♠ is a very weak, unsuited hand and I'm out of position to the button. Pro opened to 250 into a 400 pot so calling 200 only gets me ~33% immediate pot odds, but 4‑8o has far less equity vs a standard button opening range and is frequently dominated. There's also the live big blind behind who may defend and turn this into a multiway pot where my hand plays even worse. Three‑betting this holding would be a pure bluff with little fold equity and unnecessary risk. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Button open to 2.5bb is likely quite wide heads-up vs blinds, and we’re getting a good price in the BB (150 to win a pot that will be 550). 9-5o isn’t a premium defend, but it has enough raw equity versus a wide BTN range and can realize reasonably well in positionless spots by keeping the pot small and playing fit-or-fold on many flops. A 3-bet bluff risks bloating the pot OOP against a shorter stack who can respond aggressively, so the higher-EV line is to defend via call.

Heads-up BB vs BTN on a low, disconnected 6-2-3 rainbow flop, the preflop raiser will c-bet a lot with overcards, and our BB range contains more 2x/3x/6x/sets. With 9c5h we have very little showdown value but do have a clean gutshot to the 4; it’s not strong enough to justify donk-leading for value, and it’s not a great check-raise candidate (few blockers, thin equity if called, and we’d bloat the pot OOP vs a shorter stack). Best is to check and let Pro bet; then we can decide to call small bets with the gutshot or fold vs larger sizing/pressure.

The board texture 6s 2h 3d is very dry and low, which generally favors the pre-flop raiser's (my) range as I retain all high overpairs. While I missed the board, Ace-Queen high is still strong relative to the Big Blind's wide defense range. A small continuation bet is the standard play here to deny equity to random overcards and fold out weak hands that missed completely. Checking back is a valid alternative to pot control, but betting allows me to dictate the action and potentially take down the pot now. A small sizing (around 1/3 pot) is sufficient on this static board.

Pro’s 200 c-bet into 750 is a small range-bet that he’ll have with lots of overcards and air. With 9c5h we have a clean gutshot to the 4 (4 outs) and can also realize some equity/implied odds versus his wide button range, especially given the favorable price: 200 to win 950 (~21% needed). Raising is unattractive (no blockers, low fold equity vs his continue range, and we bloat OOP), while folding gives up a profitable float. Plan: call now, then continue on 4x turns; otherwise mostly check/fold vs bigger turn pressure and consider selective stabs if he checks back.

Turn is 6-2-3-8 with 9c5h: we still only have a gutshot to the 4 (4 outs) and essentially no showdown value. As BB, our range is stronger on these low boards, but this particular combo isn’t a good donk/value hand and isn’t a great bluff to inflate the pot with OOP (no relevant blockers, low equity when called, and Pro can apply pressure with position). Best is to keep the pot controlled and realize our equity: check and respond based on sizing—continue only versus small bets with correct price, otherwise fold.

I hold Ah Qc on a board of 6s 2h 3d 8d. After raising pre-flop and c-betting the flop, the Big Blind called. The turn 8d brings a potential flush draw and improves some of the BB's range (like 8x or straights with 45/57). My Ace-high still has showdown value, but betting again is risky as it likely only gets called by better hands (pairs) or strong draws, and folds out hands I'm already beating. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, trying to hit an Ace or Queen on the river, or potentially get to showdown cheaply with Ace-high. Pot control is the priority here.

River comes 6-2-3-8-5 and we improve from a pure gutshot float into a weak made hand (pair of 5s, 9 kicker). This has some showdown value versus Pro’s turn-check range (lots of A/K/Q-high give-ups and missed overcards). Betting turns our hand into a thin/blocking bet that mostly gets called by better pairs (6x/8x/77–TT/A5 type hands) and folds out hands we already beat, while also exposing us to a raise OOP. Checking keeps the pot controlled and gives Pro room to bluff missed overcards, which we can often pick off depending on sizing.

I hold Ace-Queen high on a final board of 6s 2h 3d 8d 5c. After c-betting the flop and getting called, I checked back the turn for pot control. The river 5c is a bad card for me as it completes a straight for any 4x (which is well within the Big Blind's defense range) and pairs up any 5x holdings. My opponent's line of check-calling the flop and checking down is consistent with a small pair or a draw that may have just made a straight or a pair of fives. While I lose to any pair, bluffing is risky because the low board connects well with the BB's range, and they may station with any pair given my sign of weakness on the turn. I will check back to realize my showdown value against missed random floats or busted diamond draws, accepting that I likely lose to a pair but saving chips.
Q8o is too weak to open from UTG. With 34BB effective stack and 4 players behind me, I need a stronger range here. Folding is the clear play.

I am in UTG+1 with T3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity. With three players yet to act behind me and no chips invested in the pot, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

I have 8♥9♥ on the button with deep stacks (≈170bb). This is a playable, positionally advantaged suited connector — raising isolates the blinds, builds a pot I can play postflop with initiative, and buys fold equity against the SB/BB. I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total) — large enough to pressure the blinds but small enough to keep worse hands in. Plan: if I face a single, moderate 3-bet I will largely continue in position and evaluate on the flop; if I face a very large 3-bet that commits a big portion of my stack I will fold to avoid marginal, bloated situations.

Minni opens to 3bb on the button and we’re in the small blind with 3h4c (offsuit). This hand has very poor raw equity and plays terribly out of position with the big blind still to act, creating a high chance of going multiway and facing squeeze pressure. Even with decent pot odds to complete, the reverse implied odds and lack of playability make this a losing defend. Best is to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qc 5c. Minni raised to 300 from the Button. After FiveTwo folds the Small Blind, the pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call. This gives me 2.25:1 odds. While Q5s is a relatively weak hand, it is suited and the price to see a flop is very attractive. In the Big Blind, defending against a Button raise with a wide range of hands is standard, especially when getting good pot odds. I will call to see the flop and play post-flop out of position.

On this K-high flop (7d 9c Ks), I have missed completely with my Qc 5c. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor flush draw. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the initial aggressor (Minni) and see how they act. Leading out here into the pre-flop raiser on a board that often hits their range is not a winning strategy. I will check and likely fold to most significant bets.

I raised preflop and got one caller from the big blind. On K♠ 9♣ 7♦ I hold 9♥8♥ — second pair (pair of nines) with no flush and only remote straight possibilities. In position heads-up this is a hand I should be getting value from and protecting, while keeping the pot manageable vs a deeper-stacked caller. Flash’s calling range here is wide (small pairs, suited connectors, some Kx), so a half-pot continuation bet will extract value from worse pairs and draws and fold out pure floats. I’ll c-bet about half the pot (≈325). Plan: if he calls I’ll continue to evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises large I’ll give up this vulnerable second pair and fold.

On this K-high flop (7d 9c Ks), my Qc 5c has missed completely. I have no pair, no straight draw, and only a very thin backdoor flush draw. Minni, opening from the button and continuation betting, is likely to have a King or at least some air that currently beats my Queen-high. Although the pot odds are roughly 3:1, my hand has almost zero equity and lacks any profitable way to continue. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

I am in UTG position with Ten of Clubs and Five of Diamonds (T5o). This is a statistically very weak hand with poor playability and no connectivity or suited potential. Being first to act pre-flop with a full table of active players behind me makes this a trivial fold. My stack is healthy at ~60BB, so there is no pressure to make desperate moves with garbage hands.

Five-handed table — UTG+1 is effectively early, but in short-handed play A5s is open-worthy: it has blocker value to Axx, backdoor flush/straight potential, and good postflop playability. Standard open sizes at this table are ~250–300, so I’ll raise to 300 to apply pressure (isolate the button/ SB and make the shortish BB Claude pay to see a flop). A flat call here is too passive and invites multiway action where A5s loses equity; a min-raise to 200 is too small. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold given the weak kicker; if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play for value/flush equity.

5-handed on the Button, A6s is strong enough to play aggressively versus Minni’s UTG+1 3bb open. The suited ace gives good playability in position plus an ace-blocker to reduce the chance Minni has a premium, and a 3-bet pressures her wide opens while also discouraging Flash from squeezing from the SB. I’ll use a sizing that’s big enough to deny the blinds a cheap peel and keep initiative, but not so big that I torch chips when I have to fold to a 4-bet or Claude’s shove.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 5c. Minni opened for 300 and FiveTwo 3-bet to 900 from the Button. 9c 5c is far too weak to call a 3-bet out of position, especially when facing action from two opponents. Folding is the only sensible play here to preserve my stack.

I have 8h3h in the BB facing a 3-bet to 900. This is a very weak suited hand that doesn't play well against a 3-bet range. I'd need to call 800 into a 1350 pot - poor odds for such a speculative hand. Easy fold.

I opened UTG+1 to 300 and FiveTwo (button) 3‑bet to 900. A♠5♠ has good playability (backdoor/spade equity and an Ace blocker) and against a button 3‑bet of this size I’m getting ~31% pot odds to call (600 into a ~1350 pot). FiveTwo has shown a tendency to 3‑bet fairly wide with small sizing, so A5s has reasonable equity and postflop playability. Calling keeps the pot manageable OOP and preserves fold equity on many flops; a 4‑bet would bloat the pot out of position and folding is too tight given the odds. Plan: call 900 total, see flop and proceed on A/spade/draws; fold to big action if I’m clearly dominated or face a large 4‑bet/shove.

I opened to 300 and called FiveTwo’s 3‑bet to 900, so I’m OOP with A♠5♠ on 9♠ T♥ 4♥. I have no pair — only Ace‑high and a very thin runner‑runner spade backdoor — and the 3‑bettor’s range hits this board well (T/x, 9x, broadways, overpairs). Betting as a bluff is attractive because I block some A‑combos, but out of position against a 3‑bettor this is a marginal spot: a c‑bet will frequently be called or check‑raised and I don’t want to bloat the pot with almost no equity. I’ll check to control the pot and see how FiveTwo reacts. Plan: if they fire a small probe (e.g. ~1/3 pot) I can call to realize my equity and see a turn; fold to large bets or any check‑raise. If they check back I get a free turn to potentially pick up a pair or redraw and can then decide whether to barrel or pot‑control further.

We 3-bet pre and have the nut flush draw (Ah6h) on a fairly dynamic board (9s Th 4h). This is an excellent spot to continue: we can fold out a chunk of Minni’s floats/low pairs with no heart, we build a pot for when we hit, and we deny equity to hands like AQ/KQ/KJ without a heart. A smaller c-bet sizing also keeps our range wide and sets up profitable turn barrels on many cards (hearts, A, some overcards) while still letting us comfortably continue versus a check-raise due to strong equity.

I opened to 300 and called FiveTwo’s 900 3‑bet preflop. On 9♠ T♥ 4♥ I have A♠5♠ — Ace‑high with a very thin backdoor spade draw and the Ace blocker. FiveTwo’s small c‑bet (700 into a 2650 pot) looks like a wide/probe bet rather than clear strength, so calling is cheap and the correct play. I have ~24% to make at least a pair by the river (3 Aces + 3 Fives) plus ~4% runner‑runner spade possibilities; given the pot odds calling to see a turn is +EV and keeps the pot manageable OOP. Raising here would be risky: I have no pair and can be check‑raised or called and put in difficult spots. Plan: call the 700 to realize equity and reevaluate on the turn — if I pair the Ace or pick up meaningful equity I can start extracting value; if FiveTwo shows heavy aggression (large bet/check‑raise) I’ll fold.

I opened to 300 preflop, called FiveTwo's 3-bet to 900 and then check‑called a small flop c‑bet on 9♠ T♥ 4♥. On the turn K♣ my A♠5♠ hasn't improved — I'm still just Ace‑high with only slim runner‑runner spade/paired outs. FiveTwo's small flop bet looks like a wide/probe range that includes many hands that currently beat me (T‑x, 9‑x, K‑x, overpairs) as well as bluffs. Out of position with no pair, the prudent line is pot control: checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize my remaining equity, and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal bluff that can be check‑raised. Plan: check the turn. If FiveTwo fires a small turn probe (roughly ≲1/3 pot) I can call to realize equity; I will fold to heavy aggression or a large shove. If they check back, I get a free river to try to pair up or re-evaluate for a small river bet.

We have the nut flush draw (Ah6h) with good equity, and we’re in position with no bet to face. The Kc is a high-impact card that improves a chunk of Minni’s continuing range (AK/KQ/KTs-type hands) and also gives her incentive to spring a check-raise (especially versus our c-bet range). If we bet and face a large check-raise/jam, our ~20% flush equity isn’t enough to continue profitably, so we risk lighting chips on fire.

By checking back, we lock in our equity realization, keep the pot manageable, and still retain strong options on the river: value-bet big when a heart comes, and consider a delayed bluff on favorable bricks where our range credibly contains strong Kx/overpairs.

I flopped A5s and rivered a pair of fives (A♠5♠ → pair of 5s with an Ace kicker). The board is 9♠ T♥ 4♥ K♣ 5♥ — three hearts are out there, so flushes are possible, and K/T/9/overpair hands from FiveTwo’s preflop 3‑bet range will often beat me. FiveTwo’s line (3‑bet preflop, small flop bet, check turn, check river) is ambiguous: it contains a lot of hands that still beat my pair (Kx, Tx, 9x, overpairs, completed hearts) and also many missed broadway/Ax hands that I now beat. Betting thin for value here risks getting called or raised by better hands and folding out the few worse hands; out of position on a coordinated board I prefer pot control. I’ll check to get to showdown and only call a reasonable river bet; I’ll fold to large raises/shoves given how often I’m behind.

River brings the third heart and we hold Ah6h for the nut flush (and effectively the nuts since Ah is in our hand). After Minni check-called flop and then checked turn/river, her range contains plenty of one-pair hands (Kx/Tx/9x), some two-pair (KT/T9), and also some worse heart combos that will pay off. I want a value bet that still gets looked up by non-flush bluff-catchers (especially Kx) while also being small enough to induce a raise from weaker flushes that we can then stack. A ~55% pot sizing accomplishes both goals without polarizing too hard.

I have A♠5♠ — a river pair of fives with the Ace kicker. The line: I opened, FiveTwo 3‑bet, they c‑bet small on the flop, checked the turn and now makes a modest river bet of 1,800 into a 5,150 pot. Calling 1,800 to win 6,950 requires ~25.9% equity, which is achievable here against a wide/thin 3‑bet/c‑bet range. FiveTwo’s line and sizing are consistent with a mix of value (Kx, Tx, 9x, overpairs, some two‑heart flushes or JQ straights) and many missed broadway/Ax combos and bluffs. My Ace blocker reduces some of their strongest A‑bluffs, and my pair will beat most missed broadway/Ax hands. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and is likely to get me raised or called by many better hands, so it’s unnecessary. I’ll call the small river probe to reach showdown; if they instead shoved or made a large raise I would fold.

UTG with 7♥2♠ — the single worst starting hand. Out of position, against the remaining seating and a pot already containing blinds, this hand has essentially no playable equity and will be dominated by any reasonable calling/raising range. Calling 100 to play a multistreet pot OOP or attempting a light open with this hand is unnecessary risk — my stack is healthy but there’s no justification to invest with garbage. Fold and wait for a much stronger spot in position.

5-handed with UTG folded, we’re effectively in a middle/late position and can open fairly wide. T6 suited has good playability deep (can make strong draws and disguised straights/flushes) and benefits a lot from having initiative. A limp just invites the Button to isolate and puts us in tougher multiway spots. I’ll open small-to-standard and be comfortable folding if Flash 3-bets large, especially given his positional advantage and stack.

Sizing: 2.5x (250) pressures the blinds, keeps our risk controlled, and still builds a pot when we realize equity.

I am on the button with Ten-Eight suited, a strong speculative hand. FiveTwo, who has been active and aggressive lately, opened for 2.5x from UTG+1. While I could fold to remain safe with my chip lead, the price to call is small (250 chips into a 400 pot), and I will have absolute position for the rest of the hand. Both blinds are relatively short-stacked, so I must be wary of a squeeze, but calling allows me to see a flop and potentially outplay FiveTwo post-flop or hit a strong draw.
5h2c is a very weak hand. Even with decent pot odds (~3:1), this hand has terrible playability and will rarely make strong hands. Easy fold from the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with [Qs Kd]. The UTG+1 player raised to 250 and the Button called. I need to call 150 more into a pot that will be 800 after my call. This gives me pot odds of roughly 5.3:1 (requiring ~18.75% equity). KQo is well ahead of that equity requirement against an early position opening range and a button flatting range. Although I will be out of position post-flop, the price is too good to fold. A squeeze raise is risky against an early position opener and large stacks, so I will take the low-variance route and flat call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind in a multi-way pot with King-Queen high on a low, dry board of 3-9-2. The pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) is in UTG+1 and the Button (Flash) is also in the hand. This board texture is unlikely to have hit anyone hard, but the pre-flop raiser still has the range advantage with potential overpairs. Leading out (donk betting) is ill-advised into two opponents out of position with just overcards. My best play is to check to the raiser and evaluate based on the action. If it checks through, I have decent showdown value with King-high and equity to improve on the turn.

Multiway and out of position, this 9-high low board connects reasonably well with the callers’ ranges (BB has lots of 9x/22/33 and Button has many suited connectors/pairs). With T6dd we have no direct draw and only backdoor diamonds/straight potential, so a c-bet gets called too often and risks being raised off our equity. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our backdoor equity, and lets us respond profitably on favorable turns (diamond/8/7/T/6) with delayed pressure.

The flop is quite dry (3c 9h 2d) and both opponents have checked to me. While I could bet to try and take the pot down now, FiveTwo has been playing very aggressively in recent hands and his check might be a trap or a prelude to a check-raise. I have 10-high with some backdoor heart and straight potential. By checking behind, I take a free card to potentially improve to a pair or a draw on the turn, while also keeping the pot small in case I'm walking into a stronger hand or a trap from the pre-flop aggressor.

The turn pairs the board with the Nine of Spades, making the board 3c 9h 2d 9s. The flop action went check-check-check, indicating weakness or pot control from the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) and the caller (Flash). While it is possible someone was slow-playing a 9 or a set, it is more likely they have overcards or small pocket pairs. I hold King-Queen high, which has some showdown value against missed broadway hands but is behind any pair. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (like lower overcards) and will only isolate me against better hands (pairs or trips). Checking keeps the pot small and allows me to realize my equity cheaply or get to showdown if the river checks through as well. Being out of position against two opponents, a defensive check is the prudent play.

Flop checked through multiway, which caps both opponents’ ranges (lots of overcards, small pairs, and some slowplays, but fewer strong made hands betting for value). The turn pairing the 9 is a good card for the preflop raiser to apply delayed pressure: I can credibly represent overpairs (TT+), strong 9x, and even boats, while many of their Ace-highs/underpairs will struggle to continue facing a bet.

With Td6d I have very little showdown value and not much equity to realize by checking, so I prefer to take a stab now at a sizing that folds out the weak portion without over-risking chips. If I get raised (especially multiway), I can comfortably fold.

On the turn, the board has paired with a second Nine (9s), and FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 400 into an 800-chip pot after checking the flop. I currently hold Ten-high with no draws and very little equity. While FiveTwo is active and could be bluffing or making a delayed continuation bet with a hand like overcards or a small pocket pair, I have no reason to 'float' here or attempt a bluff with multiple opponents still in the hand (Pro has yet to fold). Calling would require hitting a Ten or Eight on the river, and even then, those outs might not be clean if FiveTwo actually has a Nine or a better pair. Given my significant chip lead, folding is the most disciplined and lowest-risk play.

I am out of position on the turn with King-Queen high on a board of 3-9-2-9. The pot is 1200 and the UTG+1 raiser (FiveTwo) has bet 400 after the flop checked through. This delayed c-bet often indicates either a mid-strength hand seeking protection (like a pocket pair 44-88) or an attempt to pick up the pot after weakness was shown. My hand (King-high) loses to all pocket pairs, any 9 (trips), and even Ace-high bluffs (AK, AQ), which are heavily present in a UTG+1 opening range. I have no flush or straight draws, only 6 outs at best to hit a pair, giving me roughly 12% equity which is insufficient for the 25% pot odds offered. Floating out of position with just King-high is a losing play here.

5-handed UTG effectively plays like a wider late-position spot; ATo is comfortably within an opening range. With two shorter stacks behind (Claude 32bb, Pro 56bb) and blinds that can defend but don’t want to bloat pots without strong hands, an open-raise prints via fold equity and lets us take initiative with a hand that dominates many ace-x and broadway continues. I’ll use a solid, non-committing size that pressures the blinds without risking too much if 3-bet.

I am in UTG+1 with 6s 3s. The player in the first position (FiveTwo) has opened with a raise to 250. 63s is a very weak starting hand and does not play well against an UTG opening range, even in a 5-handed game. My stack is healthy, and there is no reason to get involved in this pot with such a marginal hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

J3o is a weak offsuit hand facing a UTG raise. Even with position on the button, this hand has poor playability - not suited, poor connectivity, and easily dominated. With 32BB stack, I should preserve chips for better spots rather than call here speculatively.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 6c facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in UTG. 76 offsuit is a weak hand, especially out of position against an Under-the-Gun opening range which is typically stronger. Calling would require putting in 200 more chips to play a marginal hand out of position. The pot odds are not compelling enough to justify the positional disadvantage and hand weakness. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack of 5675 (57 BB).

I’m in the big blind with Ah8h facing FiveTwo’s UTG open to 250. A8s is too strong to fold — it has good blocker value and excellent postflop playability — but I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet. Calling 150 more (250 total) defends a wide portion of my range, keeps the pot manageable, preserves effective stack depth for postflop maneuvering, and lets me continue on ace/heart or favorable textures. If FiveTwo responds with a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to heavy 4-bet aggression); otherwise I’ll proceed with pot-control and target value when I connect.

I have Ah8h on 8s As 5d — top two pair (Aces & Eights) heads-up vs the UTG opener. Very strong hand; few hands beat me (pocket 8s, pocket 5s, pocket AAs) and I block some combos by holding an Ace and an Eight, but those sets remain possible. Betting here extracts value from worse A-x, 8x and medium pairs and charges any backdoor spade/straight equity, while a modest sizing keeps the pot controlled in case I face heavy action. I'll make a ~55% pot value bet to get called by worse hands but not bloat the pot: bet 300 into 550. If called I’ll re-evaluate the turn; if I face a large check-raise I’ll be cautious (likely a set) and re-assess, folding to extreme aggression if the line is consistent with trips/full house.

Minni’s small donk lead (300 into 850) on A♠-8♠-5♦ can be a wide, protection/blocker type bet: weaker Ax, 8x/5x, and a lot of spade draws. With A♣T♥ we have top pair but a medium kicker and no spade—good enough to continue, but not strong enough to happily build a big pot if Minni’s donk range is weighted toward better aces/sets when facing pressure. Calling keeps all the worse Ax and bluffs in, lets us realize our positional advantage, and we can respond to turn sizing/cards (raising later on safe turns, or pot-controlling when the board/line indicates strength).

I have Ah8h on 8s As 5d Kh — I currently hold top two pair (Aces & Eights) heads-up vs FiveTwo, who opened UTG and called my flop c-bet. Very few hands beat me (sets: AA, 88, 55); the King on the turn helps some Kx hands but they still lose to my two pair. I should extract value and deny a free river card rather than give a free card by checking. I’ll make a value bet sized to get called by worse A-x, Kx, 8x and medium pairs while not bloating the pot excessively if I face strong resistance — about 65% of the pot (~750). If I face a large check-raise that’s consistent with trips/boats I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold to extreme aggression; if called I’ll re-assess on the river and target thinner value or shove as appropriate.

Minni’s line is consistent with a wide donk range: small flop lead then another small-ish turn lead (~40% pot). The K♥ is not a great card for us (it improves AK and some K♠X♠ hands pick up extra equity), but our A♣T♥ is still ahead of a lot of their continuing/value-blocking range (weaker Ax, some 8x/5x that led for “protection,” and spade-draw/semi-bluff combos). Folding to this sizing would be too tight, and raising mostly isolates us against better aces/two pair/sets while allowing draws to continue anyway at this depth. Best is to call and re-evaluate river based on sizing and runout (especially spades).

I have A♥8♥ — top two pair on A♠ 8♠ 5♦ K♥ J♣. Villain (FiveTwo) opened UTG and passively check‑called my flop c‑bet and turn bet, a line consistent with medium‑strength hands (weaker A‑x, K‑x that turned a pair, 8x, or pocket fives) that will often call a river value bet. Very few hands beat me (sets: AA, 88, 55) and my holdings block some strong combos, while the river J is a fairly meaningless card. I want thin value without bloating the pot in case I’m up against one of the rare sets — a modest bet will get called by worse hands while controlling downside. I’ll bet ~45% of the pot (1200). If FiveTwo check‑raises big, that’s likely trips/boats and I’ll fold or re‑evaluate based on sizing.

Minni’s triple-donk line (small flop, medium turn, ~45% pot river) can still contain plenty of worse Ax/protection bets plus missed spade draws. The river J improves some value hands (AJ, KJ, QT) and we’re not thrilled, but the sizing isn’t polar enough to justify a fold with top pair. We’re getting ~4.2:1 (need ~24% equity), and raising would mostly isolate us versus better aces/two-pair/sets/straights while folding out bluffs. Best play is to bluff-catch and take the showdown.

Holding 6c 2s in the Under The Gun (UTG) position is an extremely weak start. In a 5-handed game, playing junk hands from early position is generally a losing strategy. With 150 hands to play and being the current chip leader, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with a hand that has very little equity or playability. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
T4o is a weak hand from early position. With 32 BBs and still 75 hands to go, I should preserve my stack and fold this trash hand.

I am on the Button with 8c 2c, a very weak suited hand. It is folded to me. The players in the blinds (Minni and FiveTwo) both have large stacks covering me significantly and have been active/aggressive in recent hands. Trying to steal the blinds here is risky because they are likely to defend, and my hand has very poor playability post-flop. The expected value of opening this hand is negative given the current dynamics. Therefore, I will fold.

5♣4♥ is a very marginal hand to play out of position, but the action has folded to me and I’m only facing the Big Blind (FiveTwo). Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to just call and play OOP with poor equity — that’s suboptimal. With deep stacks and decent fold equity vs a single defender, the best play is an exploitative open-raise to steal the blinds. I’ll raise to the minimum open (200 total) to apply pressure while risking a small amount; if FiveTwo folds I pick up the 150, if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously postflop based on texture, and I’ll fold to a large 3-bet.

Minni’s small-blind min-raise should be quite wide 5-handed, and we’re getting an excellent price (100 to win 300). With 33 we’re not strong enough to 3-bet for value and a 3-bet bluff would bloat the pot OOP with a hand that realizes equity best by flopping a set or cheaply reaching showdowns. Calling keeps their range wide, preserves implied odds versus overpairs/top-pair hands, and lets us continue on many favorable textures (set/low boards) while folding efficiently when we miss and face heavy pressure.

I raised preflop and now have a marginal made hand: pair of fives (5♣4♥) on Q♥ J♠ 5♠. Pot is 400 and stacks are deep (~16250). The BB called preflop with a wide defense range (many overcards, broadways, small pairs and draws). Betting here lets me leverage my initiative and fold out unpaired overcards and many turn-draw hands; posting a ~50% pot c-bet (200) is small relative to stacks but buys a lot of fold equity. Plan: bet 200 to take the pot now or get called by worse; if I face a significant raise I’ll fold (hand is vulnerable), if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn — check for pot control on blanks and fold to heavy aggression.

Minni’s SB open is wide 5-handed and this 1/3-pot c-bet (200 into 600) will be range-heavy and often includes overcards/backdoors that we’re currently ahead of with our pocket pair. While 33 has no spade/backdoors and won’t realize perfectly OOP, the price is good (need ~25%), we can still pick up significant implied odds when we spike a 3, and we avoid overfolding our BB defend range versus small sizing. Plan: call once, then proceed cautiously—continue aggressively on a 3, consider checking through on many turns if they slow down, and comfortably fold versus larger turn barrels on bad cards.

I have 5♣4♥ — bottom pair on Q♥ J♠ 5♠ with the Ten of Clubs on the turn. I c-bet the flop and was called, so the BB’s range includes a lot of hands that now beat me (Qx, Jx, Tx, K9/98/AK straights, plus some pairs and backdoor spade hands). The turn is a relatively dangerous card that increases straight and paired-board combinations. Out of position with a weak pair and a poor kicker, I don’t want to inflate the pot and get raised off; I should control the size of the pot and see what the opponent does. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable — if he checks back I get a free river; if he bets a small amount (≈≤200) I can consider calling to realize equity, but I’ll fold to heavy aggression (large bet/raise) because my hand is often behind.

We have essentially no showdown value with 33 on Q-J-5-T (our pair is an underpair to the board), so checking back just realizes ~4% to spike a set and otherwise loses at showdown a huge percentage of the time. Minni’s flop 1/2-pot c-bet can be very wide, and the turn check caps a chunk of their range (missed overcards, weak pairs, some draws that don’t want to bloat). This is a good spot to apply pressure before many rivers (A/K/9/spade) change the texture and make bluffing harder. I’ll take a strong stab with a bigger sizing to maximize fold equity versus A-high/K-high and some marginal pairs; if I’m check-raised I can comfortably fold.

Fold. I have only bottom pair (5♣4♥) with a terrible 4-kicker on Q♥ J♠ 5♠ T♣. The pot is 1,450 and FiveTwo’s turn raise to 650 (into ~800) is a large sizing that heavily favors value-heavy hands (Qx/Jx/Tx, two pair, K9/98 straights) or strong semibluffs. Calling 650 gives ~31% pot equity (650 / (1450+650)), but my hand rarely has that equity vs his raising range and is very difficult to play if called or raised again. I planned to check the turn and fold to heavy aggression — preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the correct exploitative line here.
K6o is a weak hand from UTG position. Even 5-handed, this is not in my opening range. With 32 BB, I need to be selective with my opens, and K6o doesn't make the cut from early position.

I am in the Cutoff position (UTG+1 in 5-handed play) with 9s6s, a suited two-gapper. While late position opens up my range, this hand is too weak to profitably open-raise, especially with active, deep-stacked opponents (Minni, FiveTwo, Flash) yet to act who have shown willingness to play back. My stack is healthy at ~57BB, so I should preserve chips for better spots rather than engaging with a speculative hand that is easily dominated.

I'm on the button with pocket deuces (2♦2♥) and both blinds to act. This is a standard situation to open-raise: I want to take initiative, isolate the blinds, and preserve set-mining value with deep stacks. A 3x BB size denies good odds to callers, keeps fold equity, and still allows me to comfortably call a normal 3-bet and set-mine. Plan: raise to 300; if I face a standard 3-bet (~800–1200) I'll call to set-mine, but fold to an uncomfortably large 4-bet/all-in. If called heads-up I'll use position to control the pot and extract value when I hit a set.

AThh is strong versus a Button opening range, but playing it as a flat from the small blind invites the big blind to come along and leaves us out of position in a multiway pot. Given Minni’s frequent opens and willingness to fold some opens to 3-bets, the highest-EV line is to 3-bet for value and isolation, denying equity to Flash and letting us win preflop a good chunk of the time. I’ll use a larger OOP sizing to discourage calls and simplify postflop play.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Minni opened from the Button, and FiveTwo 3-bet significantly from the Small Blind. Looking at Hand #67 and Hand #73, FiveTwo has shown a tendency to 3-bet with very weak hands (93d, 95s) and apply post-flop pressure. However, A7o is a poor hand to play out of position in a 3-bet pot, and I am sandwiched between an aggressive 3-better and the original raiser. Despite FiveTwo's loose 3-betting history, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chip lead and wait for a better spot where I have more equity or position.

I opened to 300 from the button with pocket deuces and FiveTwo (SB) 3-bet to 1100 (I must call 800 more). With deep stacks (15,750) this is textbook set-mining: calling preserves my implied odds and keeps me in position post‑flop. Immediate pot odds (800 into a 1,500 pot) aren’t great for a raw 12% flop set chance, but the stacks behind mean a hit set will often pay off. A small 4-bet here would bloats the pot with a hand that rarely improves and commits more equity vs a 3‑bettor’s value range; I’ll fold to an uncomfortably large 4‑bet/all‑in, but will flat-call and see a flop. Plan: call 1100 total; if I flop a set play for value, otherwise use position to check/control or fold to heavy action depending on texture and opponent tendencies.

We’re the preflop 3-bettor and still retain a range advantage here (overpairs, strong broadways), even though 7-8-3 with two spades can connect with Minni’s call range (pairs, suited connectors, spade floats). With AThh we have two overcards plus some backdoor potential, and we want to deny equity to hands like KQ/QJ/JT and pick up the pot immediately a fair amount. A small c-bet accomplishes that while keeping our risk controlled if he continues with pairs/draws in position; it also sets up profitable barrels on A/T, many broadway turns, and heart turns that improve our equity.

I called preflop with 22 as a set-mining call; I missed the flop (7♥ 3♠ 8♠). FiveTwo (the 3-bettor) fires a very small probe-bet (700 into a 3000 pot). Raising here would be a pure bluff with virtually no fold equity vs a 3-bettor’s range and would bloat a pot I have almost no equity in. Folding surrenders my set-mine investment; calling is cheap enough to see a turn (700 into 3000 ≈ 19% immediate pot odds) and preserves implied odds — if I hit a deuce I can extract a lot. Plan: call 700 to see the turn, keep pot control, and fold to heavy turn aggression unless I hit the set (or the turn changes the story dramatically).

Turn brings the Ac, giving us top pair with a decent kicker (AT) and strongly favoring our 3-bet range (we have many Ax, including strong ones). Minni’s flop call contains lots of 7x/8x/PP and spade-draw floats that now need to pay to realize equity. Checking gives free cards to flush/straight draws and lets his pairs realize too easily. I want a value/protection barrel that also sets up a clean river decision (often a shove on safe runouts). A ~60% pot sizing pressures his marginal pairs and charges spades while still getting called by worse Ax and draws.

Called preflop and on the flop as a set-mining attempt, but the turn A massively reduces my equity. I have only two outs to make a set on the river (~4.3%). Facing a 2,200 bet and a final pot of ~8,100 if I call, I would need ~27% equity to break even (2200/8100 ≈ 27%), far more than my ~4% chance. Opponent’s line (3-bet pre → small flop c-bet → large turn bet) is consistent with a strong value range (Ax, overpairs, possibly two pair/set), so calling or bluff-raising is negative EV. My earlier calls are sunk costs — the correct play is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Five offsuit (T5o). This is a statistically very weak hand with poor playability and no potential for straight or flush draws that justify entering the pot from early position. My stack is 5675 (57 BB), which is healthy enough to simply fold and wait for a better spot. There is no strategic merit to limping or raising here.

5-handed game — I should be opening a wider range. 7♦8♠ is a playable connector with good postflop playability and equity against wide calling ranges. Limping from UTG+1 invites multiway pots and steals, so I’ll open and take the initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (a standard ~3x BB-sized open) — big enough to discourage some callers but small relative to my deep stack. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and fold to strong resistance on dry boards; if Claude (BB, ~31bb) jams over my raise I will fold to an all-in shove (calling ~30bb with 7-8 offsuit is a marginal spot). If I face a modest 3-bet from a deep, aggressive player I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and player tendencies.

QcJs is a strong, playable broadway on the Button versus Minni’s 3bb open, but it’s not strong enough to happily play for stacks pre-flop if she 4-bets or if the short BB (Claude, ~31bb) jams. A 3-bet here increases the incentive for Claude to shove and can force us into awkward, higher-variance decisions with a hand that realizes equity very well in position. By flatting, we keep the pot manageable, retain position on the raiser, allow ourselves to outplay postflop, and we still have the option to apply pressure on favorable boards.

I am holding Ah Qh, which is a premium starting hand. Minni opened from early position and FiveTwo called on the button. Playing from the Small Blind puts me in the worst position post-flop, so I prefer to 3-bet to take control of the hand, define my opponents' ranges, and potentially take the pot down pre-flop. A raise to 1300 (roughly 4.3x the open) is a standard sizing for a squeeze play in this spot.

I have 65s in the BB facing a 3-bet to 1300. With only 3125 chips, calling 1200 commits nearly 40% of my stack with a speculative hand. 65s plays poorly against a 3-bet range, I'm out of position, and my stack is too shallow for good implied odds. Clear fold.

5-handed and deep-stacked — 7♦8♠ is a playable suited connector with good equity and postflop playability. Flash’s 3-bet to 1,300 is a relatively small sizing and likely contains a lot of steals and broadways; folding loses too much implied equity. Reraising to the minimum 2,300 with 7♦8♠ is too thin (we’d face realistic 4-bet/5-bet pressure and be playing a marginal hand for stacks). Calling 1,000 more (1300 total) keeps the pot controlled, retains the ability to realize equity, and leaves us with position dynamics we can exploit on many flops (we’ll act after Flash on the flop but before the button). Plan: call to see a flop; if FiveTwo calls behind treat multiway boards more cautiously, c-bet favorable low/connected/flush-draw flops heads-up, and fold to heavy resistance on dry high-card boards. If Flash 4-bets large or jams preflop, fold.

We’re getting a very good price (1000 to win 3000) and QJo has plenty of equity versus a SB squeeze range, especially with position on both Flash and Minni. Claude has already folded, so we don’t have to worry about a short-stack jam behind. A 4-bet would turn a solid, equity-realizing hand into a high-variance bluff that will often get called by better (AQ/KQ/TT+) and forces us into awkward stack-off spots. Folding would be too tight given pot odds and positional advantage. Best play is to call and realize our equity postflop, applying pressure on favorable textures.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Queens) on a board that contains both a flush draw and potential straight draws. As the pre-flop aggressor who squeezed, I have a significant range advantage on this board. I need to bet for value against weaker Aces, Queens, and various diamond draws while also protecting my hand. A bet of 1,500 into the 4,000 pot (37.5% pot) is a standard continuation bet that keeps my opponents' ranges wide and allows me to build the pot effectively.

Flop: Q♦ 8♦ A♣. I hold 7♦8♠ — a pair of eights (middle pair) with a weak 7 kicker and one diamond (so only a runner-runner flush possibility). Flash was the preflop 3-bettor and now makes a small c-bet (1,500 into ~5,500), which looks like a wide continuation range rather than a polar value shove. FiveTwo is still to act behind me, so this is effectively a multiway pot. Calling 1,500 gives me roughly 21% pot odds to continue (1,500 to win ~7,000), and my hand is ahead of a lot of bluffs/overcards and has decent equity to improve. Raising here would overrepresent a very strong hand out of position against the 3-bettor and likely gets me in trouble; folding to such a small bet is too nitpicky with a made pair. Plan: call to keep the pot controlled, re-evaluate on the turn — proceed for value if I improve or if opponents show weakness; fold to significant turn/river aggression when clearly beat.

We have second pair (QJ) on A-Q-8 with no diamond, facing a small-ish c-bet from the SB squeeze range and a flat-call from Minni. Multiway, a raise is high-variance and mostly gets action from better (Ax, AQ, strong Qx, big diamond draws) while folding out a lot of hands we already beat (JJ/TT, some worse Qx) and risking getting 3-bet/jammed. Calling needs very little equity (1500 to win ~7000 now / ~8500 after we call), and position lets us realize well, control pot size, and make better decisions on turns (continue on Q/J/T or when action checks; proceed cautiously versus big turn barrels on blanks/diamond turns).

The turn card (Ten of Diamonds) is highly unfavorable for my top two pair (Aces and Queens). It completes a possible flush (three diamonds now on board) and also completes a straight for any opponent holding King-Jack. Given that both Minni and FiveTwo called my flop bet in a three-way pot, their ranges are likely to include several diamond draws, suited connectors, or Broadway cards that have now improved. Betting again risks running into a superior hand while folding equity against potentially worse hands. By checking, I can gauge the strength of my opponents' hands based on their betting behavior and keep the pot manageable while looking for a Full House on the river.

I have middle pair (8s) with a weak 7-kicker and only one diamond on a turn that now brings a third diamond (Q♦ 8♦ A♣ T♦). That board heavily favors hands that beat me: any two diamonds have a made flush, A/Q/T pairs beat me, and KJ type combos have a Broadway straight. Two opponents remain and Flash’s line (preflop 3-bet, small flop c-bet, then checking the turn) is consistent with pot-control or a medium-strength holding — he can still have a hand that crushes my pair. Betting here would bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand and will get called or raised by better hands while folding out very little; a raise would be reckless. I’ll check to keep the pot controlled, see the river, and then decide (call small river bets or fold to large aggression unless I improve).

Turn brings a very dynamic Td completing a lot of opponent range equity (KJ makes Broadway, AT improves to two pair, and all diamond holdings pick up huge draw equity). After Flash (preflop squeezer) and Minni both check, our QJ (no diamond) has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to build a big pot multiway. If we bet, we’re rarely getting called by worse (most worse Qx/pairs hate this texture) and we reopen the door to a check-raise from made straights/two-pair/strong diamond draws, which puts us in a high-variance, dominated spot. Best is to take the free card, keep the pot under control, and make a clearer river decision based on sizing and runout.

I have top two pair (Aces and Queens) on a board that ended with three diamonds and a potential straight (KJ). While my hand is very strong, the turn check-around suggests that my opponents might be cautious or holding mediocre made hands like a single Ace. However, since the pot is already large and any diamond or a King-Jack beats me, betting risks a raise from a better hand while potentially folding out the hands I beat. Checking allows me to get to showdown and win against weaker Aces or Queens without risking more chips on a dangerous board.

I have 7♦8♠ — river is Q♦ 8♦ A♣ T♦ 7♠, so I’ve made two pair (8s and 7s). It’s a wet board with three diamonds and numerous straight/ two‑pair/sets that can beat me (AQ, A8, Q8/Q7, sets like TT/QQ, straights like KJ/J9/96 and any two‑diamond combo that made a flush). Flash (the preflop 3‑bettor) checked both turn and river, and FiveTwo has been passive; that line looks like missed broadways or medium strength rather than a slow‑played monster. With two opponents in the hand, a thin river value bet has limited EV — it will be called by many worse one‑pair hands but also by better hands and can get raised into a tough spot. Pot control and going to showdown is the safest +EV line here. I’ll check and take my two pair to showdown (if someone bets, I’ll call small bets but fold to large raises that indicate flush/boat/rivered straight).

River is checked to us in a large 3-way pot and we hold QcJs on A-Q-8-T-7 with three diamonds. We have decent showdown value (a queen) but the texture heavily favors strong made hands that can comfortably check (Ax for pot control, KJ straight, some two-pair like AT/QT, and occasional slowplays). A bet here is rarely called by worse (most worse pairs/Qx will fold on this runout), while better hands either call or sometimes check-raise, forcing us to put in extra chips with a bluff-catcher. Since we’re getting a free showdown and our hand can be best versus missed one-diamond holdings / underpairs, the highest-EV, lowest-variance play is to check back.

I’m UTG with Q♠8♣ — a marginal offsuit queen in early position. The pot is 150 and I’d have to call 100 or open (min to 200). Limping would invite multiway pots where Q8o is often dominated; opening here is too loose — I can get 3-bet by the big stacks (Flash, FiveTwo) or forced into a tough call if the short-stack (Claude) jams. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left in the tournament, folding and waiting for a better spot is the +EV play.

We’re effectively in the CO (5-handed) with a weak offsuit hand (T5o) that plays poorly postflop and doesn’t want to face pressure. With an aggressive big stack on the Button behind and a capable/short SB who can 3-bet shove light, opening becomes high-variance and often forces us to fold to action. Limping invites isolation and puts us in tough spots. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for better steal/value candidates.

I am on the button with 98o and a massive chip lead. The action has folded to me. Both the small blind (Claude, ~31 BB) and the big blind (Pro, ~56 BB) are relatively short compared to me. This is a standard spot to attempt a steal from the button. 98o has some playability post-flop, and if either blind shoves, I can comfortably fold without damaging my stack. A raise to 2.5x the big blind is a standard opening size.
AJs is a premium hand. Flash raised from the button which is often a wide stealing range. With ~30bb, I should 3-bet for value. Standard sizing would be around 3x the open to 750-800.

I am holding 2h 5s in the Big Blind, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, my equity is negligible. The Small Blind has committed a large portion of their short stack, indicating strength. I have no reason to get involved in this pot. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I opened from the button with 98o to steal the blinds, but Claude in the Small Blind has 3-bet to 800. Claude started the hand with roughly 31 BBs and now has about 23 BBs left. While I have a massive chip lead and am getting reasonable pot odds to call (550 to win a total pot of 1700), 98o is significantly behind Claude's likely 3-betting range (big pairs and high connectors). Given the low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of approximately 1.36 if I call, I will be forced into a high-variance post-flop situation where Claude will likely commit the rest of his chips. There is no need to gift chips to a short stack with a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

5-handed UTG (first to act), 97o is too weak to open profitably and plays poorly out of position. Limping invites isolation from the aggressive stacks behind (especially Flash) and forces us into tough, dominated postflop spots with little fold equity. With plenty of stack depth, preserving chips and waiting for better UTG opens is higher EV.

I am the significant chip leader with over 27,000 chips. In this 5-handed situation, I am in the UTG+1 (Hijack) position. My hand, 8-7 offsuit, is quite weak for an opening raise. While I could attempt to pressure the shorter stacks (Claude with ~35BB and Pro with ~55BB), 87o is not robust enough to call a 3-bet shove from them. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to wait for better spots to utilize my stack advantage.

Button with 67o after two folds. This is a decent stealing spot from the button. While 67o isn't premium, it's playable in position when folded to me. With 35BB effective stack, I'll make a standard 2.5x open to steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Q9o. The Button (Claude) has raised to 250 (2.5 BB). Q9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, often dominated by hands like KQ, QJ, AQ, or A9. Although the Button's range is wide, calling creates a difficult post-flop situation where I am out of position against an aggressive player, and the Big Blind is still left to act behind me with a large stack. The risk of being squeezed or dominated outweighs the potential reward. Therefore, folding is the most prudent long-term play.

Fold. Facing a standard button open to 2.5bb, I only need to call 150 (250 total) but that requires ~27% equity (150 / (400+150) ≈ 27%). K3o is a very weak, easily dominated hand (many of Claude’s raiser combos include better Ks, broadways and pairs) and I’ll be out of position postflop against a player who has shown a willingness to raise. A small 3‑bet to 400 is a thin bluff with poor blockers and invites a shove or a call that commits me out of position. With a healthy 113bb stack vs his ~32bb I’d rather preserve chips and wait for a better spot to apply pressure, so I fold the big blind.

I am holding Ah Jc under the gun in a 5-handed game. This is a strong starting hand. As the chip leader, I want to maintain my aggressive table presence and build the pot. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to narrow the field and potentially isolate or take the blinds.

KJs is a solid hand facing an UTG open of 300. With ~36BB effective, I can profitably flat call here. 3-betting risks getting 4-bet by Flash who has a huge stack, and I'd be OOP. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity with a hand that plays well postflop.

I hold 7h 6c on the button. Facing an UTG raise to 300 and a call from UTG+1, the pot is 750. My hand is an unsuited connector with low equity against early position opening ranges. Although I have position, 76o is too weak to profitably call a 3BB raise multi-way, as it lacks flush potential and is easily dominated. Preserving my 55BB stack for better opportunities is the correct play.

Ad2h is marginal but defendable from the small blind here. Flash opened UTG to 300 and Claude called, so this is a multiway pot and I’m out of position — 3-betting with A2o offsuit is too thin versus an UTG range. Pot is 750 and it only costs me 250 more (300 total with my SB) to see a flop, so I’ll call to realize equity and keep the pot manageable. Postflop plan: fold to heavy aggression unless I hit an Ace or a very favorable board; if I flop top pair (Ace) or a strong draw, play more aggressively and exploit spot-specific tendencies (Claude is short-stacked and Flash is aggressive).

We’re closing the action getting a very good price: 200 to win a 1000 pot (~5:1), so we only need ~17% raw equity. Even offsuit 6-5 has decent multiway playability (straight/two-pair potential) and strong implied odds when we connect. A squeeze here would need to be large into multiple callers and risks running into strong UTG ranges while bloating the pot with a marginal hand, so we take the profitable overcall and play postflop from the BB.

I defended preflop but this flop (Q♦ 3♥ 5♣) gives me only Ace-high and a very small backdoor wheel draw (four outs). It’s a four‑way pot and Flash opened UTG, so ranges are strong — I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Pot control is best: check and see how the table acts. If someone leads a small bet I can call to realize equity (I have ~8.5% to hit on the turn, ~16.5% by river) but will fold to heavy aggression. Only become aggressive if I pair the Ace or complete the straight.

Multiway pot on a Q-high board that strongly favors the preflop raiser’s range. With 6♠5♥ we’ve flopped a weak bottom pair that doesn’t want to build a big pot OOP into 3 players, and leading (donking) is unlikely to fold out better hands while often getting raised/put in tough spots. Best is to check, keep ranges wide, and reevaluate versus sizing if Flash (or someone else) bets—continuing mainly versus small bets and favorable runouts.

I am in a 4-way pot after raising pre-flop with Ah Jc. The flop of Qd 3h 5c is relatively dry, but with three opponents, the likelihood that someone connected with the Queen or has a pocket pair is significantly higher than in a heads-up pot. As the chip leader, I want to avoid unnecessary risks. Checking here allows me to see a turn for free, realize my equity if an Ace or Jack falls, and control the size of the pot. Since both the Small Blind and Big Blind have checked, I will check behind to see how the action develops on the turn without committing more chips with just Ace-high.

Missed the flop completely with KJs on Qd3h5c. I have two overcards but no pair, no draw. Four-way pot where someone likely has a Q or a pair. Betting here as a bluff into 3 opponents is too risky with my stack size (~33BB). Best to check and potentially see a free turn card where I could improve to a pair or pick up a straight draw.

Multiway pot (4 players) with A♦2♥ and only Ace-high on Q♦ 3♥ 5♣ 5♦. I have essentially no equity and no real draws — the turn pairing the board makes trips/full house combos possible and greatly reduces fold equity if I bet. Flash opened UTG (likely strong) and Claude called; betting into multiple opponents out of position is a thin bluff that’s unlikely to work and would only bloat the pot when I’m almost always behind. Pot control is best: check and concede the initiative. If someone barrels a small probe I can consider a fold/call depending on size and reads, but I’ll fold to heavy aggression and only continue if I improve (pair the Ace) or pick up clear information.

We turn trips (5♠5♣5♦) with a mediocre kicker (6), which is strong in a 4-way limped/called pot but not the nuts (better 5x and full houses exist: Q5/35/33/QQ). After the flop checks through, ranges are capped and will still contain plenty of Qx, pocket pairs (66–JJ), and random floats that can call a reasonable turn bet. If we check again, this often checks through and we miss value.

I’ll lead for a medium sizing to (1) get value from Qx/pairs, (2) charge straight draws like A4/64/76, and (3) keep the pot controllable so we can comfortably fold if a multiway raise comes in representing boats/better trips.

I am holding Ace-high (Ah Jc) in a four-way pot on a board of Qd 3h 5c 5d. FiveTwo, the Big Blind, has led out for 500 into a pot of 1200 after checking the flop. This bet into three opponents on a paired board often indicates a marginal made hand like a Queen, a pocket pair, or potentially a 5. Although I have six outs to improve to top pair, those outs might not be clean if my opponent has a set or trips. Furthermore, I still have Claude behind me who could raise or call, making my position even more difficult. As the chip leader, I prefer to avoid high-variance situations with weak holdings in multi-way pots. Folding here is the most disciplined play to protect my stack.

I have KJ high with no pair, no draws on Qd3h5c5d. FiveTwo's 500 bet likely represents at least a pair or a 5 for trips. With only two overcards and no equity besides catching a K or J (which might not even be good), calling here is burning chips. Clear fold.

Call 500. Pot is 1700 and he’s betting a small 500 into a now heads‑up pot (I get ~22.7% pot odds). I only have Ace‑high with Ad2h and only 3 outs to improve to top pair on the river (~6.5%), so purely on raw equity I’m behind vs most value hands (Qx, 3x, any 5). But the bet size is small and coming from the BB who called preflop — his turn range can contain thin value and a fair number of bluffs/probes. Folding here surrenders a sizable pot for a cheap price; raising OOP with just Ace‑high would be a bad bluff and unnecessarily bloats the pot. I’ll call to see the river and re‑evaluate: if I hit an Ace I’ll extract value; if I miss I’ll fold to a large river bet but can call a small river probe or check through to showdown.

I missed — Ad2h has no pair on the final Q♦ 3♥ 5♣ 5♦ Q♠ board, so I only have Ace‑high. FiveTwo showed strength by raising the turn and I called; that range is heavily weighted toward Qx/5x/sets or at least hands that improved. Bluffing OOP into a player who provoked action is thin and likely to be called by any Queen, Five or a reasonable showdown hand. With essentially no equity and no fold equity against a reasonable turn‑raiser, the +EV line is to avoid bloating the pot: check and see what FiveTwo does. If they bet a thin probe I’ll fold to a value‑sized bet and only entertain calling an extremely small river probe; otherwise check through to showdown.

River pairs the Q giving us a very strong hand: 5♠5♣5♦QQ (fives full of queens). In this line (flop checks through, I bet turn and only Minni calls), her range is heavy on Qx (now beats us with queens full), but also contains plenty of worse hands that will still pay: any 5x (chops), 3x (now a smaller full house), and pocket pairs/QQ55x type holdings that may bluff-catch. We should value bet, but keep sizing modest to (1) get called by those worse bluff-catchers and (2) avoid overexposing ourselves if Minni is trapping with a queen and check-raises big.

Fold. I have A♦2♥ — only ace‑high on a Q♦ 3♥ 5♣ 5♦ Q♠ board. FiveTwo raised the turn to 500 (I called) and now makes a small 900 river bet into ~3100; on the turn any Q or 5 already becomes a full house, so his line is strongly value‑weighted. Calling 900 to win ~4000 requires ~22.5% equity, which I don’t have — only a bluff call would make sense and this line is unlikely to be a bluff. Raising would be a thin, high‑risk bluff with little fold equity. Preserve the stack and fold to this river value bet.

3c 4s is a very weak hand - unsuited, low cards with no high card value. UTG position requires tight play as I have the entire table to act behind me. With only 33bb stack, I can't afford to waste chips on marginal hands. Easy fold.

I am in the Cutoff (UTG+1) in a 5-handed game with 9d8d, a suited connector. This is a standard opening hand from this position given my stack depth of roughly 55BB. I have been playing tight recently, which may increase my fold equity. Raising to 2.5BB targets the blinds and allows me to play a pot in position if the blinds call, or potentially take it down pre-flop. Folding is too passive for this hand in this position, and limping is generally suboptimal.

I’m on the button with Js7s facing Pro’s open to 250 (2.5x BB). Pot is 400 and I only need to call 250 with a 10,550 stack (~105 BB). J7s is a playable suited connector in position — it has flush/straight potential and plays well postflop. A tiny 3-bet to 400 is too small to generate meaningful fold equity and just invites calls or a cold 3-bet from the big-stack players; it’s not worth turning this hand into a confrontation out of position against likely callers. Calling preserves positional advantage, gives good implied odds to hit spades or a pair, and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: see the flop, barre l/c-bet or turn aggression only when I pick up equity or favorable texture; fold to heavy action on dry boards where I’m likely dominated.

62o plays terribly multiway and we’d be out of position against both the original raiser and the button caller. Even getting decent immediate odds to complete, our realization is very poor (dominated 6x, weak low pairs, bad playability) and we risk facing a squeeze from the big blind. With solid stack depth in a 5-handed spot, this is a clear discipline fold rather than bleeding chips from the small blind.

I am holding Q4o in the Big Blind. Pro raised from UTG+1 and Minni called on the button. While I am getting decent pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 650), Q4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position in a multi-way pot. Given that I have a significant chip lead, there is no need to involve myself in marginal situations where I'll likely be forced to fold on most flops or find myself dominated if I do hit a pair. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I hold Middle Pair (9s) on an Ace-high board (9h As 2s). While the Ace hits my perceived opening range, it also heavily favors the Button's flat-calling range (Ax, broadways). Being out of position against a deep-stacked opponent (Minni) who called pre-flop, my goal is pot control. Betting risks isolating myself against better hands (Ax) or getting raised by draws/strong hands, forcing a fold with a hand that has showdown value. Checking allows me to keep the pot manageable, evaluate Minni's action, and potentially catch a bluff or see a cheaper showdown.

Flop: A♠ 9♥ 2♠. I have J♠7♠ — a very strong drawing hand: four to a spade flush (9 outs) and some backdoor improvement. Pro checked; in position with a premium draw I want to semi-bluff and take the initiative. A half-pot bet pressures unpaired overcards and marginal holdings, generates fold equity, and builds the pot when I have ~35% equity if called. I’ll keep the sizing small (325) so I can fold to a sizable check‑raise or continue on favorable turn cards (spades or low non‑A cards).

I hold Middle Pair (Nines) on an Ace-high board. My check on the flop induced a half-pot bet from Minni. Minni's range is wide here; she could be value betting an Ace, but she could also be stabbing with flush draws (Spades), lower pairs, or pure air simply because I showed weakness on a board that favors the pre-flop caller's range. Given the price of 325 to win 975 (3:1 odds), I only need 25% equity. Against a range consisting of Ax, flush draws, and random floats, my hand has sufficient equity to call one street. Folding immediately to a standard stab with middle pair is too weak/exploitable. I will call to keep the pot manageable and re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold middle pair (Nines) on a board of 9h As 2s 4d. The turn card is a relative brick, changing little about the board texture other than adding a backdoor diamond draw which is negligible with one card to come. I am out of position against the pre-flop caller who bet the flop. Leading out here (donk betting) serves little purpose as worse hands will fold and better hands (Ax) will call or raise. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or catch a bluff. Checking to the aggressor is the standard line to control the pot size and keep my opponent's range wide, including missed flush draws and lower pairs.

I continue to semi‑bluff. I have J♠7♠ on A♠9♥2♠4♦ — a strong drawing hand with nine spade outs (≈19–20% to hit the river). The turn 4♦ is a low, non‑Ace card so it’s a good card to apply pressure: Pro’s calling range from UTG+1 (Ax, 9x, small pairs, some spade combos) will include many hands that will fold to a modest turn c‑bet (Kx, Qx, missed pairs/draws). Pot is ~1,300, so I size small to preserve fold equity and keep the pot manageable if called. A bet of 400 (~30% pot) continues to represent strength, folds out weaker holdings, and builds the pot when I have ~20% equity; if I get a substantial raise I will fold (I’m only a draw), if called I’ll re‑evaluate the river (value‑bet if I hit, otherwise proceed cautiously). Betting 400 maximizes my chance to win the pot now while keeping future options flexible.

I hold middle pair (Nines) on a board of 9h As 2s 4d. The turn card 4d is a blank. After my check, Minni bets 400 into a pot of 1300. This is a very small bet (roughly 30% of the pot). This sizing is often indicative of a blocking bet with a marginal hand (like a weak pair or a draw setting its own price) rather than a strong value hand like a strong Ace or better, which would likely size larger to extract value or deny equity. Given the pot odds, I only need about 19% equity to continue. My hand beats all her bluffs (missed flush draws, straight draws) and potentially some lower pairs. Even if she has a weak Ace, the price is too good to fold a pair here. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate.

I have improved to Two Pair (Nines and Eights) on the river, which is a strong hand given the action. Minni's small bet on the turn (400 into 1300) suggests a capped range, likely consisting of a weak Ace, a mid-pair, or a drawing hand trying to set a cheap price. Donk betting the river here is generally suboptimal as it folds out all her bluffs (missed spade draws) and might scare away the weak Aces I now beat. By checking, I induce her to either bluff with her missed draws or potentially make a thin value bet with a top pair hand thinking I only have a Nine. If she checks back, I am confident my Two Pair is good against her pot-control range. Therefore, checking is the best play to maximize value and minimize risk.

I missed my spade flush and have no pair on A♠ 9♥ 2♠ 4♦ 8♥ — J♠7♠ is just high card. Pro (UTG+1) opened pre and called both my flop and turn bets, which is exactly the line of a sticky top‑pair (Aˣ), a 9x, or a small pocket pair — hands that are unlikely to fold to a single river bluff. The pot is ~2,100. To make a bluff profitable I’d need a large enough fold rate (≈25–33% for typical 1/3–1/2 pot bets); given his passive calls earlier I don’t give myself that fold equity. No showdown value and no blocker to A, so the lowest variance +EV play is to check and preserve my stack. If he bets a very small amount I’ll re‑evaluate (fold to medium/large bets, consider a thin call only to very small bets where pot odds and his range justify it).

I am in UTG position with 5c 4d, an offsuit connector. This is a very weak hand to open from early position, especially with active players behind me. My stack is 66.5 BBs, so I should play solid poker and fold junk hands from early position to preserve chips for better spots.

5-handed table and UTG+1 in this game is effectively an early/mid position but ranges are wider than full-ring. K3s is marginal but has suit and a King-blocker; I want to seize the initiative against the blinds and keep the pot manageable. Most opens at this table have been around 250–300, so a standard 3x-type open to 300 is appropriate — it folds out worse hands and gives me position leverage vs the button. I will be cautious if Claude (32BB) or the deep stacks 3-bet; I can release to a big shove from a short, and proceed postflop versus single callers.

J7o is a dominated, low-playability hand (especially unsuited) versus a 3bb open from Minni in the CO/UTG+1 seat. Calling invites tough postflop spots where we make second-best jacks/sevens, and there’s also squeeze risk from the blinds (SB deep, BB shorter and capable of reshove). It’s not a good 3-bet bluff candidate either (poor blockers). Preserve stack and wait for a better BTN defend/3-bet spot.

I am holding a pocket pair of fours in the Small Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG+1. The cost to call is 250 chips, and the current pot is 450. My stack is very deep (over 270bb), giving me excellent implied odds to set-mine. If I hit a set, I can potentially stack Minni or Claude. While Claude in the Big Blind is short-stacked and might move all-in, the pot odds and the potential for a high-value hand on the flop make calling the most profitable play here.

J5o is a weak hand. While I'm getting ~3.5:1 pot odds (200 to win 700), J5 offsuit plays poorly multiway and out of position. I'll be dominated by better jacks and have no flush potential. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I have an underpair on a board with two broadway cards (Q and T). This flop likely connects well with Minni's UTG+1 opening range (which contains hands like AQ, KQ, TT, etc.). Being out of position with very little equity to improve, I will check to see how the pre-flop aggressor acts. My plan is likely to fold to any significant bet as the board is quite unfavorable for pocket fours.

I missed this flop with K3s (board: 6c Qh Td). The small blind called preflop with a wide range that contains many Qx/Tx/Kx and pocket pairs, so this texture hits his calling range well and my continuation bet would have poor fold equity. I’m in position and can control the action, so I’ll check to keep the pot manageable and gather information.
