Plan: call a small lead to use positional advantage and re-evaluate on the turn, fold to heavy pressure since I have little showdown value, and if he checks back I can look to bluff or value-bet only on turns that actually improve me (K/3 or a spade). Checking preserves my stack and keeps options open.

The turn card is a Jack, making the board 6c-Qh-Td-Jh. This is a highly coordinated board that likely connects with many hands in an UTG+1 opening range, such as broadway cards and potential straights. My pocket fours are now a very weak underpair with negligible equity. After checking the flop and seeing a check back, I will continue to check on the turn. I have no reason to bet here as I cannot represent a strong hand effectively, and I want to see a cheap showdown if possible, though I am ready to fold if Minni bets.

I have K♠3♠ on 6♣ Q♥ T♦ J♥ — I missed completely: only King-high with ~6 live outs to pair (three Ks and three 3s) and no flush draw. Flash (SB) called preflop and has checked flop and turn, so his range is heavy with Qx/Tx/Jx, Kx, and pocket pairs — this board hits his calling range well. A bet here would have very poor fold equity and will often get called by better hands, turning a marginal bluff into a bloated pot. Pot control is the priority: with 9,275 behind and a 700 pot I should avoid committing chips without a real hand or a clear fold target. I’ll check to see the river for free, preserve stack flexibility, and only bluff on very favorable rivers (K/3/spade or an Ace that can credibly represent a made hand) or fold to significant aggression. Therefore: check.

The river completes a very coordinated board (6-Q-T-J-9). Any King or Eight now makes a straight. Given Minni raised from UTG+1, her range is heavily weighted toward high broadway cards, many of which (like KQ, KJ, KT, AK, AJ, or even pocket 8s) have now improved to either a straight or a superior pair. My pocket fours have essentially zero showdown value here. I will check, and if Minni bets, I will fold, as I cannot profitably bluff into a range that connects so well with this board or win at showdown.

River completed 9♠ and my K♠3♠ now makes a Broadway straight (9‑10‑J‑Q‑K). This is a strong, but not absolute‑nut hand (a player holding A+K can make a higher straight; other players with a K tie). Villain (SB, Flash) has been passive check‑calling every street — a line consistent with one‑pair/top‑pair hands (Qx/Tx/Jx), small pairs, and occasional Kx/AK. The 9 on the river crushes most of that calling range, so betting for value is the highest EV play. Checking risks giving up value on many worse hands that will now call a river bet. I want a size that gets called by Qx/TX/Jx/9x and medium pairs but doesn’t overcommit me to unlikely river raises; ~2/3 pot is a good compromise. Action: value bet 450 into 700. If he raises dramatically, I’ll re‑evaluate (very large raises narrow his range to the combos that beat me or tie).

The board (6-Q-T-J-9) is extremely coordinated, with many straights possible (any King or any Eight). I have pocket fours, which is an underpair to almost the entire board. Minni, who raised pre-flop from UTG+1, has many Broadway cards in her range that have either made a straight or a superior pair. Even if she is betting for thin value with a hand like pocket sevens or eights, or even a random 6, she beats my hand. Given that I have almost zero showdown value on this board and Minni has shown aggression on the river after checking the flop and turn, I must fold. I am not getting the price to justify a hero call with the literal bottom of my range.

UTG with 4♣6♦ — a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Out of position against four opponents, this hand is frequently dominated and has poor post‑flop equity; there’s no strategic reason to limp or open‑raise from UTG, especially facing deep and potentially aggressive players (Button very deep, SB/BB capable of defending). Preserve stack and wait for a much stronger, playable UTG hand or a position advantage. Fold.

Td6h offsuit is too weak to enter from UTG+1 even 5-handed. Limping creates tough multiway spots with dominated top-pair potential, and opening will get defended often by the blinds (including a short SB who can jam) leaving us in poor shape. Preserve stack and wait for better open/steal candidates.

I am on the button with a massive chip lead. Both players in the blinds have significantly smaller stacks, particularly Claude in the Small Blind with about 31 big blinds. While 8c7s is a marginal hand, it's typically an opening candidate in an unopened pot from the button to steal the blinds. However, looking at previous hands (like Hand #79), Claude has shown a willingness to 3-bet from the blinds against my button opens. With 8-high offsuit, I don't have much post-flop equity if called, and I'd be forced to fold to any significant aggression (like a shove from Claude's short stack). Given my current position as the dominant chip leader, I can afford to fold the bottom of my range and wait for better opportunities to apply pressure without risking chips on a very weak holding.

Holding 2c6s in the SB with action folded to me. This is one of the worst hands in poker - offsuit, disconnected, no high card value. While I'm getting 3:1 to complete, this hand plays terribly postflop and has minimal equity even against a random big blind hand. Folding is the disciplined play rather than bleeding chips with junk.

We’re 5-handed but still first to act, and 6♥5♠ offsuit is a low-equity, reverse-implied-odds hand that plays poorly against calls and 3-bets. With a short-stacked button (Claude) who can punish opens by jamming, and an aggressive player behind (Flash), opening becomes a high-variance open/fold spot with limited upside. Limping is also unattractive since it invites isolation and multiway play. Best EV is to pass and preserve our stack for stronger/openable holdings.

In a 5-handed game, QJo is a solid opening hand from the UTG+1 position (effectively the Cutoff). I have the largest stack at the table and should look to apply pressure on the remaining players, especially with several shorter stacks behind me. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate to take control of the pot.

A6o on the button facing a 3x open from Flash. With only 32BB stack, this is a marginal spot. A6o plays poorly post-flop even with position. Flash has a massive stack (267BB) and has been active. I could 3-bet as a bluff but that commits too much with a weak hand. Calling builds a pot I can't easily navigate with this hand. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot.

I am in the Small Blind with As7s. The action is 5-handed. FiveTwo folded, and Flash raised to 300 from the Cutoff (UTG+1). Flash is the chip leader with a very deep stack and has been opening frequently, but has also shown a tendency to fold to re-raises pre-flop. Against a Cutoff open, A7s is a marginal hand from the Small Blind. While suited Aces have equity, playing out of position against a deep-stacked aggressor is difficult. Calling leaves me vulnerable to being squeezed by the Big Blind or dominated post-flop if an Ace hits (kickers like 8-K are in Flash's range). A 3-bet bluff is a viable option given Flash's fold frequency, but committing ~1000 chips (15% of my stack) with a marginal hand against the chip leader carries significant variance. Folding is the disciplined, low-variance play to preserve my healthy stack (66bb) for better spots.

I'm in the big blind with 8♥6♣ facing a UTG+1 open to 300. Pot = 450, I must call 200; calling would require ~31% equity (200 / (450+200) ≈ 30.8%). 8-6 offsuit has poor equity and playability out of position against an early-position open and is frequently dominated. 3-betting is a bad bluff here (no useful blockers, deep stacks, opponent is capable of calling/4-betting). Preserve my stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am UTG with 8s 5c, which is a very weak hand. In a 5-handed game, the opening range from UTG should be significantly stronger than this. Given my massive stack lead and the fact that there are several shorter stacks (especially Claude at ~31 BBs), there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a marginal hand from early position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
J3o is a very weak hand from UTG+1. With 32BB stack, I should fold this and wait for better spots.

I am on the Button with Jack of Diamonds and Deuce of Spades (Jd 2s). The action has folded to me. My stack is 66.5 BB. While the Button is an advantageous position to steal the blinds, J2o is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability. The blinds (Minni and FiveTwo) have large stacks (98 BB and 130 BB respectively) and are capable of defending or 3-betting. Opening with this hand is generally negative EV due to the high likelihood of domination or being forced to fold to aggression. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold.

I'm in the small blind with Kc Jh heads-up vs the big blind. This is a strong, playable hand that should be raised for initiative rather than simply completing. Raising to 300 total (3 BB) applies pressure, denies cheap odds to draws and weak broadways from the BB, and still keeps the pot controllable with deep stacks. If called I'll continuation-bet advantageous flops (K/J or dry boards) and play more cautiously on coordinated/ace-high boards; if I face a sizable 3-bet I'll fold (or re-evaluate vs a small 3-bet depending on sizing and read). Overall this raise balances value and fold equity while avoiding playing passively out of position.

Minni’s small blind raise is likely fairly wide, but 93 offsuit is near the bottom of any reasonable BB defend range and plays very poorly out of position (dominated when we make a pair, little ability to realize equity). Calling 200 to see a flop doesn’t have enough equity/realization, and turning it into a 3-bet bluff risks getting 4-bet jammed or called and forced to navigate a bloated pot with a trash hand. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Q6o from UTG is a clear fold. This hand is too weak to open from early position, especially with 31.75 BB stack where I need to be selective. It plays poorly postflop and will often be dominated by better queens.

I hold 7c 3s in UTG+1 (Cutoff), which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With three players yet to act behind me, including the blinds, opening with this hand has a very negative expected value. There are no situational factors justifying a loose open here. Folding is the only standard play.

On the button with 8♣3♥ and only the blinds left, this is a classic spot to attempt a steal. Hand has very little showdown value, but my positional advantage and the deep stacks (~100 BB) make an open profitable vs two players in the blinds who often fold to pressure. I’ll use a standard 3x BB open (300) to reduce caller frequency and take the initiative. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet, but if I get one caller I can c-bet many flops from position and try to win the pot as a bluff; avoid bloating the pot with this weak hand.

Minni is opening from the button and has shown a fairly active raising frequency. 8d5d has reasonable equity and good playability (can make straights/flushes), and we’re getting a direct price to defend (250 to win 700 after calling). From the small blind I don’t love 3-betting this particular suited hand because it has no strong blockers and can end up playing a bloated pot out of position versus a button range that can continue. Best line is to defend by calling and keep the pot manageable; if the BB squeezes big we can comfortably release.

I am in the Big Blind holding JcTs, which is a strong enough hand to defend against a Button raise and a Small Blind call. The pot is currently 700 and it costs me 200 to call, giving me 3.5:1 pot odds. Both Minni and FiveTwo are active, and with my deep stack of 27,025, calling to see a flop in position against the Small Blind is a solid play. I am not looking to 3-bet here as J-10 offsuit doesn't play exceptionally well against a 4-bet, but it has great playability on many board textures.

We’re 3‑way and out of position with 8♦5♦ on A♦T♣9♥. This board strongly favors Minni’s button-raising range (lots of Ax/Tx/broadways) and also connects with Flash’s BB defend. Our hand has no made value, no meaningful direct draw (only backdoor diamonds/backdoor straight possibilities), and doesn’t block many continues. Donk-betting into two players would burn chips and get called/raised too often. Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and respond to sizing (mostly folding vs significant action, continuing only versus very small bets with our backdoors).

On this flop of 9h-Ad-Tc, I have middle pair with a Jack kicker (Ts Jc) and a backdoor straight draw (9-T-J). FiveTwo has checked from the Small Blind. Given that Minni was the pre-flop raiser from the Button, it is likely she has an Ace or will continue with a C-bet. Checking is the most solid play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts and to control the pot size with a medium-strength hand.

Flop is 9♥ A♦ T♣ and I hold 8♣3♥ — no pair, no real straight draw and only a single heart (no realistic flush). I opened preflop and got two callers, so I'm facing multiway ranges full of Ax, KQ/TJ type broadways, tens/nines and pocket pairs. Bluffing here has poor fold equity multiway and I don’t block Ax/Tx combos. With ~96 BB effective stacks and a 900-chip pot, I should control the pot size and avoid turning a marginal hand into a big bluff fight. Checking preserves my stack, lets me see a free turn, and fold to meaningful aggression (or occasionally call a tiny probe) rather than bloating the pot with almost no equity. Action: check.

We’re 3‑way OOP with 8♦5♦ on A♦T♣9♥7♠ after the flop checked through. The turn gives us only a weak gutshot to a 6 (4 outs) and essentially no showdown value. Betting into two players here is high-variance: any Ace/Tx/9x continues a lot, and the turn also completes 86 for a straight (a hand both opponents can have), so we risk getting called or check-raised too often with little equity and poor barreling prospects. Taking the free card keeps the pot controlled, preserves stack, and lets us realize our small equity; if it checks through again we can reassess river (value if we hit 6, otherwise selective bluffs on good runouts).

On a board of Ad-Tc-9h-7s, I hold middle pair (Tens) with a Jack kicker and a gutshot straight draw (needing an Eight). The flop checked around, which significantly decreases the likelihood that anyone is holding an Ace, as the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) would typically C-bet that texture. However, with three players in the pot, my Tens are a marginal made hand. Checking is the most prudent play here to control the pot size and see a river card. If the action checks through again, I can potentially value bet a safe river or call a reasonable bet from Minni, who might be attempting a delayed bluff or value betting a weaker pair.

I have 8♣3♥ on A♦9♥T♣7♠ — essentially air. No pair, no real straight/flush draw (only a runner J or 6 would give me a straight). This is a checked multiway pot (SB + BB), so bluffing the turn has very poor fold equity and will be called by a lot of Ax/Tx/9x and pairs. Pot control is priority with ~96BB effective and a 900-chip pot. I’ll check to keep the pot small and see a free river; I’ll fold to any meaningful bet on the river unless the board bricks and a very cheap probe appears or the river completes a clear draw that I can credibly represent.

River comes AAT97A after two streets of checks in a 3-way pot. Our 8♦5♦ has zero showdown value and, crucially, we don’t block any of the most common calls (Ax). Both BTN open and BB defend ranges contain a lot of aces, and the double-check line also keeps plenty of slow-played Ax in everyone’s range. In a multiway spot with no relevant blockers and a board that heavily favors opponents’ continuing ranges, a bluff lead will get looked up too often for poor risk/reward. Best play is to check and concede when we miss.

On a board of 9h-Ad-Tc-7s-Ac, I hold Tens and Aces with a Jack kicker. The flop and turn both checked through, which strongly suggests that neither Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) nor FiveTwo holds an Ace. However, with the river doubling the Ace, the value of my Tens decreases slightly as it's now easier for a random King-high or a small pocket pair to have showdown equity or for a slow-played Ace to finally reveal itself. Given the pot is small (900) and I have solid showdown value with second pair, checking is the most logical move. There isn't much value to be gained by betting, as weaker hands like a 9 or a 7 are unlikely to call, and better hands like JJ-KK or a slow-played Ace will certainly call or raise. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown.

I have 8♣3♥ on A♦A♣T♣9♥7♠ — no pair and essentially no showdown value. This was a multiway pot (both blinds called my open), so my fold equity as a bluffer is very poor and the river pairing the ace improves opponents’ calling ranges (Ax, tens, pocket pairs). The pot is small (~900) relative to deep stacks, so any bet large enough to get folds would be unnecessarily bloated with no equity. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve my stack; if facing a bet I will fold rather than bluff at a multiway caller-heavy spot.

I am in UTG position with 6s 5d, which is a weak unsuited connector. Given the early position and full table of 5 active players, this hand falls well outside a profitable opening range. I have 66.5 BBs, so there is no desperation to make moves with marginal holdings. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Jc7d in UTG+1 is a weak, offsuit jack and I'm effectively in early position in a 5-handed game. The hand has poor playability and will often be dominated or put in awkward OOP decisions in multiway pots. Calling 100 to limp in invites 3-bets/isolations from deeper players and a possible shove from Claude (BB) who is relatively short (~30bb). With ~96bb I should conserve chips for better spots and avoid marginal hands in early position, so folding is the optimal play.

Button vs blinds with two folds, T8s is comfortably in an opening range. We want to leverage position and initiative to pick up the dead money, while keeping the price small against Claude’s shorter stack (possible shove) and Flash’s wider 3-bet tendencies. A small open accomplishes this: strong steal frequency, good playability when called, and manageable loss when we have to fold to aggression.

I am in the Small Blind with A9o. The Button (FiveTwo) has opened with a standard raise to 225. A9o is a strong enough hand to defend against a Button opening range, but playing it out of position by just calling can be tricky, especially with the Big Blind (Claude) sitting on a relatively short stack of 3075 chips (about 31 BBs). If I call, I invite Claude to shove or see a cheap flop. By 3-betting to 750, I take the initiative, put pressure on the Button's wider opening range, and force Claude to have a very strong hand to continue. If the Button calls, I have the betting lead heading into the flop. If Claude shoves, I will have to evaluate based on the pot odds, but for now, the 3-bet is the most proactive play to maximize the value of my hand and stack advantage.

JTs is a playable hand but facing a 3-bet to 750 from Flash (huge stack of 26k+) while I'm OOP in the BB with only 3075 chips is problematic. Calling commits over 20% of my stack preflop and I'll be playing OOP against an aggressive player. JTs plays better multiway and in position. Given stack dynamics and position, folding is the disciplined play here.

We opened the button and face a relatively small 3-bet from Flash in the SB, with Claude (BB) already folding so we’re heads-up and in position. Given Flash’s demonstrated aggression/3-bet tendencies, his range here should be fairly wide; T8s has solid playability IP (can realize equity well, make strong draws, and apply pressure on favorable textures). Folding would overfold vs this sizing, while 4-betting turns a hand with good realization into a bluff that risks getting 5-bet/put in tough spots deep. Best is to defend by calling and use position to outplay postflop.

On this low, disconnected board of 2h-3h-6c, I still hold the initiative as the pre-flop 3-better. While the flop doesn't directly hit my A9o, it likely missed a significant portion of the Button's calling range as well. I will lead with a continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot to represent a strong overpair (like 77-AA) and to fold out better hands like small pocket pairs or overcards that have equity against me. If called, I can evaluate the turn based on the card and the opponent's behavior.

We have a 9-out heart flush draw (plus some thin backdoor/runout possibilities) in position versus a very small c-bet (650 into 2250). Our pot odds are excellent (~22% needed), and we have ~35% equity to the river when clean, so folding would be a big mistake. Raising as a semi-bluff is less attractive here because (1) his sizing is designed to let him continue wide, so we won’t generate enough folds, and (2) when called/3-bet we often isolate ourselves versus overpairs and higher-heart combos, reducing our implied odds. Calling keeps his range wide, preserves positional advantage, and lets us apply pressure on favorable turns (hearts, overcards) while comfortably continuing versus reasonable barreling sizes.

The turn brings a King of Spades, which is a card that theoretically favors my 3-betting range (as it contains hands like AK and KK). However, after being called on both the pre-flop and the flop, FiveTwo's range likely includes pocket pairs (44-TT), suited connectors like 67s, or heart draws. While a second barrel could potentially force a fold from some middle pairs, my Ace-high still has some showdown value against missed draws. By checking, I am controlling the size of the pot and hoping to see a cheap river or catch a bluff. If FiveTwo bets, I will likely have to fold given the strength shown by calling two streets, but checking is the most cautious and strategic move here to avoid over-committing with a marginal hand.

Turn goes check to us in a 3-bet pot on 2h3h6cKs holding Th8h. We have a strong but non-nut flush draw; betting here can win the pot sometimes, but it also (a) risks getting check-raised off our equity by his stronger range (AK/sets/overpairs with Ah), and (b) inflates the pot in spots where our made flush can be dominated by Axh/Kxh. With position and a free card offered, the highest-EV/lowest-risk line is to take the card, realize our equity, and keep his range wide—then decide river based on whether we hit and whether he checks or leads.

On the river, the board is 2h-3h-6c-Ks-4c. I hold Ace-high (As9d). After 3-betting pre-flop and leading on the flop, I checked the turn when the King arrived. FiveTwo checked back on the turn, which suggests they likely do not have a King and are either holding a marginal made hand (like a middle pocket pair or a 6) or a draw that missed (like hearts). My Ace-high has significant showdown value against missed draws but is unlikely to get a better hand to fold or a worse hand to call if I bet. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown cheaply or potentially catch a bluff from a missed draw. If FiveTwo bets, I will have to evaluate their sizing and the likelihood of a bluff, but for now, checking is the optimal move.

We have pure T-high with no pair and no flush/straight, so checking back is basically conceding the pot to any A-high, pocket pair, or Kx that took this check-check line. Flash’s double-check (turn and river) in a 3-bet pot after c-betting small strongly caps him toward give-ups and medium showdown hands (AQ/AJ, TT–99, some 7x–8x) more than nutted hands, so this is a good spot to apply positional pressure.

Our perceived range can credibly contain Kx (that improved on the turn), overpairs that slowplayed, and some sets/2p, so a polarized stab should get folds from the bulk of his missed broadways and many medium pairs. I’ll bet ~70% pot to avoid giving his bluff-catchers an easy call while keeping risk controlled vs a check-raise (we can fold to heavy aggression).

On a board of 2h-3h-6c-Ks-4c, I am holding As9d (Ace-high). I was the pre-flop 3-better and led the flop, but checked the King turn and the river. FiveTwo checked back the turn but has now fired a large bet of 2000 into a 2900 pot (roughly 69% of the pot). While FiveTwo has shown a tendency to be aggressive on the river in previous hands, my Ace-high is an extremely weak bluff-catcher here. FiveTwo's range for calling a 3-bet and a flop continuation bet includes many pocket pairs (44, 55, 77, 88, TT) and suited connectors (like 67s) that have me beaten. Furthermore, the river 4 completes a straight for any A5s or 57s that might have stayed in. Even if FiveTwo is bluffing with missed hearts, I only beat those specific air hands, and I lose to any 'bluff' that happened to pair a small card. Given the pot odds and the strength shown by the bet size, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my large stack.

UTG in a 5-handed game is effectively an early position — I’ll be out of position postflop against the whole table. Q♠4♠ is marginal: suited helps, but the Q is weak and the 4 is a poor kicker. There’s frequent preflop aggression at this table (Flash and FiveTwo open/3‑bet a lot) and Claude is a short stack that can call or shove, which makes raising here vulnerable. Limping/calling to see a flop keeps me multiway out of position with a hand that plays poorly multiway. With ~97 BBs I can afford to be selective and wait for a better spot in position, so folding is the +EV play.

First-in from UTG+1 (5-handed), 84s is a marginal suited gapper that struggles to realize equity out of position versus the blinds and especially versus an aggressive, deep-stacked Button (Flash) who can 3-bet frequently and put us in tough spots. As the clear #2 stack, we don’t need to take thin opens that invite pressure and force too many preflop folds to 3-bets. I’ll pass and preserve stack for better steal/runout opportunities.

I am on the button with 83s. While I have the chip lead and positional advantage, this hand is extremely weak. Claude in the small blind has a relatively short stack (30 BBs) and might look to shove over a button raise. Pro is also capable. There's no need to play such a marginalized hand in this spot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

72o is one of the worst hands in poker. Even though it's heads up against just the big blind and I'm getting decent pot odds to complete, this hand has almost no playability - it's offsuit, not connected, and has no high cards. Better to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

5-handed, AKo is near the top of my UTG opening range and plays best with initiative. Limping gives up fold equity and invites multiway action where AKo’s equity realization drops. I’ll open to a standard sizing that pressures the blinds and keeps my range uncapped while still allowing me to respond appropriately versus a 3-bet (call in position vs reasonable sizes, 4-bet vs polar/large or short-stack jams).

I am holding 9c 5h, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has opened from UTG with a raise to 250. Being in UTG+1, I have the entire table behind me and am facing a raise from one of the tightest positions. There is no strategic reason to play this hand, so folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my chip lead.
9-6 offsuit is a weak hand. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, this is a clear fold even on the button. The hand has poor playability and little value against an early position raising range.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kd) in the Small Blind, a premium hand. FiveTwo has opened from UTG to 250. To maximize value and reduce the field (isolating the opener and pushing out the Big Blind), I should 3-bet rather than call. Out of position, a standard 3-bet size is roughly 3-4x the opening bet. A raise to 850 is consistent with my previous play (e.g., Hand #3) and builds the pot effectively while denying equity to the Big Blind.

Facing an UTG open to 250 and an immediate 3-bet to 850 from the small blind (Pro). I'm on the big blind with Jh7c — a weak, easily dominated offsuit hand with poor postflop playability and bad blockers for a bluff. Pot is 1,200 and calling 750 gives ~38.5% immediate pot odds, but my equity versus a typical SB 3-bet (value-heavy: pairs, broadways, AK/AQ) is well below that and would be worse in a multiway pot if the original raiser comes along. Raising to 1,450+ would be a light bluff with little fold equity and poor postflop prospects versus these stacks. With position disadvantage, marginal equity, and the risk of getting squeezed or outplayed postflop, the +EV play is to preserve stack and fold.

With AhKs facing a small SB 3-bet to 850, we’re at the top of our continuing range and want to play for value while denying equity. Calling keeps in dominated broadways and plays fine with position, but a 4-bet is higher EV here because (1) Pro’s 3-bet sizing gives him room to 3-bet a bit wider, (2) we isolate and often win preflop, and (3) the effective stack is only 5,850, so we can comfortably 4-bet to a size that sets up a profitable call vs a jam (we’d need ~30% equity and have well over that versus typical shove ranges). I’ll 4-bet to a pressure/value size that still allows him to continue with worse and makes his jam decision clear.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kd) in the Small Blind, a premium monster hand. After 3-betting to 850, the original raiser (FiveTwo) has 4-bet to 2200. With a starting stack of 5850 (approx 58 BB), I effectively have ~50 BB behind. Facing a 4-bet, my range with KK is to get all the money in pre-flop. Calling would leave an awkward Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of less than 1 out of position, making it difficult to navigate post-flop if an Ace appears. Shoving (5-bet jamming) maximizes value against hands like QQ, JJ, and AK, and ensures I realize my equity fully. I am only behind AA, which is a standard cooler at this stack depth.

We’re at the very top of our range with AKo after 4-betting, and versus a 5-bet this is still a mandatory continue. Pot odds are strong: calling 3,650 more to win ~11,800 (about 31% required). Even against a tight 5-bet range like {QQ+, AK}, AKo has ~40% equity, and if Pro ever includes some bluffs or worse value (AQs occasionally), the call improves further. Folding would be far too tight given the price and our preflop line committing us to continue with this hand class.

I have an extremely low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of roughly 0.07 (850 chips remaining in a 11,800 pot) after 5-betting pre-flop with Pocket Kings. I hold an overpair on a Q-8-10 board with a flush draw. I am completely pot-committed and will never fold for this price. Checking risks giving a free card to hands like AK or underpairs that might check back. Betting all-in immediately realizes my equity, charges draws, and extracts value from hands like JJ or AQ. My holding of the Kc also gives me a blocker to flush draws. The correct play is to put the remaining chips in.

Pro’s 850 flop bet appears to be his last chips (stack shows 0), so this is effectively an all-in for a tiny fraction of the pot. With pot 12,650 and 850 to call, I’m getting ~15:1 and need only ~6% equity.

Even versus the tightest 5-bet range (AA/KK/QQ/AK), AhKs has ample equity: we have 4 clean J outs to Broadway by the river (~16% to hit by showdown), plus some chop equity when he also has AK and occasional additional runouts. Folding would be a massive mistake at this price, and raising is unnecessary since he’s already all-in.

I’ll call and realize my equity.
5h9d is a weak offsuit hand from UTG. With 30BB effective stack, I need to play tight from early position. This hand has no real value - not suited, not connected, no high cards. Easy fold.

Td5s is a weak, marginal hand but I'm on the button with ~96bb — position is valuable. Pot is 150 and the cost to see a flop is 100. I want to take the initiative and pick up the blinds rather than play a multiway pot with a poor hand. I’ll open to a respectable 3xBB (300 total) to generate fold equity and avoid being priced in; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold, and if I'm called I’ll use my positional advantage to C-bet favorable flops or control pot size and fold to heavy resistance on dangerous boards.

KQs (suited) is well ahead of a Button opening range 4-handed and plays strongly as an isolation 3-bet. Calling from the SB invites the BB in and forces us to play multiway and out of position; 3-betting lets us take initiative, deny equity, and win the pot preflop a good portion of the time. Versus Minni’s shown willingness to apply pressure, I’ll use a slightly larger OOP sizing to reduce the BB’s calling frequency and still keep worse hands in (KJ/QJ, suited connectors, weaker broadways).

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 6c, a speculative suited connector. Minni opened from the Button to 3 BB, and FiveTwo 3-bet to 12 BB from the Small Blind. This 3-bet size is quite large. While 5c 6c can play well in some deep-stack scenarios, calling a large 3-bet out of position against a player who has shown high aggression (like FiveTwo in Hand #90) is generally a losing play. I would be forced to hit the flop hard to continue, and I'll be playing the rest of the hand without position. The pot odds (1100 to win 1600) are not favorable enough to justify a call with this hand strength. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I opened to 300 on the button with Td5s and FiveTwo (SB) 3‑bet to 1200. The pot is 1600 and it costs me 900 to call, so my pot odds are roughly 900/(1600+900) ≈ 36%. Td5s is a weak offsuit hand that is often dominated by a standard 3‑bet range (pairs, A‑X and broadways), so its equity versus that range is well below the required ~36%. A min 4‑bet to 2100 would be an ill-advised bluff with poor hand quality and could lead to unnecessary commitment against a deeper stacked opponent. I planned to fold to a large 3‑bet, preserve my ~92bb stack, and wait for a better spot in position. Folding is the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value play here.

UTG with Q♠4♥ (offsuit) — a weak, easily dominated hand. With 92 BB effective depth and three players to act behind (including two very aggressive, deep stacks), limp-or-open will often put me OOP in multiway pots or face 3-bets/shoves I’m poorly priced to play. Claude in the BB is short (~29 BB) and could create awkward shove/call dynamics. Best play is to preserve my stack and wait for a much stronger spot in position. Fold.

3-handed with Minni folded, we have a profitable button-steal spot. K7o isn’t a premium, but it has decent high-card value and benefits a lot from position. Both blinds can defend/3-bet (Flash is deep and active; Claude is shorter and may jam), so I want a sizing that pressures folds without risking too much. I’ll open to 2.5x and play straightforward versus aggression (fold to a big 3-bet or a BB jam).

I am in the Small Blind with K8o. FiveTwo, who has been quite active and aggressive recently, opened from the Button to 250. While K8o has some high-card value, playing it out of position against an aggressive player is difficult. Furthermore, Claude in the Big Blind is very short-stacked (less than 30 BBs) and is likely to shove a wide range of hands over a call or a raise. I don't want to commit chips to a pot only to be forced out by a short-stack shove or to play a marginal hand out of position. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I have T3o in the BB facing a button open to 250. This is a very weak hand - T3o is near the bottom of playable hands even against a wide button range. While I'm getting 2.7:1 odds (150 to win 400), T3o plays poorly postflop and will rarely make strong hands. FiveTwo has been active and has a massive stack advantage. Folding is the disciplined play here.

4-handed UTG ranges are wider and A9o is strong enough to open for value and initiative. Raising also pressures the blinds, especially the short-stacked SB, and lets us win many pots preflop/with a c-bet when we miss. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x open to reduce overly wide BB defense while keeping risk controlled.

I am on the button with 7-4 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. FiveTwo has raised from UTG. Given the poor strength of my hand and the fact that I am facing a raise from early position, the standard and correct play is to fold. There is no strategic reason to commit chips to the pot with this holding.
Q4o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. I'm out of position with a dominated queen and a weak kicker. With 2875 stack, I shouldn't be calling 200 chips here with such poor equity. Easy fold.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 250 (2.5bb) — a range that will be fairly strong from early position. I hold Tc4s: offsuit, poorly connected, and heavily dominated by most Ten-x and broadway hands. Calling 150 into a 400 pot requires ~27% equity; versus a standard UTG open my hand’s equity is well below that and it has terrible postflop playability out of position. A bluff 3-bet with this holding is thin and unnecessary with a healthy ~91bb stack. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am holding 3c 2h in the UTG position. This is the absolute weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em and provides virtually no value or playability, especially from early position. Even though the stack sizes of some opponents like Claude are relatively small, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

K8o on the button with ~29BB. Flash folded, so it's folded to me. This is a standard button open - K8o is easily in my stealing range against just the blinds. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to try to take down the blinds or play in position if called.

I'm in the small blind with Tc8d, facing a button open to 250 (I must call 200 more). This is a marginal offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position — and with the big blind deep behind, this is likely to become a multiway pot or force tough decisions if there's a 3-bet. Claude still has ~26 BB and could shove or apply pressure; I have a comfortable stack (≈91 BB) so there's no reason to spew chips with a weak hand. I'll fold and wait for a better spot or positioned hand to defend/3-bet.

Claude is very short (2625) and opening button wide; calling gives him a cheap look at a flop with decent realization while we’re OOP. A9o is strong enough versus a wide BTN range to 3-bet for value/pressure, deny equity, and set up a low SPR where we can often continue profitably. I’ll use a sizing that leverages his stack without overcommitting unnecessarily.

K8o facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo. With only ~26BB effective and K8o being a weak offsuit hand, this is a clear fold. K8o doesn't have good playability against a 3-bet range and calling would commit too much of my stack with a marginal hand. FiveTwo has shown aggression throughout the tournament.
2-5 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. I'm in UTG with 26BB - this is a clear fold. No reason to open or limp with such a weak hand from early position.

I’m on the button with K♥T♣ (a playable broadway hand) and 91bb effective. Only the SB and BB to act — this is an ideal spot to apply pressure and pick up the blinds or play a multiway pot in position. A standard 3x open (300) gives good fold equity against the blinds, keeps weaker hands (and some blind defenses) honest, and leaves plenty of stack for postflop play. Plan: proceed postflop with position — c-bet good textures; fold to large 3-bets that commit a big portion of my stack.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3bb BTN open with 8♣4♦. This hand has poor playability, is frequently dominated when we make a pair, and has no useful blockers for an aggressive 3-bet bluff (unlike Ax/Kx). Flat-calling also invites the big blind to come along, creating a multiway pot out of position with a weak offsuit hand. With plenty of stack depth and better candidates to defend/3-bet, the highest EV choice is to fold and preserve our edge.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Seven of diamonds, a strong suited hand that plays well in a heads-up pot. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button, which is a standard opening range. I am getting excellent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450) and have a significant chip lead over Minni. While 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my position's defensive equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a medium stack.

On a board of 10-9-7 with two hearts, I have flopped bottom pair (sevens) with a King kicker. This is a marginal made hand on a very coordinated and wet board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check to the raiser. Leading out would likely only get called by better hands or draws that have significant equity against me. By checking, I can see how Minni proceeds and decide whether to call a bet based on the sizing and potential turn cards. This approach helps control the size of the pot with a hand that has limited showdown value.

I have top pair (T) with a very strong kicker (K♥) on T♥‑9♣‑7♥. I’m heads‑up vs the BB who check‑called my preflop raise, so his defending range is wide (broadways, suited connectors, some pairs and Tx). The board has straight potential (8 completes the open‑ender; J/6 are gutshots) and two hearts, so I need to charge draws and get value from worse tens/medium pairs. Betting for value and protection is the right line. Size to ~1/2 pot (325 into 650) — big enough to charge 8/4‑out draws and extract calls from worse Ts and 9x/7x, but small enough to keep maneuvering post‑turn with deep stacks. Plan: bet 325; if called re‑evaluate on the turn and continue vs draws or barrel safe runouts; fold or proceed cautiously to a large check‑raise which likely shows straights/sets.

On a wet flop of 10-9-7 with two hearts, I have bottom pair (sevens) with a King kicker. Minni's continuation bet of 325 into a 650 pot is a standard sizing. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, which is sufficient for a bottom pair even on this coordinated board. I will call to see a turn card, hoping to either improve to trips or two pair, or for the action to check through if a non-threatening card peels. Raising here would likely only isolate me against hands that have me beat or strong draws with significant equity, so calling is the most prudent defensive play with a marginal made hand.

The turn brings the four of hearts, completing a potential flush and further coordinating an already wet board (10-9-7-4 with three hearts). My bottom pair of sevens has very little relative value now and is vulnerable to almost any semi-bluff or made hand in Minni's range. Betting into the pre-flop aggressor here would turn my hand into a weak bluff, which is unnecessary. I will check to control the pot and evaluate Minni's response. If she bets, I will likely have to fold given the strength of the board and the fact that many of her draws have now arrived.

Turn (T♥ 9♣ 7♥ 4♥): I have top pair with the best kicker (T with K♥) and a 4‑card heart draw (9 outs) — a very strong hand here. Flash (BB) cold‑called preflop and my flop c‑bet, so his calling range is wide (worse tens, 9x/7x, pocket pairs, hearts and some connectors). Betting for value and protection is the right line: we extract calls from worse tens/pairs and charge straight/heart draws while building the pot in position. I’ll size up to ~60% pot to deny correct odds to draws but still keep worse hands in — bet 800 into the 1,300 pot. Plan: if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and go for value on blanks or a made flush; if he check‑raises large on the turn I’ll respect that as a very strong/rolled hand (flush/straight/set) and fold to avoid being crushed.

On the turn, the four of hearts completes a potential flush and further coordinates an already wet board (10-9-7-4 with three hearts). My bottom pair of sevens is now extremely vulnerable. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor and flop c-bettor, has followed up with a 60% pot-sized bet (800 into 1300). Given my lack of a heart and the high likelihood that Minni's range contains flushes, straights, or higher pairs, calling is no longer profitable. I am essentially drawing to two outs (a seven) and even then, I could still be beat by a flush or straight. To protect my stack and focus on better spots, I will fold.

4-handed table — open-raising ranges should be much wider than in a full ring game. K♦6♣ is marginal UTG in 9-handed but perfectly acceptable to open in a 4-handed spot to seize the initiative and pick up the blinds. Table precedent shows standard opens around 300, so I’ll raise to 300 (3bb) to be consistent and give myself post‑flop leverage. I’m deep (≈98bb) so I can play postflop, but I’ll be cautious vs a large 3‑bet from the deep stacks and will fold K6o to heavy pressure. If the short-stacked BB (~25bb) shoves, I’ll evaluate his range — call only if it looks very wide; otherwise fold. Postflop plan: c‑bet favorable, give up on highly coordinated boards, play aggressively when I pair my king or pick up strong equity.

Button with KK vs Minni’s UTG 3bb open is a clear 3-bet for value. We want to build a pot against the opener’s strong-but-not-only-premium range, deny equity to the blinds, and set up an easy stack-off if Minni 4-bet jams (effective ~95bb) or if Claude (25bb) back-jams. Sizing to 1000 pressures calls, discourages multiway, and still keeps worse hands in while enabling us to comfortably continue versus any shove.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 5s. Minni (UTG) opened to 300 and FiveTwo (BTN) 3-bet to 1000. 75s is a speculative hand that performs poorly when out of position against a 3-bet range. Given the strength shown by both players and the risk of the short-stacked Big Blind (Claude) shoving, folding is the most disciplined and +EV play to preserve my stack.
J6o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. I need to call 900 into a 1450 pot. J6o doesn't play well against a 3-betting range and has poor equity. Easy fold.

I opened to 300 UTG in a 4‑hand game but FiveTwo (button) 3‑bet to 1,000. The pot is 1,450 and it costs me 700 to call. I hold K♦6♣ — a hand that is easily dominated by a button 3‑bet range and has poor playability out of position. A minimum 4‑bet to 1,700 would be a thin bluff with very little backup equity and risking a bloated pot OOP. I already planned to fold K6o to heavy 3‑bet pressure, so I’ll preserve my ~9,500 stack for better spots and fold here.

4-handed from UTG, A9o is comfortably within an opening range. We have plenty of stack depth and good blockers (A) to take down blinds and play in position against the BB/Button. Claude is very short in the SB and may reshove; opening still profits and we can evaluate/call reasonably versus a wide SB jam given the pot odds and our ace-high equity. Use a standard size to apply pressure without over-investing.

Ten-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand with almost no playability. Even though I have position on the Button, calling or raising against an UTG open from FiveTwo with this holding would be a significant mistake. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
8s5c is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a UTG raise, I'm out of position with garbage. Clear fold.

I have T♠3♣ in the big blind facing an UTG raise to 250. This is a very weak, offsuit, poorly‑connected hand that will be dominated by a standard UTG opening range and has terrible playability out of position. The pot is 400 and calling costs 150, so I’d need ~27.3% equity (150 / (400+150)) to break even — far more than T3o will have vs an UTG range. A 3‑bet bluff here against a deep, likely strong opener is unnecessary and high variance. Prefer to conserve my ~94BB stack and wait for a better spot. Folding is the optimal play.

In a 4-handed game, Queen-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand to open from the Under-the-Gun (UTG) position. Given that FiveTwo in the Big Blind has been playing very aggressively with frequent 3-bets and Claude on the Button is short-stacked (~25 BB) and looking for a shoving opportunity, opening with such a weak hand is likely to lead to difficult situations out of position or a forced fold to a shove. Preserving my chip lead is a priority, so I will fold this hand.

Pocket deuces on the button with Flash folding. Standard spot to raise to steal the blinds or play in position if called. With ~25BB stack, a 2.5x raise to 250 is appropriate. Small pairs play well in position with set-mining potential.

I have 4♥5♣ — a weak, low unsuited connector. Claude opened to 250 from the button; pot is 400 and it’s 200 more to call. I’ll be out of position to both the button and the big blind postflop and a call here will often create a multiway pot where my hand has poor equity and playability. Claude’s open size is standard and he opens fairly wide, but 45o is still too flimsy to defend this spot profitably, and squeezing/3‑betting 4‑5o is a losing bluff against deep stacks here. With a healthy stack (9,350) there’s no need to risk chips in a dominated/multiway spot — fold and wait for a better positionable hand.

Claude is opening from the button with a very short stack (~22bb) and I cover. A3s is a strong defend and an excellent 3-bet candidate: it blocks Ax holdings, has nut-flush potential, and benefits a lot from fold equity. Versus a short-stack opener, I want to apply maximum pressure and often either win preflop or get it in with reasonable equity if he jams wider than just premiums. I’ll size to 800 to isolate and set up a low SPR situation.

Facing a 3-bet with pocket deuces. With 2225 stack, calling 550 more commits ~25% of my stack preflop. Set mining requires ~15:1 implied odds, but I'd have only ~1425 behind after calling into a 1650 pot - terrible implied odds. Pocket 2s don't play well OOP vs 3-bets with shallow SPR. Clear fold.
5d8h is a very weak offsuit hand from UTG. With only ~22 BBs, I need to be selective and this hand has no real playability from early position. Easy fold.

Button with A♣4♦ is a playable opener — I have position on the blinds and a real chance to steal or play postflop with initiative. With a healthy stack (~93 BBs) and deep, aggressive blinds who defend wide, I want a standard-sized isolation raise to take the lead and narrow the field. Raising to 300 (≈3×BB) is a conventional sizing: enough fold equity to pick up the blinds often, but small enough to keep playable postflop if called. Plan: c-bet good-looking flops and fold to significant 3-bets I’m not getting the right odds for; call or continue cautiously if I face big aggression out of position.

Minni is opening from the Button and will have a wide range. In the small blind, ATo plays awkwardly as a flat (we’re OOP vs the raiser and we invite the big blind in), but it performs well as a 3-bet: we deny equity to BTN/BB, often win the pot pre, and when called we can realize decent equity with strong blockers (A) and domination vs worse Ax/TX in a wide BTN range. With stacks deep enough, we can comfortably fold to a strong 4-bet range if needed. I’ll use a standard OOP sizing vs 3x open.

I am holding Ace-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind. The Button (Minni) opened for 300, and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) has 3-bet to 1200. While A2o has some raw equity, it is a very weak hand to play out of position in a 3-bet pot against two active opponents. FiveTwo has shown significant aggression in previous hands, and their 4x 3-bet from the Small Blind indicates a strong range or a willingness to polarize. If I call, I am likely dominated by any other Ace and will be playing a large pot out of position. Given I am the chip leader, there is no reason to take a high-variance marginal spot here. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I opened to 300 and FiveTwo (SB) 3‑bet to 1,200. The pot is ~1,600 and it costs me 900 to call, so I need ≈36% equity to break even. A♣4♦ offsuit is frequently dominated by a typical SB 3‑bet range (stronger Ax, broadways, pairs) and has poor post‑flop playability, so my realistic equity is below the threshold. 4‑betting would be a thin, high‑variance bluff against a deep, aggressive opponent. My earlier plan was to fold to significant 3‑bets when I don’t get the right odds, preserve my ~90BB stack, and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

UTG with 2♠9♥ — essentially one of the weakest starting hands. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call, so calling gives me 40% pot odds (100 into a 250 pot). 2-9o has far less equity than that vs reasonable calling/raising ranges and I'm at a positional disadvantage with three players to act (Button and both blinds). Open-raising here is reckless (vulnerable to 3-bets and dominated) and limping/calling is poor value. With a healthy stack (~90BB) there’s no need to gamble from UTG with trash — preserve chips for better spots. Action: fold.

Minni has folded and we’re on the Button 3-handed with position and a healthy stack. This is a prime spot to apply pressure and steal blinds, especially with Claude very short in the BB (likely to play shove/fold). Th4h is weak but suited and playable in position; raising keeps initiative and maximizes fold equity. I’ll use the minimum raise size to risk the least against a possible BB jam or SB 3-bet, planning to fold if either opponent comes back aggressively.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 4c. The button (FiveTwo) has opened for 200. My hand is extremely weak and doesn't play well out of position. Additionally, the Big Blind (Claude) is very short-stacked with only about 21 BBs, making him highly likely to shove over any non-fold action from me. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here to preserve my stack.

BB with Qh5h facing a button min-raise to 200. Getting 3.5:1 pot odds to call 100. Suited queen-high has some playability and I'm closing the action. FiveTwo has been active with raises. Standard defend from BB at these odds.

Flopped top pair with Q5h on Td Qd 6h. I have a weak kicker but hit the queen. Board has diamond flush draw. As the preflop caller, I should check to the raiser. If FiveTwo c-bets, I can call with top pair. Don't want to bloat the pot OOP with weak kicker.

Heads-up vs Claude’s BB defend range on Q♦T♦6♥, I have second pair with a weak kicker (T♥4♥). As the preflop raiser I retain a range advantage (more Qx/overpairs), and a small c-bet can fold out a lot of his air/A-high and some small pairs while denying equity to overcards/diamond floats. I don’t want to bloat the pot against a short stack where his check-jam range is mostly Qx/strong draws that dominate me, so I’ll use a low-risk sizing and comfortably fold if he shoves.

I flopped top pair queens on Td Qd 6h. FiveTwo made a small 1/3 pot c-bet of 150. With top pair weak kicker, I have a standard call - folding is too tight, raising bloats pot OOP with marginal hand. Board has diamond draw potential. Easy call and reassess on turn.

Turn brought the Ad completing a three-diamond board. My top pair queens is now second pair with weak kicker. Board is extremely dangerous - any diamond gives villain a flush, and the ace could have improved their range. I'm OOP with a marginal hand that's been significantly devalued. Standard check here - if FiveTwo bets, I'll likely have to fold given the board texture.

Turn A♦ is a major range-shift card: it strongly improves my perceived range (Ax, nut diamonds), but it also completes a lot of Claude’s flop continues (especially diamond draws) and gives him more Ax/2-pair possibilities than before. With Th4h I’m now sitting on a thin showdown hand that does poorly versus his likely continue/jam range if I bet (Qx, made flushes, strong draws). Since he’s short, a bet often just invites a check-shove where I’m forced to fold and burn chips. I’ll take the pot-control line, check back, realize equity, and reassess on the river (likely folding to a big jam, possibly calling a small stab).

River 7c is a blank. I have second pair queens with weak kicker on a 3-diamond board. FiveTwo checked back the turn after the Ad came, showing weakness or pot control. My hand has showdown value against missed draws and weaker pairs, but betting would only get called by better hands (flushes, aces, two pair). Standard check to get to showdown.

River goes check to me on Qd Td 6h Ad 7c holding Th4h (one pair, tens). My hand has decent showdown value versus Claude’s BB defend range that called a small flop c-bet then checked the ace turn (lots of 6x/7x, underpairs, and missed overcards/backdoors). A thin value bet is risky because Claude is short and his most natural response with many hands is a check-jam all-in; if that happens I’m forced to fold and burn chips with a marginal hand. Since there’s no flush completed (only 3 diamonds on board) and no obvious straight got there, checking back captures the pot frequently and avoids donating to a shove range that’s heavily Qx/Ax/two pair+ when he does go for it.

4-handed UTG is effectively a late position spot, so opening ranges widen a lot. 7♠6♠ is a strong enough suited connector to open because it realizes equity well postflop (straight/flush potential) and can win without showdown by taking down blinds. A raise also pressures Claude’s short SB stack and avoids going multiway via limping. If we face a shove/large 3-bet from the deeper stacks, this hand is not strong enough to continue and we can comfortably fold, so a small-but-standard open is best.

I am on the button with 5d 4d. FiveTwo, who has been very active in recent hands, raised to 2.5bb from UTG. While 54s is a speculative hand that plays well in position, calling an early position raise with it is marginal. Furthermore, Claude in the Small Blind is short-stacked with only about 25bb, making him a high risk for a squeeze or an all-in shove, which would force me to fold and lose my investment. Given the stack dynamics and the strength of my hand relative to an UTG opening range, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

T6 suited is a marginal hand, especially facing a UTG raise which typically indicates a stronger range. With only 26BB stack, I don't have great implied odds to chase a flush or straight draw. The hand doesn't play well multi-way if BB also calls. Folding is the disciplined play here.

FiveTwo opened to 250 UTG and both the button and SB folded — I’m effectively heads-up to an early raiser. My hand J♥7♦ is weak and unsuited and will be dominated a large portion of the raiser’s range. Calling 150 gives marginal pot odds but commits me to difficult OOP postflop decisions; a 3-bet would be an ambitious bluff vs a deep, aggressive opener. Stack preservation and table dynamics favor folding this marginal holding and waiting for a better spot.

I am UTG with 5d 6c, which is a very weak starting hand. In a 4-handed game, while ranges are wider, 56 offsuit still doesn't warrant an open from under the gun, especially with a very short-stacked player like Claude on the button and two other players behind me. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

On the button with T9o after UTG folded. This is a good stealing spot with a decent connected hand. My stack is ~26BB which is still healthy enough to make a standard raise. I'll raise to 250 to try to take down the blinds.

Facing a standard button open to 250 from Claude while I’m in the small blind with Kc Js and 89BB effective, I’ll just defend by calling to 250 (add 200). KJo is strong enough to see a flop and realize equity, but out of position vs the button I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet or a tiny reraise to 400 that invites the deep big blind to squeeze. Claude is relatively short (~23BB) so I’ll be ready to fold to heavy shove/3‑bet pressure preflop, but otherwise call keeps the pot manageable and lets me evaluate the flop. Plan: if FiveTwo folds and it’s heads-up vs Claude I’ll proceed cautiously OOP; if FiveTwo calls and we see a favorable flop I’ll take the lead when appropriate, otherwise control the pot and re-evaluate on later streets.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action: 150 to win a 600 pot (~20% immediate pot odds). 9c8h has solid multiway realization (straight potential, some pair+draw boards) and plays better as a defend than as a squeeze here. A 3-bet risks Claude (23bb) jamming and forcing us to fold after burning extra chips, and Minni’s cold-call range will contain hands that defend well vs a squeeze. Take the cheap flop and look to outplay postflop with positionless but strong equity realization spots.

Pot 750, board 5♥-3♥-Q♠. I hold K♣J♠: no pair, one overcard (K) and a marginal backdoor with a single spade; two hearts on board give opponents heart draws and the Q on board heavily favors many of the button’s opening ranges. I’m out of position and there are two opponents — a c-bet here is a marginal bluff and will be called too often. I prefer pot control and to realize equity OOP: check and see how the action develops. Plan: if checked through I’ll re-evaluate the turn (bet K-turn for value, otherwise keep controlling the pot); if I face a small bet I can call to see a turn, but I’ll fold to large aggression (Claude can easily shove/raise with a made Q or strong draw).

We’re 3‑way OOP versus the preflop raiser on a Q‑high, two‑tone board that should heavily favor Claude’s button opening range (lots of Qx and overpairs), while our 9c8h has very little immediate equity (no made hand, only weak straight backdoors and a runner-runner heart possibility). Donk-betting risks getting raised/jammed by the short-stack (2325) and forces us into burning chips with a hand that doesn’t want to build a pot. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets Claude decide whether to c-bet in a tougher multiway spot, and preserves our ability to continue selectively on better turns or bluff later when ranges narrow.

I completely missed this flop with T9o - no pair, no draws. However, both opponents checked to me as the preflop aggressor. This is a good spot for a c-bet since I can represent the Queen. A small bet of ~40% pot should be enough to take this down often enough to be profitable.

I have K♣J♠ on 5♥-3♥-Q♠ and Claude (button) has made a small c-bet of 300 into a 1050 pot. I’m out of position and currently have no pair—only one overcard (K) and very little actionable draw—so raising OOP in a multiway pot with a marginal hand would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity and would often just get called by better hands (or induce a squeeze/call from the deep BB). Calling 300 is cheap relative to the pot and lets me realize equity and see a turn while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: call now; fold to heavy turn aggression from Claude (he’s short enough to shove), but if I pair the K or see checks on the turn I can pursue value or control the pot accordingly. A raise here is unnecessary and too risky multiway, and folding precludes seeing a favorable turn for only 300 into 1050.

We’re getting a tempting price (300 into a 1350 pot), but 9c8h on Qs-5h-3h has almost no immediate equity: no pair, no gutshot/OESD, and only very thin backdoors (runner-runner hearts; runner-runner straight). Multiway, Minni’s call heavily weights toward Qx, heart draws, and some 5x/3x that won’t fold, while Claude’s short stack can easily shove many turns and deny us realization. Calling burns chips in a spot where we’ll be forced to overfold later; raising is worse because we get jammed/called too often. Preserve stack and move on.

Heads-up on the turn vs Claude (short, 2,025). I hold K♣J♠ on 5♥-3♥-Q♠-9♦: no pair, only two live card outs to improve to a pair on the river (3 kings + 3 jacks = 6 outs). With one card to come my equity is ~6/46 ≈ 13.0%. Pot is 1,350 and I’m out of position. I called the flop to control the pot and realize equity; leading now would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and be easy for a short-stacked opponent to shove or call with better hands. Numerically, with 13% equity the largest bet I can profitably call on this street is about (E*P)/(1−E) ≈ (0.13*1350)/(0.87) ≈ 200 chips. That means I only want to continue to the river for cheap — call small bets (~≤200) to see my 13% shot, fold to larger turn aggression, and check if he checks back to realize equity for free. Plan: check now to keep the pot controllable. If Claude bets small (≤~200) I will call; if he overbets or shoves I will fold since his range is polarized and my equity is too small. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the river and only commit if I improve or get the correct pot odds.

I hit middle pair with my 9 on the turn. Minni called my flop c-bet and checked again. I have value now against heart draws and weaker holdings. With 2025 behind and 1350 in pot, I should bet for value/protection. A bet around 500-600 targets draws and weaker pairs while keeping my commitment reasonable if raised.

I’m facing Claude’s turn bet of 550 into a 1,900 pot with KcJs. I have no pair and only ~6 clean outs (three kings + three jacks) — roughly 13% to improve on the river. Calling 550 to win a 2,450 pot requires about 22.5% equity, so the call is +EV only if Claude is bluffing a large portion of the time. Claude is short (1,475) and has opened/c-bet/continued — this sizing on the turn is consistent with a value‑heavy or polarized line (Qx, small pair, or setting up a river shove). Raising here OOP with a marginal hand would be thin, likely get called by better hands or commit me to a big pot against a short stack. Given the poor pot odds, my low equity, and the likelihood Claude is value/committed, folding is the best play to preserve my stack for better spots.
9c7d is a weak offsuit hand from UTG position. With 33.75 BB, I should be playing tight from early position. This hand is not in my opening range - it's an easy fold.

On the button with K♠4♣ I prefer to take an aggressive, position-based approach — the hand is marginal but has good postflop leverage from the button and I can often pick up the blinds. With a healthy 84BB stack, I'll open to a standard 3x BB to maximize fold equity and avoid playing passively out of position. If I face a sizable 3-bet I will fold K4o (it's often dominated); if called I'll play postflop in position, c-betting appropriate dry flops and proceeding cautiously when I have little equity.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3bb button open. J3o is too dominated versus a competent/wide button range (lots of better jacks and broadways), plays poorly out of position, and calling invites the big blind to squeeze, putting us in tough spots for 250 chips with low realization. A bluff 3-bet with J3o has poor blockers and will realize badly when called. Best EV is to simply let it go and preserve stack for better defend/3-bet candidates.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4s 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni raised from the Button to 300. Although the cost to call is only 200 more into a pot of 450, playing 43o out of position against a range that likely dominates it is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Q2o is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand and I'm first to act (UTG) at a 4-handed table. My UTG open-raise range should be much stronger (pairs, suited broadways, suited connectors). Calling 100 or min-raising to 200 with Q2o will put me out of position against deeper stacks and risk a shove/call from the shorter big blind — unnecessary risk with 85 BBs. Fold and wait for a better spot.

4-handed on the button, we should be opening very wide and pressuring the blinds. 7-6 offsuit is not premium, but it has decent playability (straight potential) and benefits a lot from position. With Claude in the BB short (~33bb), a small open risks little while still generating strong fold equity; if we face a BB shove or a 3-bet from Flash, we can comfortably fold and preserve stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 56 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 225. While FiveTwo has been quite active, 56o performs poorly out of position and has very little equity. Additionally, Claude in the Big Blind is short-stacked with only about 33 big blinds, making it likely he could shove over a call or a 3-bet. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead and wait for a better spot.

I have 6c8c suited connectors in the BB facing a button min-raise. Getting 3:1 pot odds (125 to win 375), this is a standard defend. Suited connectors play well postflop with straight and flush potential, even out of position.

Three Jacks on the board is extremely rare. We're both essentially playing the board with trip Jacks, and it comes down to kickers. My 8-high kicker is weak - FiveTwo likely has a better kicker given they raised preflop. No reason to bet here since I'd only get called by better hands. Check and see a free turn.

Flop comes JJJ, so everyone effectively has trips and it’s a kicker battle. With 7-high kickers we rarely win at showdown unless Claude has very low cards, so checking back gives up too much equity and invites him to start betting turns/rivers. As the preflop raiser my range contains far more A/K/Q-high holdings (better kickers) than the BB defend range, so a small c-bet should fold out a lot of his low-kicker floats while risking very little. If he check-raises (often a pocket pair/full house or Jx), we can comfortably fold.

Board is JJJ - we both have trip jacks. My 8-high kicker is weak, but FiveTwo's bet is tiny (150 into 500 pot). Getting over 4:1 odds to see the turn. At these odds, I can call and re-evaluate on later streets. If they bet bigger on turn, I can fold with my weak kicker.

Board is JJJ-A. We both have trip jacks playing the board, and the Ace is now a shared kicker. My 8c is my best kicker after the Ace. FiveTwo raised preflop, so they likely have a card higher than 8 (9+, face cards, or another ace). With my weak 8-high kicker, I have no reason to bet - I'd only get called by better. Check and hope to see a cheap showdown.

Turn brings the Ad, a major range-advantage card for us as the preflop raiser: we credibly have lots of Ax that now makes a full house (JJJAA), while BB has plenty of low junk that’s now drawing dead and should fold. Our actual kicker (7) is very weak on this texture, so checking back just realizes a losing showdown too often and invites river pressure from a short stack. A solid barrel size applies maximum pressure to his non-Ace holdings while still letting us comfortably fold if he check-jams (which will be heavily weighted to Ax/Jx).

Board is JJJ-A. We both play trip jacks with Ace kicker from the board. My 8 is my personal kicker. FiveTwo opened from button - their range is heavily weighted toward broadway cards (9, T, Q, K) which all beat my 8. Getting 2.33:1 odds (600 to win 1400), I need ~30% equity. Given a typical button opening range, they have a card higher than 8 most of the time. Fold is correct with such a weak kicker.

Q2o is too weak to open from UTG even 4-handed: it’s dominated by all Qx that continue, has poor playability out of position, and invites frequent 3-bets from the button (Flash) plus awkward spots versus the blinds (including a short SB who can reshove). With little equity realization and high reverse implied odds, the best stack-preserving line is to pass and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with J9o. In a 4-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. My stack is the largest at the table (252 BB), while the Small Blind (Claude) is quite short (under 30 BB). I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If Claude shoves, I will have to evaluate the odds, but for now, an aggressive open is the best play to maintain my chip lead.

I have pocket 9s in the SB facing a button raise to 300. This is a solid hand - too strong to fold but also a reasonable spot to just flat call. With my ~30BB stack, 3-betting to ~900 would commit significant chips and put me in a tough spot if Flash 4-bets. Calling lets me see a flop with good set-mining potential and keeps the pot manageable. If I hit a set, I can extract value; if I face overcards and aggression, I can get away cheaply.

Facing a 300 open from the Button with the small blind already calling, Qh9d is a marginal but playable BB hand. The price to call is small (200 more into a 700 pot; final pot 900) so I only need ~22% equity to break even — good odds to see a flop. I don’t want to 3-bet out of position with a dominated, medium-strength hand (raises will bloat the pot and can induce shoves/calls from better ranges or the short SB). Plan: call cheaply to see the flop, proceed only with a real piece (top pair/strong draw) and fold to heavy action if I miss.

I flopped an overpair with 99 on 5d8d8s. My hand is ahead of most hands here except trips or a higher pocket pair. The board has two diamonds creating flush draw potential. With 3 players in the pot and it checked to me, I should bet for value and protection against draws. Betting around 1/3 pot keeps the pot manageable while charging draws and extracting value from weaker pairs.

Fold. I missed the flop: Qh9d is just Q‑high with one diamond on a paired 5d 8d 8s board. The pot is 1,250 and Claude’s bet is 350, so I need ~350/(1,250+350) ≈ 21.9% equity to call. My realistic equity is likely below that once you account for Claude’s continuing range (pocket pairs, 5x/8x, diamond draws and other c‑bet value hands) and the fact Flash still acts behind. I have only runner‑runner backdoor outs (≈4.2% to make a flush by river) and modest pair equity (~24% to hit a Q/9 by river, many of which will still lose). With a healthy stack and no real piece, folding preserves chips for better spots and avoids facing a raise or shove with a very marginal hand.

On the flop of 5d-8d-8s, Claude leads out for 350 into a 900 pot after I raised pre-flop. I have Jack-high (J9o) with no piece of the board and no significant draws. Claude's lead on a paired board after calling from the Small Blind often indicates he has a piece of the board (like an 8 or a pair) or a flush draw. Since Claude is relatively short-stacked, I have little room to maneuver, and my hand is too weak to justify a call or a bluff-raise. I will fold and preserve my chip lead.

I am holding Ac 4d in a 4-handed game. In this position (UTG/CO), Ace-rag hands are typically at the bottom of an opening range but are standard opens to pick up the blinds or define the hand. Claude is short-stacked with 36BBs, and FiveTwo (Big Blind) has been playing very aggressively with 3-bets. However, I have a significant chip lead and want to maintain pressure. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300.

KJh on the button is a strong hand to 3-bet against an UTG open. I'm in position with suited broadway cards. With 36 BB, a 3-bet to 900 lets me take control while maintaining fold equity. If Flash 4-bets, I can fold without being committed. Good spot to apply pressure from the button.

Fold. 4♠2♦ is essentially a muck and I’m out of position facing an UTG open plus a button 3‑bet to 900. Pot is 1350 and to call costs 850 (~10% of my stack) but leaves me with almost no equity and vulnerable to a shove from Claude (2700 behind) or further action from deep stacks. A squeeze to 1500 with this holding is too spewy. Preserve my 8200 for a better spot — fold.

We’re in the BB facing an UTG open and a button 3-bet from a very short stack (Claude has only 2.7k total and will be heavily committed). 6-4 offsuit performs extremely poorly versus an UTG opening range plus a tight/committed 3-bet range, and we’ll be out of position with dominated equity and reverse implied odds. Even with tempting pot odds, we won’t realize anywhere near the equity needed multiway. No profitable squeeze options with this hand either.

I opened for 300 with Ac 4d, and Claude 3-bet to 900. Claude is relatively short-stacked with only about 3600 total. His 3-bet to 900 represents a quarter of his stack, meaning he is likely committed to this hand. My hand, A4o, is very weak against a standard 3-betting range from a short stack, which usually includes strong pairs and high Aces. While I have the chip lead and the pot odds are tempting, calling out of position with a hand that has poor playability and is likely dominated is a losing play. 4-betting to put him all-in would be a bluff that he is unlikely to fold to given his stack size. The most disciplined play to protect my large stack is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.
KJd is a strong suited broadway hand. At a 4-handed table from UTG, this is a clear opening raise. Standard sizing of 2.5x BB to 250.

I have A♥6♠ on the button vs an UTG open to 250. A6o is marginal vs an early-position open but is very playable in position — I can realize equity and extract value when I hit an Ace. A minimum reraise to 400 is too small to be a credible 3-bet and a larger 3-bet for isolation isn’t great with this hand facing deep SB/BB behind. Folding is too weak. I’ll call 250 to keep the pot manageable and use my positional advantage on the flop; if SB/BB fold I’ll be heads-up vs a 38bb opener and can apply pressure, otherwise I’ll proceed based on flop texture (c-bet with favorable boards, check/realize equity on others).

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open (2.5bb) and a button flat, creating a multiway pot where we’ll be out of position against at least two players. 7♠3♥ has very poor raw equity and terrible playability (dominated, makes weak pairs/low draws) and doesn’t function well as a squeeze candidate either (too little blocker value and too hard to continue if called/4-bet, especially with Claude short). Completing 200 more just to see a flop is a negative-EV leak here. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 3c. Claude opened to 250 from UTG, and Minni called on the button. The pot is currently 650, and it costs me 150 more to call. This gives me pot odds of approximately 4.3 to 1, meaning I need about 19% equity to justify a call. While 93s is a weak hand, it performs reasonably well in multi-way pots when suited, and I am closing the action pre-flop. Given my large stack size and the excellent price, I will call to see a flop and plan to play conservatively unless I hit a strong hand or draw.

The flop of Ts-6h-2c is very dry and does not improve my 9c-3c hand in any meaningful way. I have no pairs and only very thin backdoor possibilities. Being first to act on this street among the remaining players, I will check to the pre-flop raiser (Claude) to see how the action develops. I have no equity to justify a lead bet or a check-call if a substantial bet is made.

I have KJd on a Ts 6h 2c board - completely missed but have two overcards and a gutshot to Broadway. Dry board texture is good for c-betting. Flash checked to me, and I should represent strength with a continuation bet around 40% pot. If I get called, I still have 6 outs to improve.

I have second pair (6s) with a strong Ace kicker on a very dry flop (Ts 6h 2c). Claude is a short UTG opener and his 325 bet is a small c-bet — his range will include many missed broadways and some medium pairs; I’m ahead of a large portion of that range but behind sets/most Tens. In position I prefer pot control and to realize equity rather than build a bigger pot and face a possible shove or a difficult decision versus stronger hands.
