Calling 325 keeps worse hands and draws in, preserves fold equity on later streets, and gives me the flexibility to extract more on the turn if I improve or to fold if I run into heavy resistance. Call 325.

On a dry flop of Ts-6h-2c, I have 9-high with only a very thin backdoor club flush draw. Claude (the pre-flop aggressor) has placed a continuation bet, and Minni has called that bet. I am facing a bet of 325 into a pot that will be 1725 if I call. While the pot odds are roughly 4.3:1, my actual equity against two opponents is significantly lower than the 19% required. I have no pair, no straight draw, and my overcards are weak. There is no reason to continue in this hand out of position against two players.

The Qc turn is excellent for me - I now have an open-ended straight draw (A or 9 gives me a straight) plus overcard outs with my King. I went from a gutshot to 8 clean straight outs. This card also hits my perceived range well (AQ, KQ, QJ). Minni called the flop bet, likely holding a ten or medium pair. Continuing to bet here applies pressure and builds the pot for when I hit. Betting around half pot (~700) keeps the pressure on while maintaining fold equity.

Call 700. I have second pair with the best possible kicker in position against a short UTG opener who made a small flop c-bet and now a modest turn bet after the Qc paired the board. Claude’s line and stack size point to a range heavy in Qx, Tx and medium pairs/occasional sets — many of which beat me if I bloat the pot by raising. Folding is too weak; raising invites a shove from a short stack and forces me to commit with a hand that is often behind. Calling keeps the pot controlled, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and gives me a chance to improve or to realize my equity. Pot odds are reasonable (700 into a ~3550 pot ≈20%), so I’ll call and re-evaluate on the river — fold to clear river shove/very large bet unless I improve or get a check/call spot.

River brought the 5c completing a possible flush. I have King-high with no made hand - just a gutshot to Broadway. Minni has called both flop and turn bets, showing real interest. Bluffing into a player who has called twice on a board with three clubs is too risky. My K-high might even win at showdown if Minni was floating with a draw. Check and give up is the correct play here.

I have second pair (6s) with the best possible Ace kicker, but Claude’s line (small flop c‑bet, small turn bet, then checking the river after a Q paired the board) is consistent with many hands that beat me (Qx, Tx, sets, overpairs). The pot is 2850 and Claude is short (~2775), so any river bet risks a shove that would put me in a difficult, marginal call. Betting risks folding out worse hands while getting called or raised by hands that beat me. Checking controls the pot, preserves my showdown equity against missed broadways/bluffs, and avoids committing chips with a hand that is often second‑best. I’ll check and show down unless he takes an aggressive action.

UTG with J♦2♥ — a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand with poor playability. Calling 100 out of position into three players (Button + SB + BB) gives negative EV: I’ll be dominated often, have terrible multiway equity, and will face pressure from deep stacks (FiveTwo/Flash) or an all‑in from the short big blind (Claude ~26BB). A min‑raise to 200 as a bluff is unnecessary with ~98BB; I can wait for a much better spot. Preserve chips and fold.

4-handed on the button with Kh9c is well within an opening range. Both blinds are incentivized to defend wide, but the BB (Claude) is quite short, so raising applies immediate pressure and can win the pot outright or isolate. I’ll use a small-but-not-min size to reduce cheap defenses while keeping my risk low versus a shove/3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind holding Jack-Ten offsuit. FiveTwo, the button, has raised to 2.5bb. While JTo is a decent hand, playing it out of position against an aggressive button raiser like FiveTwo is difficult. Additionally, Claude is in the Big Blind with a very short stack (approx. 26bb), and calling here might invite a squeeze or shove from him, putting me in a tough spot. To preserve my stack and avoid a marginal post-flop situation out of position, I will fold.

I have As9s in the big blind facing a button open from FiveTwo. A9s is a solid hand to defend with - suited ace with decent kicker. With ~27BB effective, I can either call or 3-bet. Given FiveTwo's likely wide button range and my suited ace, calling is reasonable to see a flop in position relative to the blind.

Flopped ace-high on a 4h7sJc board with backdoor spade flush draw. This board doesn't connect with my hand - I have no pair and missed the flop entirely. My options are check or lead out. With just ace-high and backdoor draws, checking makes the most sense to control pot size and see what FiveTwo does. If he checks behind I can try to improve or win at showdown with ace-high. If he bets, I'll likely fold as I have minimal equity against his value range.

Heads-up vs the BB who defended wide and is quite short (SPR ~4.5). On J-7-4 rainbow, my hand (K9o) has some overcard equity but mostly wants to win the pot with fold equity. As the preflop raiser, I have a clear range advantage (strong Jx, overpairs), so a small c-bet pressures his air and some weak overcards while keeping my risk low versus a shove. If he check-jams, I can comfortably fold without investing much.

I have ace-high with only a backdoor spade flush draw on 4h7sJc. FiveTwo's small c-bet (200 into 550) is standard from the button. My equity is poor - I need runner-runner spades or to hit an ace/nine that may not even be good. With 25BB effective stack and no real draw, floating here just bleeds chips. Better to fold and wait for a better spot.

4-handed UTG is still an early position and we’ll be out of position to the Button postflop. J7o is a low-equity, poor-playability offsuit hand that performs badly versus a likely 3-bet from Flash and also puts us in an awkward spot if the short SB (Claude ~25bb) decides to shove over an open. The steal value doesn’t justify building a pot OOP with this holding, so I’ll pass and keep my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with a premium suited broadway hand, King-Queen of Spades. With it being folded to me in a 4-handed game, this is a standard opening situation. Claude in the Small Blind has a relatively short stack (around 25 BBs), so I should be prepared for a potential shove, but KQs is strong enough to call a shove from a 25 BB stack in many scenarios. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and take control of the hand from the best position.

Holding 65o in the SB facing a button raise of 300. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. With only 25bb stack and needing to call 250 into a 450 pot, I don't have the implied odds or hand strength to profitably continue. Fold.

Flash (button) opened to 300 (3x) and Claude (SB) folded, so it's just me (BB) vs a button raiser. I have K♥8♥ — a suited king with decent playability and equity vs a wide button opening range. The pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (total 300 committed) — calling gives me ~30% break-even equity to see a flop and preserves stack depth for postflop play. 3-betting OOP with K8s is thin and folding is too nitty vs a likely wide BTN range, so I will defend the big blind and see a flop.

I have K♥8♥ on 5♥4♥3♣ — the nut (king‑high) flush draw plus extra pairing outs. From the flop I have 9 heart outs (≈35% to hit a flush by the river) and 6 non‑heart pair outs (3 kings + 3 eights), so ~54% to pair or make the flush by river (though pairing can still be second best). Flash was the preflop raiser but checked the flop, showing potential weakness or pot control. Betting here is a strong semi‑bluff: it builds the pot when I hit, gets value/folds from many worse hands and unpaired overcards, and keeps the pot manageable versus a deep stack. I don’t want to overcommit OOP with only a draw, so I’ll bet about half the pot to balance fold equity and playability. If he calls I can continue with equity; if he raises large I’ll fold or re-evaluate based on sizing.

On a board of 5h-4h-3c, the Big Blind's range is heavily favored as it contains many combinations of sets, two pairs, and straight draws (like 6-x, 2-x, or suited connectors). Minni's lead for half the pot indicates they likely connected with this low, coordinated texture. My hand, King-Queen of Spades, has no direct draws to the straight or flush and relies purely on hitting an overcard. Even if a King or Queen falls, the board remains extremely dangerous. Given my healthy stack and the unfavorable board texture, I will fold and wait for a more profitable situation.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) is a strong starting hand, especially from the Under the Gun (UTG) position. Standard opening strategy dictates a raise to thin the field and take control of the pot. Given the stack sizes, specifically Claude being quite short at approximately 25 big blinds, I expect a potential shove from him if he has a mid-range hand. However, ATo is strong enough to open here. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size throughout the tournament.

T5o is a weak hand. Against an UTG raise from Flash, this is a clear fold. With only ~25BB stack, I need to be selective and wait for better spots. This hand has poor playability and isn't worth defending even from the button.

Holding A♣6♥ in the small blind facing an UTG open to 300. I already have 50 posted, so calling 250 (300 total) is a cheap price to see a flop with an Ace blocker and some showdown/turning equity. UTG open from early position is likely strong, so 3-betting out of position with A6o is too high-variance (I’ll be dominated a lot and could face a 4-bet or a multiway pot if the BB defends). With ~100BB effective stacks I prefer to defend and realize equity postflop, folding to heavy preflop 4-bets or large postflop aggression unless I improve. Call 250 (300 total).

We’re in the BB with AKo facing a likely wide UTG open from Flash and a capped SB flat from Minni. This is a great spot to squeeze: we pick up dead money often (Flash has shown open/folding tendencies), isolate vs dominated Ax/Kx, and avoid realizing equity multiway out of position. With the SB call in there, we size up to apply real pressure and set a manageable SPR if called.

I opened for 300 with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo), a standard play from UTG in a 4-handed game. Minni called, but FiveTwo in the Big Blind has now 3-bet to 1,600. Looking at FiveTwo's history, specifically Hand #90 where they called a 5-bet with 9-5 offsuit, they are clearly a highly aggressive and unpredictable player. However, ATo is a marginal hand to defend against such a large 3-bet, especially out of position. While FiveTwo could be bluffing, calling 1,300 more creates a bloated pot where I will be guessing on most flops. As a chip leader, I prefer to avoid high-variance confrontations with the other big stack with a non-premium hand. I will fold and wait for a more dominating hand to exploit FiveTwo's aggression.

I called the UTG open to realize equity with A♣6♥, but FiveTwo’s 3‑bet to 1,600 changes the math. I have to call 1,300 more into a 2,200 pot (pot would be 3,500 after my call), so I need ~37% equity to break even. A6o out of position will be dominated by much of a typical 3‑bet range (better A‑x, pairs) and won’t have the required equity or playability OOP. A 4‑bet bluff here is too high variance (I lack strong blockers and can face a shove/call), so the prudent, +EV play is to release the hand and preserve my ~9.8k stack for better spots.
7-2 offsuit is the worst hand in poker. I'm in UTG position with a short stack of ~25BB. No reason to open this trash - easy fold.

I'm on the button with pocket sevens (7s7d), ~98 BB effective — a strong hand in a 4-handed game and ideal to play for value in position. The pot is just the blinds (150) and the call is 100; I want to take the initiative, deny cheap multiway flops and isolate the blinds. I size to 3x the big blind (raise to 300 total) — large enough to get fold equity and extract value from worse hands (broadways, Axs, Kx, small pairs) but not so large as to unnecessarily bloat the pot or invite huge 3-bets. If called I'll play postflop in position: c-bet favorable textures, control size on draw-heavy/overcard boards, and fold to overwhelming aggression on very hostile lines. If I face a sizable 3-bet I will re-evaluate based on sizing/player (likely fold to very large 3-bets, consider calling/4-betting vs small/medium 3-bets).

4-handed, Minni’s button open to 3bb is likely quite wide. Calling from the small blind with JTo invites the big blind to come along/squeeze and leaves us playing a marginal hand out of position without initiative. A 3-bet pressures Minni’s weaker opens, denies equity to Flash in the BB, and lets us realize our hand’s equity more cleanly with initiative. If Minni 4-bets (especially jam given her ~95bb), we can comfortably fold this combo.

I am holding 5s 3c in the big blind, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. The action has been heavy with an open from Minni on the button and a significant 3-bet from FiveTwo in the small blind. Given my hand strength and the aggression shown by the other players, there is no strategic justification for calling or raising. I will fold to preserve my stack.

FiveTwo (SB) 3-bet to 1,200 after my button open to 300. I have 7♠7♦ and ~95BB effective. Against an SB 3-bet in a short-handed spot his range is fairly wide (AQ/AK, broadways, suited connectors, some medium pairs and bluffs) — calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me use position postflop. Calling 900 more (1200 total) gives me set-mining/implied-value potential and the flexibility to fold to heavy aggression on overcard-heavy boards; a small 4-bet here with 77 would be too often dominated by the stronger part of his range and commits too much against possible 5-bets. Plan: call, see flop in position; extract value if I hit or play cautiously and fold to strong lines on scary textures.

3-bet pot HU vs BTN caller: this KTx two-tone flop favors my range (strong Kx, overpairs), so I want a small, range-oriented c-bet. With TdJc I have second pair with a decent kicker but no heart, so betting accomplishes (1) thin value vs worse Tx and some pocket pairs, (2) protection/denying equity to hands like AQ/QJ and random floats, while (3) keeping sizing small enough that I can comfortably fold if Minni springs a big raise/jam. Checking would invite stabs on a wet board and give free cards too often.

Flop is Kc 2h Th after I called a preflop 3-bet with 7s7d. FiveTwo makes a small c-bet (800 into a 2500 pot → pot now 3300). I’m an underpair to a lot of his value hands (Kx, Tx, TT) but ahead of many missed overcards and bluffs. Calling 800 gives me ~19.5% break-even equity (800 to win ~4100) and keeps the pot controllable while using my position. With ~95BB effective I have good implied odds to hit a set and can fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve. Raising here would bloat the pot and often be called by better hands or force unrealistic fold equity against a 3-bettor’s turn/river lines. Plan: call the 800, see the turn, value bet or fold to strong turn aggression depending on runout/response.

Turn is a relatively blank 7c in a 3-bet pot where I have medium showdown value (pair of tens, J kicker) but I’m not thrilled to face a jam/raise if I bet—especially with Minni’s short-ish remaining stack (~7.8k) and an SPR around 2. Betting thinly here mostly gets called by better (Kx, KT, sets) and strong draws, while worse Tx is a narrower portion and can still put me in ugly spots. By checking, I keep the pot manageable, protect my range (I can also check strong hands), and allow Minni to stab with missed overcards/hearts that I can often call down against at reasonable sizing.

I turned a set (7s7d with 7c on the turn). Opponent (FiveTwo) 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop, so his range includes Kx, Tx, medium pairs and bluffs. He checked the turn into me — this is a clear value spot. I will bet roughly half the pot (2000 into ~4100) to extract from Kx/TX and worse pairs, charge any backdoor/air, and keep the pot manageable to induce calls on the river. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river card and continue to extract thin value or pot‑control as appropriate; if he check‑raises large it’s likely TT/KK, and given I have trips I’ll strongly consider calling/shoving depending on sizing. Betting now maximizes EV while protecting my hand and using position.

Minni’s turn stab (2,000 into 4,150) is a ~48% pot bet with her stack getting short, which can represent value (Kx, some two pair like KT, occasional sets) but also a lot of floats/semi-bluffs from the flop: heart draws (AhQh/AhJh/QhJh, etc.), some A-high/QJ types that peeled the small c-bet, and occasional club backdoors picking up equity. With TdJc I have second pair and need only ~25% equity to continue; folding would be too tight versus her likely betting frequency after I check in a 3-bet pot. Raising is bad because it mostly folds out the bluffs I want to keep and gets action from better hands. I’ll call and re-evaluate rivers—comfortably folding on many heart-completing runouts, and bluff-catching more often on bricks.

River (Kc 2h Th 7c 9h) completes the front-door hearts and also improves parts of Minni’s turn betting range (made flushes, some Kx continuing, occasional QJ). With TdJc (no heart) we have thin/medium showdown value and don’t want to value-bet into a range that will mostly call with better and fold worse. Checking keeps us from isolating ourselves vs strong hands while allowing Minni to check back missed bluffs/overcards; we can then decide based on her sizing if she bets.

I have trips (7♠7♦ with 7♣ on the turn). FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop, c‑bet the K♣ 2♥ T♥ flop and then check‑called my half‑pot turn bet — his line contains Kx/Tx/pocket pairs and also two‑heart combos that had a backdoor flush draw. The 9♥ on the river completes a possible heart flush (and there are still pocket sets like TT/KK/99 that also beat me). Betting here mostly charges hands that now beat me or folds out the thin value I could get; I already extracted value on the turn. Pot control and going to showdown is the highest EV line: check and see a showdown rather than risking a large portion of my stack to be called by flushes/sets.

4-handed table — open-raising wide is standard. A♦4♣ is marginal UTG in full-ring but perfectly playable in 4-handed: it has an ace for high-card value and decent steal/position potential. I want to take the initiative, avoid a limp that invites isolation 3-bets, and use a standard 3x sizing to get fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. Claude (BB) is short (~23bb) and could shove — I’m not turning this into a coinflip-heavy all-in spot with A4o, so I’ll open and reevaluate if I face an all-in. Raise to 300 (3x the BB) — applies pressure to the blinds and keeps postflop playability if called by the button/SB.

Facing an UTG open to 3bb, T3o is far too weak to defend even on the button: it has poor equity, is frequently dominated when it makes a pair, and realizes equity badly postflop. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive because (1) the BB is very short and can jam over our 3-bet, and (2) if Minni continues, we’re forced into a bloated pot with terrible playability. Best chip-EV is to preserve stack and wait for better steal/3-bet candidates.

I am holding 5c 3s in the Small Blind. Minni opened for 300 from UTG. This is a very weak hand and does not warrant a call or a raise against an early position open, especially with a short stack (Claude) still to act in the Big Blind who might shove. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

5d9d is a weak suited hand. Facing a Minni UTG raise (typically a tighter range), I'm out of position for the rest of the hand. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds, this hand doesn't play well OOP against an early position raiser. The suited diamonds give some equity but not enough to justify calling here with limited stack depth.

4-handed UTG is wider than full ring, but 2-4 offsuit is still bottom-tier: no high-card value, poor playability, and gets dominated whenever we make a pair. Limping invites the button to attack and forces us into tough postflop spots OOP versus bigger stacks, while opening as a steal with this hand is unnecessary given our comfortable stack and the BB’s ability to defend. Best EV is to simply release and wait for better spots.

I am on the button with Ten-Eight offsuit. In a four-handed game, this is a borderline hand for a steal attempt. However, Claude in the Small Blind is relatively short-stacked with only about 23 big blinds, making him likely to shove over a button raise. I have a significant chip lead and do not need to contest marginal pots or risk calling a shove with a weak hand. I will choose the low-variance path and fold.
ATs is a strong hand in the small blind with only the BB left to act. With 23BB effective stack, I should raise for value. Standard raise to 250-300 to build the pot or take it down.

Claude opened to 250 from the small blind and I have Q♠5♥ in the big blind. Calling 150 more (250 total committed) is cheap relative to my 13,975 stack and gives me a chance to play postflop against a likely wide SB opening range (Claude is relatively short-stacked). Pot odds (~150 into a 500 pot → ~30% break-even equity) and positional/postflop flexibility make a flat call the lowest-risk +EV line: see a flop, fold to heavy aggression if I completely miss, and continue with top pair/draws or good turn odds. I won’t three-bet light here against a short stack who can shove — just defend and evaluate on the flop.

Flopped middle pair with top kicker (AT on K-T-4 board) plus backdoor nut flush draw with the Ac. This is a good spot to continuation bet for value and protection against draws. Minni just called preflop, likely has a wide range. I can get value from worse tens, flush draws, and floats. Betting around 1/3 pot to extract value while keeping the pot manageable with my medium-strength hand.

Fold. I defended preflop as planned, but on Kc-Tc-4h my Q♠5♥ has missed completely — no pair, no meaningful draw. Claude is short-stacked (1,950) and his small 175 into a 675 pot is consistent with a wide but often-better c-betting range (Kx, Tx, club combos). Calling 175 only requires ~20.6% equity to break even, and my hand has well under that vs his range; calling also leaves me vulnerable to a shove where I’d face a tough, negative-EV decision. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold here.

I am UTG with Jack-Deuce offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in the deck. While I have the chip lead, there is no strategic benefit to opening this hand from early position, especially with a short-stacked opponent (Claude) on the button who is likely looking for a spot to shove. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

On the button with Q6o, Flash folded UTG. This is a decent spot for a button steal attempt against just the blinds. I'm short-stacked at ~26BB so I need to pick up some pots. Q6o isn't strong but acceptable for a button steal. Standard raise to 2.5x.

I have A♣T♠ in the SB facing a button open to 250 (pot 400, 200 to call). A‑ten offsuit is worth defending but it's a marginal hand out of position against both the raiser and the big blind. Claude is short (2,375) and likely opening a wide range, so there's value in seeing a flop, but raising to isolate risks bloating the pot and inviting FiveTwo (very deep and often active) to call or 3‑bet. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity, and lets me play postflop based on texture (c‑bet or probe vs heads‑up; check‑call/float if multiway and unfavorable). If FiveTwo makes a large 3‑bet preflop I will fold; otherwise I plan to continue with top pair or good draws and capitalize on Claude's shorter stack when appropriate.

We’re getting a very good price in the big blind (150 to win 600, and we’ll see a flop in position disadvantage but with a shallow SPR vs Claude). Claude’s short stack/button open is likely wide, and Minni’s flat from the SB further improves our pot odds. Q3o is weak and can be dominated, so the plan is to realize equity cheaply: mostly check/fold unfavorable boards and avoid putting in big money with marginal one-pair hands.

I have A♣T♠ on 8♠9♠2♥ in a three‑way pot. I currently have no pair and only a weak backdoor flush (one spade in my hand + two on board) and otherwise very little equity. In a multiway pot out of position I have poor fold equity and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Claude is short and may probe, but checking preserves options: I can call a small bet for pot odds to chase backdoors, fold to big pressure, or take a free turn to reassess (bet/lead only if I pair or pick up a strong draw). Pot control and hand preservation is the right line here.

Multiway pot versus the button raiser and a SB caller on a wet 9♠8♠2♥ board. With Q♣3♦ we have only weak Q-high, no pair, no spade, and no meaningful backdoor draws. Donk-betting here is too thin and risks getting raised off our hand, while checking keeps the pot small and lets us realize whatever minimal showdown value we have (and play straightforwardly versus a c-bet).

I have Qd6s on 8s9s2h - complete air with no pair, no draw, just one overcard. Both blinds checked to me. While this is a standard c-bet spot with position, I have two opponents on a connected board with a flush draw possible. My hand has almost no equity if called. With ~24BB stack, burning chips here isn't ideal. Check back and take a free card - I can give up cheaply or potentially improve.

Turn analysis: A♣T♠ on 8♠9♠2♥7♣ in a three‑way pot (pot 750). My backdoor spade draw died on the turn, so I have only A‑high with no pair and very little showdown equity. Against two opponents (Claude short on the button and a deep, often‑calling FiveTwo in the BB) my fold equity is thin — a bet here risks getting raised/shoved by the short stack or called by the deep stack and bloating a pot I’m unlikely to win. Optimal line is pot control and information: check and see the river. On the river I’ll call small bets that offer correct pot odds or fold to heavy pressure; if I pair or pick up a real hand I can start betting for value or even raise.

Both opponents have checked twice on a very dynamic board (9♠8♠2♥7♣), which is a strong signal of capped/weak ranges in a multiway pot. From the big blind, my range contains a lot of 7x/8x/9x, two-pair combos, and spade/straight draws that can credibly bet this turn, while Q♣3♦ has essentially no showdown value. A small-to-mid stab risks little relative to the pot and should fold out a lot of unpaired overcards and missed hands from Claude’s wide button open and Minni’s SB flat. If I get called (or raised) I can comfortably shut down and fold later without investing much.

The 7c turn gives me an open-ended straight draw - I have the 6 and the board shows 7-8-9, so I need a 5 or 10 to complete. That's 8 outs (~16% to hit). Pot is 1100, I need to call 350, getting ~3.1:1. Not quite enough direct odds, but with position and implied odds if I hit against FiveTwo's 19k stack, calling is reasonable. My straight would be vulnerable (5-6-7-8-9 or 6-7-8-9-T) but playable. At 23.75BB I can afford this speculative call.

I checked the turn with A♣T♠ intending to see a river, but FiveTwo bets 350 and Claude calls. The pot is 1450 and calling 350 gives me ~19.4% pot odds (350/(1450+350)). I only have ~6 clean outs (3 Aces + 3 Tens) to pair on the river — roughly 13% — so calling is -EV. This is a multiway pot with a deep, calling opponent and a short button who just called; my showdown equity with just A‑high is poor and a raise would be a thin semi‑bluff with low fold equity and high risk of a shove or a call from a better hand. Preserve my stack and wait for a better, clearer spot — fold.

River is K♠ after we stabbed turn and only Claude called, leaving him very short (2,025). With Q♣3♦ we essentially have zero showdown value, so checking is mostly just conceding the pot versus any pair. K♠ is a strong barrel card: it’s an overcard to 9/8/7 that pressures a lot of the one-pair holdings that call turn, and it also completes the front-door spade draw, which we can credibly represent from the BB. I don’t want to shove because his pot odds versus an all-in are quite good and he may station with too many pairs; instead I’ll use a medium-large bluff sizing to generate folds from 7x/8x/weak 9x while keeping my risk controlled. If he jams, I fold.

My OESD missed - needed a 5 or T. The Ks completes the flush on this 3-spade board. I have Q-high, no showdown value whatsoever. FiveTwo bet turn and river, showing strength. Even with 3.6:1 pot odds, I need to be good 22%+ but Q-high beats nothing except complete air. FiveTwo's line is consistent with value (made hand, flush, straight). Easy fold.
Q4o from UTG with 20BB stack. This is a weak off-suit hand that plays poorly from early position. I'm easily dominated by better queens and have poor playability postflop. Standard fold.

Button vs only the blinds with 6♠5♥: a marginal but playable hand that gains a lot from position. Pot is 150 and it’s cheap to apply pressure — I want to take the initiative and try to steal the blinds or play the flop in position. I’ll open to a standard 3x-type size (300 total) to make callers less comfortable and get folding equity. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet (I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with 5-6 offsuit), but if I get single or multi-way calls I’ll use position to c-bet appropriate flops and realize equity on connected/drawy boards.

Button is opening a wide range and I cover them, so this is a good spot to apply pressure from the SB. A3o isn’t great as a flat (dominated often, plays poorly multiway with BB behind), but it has an ace blocker and decent equity when called, making it a solid light 3-bet candidate. Plan: take initiative, win the pot pre, and fold comfortably to a 4-bet from either opponent.

I am holding AKo in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the Button and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Small Blind. AKo is a premier hand that performs well in 4-bet pots. By raising here, I take control of the hand, potentially force Minni out, and put pressure on FiveTwo. A raise to 3000 is a standard sizing to build the pot and define my hand strength while remaining well-covered by my stack.

I opened to 300 on the button with 6♠5♥ and got 3-bet to 1,000 by FiveTwo and then a 4-bet to 3,000 from Flash. Pot is 4,300 and it costs me 2,700 more to call (I’d be committing 3,000 total). That call requires ~38.6% equity (2,700 / 7,000) to break even preflop. 6‑5 offsuit is a marginal, speculative hand and has far less equity against two strong 3‑bet/4‑bet ranges (pocket pairs, AK, broadways, suited connectors). While I do have position, the size and sequence of aggression show strength and I lack the blockers or showdown value to continue profitably. A raise (min to 5,000) or shove would be too thin/bluffy here and would need huge fold equity I don’t have. Stick to my plan of folding marginal hands to substantial 3/4‑bets — fold and preserve stack for better spots.

We executed the light 3-bet with A3o to leverage the ace blocker and fold equity versus the BTN open, but the BB’s 4-bet to 3000 (after I show strength) is a clear inflection point. Calling puts me OOP in a 4-bet pot with a hand that is dominated by BB’s value range (better aces, broadways) and doesn’t realize equity well; there’s no attractive postflop playability here. Turning this into a 5-bet bluff/jam would require an unrealistically high fold frequency given stacks and the line. Sticking to the preflop plan, I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

UTG in a 4‑hand game with Q♣8♠ — a marginal, poorly‑playing hand out of position. Calling 100 is cheap but will likely leave me OOP against two deeper, aggressive stacks (Flash and FiveTwo) and a raise risks Claude (BB, ~19bb) shoving or a 3‑bet squeeze putting me in a tough spot with weak showdown value. With a healthy stack I should conserve chips for better position and stronger holdings. Fold and wait for a more playable spot.

Minni has folded and we’re on the button with Q9o, a reasonable steal hand in a short-handed spot. Raising applies maximum pressure to the blinds, especially the very short BB (who is in shove/fold territory), and also gives us the initiative versus Flash in the SB. I’ll use a small-but-standard open size to keep risk low while still generating fold equity and allowing us to profitably respond if the BB jams and the SB gets out of the way.

I am holding 5s 7s in the small blind. FiveTwo, who has been quite active and aggressive, opened for a standard raise from the button. While 5s 7s is a decent speculative hand with good post-flop playability, the big blind (Claude) is currently very short-stacked with only 1,925 chips. If I call, I invite Claude to shove over the top, which would force me to fold and lose the chips I put in. Additionally, I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand against FiveTwo. Given my comfortable chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament, I will take the low-variance route and fold this marginal hand.

J8o in BB facing a button raise of 250. Need to call 150 into a 400 pot. J8o is a marginal hand - while it has some playability, it's on the weaker side of defendable hands. FiveTwo has been active throughout the tournament. With position disadvantage and a mediocre holding, folding is reasonable here to preserve stack.

4-handed UTG is relatively wide; A7s is comfortably in an open-raising range. Raising (instead of limping) lets me leverage fold equity vs the blinds, take initiative, and my Ace blocker reduces the chance I get played back at with strong Ax. Also, the SB is short (~19bb), so a raise puts immediate pressure on their reshove/fold decisions while still keeping my sizing small enough that I can fold if the button applies heavy pressure with a large 3-bet.

I am on the button with Qs 4d, a very weak hand. FiveTwo opened with a raise from the first position. While I am the chip leader, there is no reason to play such a weak hand against an open raise, especially with a very short-stacked player in the small blind who might jam over any action I take. Folding is the standard and most prudent play.

J8o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. With only ~19BB remaining, I need to be selective. This hand doesn't play well out of position and isn't strong enough to 3-bet light. Folding is the disciplined play here.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 250 (about 2.5x). I have Kc2h in the big blind — a marginal, easily-dominated hand versus an early-position raising range and a poor candidate for a 3-bet/bluff with deep stacks. Calling 150 more (250 total committed) is cheap: I’m getting ~27% pot odds to see a flop and keep the pot manageable. Plan: see the flop, fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I don’t improve, and play aggressively only if I pair the King or hit a clear draw. I avoid a 3-bet to prevent getting multi-street value-caught by stronger UTG hands.

Flop is A♣ A♦ 9♠ and I hold K♣2♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw, and we’re heads-up to an early-position raiser whose range contains many A‑x and sets. I want pot control and to preserve my stack: I called preflop cheaply to see the flop but this is a terrible board for K2. Checking keeps the pot manageable and allows me to fold to any continuation bet (we have virtually no equity unless FiveTwo is bluffing). If FiveTwo checks back I can re-evaluate on the turn, but I will not lead or raise here.

Board is extremely favorable for the preflop raiser (A-A-9), and with Ah7s I have trips with only one possible remaining Ace (As) and a very low frequency of boats beating me (99 or A9 with As). Checking risks losing value because Villain will often check back their missed hands; a small c-bet gets called by 9x, pocket pairs, and some floats while keeping their range wide and allowing me to barrel later streets for value. I’ll use a small sizing to target those weaker continues and avoid polarizing them into only strong hands.

Board A♣ A♦ 9♠ vs UTG opener — I hold K♣2♥. Preflop I called cheaply to see the flop and checked for pot control. FiveTwo now fires a small c‑bet: pot was 750, bet 200, so calling would cost 200 to try to win ~950 (≈4.75:1, so I need ~17% equity). My real equity is far lower — only three kings to hit (~6% on the turn, ~12–13% by river) and pairing the king still loses to A‑x or many better hands. UTG’s range is heavy in A‑x and pocket pairs that dominate me; a bluff‑raise is high risk against an early‑position raiser who can easily call or shove. With virtually no fold equity and little chance to improve, the +EV play is to preserve my 13k stack and fold this flop rather than chase or bluff into a strong range.

I am holding Ace-Jack offsuit (AJo) in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. AJo is a premium hand in this configuration, and I have a significant chip lead. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300 chips) is the most effective play here to take control of the pot, realize my equity, and apply pressure on the shorter stacks, particularly Claude on the button who is under 20 big blinds.

J4 suited is a weak hand. Flash raised from UTG which typically shows strength. With only 1875 chips (about 18.75 BB), calling 300 (16% of my stack) with a marginal suited hand is not profitable. Need to preserve chips for better spots.

Flash opened UTG to 300. I’m in the SB with Qc7d — a weak offsuit queen that’s often dominated by an UTG opening range (AQ, KQ, Qx, broadways). Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires ~35.7% equity to break even (250/(450+250)), and out of position against the opener (with a big stack behind who can still enter) Q7o won’t have that equity or post‑flop playability. A 3‑bet bluff here would be thin given the stacks and likely responses. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Flash opens to 3bb heads-up versus the blinds after folds. Ac7c is a strong defend: suited ace has solid equity versus a likely wide opening range and plays well with nut-flush potential and decent top-pair value. A 3-bet is viable, but out of position with a hand that doesn’t love getting 4-bet, calling keeps weaker hands in and realizes equity at a good price (200 to win 650).

Heads-up vs the preflop raiser on a Q-9-4 rainbow board, our Ac7c is just ace-high with limited immediate equity (only a weak backdoor club draw and some overcard potential). This texture favors the opener’s range advantage (broadways, Qx, overpairs), so leading doesn’t accomplish much and risks getting raised off our equity. Best is to check and let Flash continuation-bet; then we can decide based on sizing and live tells (small bets can be floated occasionally with Ac as a strong blocker, bigger bets we can mostly fold).

I missed the flop with AJo on a board of Qd 9h 4s. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture (Q-high with middling cards) often connects with a Big Blind defending range. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control, avoid a check-raise from the second-largest stack (FiveTwo), and see a free turn card where I can potentially improve to top pair with an Ace or a Jack.

Flop checked through, which caps Flash’s range a bit (fewer strong Qx/overpairs than if he routinely c-bets) and keeps a lot of ace-high / broadway air in. The 5c is a good card for the BB’s perceived range (we have more 55/44/54/65/75-type hands than the UTG opener), so a turn probe can pick up the pot immediately at a high frequency.

With Ac7c we don’t have a real draw and checking invites Flash to stab river and put our ace-high in a tough spot. I’ll lead small-to-medium to attack his missed c-bets and deny equity. If raised, we can comfortably fold; if called, we’ll often shut down unless we improve (A/7) or get a very favorable scare river.

I missed the flop and the turn completely with my Ace-Jack. After I checked back on the flop, FiveTwo's lead on the turn for roughly half the pot (300 into 650) suggests he either has a marginal made hand (like a 9, 5, or 4) or is attempting to take the pot away now that I've shown weakness. While I have Ace-high and 6 potential outs to improve (Aces and Jacks), calling here creates a difficult situation on the river where I will likely have to fold to further aggression if I don't improve. Given my significant chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament (Hand 118/150), I will choose the low-variance route and fold to preserve my stack.
6d3s is a very weak offsuit hand from UTG position. With 19 BBs and three opponents with significantly larger stacks, this is a clear fold. Need to wait for better spots.

I’m on the button with QQ and ~130bb effective, facing only the SB and BB (Claude folded). This is a textbook spot to open-raise for value and to isolate — I don’t want to limp and invite multiway action. I’ll make a solid 4x BB open to 400 total: big enough to deny proper pot odds to speculative hands (small pairs, suited connectors), to thin the field, and to build a pot when I’m likely ahead, but not so large that I commit unnecessarily preflop. Plan: if called, play postflop in position, c-bet favorable textures and protect against draws; if I face a 3-bet, re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent (call smaller 3-bets, 4-bet or shove vs very large/aggressive 3-bets).

Button has opened large (to 4bb) and we’re out of position in the small blind with JTo, a hand that plays poorly as a flat (often dominated, invites the BB to squeeze, and we end up guessing postflop). With stacks deep enough, I prefer turning this into an aggressive 3-bet to take initiative, deny the BB a cheap entry, and apply pressure to Minni’s likely wide button range. If Minni 4-bets/jams, we comfortably fold; if called, we can continue on favorable broadway/connected textures and leverage positionless aggression selectively.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni opened from the button and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Small Blind. Given the strength shown by both players and the fact that my hand has almost no equity against their ranges, there is no reason to commit any chips to this pot. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

FiveTwo (SB) 3-bet my 4x open (400) to 1300. Pot is ~1800 and I need 900 more to call. I’m on the button with QQ and ~125bb effective. QQ is a premium but vulnerable to A‑cards; facing a standard/moderate 3‑bet I prefer to keep the hand in position rather than laddering into a small 4‑bet that won’t fold many hands. A meaningful 4‑bet for value would be much larger (~3500–4500); the minimum 4‑bet to 2200 is too small to accomplish that. Calling keeps worse hands (smaller pairs, broadways, suited connectors) in, allows me to use position postflop, and controls pot size with deep stacks. Plan: call to 1300 total; on a dry flop I’ll c‑bet for value/protection (~1/2 pot), but on Ace/King boards I’ll proceed cautiously (checking or pot‑control), and I’ll re‑evaluate if FiveTwo shows heavy aggression (5‑bet/jam) using pot odds and read.

We 3-bet pre and get a heads-up flop on a very dynamic board (Jh-9c-8s). JTo is a strong combo here: top pair plus an open-ended straight draw (7 or Q), so we have both value and lots of equity versus draws/pairs. Given how many turn cards change the nuts, I want to deny equity and start building a pot that can comfortably get stacks in by the turn/river (SPR ~4.3). A medium-large c-bet pressures Minni’s wide button range and charges hands like T9/98/QT/Tx gutters and random floats.

I’m on the button with QQ facing a SB 3‑bet preflop and a flop bet into me on Jh‑9c‑8s. This is a connected but non‑flushy flop; the hands that realistically crush me are sets (JJ/99/88) and the very unlikely T7 straight — most of FiveTwo’s 3‑bet range (AK, AJ, KJ, pocket pairs below QQ, bluffs) is behind. I want to accomplish two things: (1) charge/force out hands with equity (single‑pair Jx, Kx, A‑x, and all the 10‑x combos that have open‑ended straight draws needing a Q or 7) and (2) get value from worse pairs and draws while using position post‑raise if they call. Calling 1700 is cheap but gives free cards to draws; folding is too weak. I’ll raise to 3400 total (the minimum raise) — big enough to charge draws and extract value from worse hands while keeping the pot manageable and leaving room to fold if FiveTwo shows heavy strength (a big raise/4‑bet). Plan: if they call I continue to evaluate on the turn (pot‑control vs big aggression, barreling for value/protection); if they raise I’ll assume sets/AA/KK and re‑evaluate (likely fold or exercise pot‑odds/ICM caution depending on sizing).

We 3-bet pre and on Jh-9c-8s we have a premium continuation hand: top pair + open-ended straight draw (7/Q), so we’re rarely crushed and we have strong equity even versus sets/two-pair. Minni’s small flop raise can be value (QT/sets/two pair) or semibluffs (T9, T7, QTx, etc). With the pot already 7,800 and Minni’s remaining stack relatively short, calling leaves an SPR well under 1 on the turn anyway; I’d rather take the initiative now, deny equity to draws, and pick up the pot immediately when this raise is air/semi-bluff while still having plenty of equity when called. Best play is to raise to put Minni all-in.

Sequence: I opened to 400 preflop, FiveTwo 3‑bet to 1,300 and I called. On Jh‑9c‑8s I raised their 1,700 C‑bet to 3,400 to charge draws and get value. They just shoved hard and I’m facing a call of my remaining 8,275 (all‑in). Pot is 17,775, so a call needs ~31.8% equity (8,275 / (17,775+8,275) ≈ 31.8%).

Range/read: FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop so his range is polarized — it contains strong value (AA/KK/JJ/QQ/TT/99/88/AK) and some bluffs/wider hands. After the flop raise he polarizes further: the pure value hands that crush me are sets (JJ/99/88) and some Jx two‑pair/top‑pair combos, but his range also contains a nontrivial block of hands I beat (AK, some broadway bluffs, lower pocket pairs that miss the board). I also hold both queens, which removes Qx combos from his range.

Equity math and decision: vs sets I only have ~8–9% equity, vs single‑pair Jx I’m a big favorite (~75–80%), and vs AK/other overcards I’m ~65–70%. Reasonable weighting of his shove (a mix of sets + top‑pairs + bluffs) puts my estimated equity well above the ~32% break‑even threshold. Given the pot odds, the texture (no flush draw) and that my flop raise already priced in weaker hands, calling is +EV. Folding concedes a huge pot with a hand that still has significant equity; there is no attractive alternative — I can’t meaningfully reraise more than an all‑in.

Action: I call the shove and commit my remaining 8,275 chips.

3-handed on the button we can play very wide, but 74o is near the bottom of the range and doesn’t want to face a squeeze from the deep SB or a 17bb shove from the short BB after we invest more. Limping (calling 100) keeps variance low, realizes position cheaply, and only risks 1bb; we can comfortably fold to an isolation raise or a BB jam without burning a 2bb+ open.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 5s, a speculative hand with good playability. FiveTwo has limped from the button, and Claude in the Big Blind is extremely short-stacked (less than 18 big blinds). Given the excellent pot odds (50 to call into a pot of 250) and the potential for Claude to shove if I raise, completing the bet is the most efficient play to see a flop and evaluate. Suited connectors play well in multi-way pots, and I want to keep the pot small until I hit the board.

Short stacked with J8o in BB. Two limpers gave me a free option. With 17.75BB effective and a marginal hand, checking to see a free flop is the best play. No need to bloat the pot out of position with this holding.

I have flopped top pair (5s) with an open-ended straight draw (6-5 on a 3-4-5 board). This is a very strong semi-bluffing or value hand in a three-way pot. The board is quite connected, so I want to build the pot while also protecting my hand against overcards. Since I am first to act and the pot is small (300), a bet of 200 (around 66% pot) is a standard size to get value from draws or worse pairs and to potentially fold out hands with overcard equity.

I have complete air - J8o on a 3-4-5 rainbow board. No pair, no straight draw, no backdoor flush draw. Flash bets 200 into 300. With only jack high and no realistic way to improve to the best hand, this is a clear fold. No need to bluff or float with this stack size (17.75BB) and zero equity.

Heads-up vs the SB in a limped pot, this 3-4-5 rainbow board hits both ranges. With 7c4d we have a pair plus meaningful improvement potential (6 for a straight, and some turns/rivers can give us two pair/trips), and we’re getting a very good price versus a small lead that can include lots of overcards and one-pair stabs. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal kicker and risks getting 3-bet by 5x/two-pair/sets/straights, so the best line is to call in position and realize equity, then reassess on the turn (value when improved, float/bluff-catch selectively when not).

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (Sixes and Fives). However, the board is now 3-4-5-6, which is extremely coordinated; any 7 or 2 in an opponent's hand completes a straight. Since I was the aggressor on the flop and FiveTwo merely called, I will continue with a lead bet to extract value from sets, weaker two pairs, or pair+draw combinations. A bet of 450 into a 700 pot allows me to maintain initiative and protect my hand, while also providing information—if FiveTwo raises, I must strongly consider that I am beaten by a straight.

Turn completes our 7-high straight (3-4-5-6-7). In this limped SB-vs-BTN line, Flash can easily be betting a lot of one-pair hands (6x/5x/4x), plus straight draws (A2/72/53-type), and club draws that will pay to continue. We’re only really in trouble versus 7♠8♠/78 specifically for the higher straight, which is a small part of the range, so we should start building the pot now rather than just calling and risking a river check-back or awkward river runout. A raise also charges draws and sets up a river shove/value bet on safe cards.

On a board of 3-4-5-6, the texture is extremely coordinated, making any 7 or 2 a straight. While I have top two pair (Sixes and Fives), this hand is effectively a bluff-catcher against a raise on such a dangerous board. FiveTwo limped from the button, a range that contains many 7-x and 2-x combinations. However, FiveTwo has shown significant aggression in previous hands (like Hand #119 and #111), suggesting they are capable of overplaying lower two pairs (like 5-4 or 4-3) or turning a pair into a semi-bluff. I am getting approximately 2.3-to-1 odds to call. I will call the raise to see a river, as folding top two pair immediately might be too exploitable against an aggressive opponent, but I will proceed with extreme caution on the final street if I do not improve to a full house.

The board has a four-card straight (3-4-5-6). I hold two pair (Sixes and Fives), but any 7 or 2 makes a straight. FiveTwo raised my lead on the turn, which strongly suggests they have either a straight or a very strong draw. The Ace on the river is a blank for the straight but could have improved an Ax hand that happened to have a straight draw (like A2 or A7). Leading here offers no value as I only get called by better hands. I will check and evaluate if FiveTwo bets, as my hand is now primarily a bluff-catcher.

River goes check to us after we raised the turn and got called. With 7c4d on A-6-5-4-3 we have a strong straight (3-7). Flash’s turn call can easily be Ax (now improved), 6x/5x/4x, and some stubborn pairs/draws that will pay off a value bet; we don’t want to let those hands check back for free. The main concern is a higher straight from 87, so I’ll value bet a solid size that gets called by one-pair/two-pair type holdings without overly committing myself if I face a big check-raise.

FiveTwo's turn raise on a board of 3-4-5-6 was a significant show of strength. On such a coordinated board, any 7 or 2 completes a straight. FiveTwo limped from the button, which is a range that heavily includes speculative hands containing those straight-completing cards. While I have top two pair (Sixes and Fives), this hand is effectively a bluff-catcher on this texture. FiveTwo followed up the turn raise with a large river bet (nearly 80% of the pot). My previous plan was to proceed with extreme caution if I did not improve to a full house on the river. Since I didn't improve and the board remains extremely dangerous, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack, as it is highly likely FiveTwo has at least a straight.

KQs is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. I have a significant chip lead over Claude (SB) and need to maintain pressure on the table. FiveTwo is the chip leader and aggressive, but KQs plays well post-flop. I'll open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially isolate the short stack.

J9 suited is a decent hand to defend against a button raise. With 17BB, I have a few options: fold, call, or shove. Calling keeps me in the pot with a playable suited connector that can make straights and flushes. My stack is short enough that shoving could be justified, but J9s plays reasonably well post-flop if I hit. Against a likely wide button range, I'll call and look to connect with the board. If FiveTwo squeezes big behind me, I can fold.

Getting 200 to win 700 (about 3.5:1) in position disadvantage but versus a wide Button open and a short SB cold-call range. Q7o isn’t strong enough to 3-bet/squeeze profitably here—too often dominated when called and awkward multiway—so I’ll take the excellent price and realize equity postflop.

I completely whiffed this flop with Jc9c on 8d2d5c. I have overcards, a backdoor flush draw, and a weak gutshot (need 7-T). With only ~15BB remaining and two opponents, betting into a dry board as a bluff is risky. Checking to see a free turn is the right play - I can improve to a pair, pick up a flush draw, or hit my straight draw on the turn.

Multiway pot with Q7o on 8d-2d-5c gives us essentially just two overcards (and no backdoor flush), so our equity realization is poor and bluff-leading is risky—especially with the SB very short, who can easily jam over a stab and force us to burn chips. Versus the Button’s range, we’re happy to take the free card and then mostly check-fold to meaningful action.

I have a strong flush draw with two overcards (King and Queen). As the pre-flop aggressor, I am following up with a standard continuation bet of 50% pot. This puts pressure on Claude, who is short-stacked, and allows me to take the lead in the hand. If FiveTwo calls or raises, I still have significant equity to hit my flush or improve to top pair on the turn or river.

With Jc9c on 8d2d5c, I completely whiffed. I have overcards and a weak backdoor club flush draw, but no real made hand. Flash bet 450 into 900, and FiveTwo is still behind me. With only 1475 chips (~15BB), calling 450 (30% of my stack) with just overcards and backdoor equity is too loose. If I call and miss the turn, I'll be in terrible shape with 10BB. I need to preserve my stack for better spots.

Button is c-betting small (450 into 1350) after it checks through to him; once Claude folds this becomes heads-up. We’re getting 450 to win 1800 (~4:1), and Q7o still has ~6 clean-ish outs versus a wide button range (Q gives us top pair; 7 gives us a pair that can be good vs his air). No backdoor flush, so we’re not looking to build a pot—just realize equity and keep his range honest versus small cbets. Call once and proceed mostly check-fold on larger turn barrels unless we improve or the runout/size gives us a good bluff-catching spot.

Turn brings the Ah, which heavily favors the Button’s perceived range and reduces our equity/outs (Q is no longer an overcard to the top card). With Q7 high and no meaningful draws, leading would be a low-quality bluff and just risks getting raised/put in tough spots versus a range that can now credibly have many Ax hands. Best is to check and decide versus his sizing: we can fold to normal/large barrels and only consider continuing versus very small bets or favorable rivers.

The Turn card is the Ace of Hearts, which is a scare card for both players but technically favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor and flop bettor. However, FiveTwo (the Big Blind) has been extremely active and has a massive chip lead. My current hand is a King-high flush draw with two overcards (though the Ace on the board reduces the value of the King and Queen as potential top-pair outs if FiveTwo has an Ace). By checking behind, I exercise pot control against a dangerous opponent and ensure that I see the river for free, allowing me to realize the equity of my flush draw without the risk of being check-raised off the hand.

River brings the 7s giving us a weak pair (7s) with a Q kicker in a pot that’s now heads-up versus the Button. This card improves us versus his flop-cbet air/overcards, but it’s not a good value-bet spot: Flash can still have many Ax hands (turn A favors his range), plus 8x/5x that continue from flop, and he’ll rarely call worse often enough to make a thin bet profitable. Checking keeps the pot controlled, maximizes our chance to realize showdown versus his give-ups, and also induces bluffs from missed diamond draws/overcards. Plan versus a bet: call some small sizes as a bluff-catch, fold to larger polarized bets.

The board has missed my flush draw, and I am left with King-high. FiveTwo (the Big Blind) called my flop continuation bet and has checked through the turn and river. While my King-high might occasionally win against a lower missed flush draw, it loses to any pair (8x, 5x, 2x, or a small pocket pair). However, Claude is extremely short-stacked (less than 15 big blinds) and likely to bust soon. Given that FiveTwo is the massive chip leader and has shown a tendency for aggressive and sticky play in previous hands, bluffing here is risky as he might call with any mediocre pair. My priority is to maintain my second-place position and wait for Claude to exit the tournament. Checking back allows me a free showdown and preserves my stack.

I have A8o on the button with ~15bb stack. This is a clear spot to open-shove against just the blinds. A8o is a strong hand for a short-stack shove on the button. With only 1475 chips (about 15bb), shoving is the right play as it denies playability to the blinds and maximizes fold equity.

Claude’s open to 1475 is extremely large for 50/100, and calling from the small blind with 56o puts us out of position in an inflated pot with a hand that realizes equity poorly and has weak implied odds. A bluff 3-bet is also unattractive versus this sizing/strength range and with the BB still to act. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Claude has shoved his remaining 1475 chips (approx. 15BB) from the button. My pot odds are approximately 45.8% (1375 to call into a total pot of 3000). Queen-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand that generally has only about 33-38% equity against a standard late-position shoving range. Since the math does not favor a call, I will fold and preserve my stack.

3-handed on the button with A5o and covering both stacks, I want to apply maximum pressure to the blinds—especially the very short-stacked BB (15bb). A small open accomplishes: (1) frequent immediate wins of the blinds/antes-equivalent, (2) isolates the short stack’s shove/call range while risking the minimum, and (3) keeps me flexible versus the SB’s potential 3-bet. A5 also blocks strong Ax holdings, improving my steal/bluff profitability and giving reasonable equity if the BB jams.

I have been dealt Ace-King suited, a premium starting hand. The button (FiveTwo), who is the current chip leader, has opened with a min-raise to 200. I am in the small blind with a healthy stack, while the big blind (Claude) is very short-stacked with only about 15 big blinds. I want to 3-bet here for value and to discourage the big blind from entering the pot cheaply. A 3-bet also allows me to take the initiative against the aggressive chip leader. I will raise to 750, which is a standard 3-bet size from the small blind.

Short-stacked with ~15BB. KTo is a marginal hand against a 3-bet from Flash. Calling 650 leaves only 875 behind, essentially committing me. Jamming for 1525 total gives Flash great odds to call. KTo doesn't play well in 3-bet pots - it's dominated by AK, KQ, and flipping against pairs. With the tournament winding down and only 27 hands left, preserving my short stack for better spots is prudent. Fold.

With Claude folding, this becomes heads-up vs Flash’s SB 3-bet range, which is typically quite wide 3-handed versus a min-open. A5o is an ideal low-cost 4-bet bluff here: it blocks strong continue hands (AA/AK/AQ/A5), has decent equity when called, and I’m the big stack so I can apply pressure without risking my tournament. Calling invites tough postflop spots with a dominated offsuit Ace and lets Flash realize equity; instead I prefer a small, disciplined 4-bet that wins the pot often and keeps me able to comfortably fold if Flash 5-bet jams.

I am holding Ace-King suited, a premium hand, especially in three-handed play. FiveTwo, the chip leader, is playing very aggressively and has 4-bet me to 2,000 after my initial 3-bet to 750. While calling is an option to keep the pot smaller and keep their bluffs in, 5-betting to 5,000 allows me to take back the initiative and maximize value from a range that FiveTwo likely plays wider as the chip leader. Given my stack size and the strength of AKs, I am comfortable playing for a large pot or even going all-in if necessary, though this specific sizing (5,000) puts the pressure back on the opponent and allows me to see how they react to further aggression.

I like the initial 4-bet bluff with A5o, but once Flash comes back with a sizeable 5-bet (to 5000) he’s either extremely value-heavy or using a sizing that creates a very low SPR if I call. Calling 3000 more would put ~10k in the middle and leave ~11.5k behind for Flash, meaning I’ll face a lot of shove/c-bet pressure on most flops with a dominated offsuit Ace and very limited realization. Turning this into a 6-bet jam would require a very high fold frequency (and when called I’m in bad shape versus the likely continue range). Given the poor playability and unfavorable risk/reward, I cut the bluff here and preserve my stack to keep applying pressure in better spots.

I am on the button with 8h 3h. While it is a suited hand, it is overall very weak. Claude is in the small blind with a very short stack of less than 15 big blinds, making him likely to shove over a button raise. FiveTwo in the big blind is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively. Given the tournament stage and the specific stack sizes, there is no need to enter the pot with such a marginal hand.

Short stacked at ~15BB with 57o in the SB heads up against the big blind. This is a weak hand - not strong enough to shove profitably, and limping OOP with a weak hand against a much larger stack is a leak. Better to save my 50 chips and wait for a stronger spot to commit.

Short stack at 14.75 BB on button 3-handed. Q4o is a marginal hand - while I need to be aggressive with my short stack, Q4o is too weak to shove here against two massive stacks who can call light. I'll fold and look for a better spot in the remaining 26 hands.

Claude (button) has folded, so this is blind-vs-blind heads-up versus Flash. KJ suited is near the top of my SB opening range and plays very well with initiative. Limping gives BB a free realization with a wide range; raising both wins the blinds often and sets up profitable c-bets/value lines on many textures. I’ll use a normal, pressure-applied but not oversized open to keep my range balanced and avoid bloating the pot unnecessarily while still denying equity.

I have pocket nines in the Big Blind against a raise from the Small Blind. This is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. FiveTwo has been playing aggressively, but I have a significant stack and position on him for the rest of the hand. I will 3-bet to 900 to take the initiative and extract value from his wider small blind opening range.

Flash’s 3-bet to 900 is a fairly standard sizing versus my SB open, and I’m getting good immediate price (600 to win 1200) with a hand that has strong playability and realization potential even OOP. KJs is near the top of my defend range here: it flops well (strong top pairs, good backdoors, and nutty draws) and keeps Flash’s wider 3-bet range in with dominated Kx/Jx and suited connectors. A 4-bet would bloat the pot OOP and often just folds out the hands I want to continue against, while rarely getting action from worse at these depths. So I defend by calling and play postflop with a protected range.

3-bet pot OOP on a fairly standard BB-favoring texture (J-high with a flush draw). My default is to check my entire range here and let the preflop aggressor c-bet: it keeps my checking range protected, avoids building a bloated pot when I’m not nutted, and allows Flash to continue bluffing with hands like AK/AQ/diamond floats. With KJ (top pair, good kicker) I’m happy to check and then mostly check-call versus reasonable sizing, reserving check-raises for more polarized value (sets/two pair) and strong draws.

I have an overpair to the two low cards on a Jack-high board, and I hold the nine of diamonds for a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I should maintain aggression here. FiveTwo's check allows me to place a continuation bet to extract value from middle pairs like 88, 7x, or various diamond draws. A bet of 600 into a pot of 1800 is a standard 1/3 pot sizing that allows me to protect my hand and gather information without overcommitting.

We have top pair with a strong kicker (KJ) in a 3-bet pot OOP on J-7-2 with a diamond flush draw. Flash’s c-bet is very small (600 into 2400), which keeps his range extremely wide (overcards like AK/AQ, some floats with diamonds, and occasional overpairs/strong Jx). Raising here tends to fold out the hands we dominate/bluff (the part we want to keep in) while getting action mainly from better (AJ, overpairs, sets) and strong draws, so it’s not ideal given stack dynamics. Check-calling protects our checking range, realizes equity, and allows Flash to continue barreling with worse hands.

Turn pairs the deuce, which doesn’t meaningfully improve our one-pair hand but does reduce villain’s incentive to bluff huge and also gives us a strong bluff-catcher that benefits from letting Flash continue with his wide range (AK/AQ, diamond floats, some worse Jx). Donk-leading here would often fold out exactly the hands we want to keep in (air/overcards), while mostly getting action from better Jx/overpairs/strong draws. Checking keeps our range protected, allows Flash to barrel bluffs, and if he checks back we can value-bet many rivers ourselves.

The turn is a deuce, which is essentially a blank card unless my opponent has exactly A2s or an unlikely deuce in their range. However, with the Jack on the board and FiveTwo calling my flop bet, I need to be cautious about over-extending with pocket nines. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size and realize my showdown value. If FiveTwo has a drawing hand like diamonds, I'm still ahead, and if he has a Jack, I avoid a potentially large check-raise. I will evaluate his action on the river.

River comes a complete brick for the missed diamonds and doesn’t change our relative hand strength: we still have top pair with a strong kicker (KJ). After Flash’s small c-bet and turn check-back, his range contains a lot of hands that will often take a free showdown (AQ/AK/KQ, some underpairs) plus some worse Jx that can pay off (QJ/JT) and some overpairs/traps. Checking risks letting all the worse Jx and medium pairs check behind, while also potentially facing a larger polar bet that puts us in a tougher bluff-catch spot.

I like a small river value/block bet to (1) get thin value from worse Jx and some stubborn pairs, and (2) keep the price controlled versus better hands. If raised big, we can comfortably bet-fold given how under-repped our value is and how rarely we’re raised as a bluff here.

On the river, the board is Js 2d 7d 2h 3s. I have pocket nines, which is an overpair to most of the board but behind any Jack or any Deuce. My opponent, FiveTwo, has led out for 1100 into a pot of 3000 after I checked back the turn. This bet size (roughly 1/3 pot) could indicate a thin value bet with a Jack, or a blocker bet/bluff with a missed diamond draw or a smaller pocket pair (like 88 or 66). Given that I checked the turn, I have capped my range, and FiveTwo might be trying to push me off a hand like AK/AQ or a missed draw of my own. I am getting excellent pot odds (roughly 3.7 to 1), needing to be right only about 21% of the time. Nines are a strong enough 'bluff catcher' in this specific sequence to justify a call, especially since the deuce on the turn and trey on the river are unlikely to have improved his specific range unless he is playing very loose with 2x hands.

3-handed on the button as the big chip leader, I want to apply pressure and pick up the blinds very frequently. Q4o is weak but still within a wide button opening range, especially with a very short BB who is often in shove/fold mode. Raising also isolates the BB: if he jams his 13.75bb, the pot odds after I’ve raised to 300 are good enough to continue versus a wide shoving range, while if the SB plays back with real strength I can comfortably fold and avoid bloating a pot with a marginal hand.

In a 3-handed game, 5d 6d is a strong speculative hand. FiveTwo has been active and is likely opening wide from the button. I am getting 2.8:1 odds to call. Claude in the big blind is very short-stacked (under 14 BB), so I must be prepared for him to shove. However, even if Claude shoves, the price to call the remainder of his all-in will likely be acceptable given the pot odds and the playability of my suited connectors. Calling allows me to see a flop relatively cheaply while keeping the pot manageable against the chip leader.

I have 68o in the BB with only 1375 chips (~14bb). FiveTwo raised and Flash called. I'd need to call 200 into a pot of 700. While pot odds are decent (22% equity needed), 68o is a weak hand that plays poorly multi-way and out of position. With my short stack and poor playability post-flop, folding is the disciplined play here.

I have 5d 6d on a 3s 9d Ts flop, giving me very little equity—only a backdoor diamond flush draw. FiveTwo is the chip leader and opened from the button, meaning his range connects well with the 9 and 10 on this board. Since I am out of position and have no made hand or strong draw, checking is the best play to keep the pot small and see how the aggressor proceeds.

Heads-up vs the SB caller on a T-9-3 two-tone board, I still have a meaningful range advantage as the button raiser (lots of overpairs/top pairs and strong broadways). My actual hand (Q4o) is mostly air with just a queen overcard and a weak backdoor diamond, but this is a great spot to apply low-risk pressure and fold out a large chunk of Flash’s whiffed holdings (A-high/K-high, some small pairs, random floats) while denying equity. I’ll use a small c-bet to keep the pot controlled and make it easy to fold if I face a check-raise.

On a flop of 3s-9d-Ts, my 6-high hand with only a backdoor diamond flush draw is extremely weak. This board texture correlates strongly with a Button opening range, which likely includes many Tens, Nines, and straight draws. FiveTwo, the chip leader, is betting 250 into a pot of 700. While the price is relatively small, I am out of position and have almost no equity or realistic path to winning the hand unless I hit runner-runner cards. It is more prudent to fold now and preserve my stack rather than floating out of position with nothing.

I am on the button with King-Ten of clubs, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. Claude is extremely short-stacked with only 1,325 chips (about 13 big blinds), so any raise I make puts him under significant pressure. I want to raise to build the pot and potentially isolate Claude or take the blinds down immediately. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

AJo in the SB with only 13BB effective. Flash opened from button. This is a standard 3-bet shove spot - AJo plays well against a button opening range, and with this stack size I don't want to call and play OOP. Shoving gives me fold equity plus good equity when called.

We’re massive chip leader and Claude is essentially all-in (50 behind), so there’s no need to take a marginal spot to try to “bust him” when Flash will often continue anyway, creating a multiway pot. Q2o is dominated by a huge portion of both ranges (any Qx, Ax, broadways, pairs), plays poorly out of position, and has very little ability to realize equity postflop. Preserving our lead and letting Flash handle the short stack is higher EV than lighting 1225 chips on fire here.

I have King-Ten of clubs on the button, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. Claude has raised to 1,325, leaving him with only 50 chips, effectively making this an all-in situation for his 13-big-blind stack. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 1,025 to win a pot that will be over 2,700) and KTs has significant equity against the range of a short-stacked player in the small blind. I will call to see a flop and likely commit the rest on any board given the pot size and his remaining 50 chips.

I have only 50 chips left with a pot of 2750. With AJ on a 678 connected board, I completely whiffed, but with less than 2% of the pot remaining in my stack, I must commit my last chips. Checking/folding makes no sense - I need to bet my remaining 50 and hope to catch an A or J.

Claude has bet his last 50 chips into a pot of 2,800. This gives me pot odds of 56-to-1. Even though the board (6-7-8) doesn't directly hit my King-Ten of clubs, I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. Mathematically, I must call with any two cards given these astronomical odds.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo), which is a solid hand in heads-up play. The opponent in the Small Blind/Button position has made a small raise, requiring me to call 50 more to match a total of 150. Given that QTo is well within the top tier of hands for heads-up defending and likely ahead of a wide button-opening range, calling is a standard move. This allows me to see a flop and realize my hand's equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily out of position against a larger stack.

Heads-up on the button/SB with J9o, we want to apply maximum preflop pressure and realize our positional advantage. J9o plays well as an open-raise (can make strong top pairs/straights) and performs better with initiative than by checking behind. Versus a competent BB, a small but non-min raise keeps our range wide, forces folds from the bottom of their range, and builds a pot when we have position. I’ll use a standard small open size rather than a limp/check.

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo) is a strong broadway hand that performs well against a typical button opening range. Given the deep stacks (over 200 BBs), calling the raise is the most balanced approach. It allows me to see a flop and realize the hand's equity without bloating the pot out of position against a larger stack. The price offered (150 to call into a 350 pot) is excellent for a hand of this strength.

We flop top two (J9) on a fairly dynamic board with a heart draw and some straight possibilities (T8/QT). In position, we want to start building a pot for value versus Jx, 9x, and all flush-draw/straight-draw floats, while also charging equity. A medium-to-large c-bet accomplishes this without forcing worse pairs to fold too often.

On the flop of 9h-4h-Jc, I have an open-ended straight draw with Queen-Ten (8s and Kings complete the straight). The opponent, FiveTwo, led out with a bet of 350 into a 500 pot. With 8 outs to a straight, my equity is approximately 16% to hit on the turn and roughly 32% by the river. Given the current pot odds (350 to call into a total pot of 850, meaning I need about 29% equity to call profitably if we go to showdown), and the fact that I have two overcards (Queen and Ten) which could provide additional outs if they don't give the opponent a better pair, calling is a standard and mathematically sound play. This allows me to realize my equity and see the turn card without over-inflating the pot out of position against a stack that is currently larger than mine.

We still have a very strong value hand (top two on J94) and the turn doesn’t meaningfully change the board texture except adding a few more straight-draw combos. Flash’s range after calling flop contains lots of Jx/9x, heart draws, and pair+draw hands that will continue versus a solid sizing. We want to (1) keep building the pot for value, (2) deny equity to hearts/straight draws, and (3) set up a larger river bet. A ~75% pot turn bet accomplishes this without folding out too much of the dominated value region.

On the turn, I am facing a 900 chip bet into a 1200 chip pot, making the total pot 2100. With my open-ended straight draw (8 outs: Kings and Eights), my equity to complete the hand on the river is approximately 17.4%. The pot odds offered are 2.33 to 1 (calling 900 to win a potential 3000), which requires roughly 30% equity to be a break-even call. Given that the card odds are 4.75 to 1, this call is significantly negative in terms of direct pot odds. While implied odds exist in heads-up play, the presence of a flush draw on the board (9h, 4h) devalues two of my outs (Kh and 8h), and being out of position makes it difficult to extract maximum value if I hit or to represent a hand if I miss. Given the large bet sizing from FiveTwo and my stack of 216 BBs, I will fold to preserve my chips for a more favorable situation.

Heads-up and getting a very good price facing a tiny 1.5x open; folding J5 suited would be far too tight. J5s has solid realization potential (flushes, some top-pair/straight connectivity) and calling keeps the pot manageable while we play deep-stacked and out of position, where a marginal 3-bet pot would be higher variance. We defend and look to outplay postflop.

In this heads-up hand, I am holding 8c 2c on the button (Small Blind). The pot is currently 200 chips and there is nothing to call (0 chips), which indicates that the big blind has already been matched. Since 8-2 suited is a very weak hand, there is no merit in raising here to build a pot, especially given the stack disadvantage against FiveTwo. Checking is the most prudent action as it allows me to see the flop without committing any further chips.

On this flop of Ace of Clubs, Ten of Diamonds, and Seven of Spades, my 8-high hand has completely missed. The board is quite favorable for the big blind's range, especially with the Ace present. I have very little equity and am out of position. Checking is the only viable play here to avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand, and I will likely fold to any aggression from FiveTwo.
