Limped heads-up pot and villain checks to us, giving up initiative. On A-T-7 we have very little made equity with J5c, but we do have some backdoor potential (clubs, some overcard turns) and this is a great spot to take a cheap stab and fold out a lot of K/Q-high and small pairs that missed. I’ll use the minimum sizing to risk little while still applying pressure; if faced with a check-raise I’ll comfortably fold, and I’ll continue selectively on favorable turns (especially clubs/broadway cards) while often shutting down on bricks.

I have 8-high on an Ace-high board with no real draws. FiveTwo's bet of 100 into 200 represents a standard continuation or value bet on a board that hits many parts of a Big Blind range. With zero current equity and very little potential to improve, calling or raising would be a waste of chips. The most disciplined action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 3c, a very weak hand. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) has completed/called the blind. In this heads-up situation, there is no reason to raise with such a poor hand. I will check my option to see a free flop and minimize the pot size.

Heads-up on the button with J4o, we should be opening very wide to leverage positional advantage and initiative. J4o is comfortably within a HU raising range (high-card value, blocks some Jx continues, and plays fine in position). I’ll use a small, standard raise size to keep ranges wide and avoid inflating the pot unnecessarily while still pressuring the big blind’s limp/check range.

I am holding 8h 3c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. FiveTwo, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 250. Calling the additional 150 to see a flop out of position with such a poor hand is a losing play in the long run, even in a heads-up situation. I will fold to preserve my stack for more favorable spots as we approach the final hands of the tournament.

Heads-up vs a limp from the button, Q8o is well within the range to attack. Raising denies equity to his wide limp/call range, lets us take initiative, and applies pressure on the shorter effective stack without overinflating the pot. A standard iso size from the big blind is ~3bb total here.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Qc Jc, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. FiveTwo has raised to 300 from the Big Blind. With my stack size and the strength of my suited broadway hand, I should definitely continue. While a 3-bet is a viable option to take control of the hand, simply calling and playing in position is also a strong strategy that keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to evaluate the flop. Given the tournament context and my current stack, I will call to see the flop.

In this heads-up situation, I am in position as the Button/Small Blind. Pre-flop, I called a 3-bet from FiveTwo. The flop comes Kd-Ks-3h, which is a very dry, paired board. FiveTwo, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has checked to me. This check could either be a trap with a King or an Ace, or an admission of missing the board with hands like AQ or smaller pocket pairs. My QcJc has some showdown value against his air and is not a strong candidate to bluff with right now because the board is so static. By checking behind, I control the size of the pot, keep my range wide, and get to see a free turn card which could potentially improve my hand or give me a better opportunity to take the pot later.

Flop is very dry and heavily favors the preflop iso-raiser: we have more strong Kx and overpairs, while his limp/call range contains lots of unpaired junk that will simply fold. With Q8o we have little showdown value, so checking invites him to start betting turns and force us off our equity. A small range c-bet pressures his A-high/low pairs, denies equity, and keeps our risk controlled; if he check-raises we can comfortably fold.

The flop Kd-Ks-3h is highly unfavorable for my holding of QcJc, especially as I am up against the pre-flop aggressor. On such a static board, my Queen-high has very little equity against a range that includes any King, Ace-high, or pocket pair. A bet of 200 into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet, but without any draws or significant showdown value, there is no reason to continue. I will fold to preserve my stack and avoid further losses on later streets.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket threes, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up match. The current pot and call amount suggest a small raise from the Small Blind. Calling is a prudent play here to see a flop and potentially flop a set or play the hand based on board texture, without over-bloating the pot against a larger stack. My total contribution to the pot for this round will be 150 chips.

Heads-up on the button with 55, I want to play an aggressive open strategy: this hand is well ahead of a typical limp/check-back range and benefits from taking initiative. Raising also denies equity to overcards, helps define ranges, and sets up profitable c-bets on many flops. I’ll use a standard non-all-in size (about 3x) rather than min-raising to apply more pressure while still keeping worse hands in.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket threes, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up match. FiveTwo has raised to 300. Calling is a prudent play here to see a flop and potentially flop a set or play the hand based on board texture, without over-bloating the pot against a larger stack while I am out of position. My total contribution to the pot for this round will be 300 chips.

Monotone J-high flop is a poor c-bet spot for 55 with no heart: Flash’s BB calling range contains a lot of hands with at least one heart (auto-continues), plus Jx/7x/4x that won’t fold to a small stab. Betting risks getting check-raised off our equity and forces us into ugly turn barrels/catch decisions with a marginal underpair. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our small showdown value (often still ahead of no-heart overcards), and lets us re-evaluate on the turn (we can fold to significant heat, or continue on favorable runouts).

On a monotone flop of 4h-7h-Jh, I hold pocket threes with the Three of Hearts. While I have a made hand, it is extremely vulnerable on this texture. My opponent, FiveTwo, has checked as the initial raiser. Checking back is the most prudent play here to take a free card and avoid bloat on a board where many turn cards could be problematic or improve my hand/flush draw significantly. This also keeps the pot small while playing out of position.

After the flop checked through, Flash’s range is still very wide and contains plenty of two-overcard/no-heart hands that currently lose to our 55. The turn (9c) adds more overcard/straight pressure and if I check back again I’ll often face a river stab that puts this marginal underpair in a tough bluff-catch spot. A small-to-medium turn bet functions as a cheap protection/value-denial bet (folding out Ax/Kx/Qx without a heart), while still keeping the pot controlled; if Flash check-raises on this monotone texture, I can comfortably fold because that line is heavily weighted to made flushes/strong hearts/Jx+.

On a board of 4h-7h-Jh-9c, my pocket threes are now weaker than any potential pair my opponent could have made with the board cards. While I do possess the three of hearts, providing a negligible flush draw, the overall coordination of the board and the fact that FiveTwo is betting after a flop check-around suggests they have either improved on the turn or are taking a stab at a pot I haven't shown interest in. Given that nearly any hand in a heads-up range has significant equity against pocket threes here, calling 250 into a pot of 850 is not a profitable long-term play with such low showdown value and minimal outs. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

Heads-up BB vs SB open to 150 gives us great pot odds and Q9o is well within a defend range. With a big stack advantage and an opponent who has shown willingness to fold to pressure in recent hands, I prefer mixing in a light 3-bet rather than flatting: it can win preflop immediately, builds initiative for profitable c-bets, and pressures their capped/weak opening range at a small risk relative to our stack. Sizing to 500 (about 3.3x their raise) keeps their weaker hands in while still denying equity and setting up clean postflop lines.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 3h, which is a weak hand. FiveTwo has opened for a 5x raise to 500. Given the weak nature of my hand and being out of position if I call, the optimal play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity, especially as we are nearing the end of the tournament and I want to preserve my stack.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Deuce suited is a premium hand. Since FiveTwo has merely completed the small blind, I have a significant range advantage. I will raise to 400 to take the initiative, build the pot, and capitalize on my equity advantage against a likely wider range from the opponent.

Heads-up SB/button I limped and got iso-raised to 400. A6s plays well in position and is ahead of a wide BB iso range; it’s also a good hand to mix in as a limp-3bet to punish frequent raises and take initiative. I’ll apply pressure with a controlled sizing that can win preflop often, yet doesn’t bloat the pot excessively if called; if faced with a large 4-bet, I can comfortably fold given stack depth.

In a heads-up game, Ace-Deuce suited is a very strong hand. My opponent, FiveTwo, has 3-bet my open to 1,400. While I am out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is not an option given the strength of my hand. A 4-bet is possible as a semi-bluff due to the Ace blocker, but calling is more prudent to see a flop and potentially realize my equity or hit a big draw without over-bloating the pot against a chip leader.

3-bet pot in position: although this low, connected board interacts with BB’s calling range, I still have strong range advantage (overpairs/AK) and can profitably apply pressure with a small c-bet. As6s has some equity (wheel straight via a 3, plus backdoor spades) and benefits from denying equity to BB’s unpaired overcards while keeping the pot controlled. If raised, this hand is too thin to continue; if called, I can barrel good turns (spades, A, 3, and some high cards) and otherwise take a check-back line when my equity doesn’t improve.

Flash has hit bottom pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a low, somewhat connected board. Given the pre-flop 3-bet from FiveTwo, their range includes many high cards that missed this flop, as well as overpairs. The bet of 900 into a 2800 pot offers excellent pot odds. Flash also has a backdoor diamond flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3). Calling is the most prudent play to see how the turn develops while keeping the pot size under control.

Turn is a strong range-advantage card for the 3-bettor: I have lots of AK/KQ/KK/AA, while BB’s flop-calling range on 2-5-4 contains many small pairs/5x/4x and some floats that don’t improve with a K. With As6s I still have equity (gutshot to the wheel on a 3, plus occasional ace-high showdown value if it checks through), so a second barrel is a good semi-bluff that also denies equity and pressures his medium-strength holdings. I’ll use a medium sizing that generates folds without overcommitting; if raised, I can comfortably fold this hand. If called, I’ll proceed selectively on rivers (value on A/3; otherwise often slow down unless river is very favorable to continue bluffing).

On the turn, the King of Hearts is a significant scare card that heavily favors FiveTwo's pre-flop 3-betting range, which likely includes hands like AK, KQ, and KJ. My bottom pair of deuces with an Ace kicker has lost much of its relative value. While I have a gutshot straight draw (to a 3) and a few outs to improve to two pair or trips, the pot odds (calling 2,400 to win a total pot of 9,400, or ~25.5%) do not justify a call when my current equity is likely only around 18% if I am behind. Given FiveTwo's aggression and the high probability that they have improved or are holding a superior made hand, folding here preserves my stack of over 16,000 chips for more favorable spots in the final hands of the tournament.

Heads-up in the BB facing a limp, we should attack very wide to deny equity and take initiative. K5s is well ahead of villain’s limping range, plays fine postflop with top-pair potential and flush/straight equity, and we cover villain so we can apply pressure across streets. I’ll use a larger isolation size to punish the limp and reduce SPR while still keeping worse hands in: 4.5x total.

In this heads-up situation, I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Jack-Eight offsuit. My opponent, FiveTwo, who has a significant chip lead and has been playing very aggressively, has raised to 4.5x the big blind (450). While Jack-Eight is a reasonable hand in heads-up play, calling a large raise out of position against a dominant chip leader often leads to difficult post-flop scenarios where I am forced to fold or commit too many chips with a marginal hand. Given the current momentum and the sizing of the raise, I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous spot to close the gap.

Based on the hand rotation, I am in the Small Blind position, despite the label. The pot is currently 150 (my 50 chip SB + the opponent's 100 chip BB). To complete the blind and see a flop, I need to call an additional 50 chips, bringing my total commitment for this street to 100. In heads-up play, it is standard practice to complete the small blind with a wide range of hands because of the favorable pot odds (calling 50 to win a pot of 150). While 9-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands, I will call to see the flop for the minimal cost of 0.5 BB.

Heads-up on the button/small blind, we should play an aggressive open-raising strategy to leverage position and our significant stack advantage. J4o isn’t premium, but it’s comfortably inside a standard HU opening range, and raising pressures Flash’s big blind, wins the pot outright often, and gives us initiative in position when called. I’ll keep the sizing modest to risk little while still denying equity and creating fold equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-2 offsuit. FiveTwo, who has been aggressive lately, has raised to 250 after I merely completed the Small Blind. To continue, I must call 150 more into a pot of 350, providing 2.33:1 odds. However, 9-2 offsuit is statistically one of the worst hands in poker and performs poorly even in heads-up play, especially against a player with a significant chip lead who is exerting pressure. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up versus a very small SB open to 150, we’re getting an excellent price (only 50 more) and should defend extremely wide. J3o is weak and dominated when we build big pots, so I prefer a flat over a 3-bet: we keep the pot small, realize equity with position disadvantage but strong pot odds, and can still leverage our large stack edge postflop with better board coverage and pressure on later streets.

In Hand #137, I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 5c 2c. The current hand is #137 out of 150, and I am facing a single opponent, FiveTwo, who has a significant chip lead. My stack is 18,125 while theirs is 41,675. The action is currently on me in the Small Blind. FiveTwo has already checked/called the 50 chip blind (effectively completing the blind or checking if the UI implies they are in the BB). Since I am on the button and it's a heads-up situation, I have the option to check or raise. 5c 2c is a weak hand, but suited. Given the tournament stage and the desire to preserve stack for better spots while occasionally applying pressure, checking behind to see a free flop is a low-risk play that keeps the pot small.

In Hand #137, I am heads-up against FiveTwo. I have top pair (Fives) with a weak kicker and a gutshot straight draw on a coordinated board of 5d-3h-4d. Since FiveTwo checked to me on the flop in a limped pot, I want to bet to protect my hand against overcards and diamond draws while extracting some value from weaker pairs or straight draws. A half-pot bet of 100 is appropriate here to define where I stand in the hand.

We’re getting a very good price versus a tiny c-bet (100 into 300): we need only ~25% equity to continue. On 5d-3h-4d, J3 makes a pair, which is often ahead of Flash’s wide SB min-open range that contains lots of two-overcard hands and air. Raising doesn’t accomplish much here—better hands (any 5/4, A2/76, diamond draws) continue and we bloat a pot with a marginal made hand—so the best line is to defend and keep ranges wide. We’ll reassess on the turn (improving on a 3/J, and otherwise proceeding cautiously versus bigger barreling).

The turn card (3d) is highly coordinated, completing a potential diamond flush and pairing the board. While I have top pair (Fives), my kicker is weak and I am vulnerable to many hands in FiveTwo's range, including flushes, trips, or better two pairs. Checking back here allows me to see a river for free and control the pot size while retaining some showdown value.

We improved to trips (3s) on a paired, 3-diamond turn. It’s a strong hand in a limped pot, but not the nuts: any 5x/4x makes a full house, two diamonds makes a flush, and some 2x+6/A combos make a straight. After Flash’s small flop stab and turn check, his range still contains lots of weak pairs and air that will fold to pressure, plus some hands that can still call a small bet (random 4x/5x, pocket pairs, occasional sticky overcards). I want to (1) take thin value/protection versus those weaker holdings, (2) deny a free river to hands that can improve, and (3) keep the pot controlled so we can comfortably fold if he springs a big raise representing boats/flushes/straights.

On the turn, the 3d pairs the board and completes the diamond flush draw. While my top pair from the flop (Fives) has been downgraded to two pair (Fives and Threes), I am facing a bet of 200 into a 400 pot (offering 3:1 pot odds). FiveTwo has been aggressive in recent hands, and this bet could be a semi-bluff with a diamond draw, a straight draw, or even a lower pair like Fours. Given the pot odds and the fact that I still have a gutshot to the wheel (any Ace), I will call to see the river. However, I must be cautious; if the river brings another diamond or significant action, I will likely have to fold as my kicker is extremely weak.

On the river, the board shows 5d-3h-4d-3d-Kc. I have two pair, Fives and Threes, with a Deuce kicker. The turn card (3d) completed the diamond flush draw and paired the board, while the river (Kc) added an overcard. Since the pot was limped and has remained relatively small, my hand has some showdown value against missed straight draws or lower pairs like Fours. FiveTwo has checked to me on the river. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and would expose me to a check-raise from a flush, trips, or a better two pair. Checking back allows me to reach showdown safely and preserve my stack.

River goes check to us on 5d-3h-4d-3d-Kc and we hold trip 3s (J kicker). After his small flop stab, turn check/call versus our small raise, and now a river check, Flash’s range is still very wide and contains lots of hands that will call a modest value bet: any 5x/4x (two-pair with board 3s), some Kx that improved on the river, and occasional sticky pocket pairs. We are not nutted (A2/62/76 straights and some 3x/boats exist), so I don’t want to bloat the pot or induce a big check-raise—this is a thin value spot where sizing down is best. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot for value and will comfortably fold if he check-raises big, since his bluffs are reduced after taking a passive river line.

In Hand 137, I am holding 5c 2c on a board of 5d-3h-4d-3d-Kc. My best hand is two pair, Fives and Threes, with a King kicker from the board. This hand beats lower two pairs (like 4s and 3s) and any missed straight draws. FiveTwo's bet of 300 into an 800 pot provides 3.6:1 pot odds. In a limped pot, FiveTwo's range is wide, and given his previous aggression, this small bet could be a thin value bet with a King or a 3, a 5-x hand (which results in a chop), or a bluff. Given the favorable pot odds and the likelihood of a chop or a bluff, I will call to see the showdown.

I am in the Big Blind with K5o. The small blind (FiveTwo) has a massive chip lead and has been very aggressive, frequently raising or limping. In this specific spot, the pot is 150 and it's only 50 more to call. Given the extremely favorable pot odds (3:1) and the fact that we are playing heads-up, K-high has decent equity against a wide button range. I will call to see a flop and evaluate, as folding for such a small price is too tight in a heads-up situation.

Heads-up on the button/SB we should be opening very wide, and 6♠5♣ plays well in position (can make straights/two pair) while also benefiting from fold equity preflop. With a sizeable stack edge, applying steady pressure with a small raise is profitable and keeps the pot controllable if we face resistance (3-bet). I’ll use a standard small open sizing to deny the BB a free look and realize positional advantage.

In this heads-up situation, I am in the Big Blind with K5o. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. With a pot of 350 and only 150 more to call, I am getting approximately 2.3:1 odds. K5o is a solid defending hand heads-up against a wide opening range from an aggressive chip leader. Folding would be too exploitable given the price, and raising might invite a 4-bet shove from a player with a much larger stack. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my K-high value or hit a pair.

This flop (8♣9♣3♠) is fairly dynamic and interacts well with BB’s defend range (lots of 8x/9x, pair+draws, and club draws). With 5♣6♠ we have only a gutshot to the 7 plus a backdoor club draw, so if we c-bet and face a check-raise we’re forced to fold off some equity, and if we get called we often have to burn multiple barrels on a board that changes a lot. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity, and lets us take stabs on favorable turns (7, clubs, some overcards) while still having strong hands in our check-back range.

I am holding King-high on a flop of 8c-9c-3s. The opponent (FiveTwo), who was the pre-flop aggressor, has checked. While I could attempt to take the pot down with a bet, King-high has some showdown value in heads-up play against a wide range of missed hands. By checking behind, I see a free turn card, keep the pot small, and avoid being check-raised off my hand. Given the chip lead FiveTwo holds and their aggressive history, a cautious approach here is best.

After the flop checks through, Flash’s range is capped a bit (fewer strong 9x hands bet for value/protection on 8♣9♣3♠), while my check-back range still contains plenty of 9x/overpairs/strong 8x that can now value-bet the paired turn. With 6♠5♣ I have very low showdown value (only a gutshot to the 7), so this is a good spot to apply pressure and pick up the pot against missed overcards, some 8x/3x that don’t want to face multiple barrels, and portions of club-draw/float ranges. I’ll use a medium-small sizing to maximize fold equity without risking much versus a check-raise (which I can comfortably fold to).

In this heads-up situation against a highly aggressive opponent (FiveTwo), I am holding King-high. After a checked flop of 8c-9c-3s, the turn 9h pairs the board, and FiveTwo leads with a bet of 325 into a 500 pot. This bet often represents a delayed continuation bet or a probe with a wide range of hands that King-high can still beat (such as club draws, gutshots, or lower overcards). Given the pot odds of approximately 2.5:1 and the fact that folding here would be over-folding in a heads-up context against an aggressive chip leader, I will call to see the river and re-evaluate based on the final card and FiveTwo's action.

River pairs my 6 and gives me two pair (9s and 6s), which is a nice improvement from my turn bluffing range, but it’s not a great value-betting hand versus Flash’s turn-call range. After check/call on the paired 9 turn, Flash is weighted toward 9x (trips/full houses), 8x (often makes a bigger two pair/full house), and some higher pocket pairs—all of which call a river bet and have me crushed. Meanwhile, the hands I beat (missed club draws, random overcards, some 3x, some weak 6x) either fold to a bet or don’t reliably pay off. So betting risks getting called by better far more often than getting paid by worse; best is to take the free showdown and realize my equity.

In this heads-up hand, the board has run out 8c-9c-3s-9h-6h. FiveTwo, who led on the turn, has now checked the river. My King-high has some showdown value against missed club draws and lower high cards. However, it is unlikely to be the best hand if FiveTwo holds any pair or even Ace-high. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a pair, and checking allows me to see if my King-high is good without risking more chips. Given the stack sizes and the tournament stage, I will take the free showdown.

Heads-up vs a limp, Ah9h is well ahead of the opponent’s completing range and plays very well with initiative (nut-flush potential, strong top-pair potential). Checking lets Flash realize equity with lots of weak hands; raising denies equity, often wins preflop immediately, and builds a pot where our value hands dominate. Since we’re out of position, a slightly larger iso size is preferred to reduce their ability to peel widely.

I am holding 5c4c, which are low suited connectors. In heads-up play, these hands have decent playability post-flop, especially when in position. FiveTwo has a significant chip lead and has been quite active, making it likely they are raising a wide range from the Big Blind. The price to call is 250 chips into a pot that will be 700, giving me nearly 3:1 pot odds. Given my stack size and the remaining hands in the tournament, taking a flop in position with a speculative hand is a sound strategy to try and chip up without overcommitting pre-flop.

On this flop of Jc 7d 3c, I have a flush draw with my 5c 4c. In heads-up play, as the button, I act last on the flop. Since the pot currently stands at 700 and the 'To Call' amount is 0, my opponent, FiveTwo, has checked. While I could bet as a semi-bluff, checking behind allows me to see a free turn card and realize the equity of my flush draw without risking a check-raise from the chip leader. Given the tournament stage and FiveTwo's significant stack advantage, preserving my chips and taking free cards when offered is a sound strategy.

We’re heads-up and have the initiative as the preflop raiser. On J-7-3 with two clubs, my A-high has some showdown value plus two overcards to the 7/3, but it benefits a lot from denying equity (hands like Qx/Tx/8x, weak club floats) and picking up the pot immediately. This board is also a good spot for a small range c-bet: it pressures his wide preflop calling range and keeps my risk low versus a raise. If he calls, we can proceed carefully and improve on many turns (A/K/Q/T/9, heart runouts).

I am holding 5c4c on a Jc7d3c flop, giving me a flush draw. In the previous action, the pot was 700, and my opponent FiveTwo (the chip leader) has now bet 250. This is a relatively small bet of approximately 35% of the pot. Given that I am in position and have a solid draw, calling is the most appropriate action. Calling allows me to see the turn card and potentially realize my equity for a small price without the risk of being shoved off my hand, which could happen if I were to raise against an aggressive chip leader. The pot odds of nearly 4:1 make this a very profitable call with a flush draw.

On the turn, the board is Jc 7d 3c 5d. I have improved my hand to a pair of fives and I still maintain my flush draw with the clubs. The pot is 1200 and my opponent, FiveTwo, has checked to me. Since I am in position and have significant equity with both a pair and a flush draw, checking back is a prudent move. It allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially improve to a flush or two pair on the river without risking a check-raise from the chip leader. Given that the tournament is nearing its end and I am trailing in chips, I want to see the river as cheaply as possible when I have a strong draw but haven't yet locked up the pot.

Turn (Jc 7d 3c 5d) is a relative blank after my small flop c-bet got called. Flash’s range still contains a lot of marginal one-pair hands (7x/3x), overcards that floated, and club/straight draws. With Ah9h I have two overcards but I’m mostly bluffing here, so I want to (1) deny equity to his draws/overcards and (2) apply pressure to his weakest continues while keeping my risk controlled versus a check-raise. A medium barrel accomplishes that and keeps my range credible (I’d bet similarly with Jx/overpairs). If raised, I can comfortably fold; if called, I’ll reassess rivers (value on A, mostly give up on bricks).

I am holding a pair of fives and a strong flush draw on a board of Jc-7d-3c-5d. My opponent, FiveTwo, has bet 800 into a 1200 pot, giving me pot odds of 2.5:1 (approximately 28.5%). I have 17 clean outs to improve to a flush, straight, trips, or two-pair (9 clubs, 3 sixes, 3 fours, and 2 fives), giving me roughly 37% equity on the river. Since my equity is significantly higher than the pot odds required and I am in position, calling is a very profitable decision. This approach also keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to realize the full potential of my hand.

On the river, the board is Jc-7d-3c-5d-6s. My hole cards are 5c-4c, which gives me a 7-high straight (3-4-5-6-7). My opponent, FiveTwo, who has been aggressive throughout the hand by betting both the flop and the turn, has checked to me on the river. While a higher straight is possible if FiveTwo holds an 8 (for an 8-high straight), a 7-high straight is an extremely strong hand in heads-up play. Given FiveTwo's previous aggression, his check could indicate a missed draw or a top-pair hand like AJ or KJ that is now wary of the straight and flush possibilities (though I only hold the club draw myself). I will bet 1,500 into the 2,800 pot to extract value from those types of hands. Checking back would be too passive given the strength of my hand.

We get to the river with Ah9h after betting flop and barreling turn, then checking this 6s river. Flash’s line (limp/call → call flop → call a fairly chunky turn barrel) already filters him toward made hands and the better draws. The 6s is a particularly bad card for our A-high because it completes a lot of the natural turn continues: 4x (A4/64/45/84), plus two-pair/one-pair hands that now improved (65/76/6x). 

When he now leads 1500 into 2800 (pot shown 4300 including his bet), we’re getting ~26% pot odds, but his betting range here is likely value-heavy: many of the missed pure club draws that might bluff are the ones that often don’t continue versus 800 on the turn unless they’re combo-draws (and many of those combo-draws just got there via 4x/straight). With no relevant blockers to his value and only ace-high showdown, this is a low-EV bluffcatch.

Best play is to fold and preserve our stack advantage for higher-leverage spots over the remaining hands.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 9h 7d. The pot is 150, consisting of my 50 SB and the opponent's 100 BB. It costs 50 more to call. Given the stack sizes and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 140 of 150), I want to see a flop with this hand as it has some connectivity and potential, rather than folding or playing a large pot pre-flop with a marginal hand against a chip leader.

Heads-up vs a limp, we should attack very wide from the BB to deny equity and take initiative, especially with a big chip lead and only 10 hands left. T4o is weak but has enough playability and benefits from fold equity; checking just realizes villain’s equity for free. I’ll use a standard iso size that pressures the limp without inflating too much with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind and initially completed the bet. The Big Blind (FiveTwo) has responded with a significant raise to 400. My hand, 9-7 offsuit, is marginal and likely to be dominated by a raise of this size. Although we are heads-up, playing a bloated pot out of position against a chip leader who has shown consistent aggression is a high-risk situation. With only 10 hands remaining in the tournament and a stack of over 180 big blinds, I can afford to fold and wait for a more advantageous position or a stronger hand to contest FiveTwo's dominance.

Heads-up BB vs a small button open, Q7s is well within a strong defend. With a large stack advantage, I prefer a light 3-bet to take initiative and apply pressure preflop (many weaker hands will fold, and we realize equity well when called with suitedness and a reasonably high card). I’ll size to a standard ~3x the open to keep his calling range wide enough while still generating fold equity; if he responds with a sizable 4-bet, we can comfortably fold this hand.

In a heads-up situation, J8o is a hand that should rarely be folded on the button/SB, particularly when we will have position for the rest of the hand. Given that FiveTwo has been increasing their aggression from the big blind, calling the raise is a solid play. We are getting attractive pot odds (calling 350 to win a total pot that will be 900) and our hand has enough high-card value and connectivity to play effectively post-flop.

On this Ace-high flop, my middle pair of Eights with a Jack kicker is a solid marginal hand but not strong enough to bet for value against a chip leader who has been playing aggressively. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and allow myself to see the turn for free. This protects me from a potential check-raise and keeps FiveTwo's range wide, allowing me to potentially catch a bluff on later streets.

Heads-up and as the preflop raiser, this A-high flop is a strong range advantage for me (I have more Ax and overpairs), while Flash’s preflop calling range contains plenty of hands that miss completely (broadways, suited connectors, small pairs that didn’t hit). With Q7o and no spade, I don’t have a great check-back plan because I’ll face lots of turn stabs and tough rivers; I’d rather take a low-risk shot at winning the pot now. I’ll use a small c-bet to apply pressure and deny equity while keeping the bet cheap versus the many Ax continues.

I have middle pair (8s) with a decent kicker on an Ace-high board. My opponent, the chip leader, is betting small (1/3 pot) after raising pre-flop. This is a standard continuation bet that could be made with many hands I'm currently ahead of, as well as various draws. Folding middle pair here in a heads-up situation against an aggressive opponent would be too exploitable. I will call the 300-chip bet to see how the turn develops while keeping the pot size under control.

I currently hold middle pair (Eights) on a board of Ace-Eight-Three-Ten. The Ten of Hearts on the turn is an overcard to my pair, making the board more dangerous and potentially giving my opponent a better pair or a straight draw. Given the pot size and the fact that I have a marginal hand with showdown value, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the pot size and observe my opponent's action. If my opponent checks back, I get closer to a cheap showdown; if they bet, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking maintains flexibility without over-committing.

We’re the preflop aggressor and c-bet got called on A♠-8♠-3♦, which leaves Flash with a lot of Ax, spade draws, some 8x, and some sticky floats. The T♥ is mostly a blank relative to the main draws (doesn’t complete spades, doesn’t pair the board), so our perceived range remains strong. With no showdown value (Q♣7♣) and a clear stack advantage, I prefer a controlled second barrel to pick up folds from underpairs/8x/no-spade floats and to deny equity, while keeping risk contained. If raised, we comfortably fold; if called, we’ll often shut down on many rivers (especially spades) and choose bluff rivers selectively on good bricks.

On the turn, the board is 3d-As-8s-Th. I have middle pair (Eights) with a Jack kicker. My opponent, FiveTwo, has been consistently aggressive throughout the match. They bet 700 into a pot of 1500, which offers me roughly 3:1 odds to call. Given FiveTwo's high frequency of betting and the possibility that they are semi-bluffing with a spade draw or a lower pair/straight draw, folding middle pair here would be too tight. I will call to see the river and evaluate the situation then, as my hand still has significant showdown value in a heads-up match against an aggressive opponent.

On the river, I have improved to Two Pair (Jacks and Eights) with an Ace kicker on a board of As-8s-3d-Th-Js. While Two Pair is typically strong in heads-up play, the board is now quite coordinated: there are three spades (As, 8s, Js), which completes a potential flush for any two spades in my opponent's hand, and several straights are possible (KQ or Q9). Furthermore, in a heads-up match where my opponent has been very aggressive, their range is heavily weighted toward Aces. While I beat a single pair of Aces, I lose to any two-pair combination involving an Ace (such as AJ, AT, A8, or A3), which are all hands they would likely play this way. Given the board's texture and the likelihood that I am either far ahead of a single pair/bluff or far behind a better two-pair/flush/straight, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot and reach a showdown safely.

River brings the third spade (A♠ 8♠ 3♦ T♥ J♠), which is a very bad card to bluff after getting called on flop and turn. Flash’s continuing range contains a lot of spade draws that now got there, plus plenty of Ax that won’t fold to a river bet. Since we have no spade and essentially no showdown value, betting risks running into a made flush very often and getting snapped, while not generating enough folds from the hands that missed. Best is to check and realize whatever thin chance we have that he checks back (or to fold if he bets big).

I am in the Big Blind with 42o, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Based on the current pot of 150 and the 'To Call: 50', it appears the Small Blind has raised to 150. While the pot odds of 5:1 (50 to call into a total potential pot of 300) might mathematically justify a call with almost any two cards in a heads-up scenario, 42o is extremely difficult to play profitably out of position, especially against a chip leader who has shown significant aggression. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament and a substantial stack of nearly 200 BBs, I prefer to fold this junk hand and wait for a better spot to close the gap on the chip leader rather than potentially losing more chips on a marginal flop with zero connectivity.

Heads-up, J8 suited is well above the continuing threshold versus a small button/SB open, and we’re getting a great price. Rather than just call and play out of position without initiative, I prefer a frequent 3-bet in this spot: it pressures a likely wide opening range, leverages our big chip lead, denies equity, and lets us win the pot immediately a lot given Villain’s tendency to fold to aggression in prior hands. Size it to a clean, playable amount that still keeps worse hands in and doesn’t bloat the pot unnecessarily at this depth.

I am on the button with T4o, which is a weak hand even in a heads-up situation. My opponent, FiveTwo, has a significant chip lead and has raised to 500 pre-flop (5x the big blind). T4o is not a hand I want to play out of position or even as a caller against such a large sizing when the opponent has been aggressive. Given that we are late in the tournament (Hand 143/150) and I need to preserve my stack to potentially overtake the lead or just maintain my position, folding is the most prudent play here.

In Hand 144, I hold Kc 7d in the Big Blind. The current state indicates a 'To Call' amount of 50 and a 'Min Raise To' of 200, with a pot of 150. This implies that FiveTwo (the Button/SB) has raised to 150 total (his 50 SB plus a 100 raise). K7o is a strong enough hand in a heads-up match to defend against a small raise, especially with a 195 BB stack. Folding is out of the question for only 50 chips, and while raising is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my hand's equity without bloating the pot out of position against a significantly larger stack. Therefore, I will call the additional 50 chips, bringing my total commitment for the street to 150.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with a big chip lead, we should apply constant preflop pressure and steal the blind very frequently. 9-3o is weak but still within a profitable HU opening range, especially in position versus an opponent who has shown plenty of folding to raises. I’ll use a small-to-middling open size to keep risk controlled while maximizing fold equity and initiative going to the flop.

In Hand 144, I am in the Big Blind with King-Seven offsuit. My opponent in the Small Blind has raised to 300. This is a heads-up situation where King-high hands are quite strong and certainly worth defending against a standard raise. With the blinds at 50/100 and being deep-stacked, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without over-committing out of position. I need to call 200 more chips to match the raise of 300.

Heads-up raised pot and we have the range/initiative advantage on Q-6-3 rainbow. This board hits our preflop raising range (Qx, overpairs) more than a limp/call range, so a small c-bet should take it down very often versus unpaired hands (A-high, K-high, 4x/5x, random floats) and also denies equity. With bottom pair (3s) and a weak kicker, I don’t want to bloat the pot, so I’ll use a small sizing and be ready to react cautiously if raised.

On a flop of Q-6-3 rainbow, my King-high (Kc 7d) has some showdown value but is mostly a weak holding. FiveTwo has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600. While I have two overcards to the 6 and 3, I am behind any Queen, any pair, and I am out of position. However, in a heads-up match, a 1/3 pot bet is often just a standard exploratory bet. Given the pot odds and the fact that I only need to call 200 to win a total pot of 800 (4:1 odds), I have enough equity to peel one card to see if I improve on the turn or if the opponent checks back. Folding now would be too exploitable in this heads-up dynamic.

Flop c-bet got called, so Flash’s range is now weighted toward at least some pair (Qx/6x/8x/medium pockets) plus a few A-high floats. With bottom pair (3s) and weak kicker, a turn bet mostly folds out the hands we already beat (A/K-high) and gets called/raised by better hands, which is a bad tradeoff. Checking back keeps the pot manageable, realizes our showdown value, and protects our overall strategy by not over-bluffing this texture after receiving flop resistance.

In Hand 144, I am holding Kc 7d on a board of 3d-Qh-6c-8s. I am out of position in the Big Blind. The action on the turn has gone check-check after FiveTwo (the Button) checked behind. King-high currently has some showdown value in a heads-up match, as my opponent could be C-betting the flop with air and then giving up on the turn. Since I have no pair and very little draw potential, betting here is unlikely to get better hands to fold and might only get called by hands that beat me. Checking is the most prudent action to keep the pot small and attempt to reach a cheap showdown or see a favorable river card.

River checks to us in a raised HU pot after we c-bet flop and checked back turn. Our hand (pair of 3s with a 9 kicker) has some showdown value versus missed A/K-high floats, but is behind virtually all of Flash’s flop-calling pairs (Qx, 6x, 8x, Tx, pocket pairs) and even some improved hands/straights on this runout. Betting doesn’t get called by worse often and won’t fold out better pairs at a good frequency—especially since our turn check caps our perceived strength and invites bluff-catches. Best play is to take the free showdown and avoid spewing into a range that’s mostly ahead of us.

In Hand 144, I am holding Kc 7d in the Big Blind. The board is 3d-Qh-6c-8s-Td. Throughout the hand, FiveTwo (the Small Blind) has shown some aggression by raising pre-flop and betting the flop, but he checked the turn and has now checked the river. In this specific tournament's heads-up format, the Small Blind appears to be acting first post-flop. Since FiveTwo has checked the river, my King-high has a small amount of showdown value, and there is little to be gained by betting. FiveTwo likely has a weak hand (like missed straight draws) or a small pair/Ace-high that he is checking for showdown value. Betting would risk a check-raise or being called by better hands like an Ace-high or a small pair. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (hand 144 of 150) and I have a decent stack to protect, I will check and take the free showdown.

Heads-up BB vs SB open, AA is the top of range and wants to build the pot immediately while still keeping weaker hands (broadways, suited connectors, worse aces, pocket pairs) in. Flatting gives up initiative and lets Flash realize equity cheaply. Given deep-ish effective stacks (~190bb), I’ll use a value 3-bet size that’s big enough to punish but not so big that it folds out too much of his opening range. If he 4-bets, I’m continuing (mix of calling or 5-betting depending on size) with the intention to play for stacks.

I am currently the short stack in a heads-up situation with only 5 hands remaining in the tournament. My opponent, FiveTwo, has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively. In this hand, I am in the Small Blind/Button position. After presumably limping or calling the big blind, FiveTwo has raised to 550. My hand, Queen of Clubs and Five of Diamonds (Qc 5d), is a weak starting hand that is easily dominated by my opponent's raising range. Given the size of the raise (5.5x the big blind) and my stack size, calling here would commit a significant portion of my chips to a pot where I am likely behind and have poor playability. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to move all-in or find a double-up in the remaining hands.

I am in the big blind with T8o. The opponent in the small blind has just completed the bet, leaving me with the option to check and see a flop for free or raise to take down the pot immediately. Given the large stack disadvantage (18,900 vs 40,950) and the proximity to the end of the tournament (Hand 146 of 150), I prefer to see a flop cheaply with a hand that has decent connectivity and high-card value. Raising might induce a 3-bet from a leader looking to apply pressure, whereas checking allows me to realize my equity and potentially outplay the opponent post-flop.

Heads-up and effective ~18.8k, K5s is well ahead of a typical limp range and plays profitably with initiative. Flash has shown a tendency to limp/fold and to give up to pressure; raising also denies equity to his junk and lets us win a lot of pots pre/post. Versus the limp, I’ll punish with a bigger isolation size rather than just checking and letting him realize for free.

I am in the big blind with T8o. The opponent in the small blind has raised to 450. I am getting roughly 2.5:1 on a call (350 to win a current pot of 550, which will be 900 after I call). In a heads-up match, T8o is well within the defending range against a standard open. Given that we are only 5 hands away from the end of the tournament and I have a significant stack disadvantage, I want to see a flop and realize my equity without bloat-folding or taking an unnecessarily high-variance line like 3-betting pre-flop out of position.

Heads-up in position after raising pre, this paired broadway flop (Q-J-J) favors my perceived range (more strong Qx/Jx and overpairs) and is difficult for BB’s limp/call range to continue with total air. With Kh5h I have mostly K-high plus a backdoor heart flush possibility, so I don’t want to check and let him realize equity for free. A small c-bet pressures his weakest holdings efficiently, keeps my sizing consistent across range on a static board, and sets up barrels on good turns (A/K/T or hearts).

On a board of Qd-Jh-Js, my T8o has very little equity. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9), a straight is extremely vulnerable on a paired board where any Jack has me crushed with trips and any Queen has me beaten with two pair. The opponent's 1/3 pot c-bet is standard, but given the coordination of the board and the fact that my 'outs' are potentially 'dirty' (not necessarily the best hand even if they hit), I choose to fold. With only five hands remaining in the tournament and a significant stack deficit, I need to preserve my chips for a higher-equity opportunity rather than chasing a weak draw on a dangerous board.

Heads-up vs a small blind limp, AcTc is well ahead of Villain’s limping range and benefits from taking initiative. Raising also denies equity to random suited/connectors and creates immediate fold equity while building a pot for value when we flop top pair/top kicker or strong draws. With stacks still deep (~90BB effective), I’ll use a standard isolation size that pressures the limp without overinflating the pot out of position.

In heads-up play, 87o is a reasonable hand to play, especially with the advantage of position post-flop as the Button/Small Blind. The opponent (FiveTwo) has been playing very aggressively with large pre-flop raises in recent hands (Hand 146, 145, 144). Calling the 4.5x raise (350 more to call) allows me to see a flop with a connected hand while keeping the pot manageable. Given that there are only 4 hands remaining in the tournament and I am significantly behind in total chips, I need to look for opportunities to win pots, but I must also be cautious of the opponent's high pressure. Calling here is a standard play in position.

In heads-up play, on a paired board of 4c-4d-Ts, my 8-high has very little showdown value. FiveTwo, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has checked to me. This check could signal a missed hand (like overcards) or a trap with a 4 or a Ten. Given that I am behind in the tournament with only four hands remaining, I need to be selective about where I commit chips. Betting here as a bluff is risky against an opponent who has shown significant aggression. By checking back, I take a free card and a chance to improve to a pair on the turn without increasing the pot size. If the turn remains favorable or I pick up equity, I can reconsider my strategy. For now, checking is the most prudent play.

On a paired, dry flop (4c4dTs) after I raised preflop, my range has the overpairs/top-pair combos and I should c-bet frequently. With AcTc I have top pair/top kicker and want value from Tx worse kickers, 9x/underpairs that peeled pre, and broadways that will float once. A small continuation bet also denies equity to two-overcard hands while keeping my range wide and not bloating the pot unnecessarily vs a check-raise.

I have 8-high on a paired board (4-4-T) with no draws or significant equity. FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 300 into a 900 pot (1/3 pot size). While this small bet might often be a continuation bet with air, my hand has virtually no showdown value and very few ways to improve. Given that I am significantly behind in total chips with only four hands remaining in the tournament, I must be selective about the pots I contest. Attempting to float or bluff an aggressive opponent on this board is unnecessarily risky. Folding allows me to preserve my stack for a better spot in the final hands.

We are at hand 148 of 150 in the tournament. I am currently the short stack with 18,000 chips against FiveTwo's 41,850. In this heads-up situation, defending the big blind with a hand like Jack-Eight offsuit (J8o) is standard, especially against what appears to be a very small raise (50 chips to call). My goal is to see a flop and find an opportunity to chip away at the leader's stack before the tournament ends in three hands. Given the pot odds, calling here to see the flop is the most appropriate strategic move.

Heads-up, A7 suited is well ahead of a limp/check range and plays very well with initiative. With a big stack edge, I want to keep applying pressure, deny equity to random overcards/connectors, and build pots where my ace-high advantage realizes. I’ll raise to a size that wins the pot often preflop and still gets called by worse aces, broadways, and suited hands.

We are at hand 148 of 150. I have Jack-Eight offsuit (Js 8h) in the big blind. The chip leader, FiveTwo, has raised to 450. In a heads-up situation, J8o is a solid hand to defend the blind with. Folding would be too conservative given my need to make up ground in these final three hands, while raising here might be unnecessarily risky against a larger stack. Calling the 350 to see the flop is the most balanced play, allowing me to evaluate the board and potentially win a pot without committing too many chips prematurely.

Paired, relatively dry flop (Q33) favors the preflop raiser’s range and is a great spot for a small range c-bet to fold out Flash’s whiffed broadways/connectors while keeping our sizing efficient. Ac7c has decent equity (often best as ace-high) plus backdoor nut-flush potential, and a small bet lets us apply pressure with the stack edge while comfortably folding if we face a raise representing trips/Qx.

We are on the flop (3c-Qh-3d) and I am holding J8o. The pre-flop action involved me defending the big blind against a raise. The current board is extremely dry and heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes all the strong Queens and potentially pocket pairs that have me crushed. I have no piece of this board and no draws to improve. FiveTwo has been consistently aggressive in these final hands, utilizing their chip lead to pressure me. Calling a bet here with only Jack-high is essentially 'chip spewing,' as I would have to bluff on a later street to win, which is high-risk against a deep-stacked opponent. With only three hands left in the tournament, I will fold to preserve my 17,600 chips and look for a more favorable opportunity to potentially double up or steal a pot in the final two hands.

Heads-up BB vs a very small SB open, we’re getting an excellent price (only 50 more) and should defend very wide. Tc3h is weak and not a great candidate to 3-bet bluff from out of position (it plays poorly when called and can get jammed on), but it realizes equity well enough at this price and can make a top pair with T or some disguised two-pair/straight textures. Calling keeps variance controlled while still capturing the EV from over-folds/over-bluffs postflop.

I am on the button with Ac 9s, a strong starting hand in heads-up play. FiveTwo, the chip leader, has just checked/called from the big blind (though it's actually the SB/button action in heads-up, the log indicates a 50/100 blind structure where I am the SB/button). I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage the strength of my Ace-high hand. This is the penultimate hand of the tournament and I need to maximize my stack.

We’re heads-up in the BB facing a 3x SB open to 300. With 100 already in, it’s 200 more to win a 400 pot, so we’re getting 2:1 immediate odds and should defend very wide versus a likely high-frequency button open. Tc3h is not a good 3-bet candidate (no blockers, plays poorly when called, and creates a bloated pot OOP), but it has enough raw equity and can realize via cheap flops (pairing a T, some low/connected boards, occasional backdoors). Calling keeps Villain’s range wide and lets us outplay postflop while taking the clearly favorable price.

I have flopped Top Two Pair (Aces and Nines) on a board with a potential heart flush draw. In heads-up play, the Big Blind (FiveTwo) typically acts first on the flop. Since no action is recorded for him yet, I will assume he has checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. With a very strong hand, I want to bet for value and to charge any potential draws or lower pairs (like an Ace with a worse kicker or a Nine). A bet of 350 into a 600 pot is a standard sizing to build the pot while keeping his range wide.

Flop A♥9♥2♠ and Villain fires small (350 into 950 ~37% pot). With Tc3h we have very weak immediate showdown value, but we’re getting a reasonable price and we do have some equity/backdoors: 6 clean-ish outs to pair (T or 3) plus a backdoor flush possibility with the 3♥ on a two-heart board. Versus a likely high-frequency c-bet range in heads-up (lots of broadway/air), continuing keeps his range wide and lets us realize equity; we can comfortably fold to significant turn pressure on bricks and continue on hearts/pair turns or when checked to.

Raising here would be a low-quality bluff (no key blockers, poor playability if called), so the best EV/variance tradeoff is to call.

I have Top Two Pair (Aces and Nines) on a board that now has two flush draws (hearts and spades). I am in position as the button. My opponent has checked to me on the turn. I should continue betting for value to get paid by weaker Aces (Ax), flush draws, or potentially a Nine. A bet of 850 into a 1300 pot (approx. 65% pot) is a solid sizing to build the pot while keeping my opponent's calling range wide. Given the tournament is almost over and I am trailing in chips, I must prioritize maximizing value with my strong hands.

Turn is a brick for us: Tc3h lost the only meaningful backdoor (no heart on turn), leaving us with essentially just 6 weak “pair up” outs (and even when we hit a T/3, we’re often still behind Ax/9x). Facing 850 into a 2150 pot, we need ~28% equity to continue, but our real equity versus a turn-barrel range (Ax, 9x, strong draws, and some bluffs) is far below that.

Turning this into a bluff-raise is also poor: we don’t block his value (Ax) or his natural continues (spade draws), and a raise invites a shove where we’re forced to fold after lighting chips on fire. Best EV is to concede now and preserve our big stack for the final hand.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am in the Big Blind with K3o and the action is on me after a small complete/raise from FiveTwo. Given the stack sizes and it being the final hand, there's no reason to play excessively tight, but I'm also significantly behind in the overall chip count. K3o is a decent enough hand to see a flop with in a heads-up situation where the pot is small. Calling the extra 50 is a standard move to defend the big blind and see if I can hit something on the flop to maximize my final stack.

Heads-up and facing a limp, T8o is well ahead of a typical limp range and benefits a lot from taking the initiative. Raising also denies equity to a bunch of overcards/connectors that realize too easily in limped pots. I’ll use a standard vs-limp isolation size that pressures his range without bloating the pot too much against a shorter stack.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am in the Big Blind with King-Three offsuit. The opponent in the Small Blind has raised to 300. I have already posted the big blind of 100, so it costs me 200 more to call into a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 pot odds. In a heads-up situation, a King-high hand is well within the defending range against a button/small blind raise. Given that this is the last opportunity to increase my stack, and I have a respectable hand for heads-up play, I will call to see the flop and look for an opportunity to win a final pot.

Heads-up SRP after iso-raise, this Q-high rainbow board is a good spot to apply range pressure with a small c-bet. My T8o has very little showdown value and only thin backdoors, so checking back just lets his overcards/weak pairs realize equity for free. A ~1/3 pot bet targets folds from A/K-high, suited connectors that missed, and some low pairs, while keeping my risk low versus a check-raise (easy fold if he shows real strength).

In this final hand of the tournament, I defended my big blind with King-Three offsuit. The flop of Q-5-2 has completely missed my hand, leaving me with only King-high and no significant draws. FiveTwo has made a standard 1/3 pot continuation bet. While King-high can sometimes have showdown value in heads-up play, calling here is speculative as I am likely behind any Queen, any pocket pair, or even some better Ace-high hands. Given that my objective is to maximize my final stack size and this is the last hand, I choose to fold and preserve my remaining 17,650 chips rather than making a marginal call that would likely lead to further losses on the turn.
